text: It was Mr. Britling's habit, a habit he had set himself to acquire after much irritating experience, to disregard Aunt Wilshire. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt; she was one of those distant cousins we find already woven into our lives when we attain to years of responsibility. She had been a presence in his father's household when Mr. Britling was a boy. Then she had been called "Jane," or "Cousin Jane," or "Your cousin Wilshire." It had been a kindly freak of Mr. Britling's to promote her to Aunty rank.

Ignoring Aunt Wilshire was a deliberate habit Mr. Britling had cultivated after years of exasperating encounters with her, a routine he adhered to reliably. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt at all; she was one of those distant cousins who seem to have already been woven into the fabric of our lives by the time we reach an age of adult responsibility. She had been a steady presence in his father’s household when Mr. Britling was a boy, back when she went simply by “Jane,” “Cousin Jane,” or occasionally “your cousin Wilshire.” Elevating her to the formal rank of Aunt had been a gentle, playful whim of Mr. Britling’s, later on.

About half a dozen men came forward; and, one being selected by the magistrate, he deposed that he had been out fishing the night before with his son and brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they observed a strong northerly blast rising, and they accordingly put in for port. It was a very dark night, as the moon had not yet risen; they did not land at the harbour, but, as they had been accustomed, at a creek about two miles below. He walked on first, carrying a part of the fishing tackle, and his companions followed him at some distance. As he was proceeding along the sands, he struck his foot against something and fell at his length on the ground. His companions came up to assist him, and by the light of their lantern they found that he had fallen on the body of a man, who was to all appearance dead. Their first supposition was that it was the corpse of some person who had been drowned and was thrown on shore by the waves, but on examination they found that the clothes were not wet and even that the body was not then cold. They instantly carried it to the cottage of an old woman near the spot and endeavoured, but in vain, to restore it to life. It appeared to be a handsome young man, about five and twenty years of age. He had apparently been strangled, for there was no sign of any violence except the black mark of fingers on his neck.

About half a dozen men stepped forward, and when one was chosen by the magistrate, he stated under oath that he had been out fishing the previous night with his son and his brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they noticed a hard northerly wind beginning to rise, and so turned in for shore. It was a very dark night, the moon not yet having risen; they did not go in at the harbour, but, as they were accustomed to do, at a creek some two miles below it. He went on ahead, carrying part of the fishing gear, while the others followed at some distance behind. As he was making his way along the sands, his foot struck against something and he fell full length to the ground. His companions hurried up to help him, and by the light of their lantern they discovered that he had fallen upon the body of a man, who seemed to all appearances dead. At first they supposed it to be the corpse of someone drowned and washed ashore by the waves, but on closer inspection they found that the clothing was not wet, and that the body was not even cold. They immediately carried it to the cottage of an old woman nearby and tried, though without success, to bring it back to life. It seemed to be that of a handsome young man, about twenty-five years old. He had evidently been strangled, for there was no mark of injury save the black imprint of fingers around his neck.

But I believed I saw my chance at last. I would form this crack regiment out of officers alone--not a single private. Half of it should consist of nobles, who should fill all the places up to Major-General, and serve gratis and pay their own expenses; and they would be glad to do this when they should learn that the rest of the regiment would consist exclusively of princes of the blood. These princes of the blood should range in rank from Lieutenant-General up to Field Marshal, and be gorgeously salaried and equipped and fed by the state. Moreover--and this was the master stroke --it should be decreed that these princely grandees should be always addressed by a stunningly gaudy and awe-compelling title (which I would presently invent), and they and they only in all England should be so addressed. Finally, all princes of the blood should have free choice; join that regiment, get that great title, and renounce the royal grant, or stay out and receive a grant. Neatest touch of all: unborn but imminent princes of the blood could be _born_ into the regiment, and start fair, with good wages and a permanent situation, upon due notice from the parents.

At long last, I thought I’d spotted my perfect opportunity. I would build this elite crack regiment entirely from commissioned officers—no rank-and-file privates whatsoever. Half its ranks would be made up of nobles, who would hold every post up to Major-General, serve without pay, and cover all their own costs; I knew they would be eager to do so once they learned the other half of the regiment would be reserved exclusively for princes of the blood. Those royal princes would hold ranks ranging from Lieutenant General all the way up to Field Marshal, with lavish state-funded salaries, uniforms, equipment, and rations. What’s more, this was my master stroke: I would issue a decree that these princely grandees would always be addressed by a dazzling, awe-inspiring custom title I planned to invent shortly, and they would be the only people in all of England permitted to receive that honorific. Finally, every prince of the blood would get a free choice: join the regiment, claim the prestigious title, and renounce their regular royal grant, or stay out of the corps and receive the grant as usual. My cleverest little touch of all? Even unborn, soon-to-arrive princes of the blood could be born directly into the regiment, so they would enter the world with a guaranteed well-paying, permanent post, as long as their parents submitted proper advance notice.

Mr. Bumble’s conduct on being left to himself, was rather inexplicable. He opened the closet, counted the teaspoons, weighed the sugar-tongs, closely inspected a silver milk-pot to ascertain that it was of the genuine metal, and, having satisfied his curiosity on these points, put on his cocked hat corner-wise, and danced with much gravity four distinct times round the table.

Mr. Mudge’s demeanor, when at last he was left to his own devices, was quite remarkable. He pried open the buffet, took a census of the teaspoons, appraised the weight of the sugar-grippers, and peered intently at a silver cream-pot to ensure it was of the genuine sort; then, having pacified his avarice, he set his three-cornered hat at a tilt and pranced with profound solemnity exactly four times around the mahogany.

Amidst the darknesses and confusions, the nightmare cruelties and the hideous stupidities of the great war, God, the Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to empire. So long as one did one's best and utmost in a cause so mighty, did it matter though the thing one did was little and poor?

There was no cause for despair, for he counted himself among the world’s feeble, unassuming souls. Truly, God abided with him, and he abided in God. The King was coming to claim what was rightfully his. Amid the darkness and disarray, the nightmarish cruelties and grotesque, senseless folly of the great war, God, Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to rightful empire. As long as one gave their full, unreserved effort to such a mighty cause, did the smallness and plainness of the task they carried out really hold any weight?

The marriage is unalterable, my life may be cut short, and I should wish to die in peace. He is my only son; and since sons are made of such stuff I am not sorry I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never expected much from her; and she has not disappointed me. But I forgave her long ago; and I forgive him now. I’ll go.”

“Well, since matters have reached this pass,” Mrs. Yeobright said mournfully, “I will confess to you, reddleman, that I had considered going. I would be far easier in my heart if we made peace. The marriage cannot be undone, my days may be few, and I long to die in charity. He is my only son; and seeing what sons are made of, I am not grieved that I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never looked for much from her, and she has not failed my expectation. But I pardoned her long ago, and I pardon him now. I shall go.”

I was at the well next day betimes. Merlin was there, enchanting away like a beaver, but not raising the moisture. He was not in a pleasant humor; and every time I hinted that perhaps this contract was a shade too hefty for a novice he unlimbered his tongue and cursed like a bishop--French bishop of the Regency days, I mean.

I showed up at the weathered stone well bright and early the next morning. Kaelen was already there, chanting incantations as busily as a beaver piling dam sticks, but not a single drop of moisture rose from the dry basin. His mood was thoroughly sour; every time I dropped a gentle hint that the contract he’d signed on for might be a little too hefty for an inexperienced spellcaster, he let loose a volley of curses coarse enough to match a bishop – a French Regency bishop, that is, the sort far fonder of wine and debauchery than pious prayer.

I tell you how we’ll manage,” said the other, who perhaps felt it would be extremely awkward to offend his leading man just at this time. “I won’t tell ’em anything about your keeping silence; go on with the piece and say nothing, doing what you can by a judicious wink now and then, and a few indomitable nods in the heroic places, you know. They’ll never find out that the speeches are omitted.”

“Alright then, let me think. I’ve got the perfect way we can make this work,” the other man said, likely well aware that crossing his leading actor right now would be deeply awkward. “I won’t say a single thing to anyone about you staying silent. Just carry on with the performance as usual, don’t utter a line, toss in a well-timed wink every now and again, and a few resolute, grand nods during the heroic beats, you know what I mean. They’ll never catch on that all your speeches are cut.”

We further verify the impact of different training objectives in Table 1 . Compared with the INTL baseline, the REC training objective can further improve the translation quality of both supervised and zero-shot language pairs. However, the SIM objective contributes to zero-shot translation quality significantly, with a slight decrease in supervised language pairs. The integration of both REC and SIM in INTL ultimately achieves balance increments between supervised and zero-shot language pairs. This suggests that constraints on Interlingua can lead to better intermediate semantic representations and translation quality.

metadata.title: Language-aware Interlingua for Multilingual Neural Machine Translation

text: We further examine the effect of different training objectives in Table 1. Relative to the INTL baseline, the REC training objective yields additional gains in translation quality for both supervised and zero-shot language pairs. By contrast, the SIM objective substantially improves zero-shot translation performance, while causing a slight degradation on supervised language pairs. Combining REC and SIM within INTL ultimately produces balanced improvements across supervised and zero-shot language pairs. These results suggest that imposing constraints on Interlingua can facilitate better intermediate semantic representations and, consequently, higher translation quality.

Little Lucie sat by her grandfather with her hands clasped through his arm: and he, in a tone not rising much above a whisper, began to tell her a story of a great and powerful Fairy who had opened a prison-wall and let out a captive who had once done the Fairy a service. All was subdued and quiet, and Lucie was more at ease than she had been.

Miss Pross had lit the lamp but pushed it into a shadowy corner so they could savor the firelight without interruption. Young Lucie nestled next to her grandfather, her small fingers laced around his arm, while he, in a voice barely above a murmur, spun her a tale about a mighty Fairy who had shattered a prison wall to free a prisoner who had once helped the Fairy. Everything felt hushed and serene, and Lucie felt more at peace than she had in some time.

Impossible, here in raging Paris, with Suspicion filling the air, for you to outlive denunciation, when you are in communication with another aristocratic spy of the same antecedents as yourself, who, moreover, has the mystery about him of having feigned death and come to life again! A plot in the prisons, of the foreigner against the Republic. A strong card--a certain Guillotine card! Do you play?”

“Well, I’ll tell you what I notice,” Carton remarked. “I’ve got another card to play, Mr. Barsad. In this feverish Paris, thick with mistrust, you can’t possibly survive being exposed—not while you’re dealing with another noble-born informer just like yourself, one who carries the added puzzle of having pretended to die and then reappeared alive! A foreign conspiracy brewing inside the prisons, aimed at the Republic. A powerful card—a sure ticket to the guillotine! Will you take the gamble?”

He was on Broadway, surrounded by pomp and style, and there were eyes to look at him. On the stage of that comedy he had assumed to play the one-night part of a butterfly of fashion and an idler of means and taste. He was dressed for the part, and all his good angels had not the power to prevent him from acting it.

At that instant, the New York mania for status and the infectious drive to show off took hold of Towers Chandler. There on Broadway, amidst the glitter and the gaze of the crowd, he found himself on a stage of his own making. He had decided to spend one night portraying a wealthy man of fashion and taste. Clad in the perfect attire, he was beyond the reach of his better judgment; he was committed to the performance, and nothing could stop him from playing the role.

Almost for the first time in his life, Troy, as he stood by this dismantled grave, wished himself another man. It is seldom that a person with much animal spirit does not feel that the fact of his life being his own is the one qualification which singles it out as a more hopeful life than that of others who may actually resemble him in every particular. Troy had felt, in his transient way, hundreds of times, that he could not envy other people their condition, because the possession of that condition would have necessitated a different personality, when he desired no other than his own. He had not minded the peculiarities of his birth, the vicissitudes of his life, the meteor-like uncertainty of all that related to him, because these appertained to the hero of his story, without whom there would have been no story at all for him; and it seemed to be only in the nature of things that matters would right themselves at some proper date and wind up well. This very morning the illusion completed its disappearance, and, as it were, all of a sudden, Troy hated himself. The suddenness was probably more apparent than real. A coral reef which just comes short of the ocean surface is no more to the horizon than if it had never been begun, and the mere finishing stroke is what often appears to create an event which has long been potentially an accomplished thing.

Perhaps for the first time since his youth, Julian, as he lingered beside the disturbed earth of the grave, felt a profound longing to be any man but himself. It is a rare occurrence for one possessed of such vital energy not to feel that the simple fact of his own identity is the very quality that renders his life more promising than the lives of those who might otherwise mirror him. Julian had realized, in his shallow fashion, countless times before, that he could never truly envy the station of another; to possess their world would require a different spirit, and he had never wished for any spirit but his own. He had been indifferent to the scandals of his birth, the turbulence of his career, and the flickering instability of his future, for these were merely the attributes of the central figure in a drama that would be nothing without him; it seemed only natural that the plot would eventually resolve itself into a graceful end. On this particular morning, however, the deception vanished, and Julian was seized by a sudden, sharp hatred for his own being. The immediacy of this change was more illusion than fact. A bank of coral that stops just short of the tide is no more visible to the sailor than if it did not exist, and the final, crowning growth is often mistaken for the creation of a thing that has, in truth, been building toward completion for a long time.

Old Pa’son Williams lifted his hands in his great holy surplice as natural as if he’d been in common clothes, and seemed to say hisself, ‘Oh for such a man in our parish!’ But not a soul in Kingsbere could hold a candle to Yeobright.”

Necks would crane instinctively, and the whisper would go round, “Indeed, I knew it could be none other!” I recall one particular Sabbath vividly—a day for the double bass, and Miller had carried in his own great timbered beast. We were set to the Hundred-and-thirty-third, sung to the tune of ‘Lydia’; and upon reaching the verse, ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbor Miller, now fully caught in the fervor of his craft, plunged his bow across the strings with such magnificent violence that he very nearly cleaved the instrument in twain. Each pane of glass in the sanctuary shuddered as though a tempest had broken overhead. Aged Reverend Thorne raised his arms within his voluminous white vestments with a gesture so unstudied it seemed he wore but his everyday coat, appearing to murmur to his own heart, ‘Would that every parish were blessed with such a talent!’ Yet, in all of Oakhaven, there was not a single person who could hope to rival Miller.

The match tugged back and forth, tipping first to one opponent, then the next, with neither ever pulling far enough ahead to claim a clear upper hand. Close to twenty minutes slipped by in this even, tense rhythm. By now, the guttering candle glow had lured moor gnats, miller moths, and all manner of small winged night creatures, which drifted in lazy loops around the glass lantern, dove straight into the licking flame, or bumped and skittered across the faces of the two competitors.

The tenderest and softest phases of Bathsheba’s nature were prominent now—advanced impulsively for his acceptance, without any of the disguises and defences which the wariness of her character when she was cool too frequently threw over them. Few men could have resisted the arch yet dignified entreaty of the beautiful face, thrown a little back and sideways in the well known attitude that expresses more than the words it accompanies, and which seems to have been designed for these special occasions. Had the woman not been his wife, Troy would have succumbed instantly; as it was, he thought he would not deceive her longer.

Now the gentlest and most yielding aspects of Bathsheba’s heart came to the fore—offered freely for his taking, stripped of the guardedness and pretense that her cooler prudence so often cast over them. Few men could have withstood that lovely face, tilted slightly back and aside in the familiar pose that says more than the words it frames, a look seemingly made for just such moments. Had she not been his wife, Troy would have yielded at once; as things stood, he resolved he would deceive her no more.

Like GPT-2, DIALOGPT is formulated as an autoregressive (AR) language model, and uses the multi-layer transformer as model architecture. Unlike GPT-2, however, DIALOGPT is trained on large-scale dialogue pairs/sessions extracted from Reddit discussion chains. Our assumption is that this should enable DIALOGPT to capture the joint distribution of P (Target, Source) in conversational flow with finer granularity. In practice, this is what we observe: sentences generated by DIALOGPT are diverse and contain information specific to the source prompt, analogous what GPT-2 generates for continuous text. We have evaluated the pre-trained model on a public benchmark dataset (DSTC-7), and a new 6k multireference test dataset extracted from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT achieves state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, lifting performance to near-human response quality.

metadata.title: DIALOGPT : Large-Scale Generative Pre-training for Conversational Response Generation

text: Similar to GPT-2, DIALOGPT is structured as an autoregressive (AR) language model, with a multi-layer transformer as its core architectural backbone. It diverges from GPT-2, however, in its training corpus, which consists of large-scale dialogue pairs and full conversational sessions extracted from public Reddit discussion threads. We hypothesize that this training data configuration allows DIALOGPT to model the joint probability distribution P(Target, Source) of turn-taking in conversational flows at a finer level of granularity. Empirical results align with this hypothesis: outputs generated by DIALOGPT are diverse, contain information specific to the input source prompt, and are analogous to the coherent outputs GPT-2 produces for long-form continuous text. We evaluated the pre-trained DIALOGPT model across two test sets: the publicly available DSTC-7 benchmark dataset, and a newly curated 6k multi-reference test set sourced from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT attains state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, pushing overall performance to near-human conversational response quality levels.

“Strange notions, has he?” said the old man. “Ah, there’s too much of that sending to school in these days! It only does harm. Every gatepost and barn’s door you come to is sure to have some bad word or other chalked upon it by the young rascals—a woman can hardly pass for shame sometimes. If they’d never been taught how to write they wouldn’t have been able to scribble such villainy. Their fathers couldn’t do it, and the country was all the better for it.”

Every gatepost and barn door you come to is sure to have some foul word or other chalked on it by the young rascals—a woman can scarcely pass without shame at times. If they had never been taught to write, they would never have been able to scrawl such wickedness. Their fathers could not do it, and the country was all the better for that.”

I threw myself into the chaise that was to convey me away and indulged in the most melancholy reflections. I, who had ever been surrounded by amiable companions, continually engaged in endeavouring to bestow mutual pleasure—I was now alone. In the university whither I was going I must form my own friends and be my own protector. My life had hitherto been remarkably secluded and domestic, and this had given me invincible repugnance to new countenances. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself totally unfitted for the company of strangers. Such were my reflections as I commenced my journey; but as I proceeded, my spirits and hopes rose. I ardently desired the acquisition of knowledge. I had often, when at home, thought it hard to remain during my youth cooped up in one place and had longed to enter the world and take my station among other human beings. Now my desires were complied with, and it would, indeed, have been folly to repent.

I sank into the chaise that was to carry me away and gave myself up to the most sorrowful thoughts. I, who had always been surrounded by kind companions, forever occupied in trying to give and receive pleasure—I was now alone. At the university to which I was bound, I would have to make my own friends and serve as my own guardian. My life had so far been unusually quiet and domestic, and this had left me with an unconquerable dislike of unfamiliar faces. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself utterly ill suited to the company of strangers. Such were my thoughts as I began my journey; yet as I went on, my spirits and hopes gradually lifted. I was passionately eager to gain knowledge. At home, I had often felt it was cruel to spend my youth shut up in one place, and I had longed to go out into the world and take my place among other human beings. Now my wishes were being fulfilled, and it would truly have been foolish to regret it.

I'll book you both for my colony; you'll like it there; it's a Factory where I'm going to turn groping and grubbing automata into _men_.”

Well, it brings a body low to reckon what your sex can do in the way of self-sacrifice. I’ll enter you both for my settlement; you’ll be suited to it there. It is a Factory where I mean to turn fumbling, scrambling automata into men.”

As Ruth’s front door closed behind them and he came down the steps with her, he found himself greatly perturbed. It was not unalloyed bliss, taking her to the lecture. He did not know what he ought to do. He had seen, on the streets, with persons of her class, that the women took the men’s arms. But then, again, he had seen them when they didn’t; and he wondered if it was only in the evening that arms were taken, or only between husbands and wives and relatives.

As Ruth’s front door shut behind them and he descended the steps beside her, he felt himself deeply unsettled. Taking her to the lecture was not the pure delight he had imagined. He did not know what was proper for him to do. On the streets, among people of her class, he had observed that women took men by the arm. Yet he had also seen them when they did not, and he wondered whether such arm-taking was done only in the evening, or only among husbands and wives and relations.

I went swiftly back, saying not a word to anybody. I told Sandy this ghastly news. We could imagine no explanation that would begin to explain. Had there been an invasion? an earthquake? a pestilence? Had the nation been swept out of existence? But guessing was profitless. I must go--at once. I borrowed the king's navy--a “ship” no bigger than a steam launch--and was soon ready.

I returned at once, speaking to no one. When I told Sandy the dreadful tidings, we found ourselves utterly unable to account for it. Had some invasion struck? An earthquake? A plague? Had the entire kingdom been blotted from the earth? Speculation was useless. I had to leave immediately. I requisitioned the king’s navy—a vessel no larger than a steam launch—and made ready without delay.

Following Wu et al. (2019) , we also evaluate the effect of mixing a small percentage of real training data in our augmented training sets. We use a naive few-shot training strategy, where we directly add a portion of the original training data in the domain of interest to the training set. Fig. 4 plots the joint accuracy achieved on the new domain with the addition of different percentages of real training data. The results for 0% are the same as the zero-shot experiment. The advantage of the synthesized training data decreases as the percent of real data increases, because real data is more varied, informative, and more representative of the distribution in the test set. The impact of synthesized data is more pronounced for SUMBT than TRADE for all domains even with 5% real data, and it is significant for the "Attraction" domain with 10% real data. This suggests that SUMBT needs more data to train, due to having more parameters, but can utilize additional synthesized data better to improve its training.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: In line with Wu et al. (2019), we also assess the impact of incorporating a small fraction of real training data into our augmented training sets. We adopt a straightforward few-shot training approach, in which we directly add a portion of the original training data from the target domain to the training set. Figure 4 shows the joint accuracy attained on the new domain as varying percentages of real training data are introduced. The results at 0% correspond to the zero-shot setting. The benefit of the synthesized training data diminishes as the proportion of real data grows, since real data is more diverse, more informative, and better reflects the test-set distribution. The influence of synthesized data is more pronounced for SUMBT than for TRADE across all domains even when 5% real data is included, and it remains notable for the “Attraction” domain with 10% real data. This indicates that SUMBT, because of its larger parameter count, requires more data for training but can more effectively leverage additional synthesized data to enhance its performance.

“I was sitting on the churchyard wall when they came up, one from one way, the other from the other; and Miss Vye was walking thereabouts, looking at the headstones. As soon as they had gone in I went to the door, feeling I should like to see it, as I knew her so well. I pulled off my boots because they were so noisy, and went up into the gallery. I saw then that the parson and clerk were already there.”

I was perched on the churchyard boundary wall when the pair of them turned up, one coming from one end of the lane, the other from the opposite side; Miss Vye was strolling around the grounds not far off, pausing to study the inscriptions on the headstones. As soon as the two had stepped inside the church, I made my way over to the door, struck by a sudden urge to see what was happening inside, given how well I knew her. I pulled off my boots first because their hard soles would make far too much noise on the old floorboards, then crept up the stairs to the gallery. When I reached the top, I saw the parson and the parish clerk were already there.

Clym took the hand which was already bared for him—it was a favourite way with them to walk bare hand in bare hand—and led her through the ferns. They formed a very comely picture of love at full flush, as they walked along the valley that late afternoon, the sun sloping down on their right, and throwing their thin spectral shadows, tall as poplar trees, far out across the furze and fern. Eustacia went with her head thrown back fancifully, a certain glad and voluptuous air of triumph pervading her eyes at having won by her own unaided self a man who was her perfect complement in attainment, appearance, and age. On the young man’s part, the paleness of face which he had brought with him from Paris, and the incipient marks of time and thought, were less perceptible than when he returned, the healthful and energetic sturdiness which was his by nature having partially recovered its original proportions. They wandered onward till they reached the nether margin of the heath, where it became marshy and merged in moorland.

Kael curled his fingers around the hand she had already slipped free of its glove to offer him—bare palm to bare palm was their preferred way of strolling, after all—and guided her through the thick drifts of ferns. They made a strikingly tender portrait of love in its full, glowing bloom as they picked their way down the valley that soft late afternoon, the sun sinking low on their right side, stretching their thin, ghostly silhouettes as high as white poplars to reach far across the gorse and fern fronds at their feet. Liora held her head tilted back in a dreamy, unburdened arc, a bright, giddy, almost luxurious edge of triumph lighting her eyes: she had won this man entirely on her own merit, a partner who matched her perfectly in intellect, bearing, and years. As for the young man, the faint pallor he had carried home with him from Lyon, and the first faint lines of weariness and deep thinking etched into his face, were far less visible than they had been in the first weeks of his return; the hearty, vigorous ruggedness that was his birthright had slowly reclaimed much of its old hold on his features. They meandered further on until they came to the lowermost edge of the heath, where the ground grew soft and waterlogged, bleeding seamlessly into the wide, boggy moor beyond.

We tune hyperparameters of the methods described above on the validation set of WCEP-100 (Appendix A.2). We also test a simple abstractive baseline, SUBMODULAR + ABS: We first create an extractive multi-document summary with a maximum of 100 words using SUBMODULAR. We pass this summary as a pseudo-article to the abstractive bottom-up attention model (Gehrmann et al., 2018) to generate the final summary. We use an implementation from OpenNMT 9 with a model pretrained on the CNN/Daily Mail dataset. All tested methods apart from ORACLE (MULTI & SINGLE) observe the length limit of 40 tokens. Table 5 presents the results on the WCEP test set. The supervised methods TSR and BERTREG show advantages over unsupervised methods, but not by a large margin, which poses an interesting challenge for future work. The high extractive bounds defined by ORACLE (SINGLE) suggest that identifying important documents before summarization can be useful in this dataset. The dataset does not favor lead summaries: RANDOM LEAD is of low quality, and LEAD ORACLE has relatively low Fscores (although very high Recall). The SUBMOD-ULAR + ABS heuristic for applying a pre-trained abstractive model does not perform well. Figure 2 shows how the performance of several methods on the test set increases with different amounts of additional articles from Common Crawl. Using 10 additional articles causes a steep improvement compared to only using the original source articles from WCEP. However, using more than 100 articles only leads to minimal gains.

metadata.title: A Large-Scale Multi-Document Summarization Dataset from the Wikipedia Current Events Portal

text: We tune the hyperparameters of the previously described methods on the WCEP-100 validation set (see Appendix A.2). We also evaluate a straightforward abstractive baseline, SUBMODULAR + ABS: first, we construct an extractive multi-document summary capped at 100 words using SUBMODULAR. This summary is then fed as a pseudo-article into the abstractive bottom-up attention model (Gehrmann et al., 2018) to produce the final summary. We employ an OpenNMT 9 implementation with a model pretrained on the CNN/Daily Mail dataset. All tested methods, except ORACLE (MULTI and SINGLE), adhere to a 40-token length constraint. Table 5 reports the results on the WCEP test set. The supervised approaches TSR and BERTREG outperform the unsupervised ones, though the margin is modest, presenting an intriguing challenge for future investigation. The strong extractive upper bounds set by ORACLE (SINGLE) indicate that identifying key documents prior to summarization can be beneficial on this dataset. The dataset does not favor lead-based summaries: RANDOM LEAD yields low quality, and LEAD ORACLE achieves relatively low F-scores despite very high Recall. The SUBMODULAR + ABS heuristic for leveraging a pretrained abstractive model performs poorly. Figure 2 illustrates how the test-set performance of several methods improves when varying numbers of additional articles from Common Crawl are incorporated. Adding 10 extra articles yields a steep performance gain relative to using only the original WCEP source articles; however, including more than 100 articles brings only marginal improvements.

When the morning came at last, I was in a bad enough plight: seedy, drowsy, fagged, from want of sleep; weary from thrashing around, famished from long fasting; pining for a bath, and to get rid of the animals; and crippled with rheumatism. And how had it fared with the nobly born, the titled aristocrat, the Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise? Why, she was as fresh as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead; and as for a bath, probably neither she nor any other noble in the land had ever had one, and so she was not missing it. Measured by modern standards, they were merely modified savages, those people. This noble lady showed no impatience to get to breakfast--and that smacks of the savage, too. On their journeys those Britons were used to long fasts, and knew how to bear them; and also how to freight up against probable fasts before starting, after the style of the Indian and the anaconda. As like as not, Sandy was loaded for a three-day stretch.

By the time dawn finally broke, I was a total wreck: ragged, groggy, and drained from a lack of sleep; sore from tossing and turning, starving from the long fast, and dying for a bath to rid myself of the pests, all while being stiff with rheumatism. And how was the high-born lady, the titled aristocrat Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise, getting along? Well, she was as lively as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead. As for a bath, it is likely that neither she nor any other noble in the country had ever had one, so she didn't feel its absence. By modern standards, those people were essentially just slightly refined savages. This noblewoman showed no eagerness for breakfast—which is another primitive trait. During their travels, these Britons were used to long periods without food and knew how to endure them; they also knew how to gorge themselves in preparation for a fast, much like a native hunter or an anaconda. In all probability, Sandy had fueled up for a three-day trek.

“Of hotel? It's what you call hostel. The man that can do this miracle can keep hostel. I can do this miracle; I shall do this miracle; yet I do not try to conceal from you that it is a miracle to tax the occult powers to the last strain.”

“Of a hotel? That’s what you’d call a hostel. A man capable of such a marvel could run a hostel. I am capable of this marvel; I will perform this marvel; still, I won’t hide from you that it’s a marvel that will push occult powers to their absolute limit.”

But, though the Doctor tried hard, and never ceased trying, to get Charles Darnay set at liberty, or at least to get him brought to trial, the public current of the time set too strong and fast for him. The new era began; the king was tried, doomed, and beheaded; the Republic of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, declared for victory or death against the world in arms; the black flag waved night and day from the great towers of Notre Dame; three hundred thousand men, summoned to rise against the tyrants of the earth, rose from all the varying soils of France, as if the dragon’s teeth had been sown broadcast, and had yielded fruit equally on hill and plain, on rock, in gravel, and alluvial mud, under the bright sky of the South and under the clouds of the North, in fell and forest, in the vineyards and the olive-grounds and among the cropped grass and the stubble of the corn, along the fruitful banks of the broad rivers, and in the sand of the sea-shore. What private solicitude could rear itself against the deluge of the Year One of Liberty--the deluge rising from below, not falling from above, and with the windows of Heaven shut, not opened!

Even as the Doctor poured every ounce of his effort into securing Charles Darnay’s release, or at the very least getting him a formal court hearing, and never once let up in that pursuit, the roaring tide of public sentiment in that era moved far too quickly and powerfully for him to push against. A new age had dawned: the king was put on trial, condemned to death, and publicly beheaded. The Republic, whose rallying cry was Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, had declared that it would accept nothing short of total victory or utter destruction in its fight against the armed monarchies of the rest of the world. A pitch-black flag flew around the clock from the towering spires of Notre Dame Cathedral. Three hundred thousand men, called to rise up against the tyrants ruling every corner of the globe, had sprung up across every inch of French soil, as if the mythic dragon’s teeth had been scattered far and wide, bearing fruit just as readily on craggy hilltops and flat lowlands, in bare rock, gravel beds, and soft alluvial silt; under the blazing southern sun and the mist-shrouded northern clouds; across windswept moors and dense old forests, through sun-warmed vineyards, gnarled olive groves, pastures of clipped grass and golden corn stubble, along the lush banks of wide, winding rivers, and in the fine sand of the coastline. What small, personal worry could possibly hold its ground against the overwhelming flood of the first year of Liberty – a flood that swelled upward from the ground below rather than pouring down from the sky, with the windows of Heaven sealed shut rather than thrown open to unleash the waters?

Restless, shifting, fugacious as time itself is a certain vast bulk of the population of the red brick district of the lower West Side. Homeless, they have a hundred homes. They flit from furnished room to furnished room, transients forever—transients in abode, transients in heart and mind. They sing “Home, Sweet Home” in ragtime; they carry their _lares et penates_ in a bandbox; their vine is entwined about a picture hat; a rubber plant is their fig tree.

Unquiet, mutable, and as evanescent as time itself is a certain great multitude of the inhabitants of the red-brick quarter of the lower West Side. Homeless, yet lodged in a hundred dwellings, they pass from furnished room to furnished room, eternal wanderers—wanderers in their roofs, wanderers in their hearts and thoughts. They croon “Home, Sweet Home” to a syncopated tune; they bear their _lares et penates_ in a hatbox; their vine winds itself about a feathered hat; a rubber plant serves them for a fig tree.

Poor child! he slumped to the floor, twisted his silken limbs in one great straining contortion of pain, and was dead. Out of the old king was wrung an involuntary “O-h!” of compassion. The look he got, made him cut it suddenly short and not put any more hyphens in it. Sir Uwaine, at a sign from his mother, went to the anteroom and called some servants, and meanwhile madame went rippling sweetly along with her talk.

The miserable youth collapsed upon the stones, his slender frame knotting in a single, violent paroxysm of agony before he drew his final breath. A sharp, unbidden cry of pity broke from the elderly monarch’s lips, but a freezing glare from his consort caused him to stifle the sound instantly, severing his grief before it could find full voice. At a subtle gesture from his mother, Prince Gaheris withdrew to the vestibule to fetch the menials; meanwhile, the lady resumed her melodic conversation, her words flowing with a terrifying, untroubled grace.

We use templates to synthesize dialogues in a domain from an abstract dialogue model and a domain ontology. In this paper, we introduce dialogue model templates which specify with grammar rules how to generate a turn of a dialogue from a transition in the abstract model. They create possible agent and user utterances matching the agent and user dialogue acts in the transition, and they include a semantic function to ensure the utterances make sense given the input state. For example, the user should ask about a slot only if its value is not already known. The semantic function returns an output state that matches the semantics of the utterances. The slot values of the output state are used as the annotation for the turn when training the dialogue state tracker.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: We employ templates to generate dialogues in a domain from an abstract dialogue model and a domain ontology. In this paper, we introduce dialogue model templates, which use grammar rules to specify how to produce a dialogue turn from a transition in the abstract model. These templates generate possible agent and user utterances that correspond to the agent and user dialogue acts in the transition, and they incorporate a semantic function to ensure that the utterances are coherent with respect to the input state. For instance, the user should inquire about a slot only if its value is not already known. The semantic function produces an output state consistent with the semantics of the utterances. The slot values in the output state are then used as the annotation for the turn when training the dialogue state tracker.

It was not a reckless manner, the manner in which he said these words aloud under the fast-sailing clouds, nor was it more expressive of negligence than defiance. It was the settled manner of a tired man, who had wandered and struggled and got lost, but who at length struck into his road and saw its end.

It was not a wild gesture, the way he spoke those words into the racing sky, nor did it betray carelessness any more than rebellion. It was the steady bearing of a weary man, who had roamed and fought and lost his way, but who had finally found his path and glimpsed its close.

“And as such,” quoth Mr. Lorry, whom the counsel learned in the law had now shouldered back into the group, just as he had previously shouldered him out of it--“as such I will appeal to Doctor Manette, to break up this conference and order us all to our homes. Miss Lucie looks ill, Mr. Darnay has had a terrible day, we are worn out.”

“Therefore,” murmured Mr. Lorry, now nudged back into the circle by the very barrister who had earlier edged him aside—“therefore I shall petition Doctor Manette to dissolve this gathering and bid us each return to our dwellings. Miss Lucie appears unwell, Mr. Darnay has endured a harrowing day, and we are all spent.”
