We always labeled type annotation errors, but tolerated minor span annotation errors if they did not change the interpretation of the relation or the entity. The category context misinterpretation refers to cases where the sentential context of the arguments is misinterpreted by the model. We identify the following context problems: (1) Inverted arguments: the prediction is inverse to the correct relation, i.e. the model's prediction would be correct if head and tail were swapped. (2) Wrong arguments: the model incorrectly predicts a relation that holds between head or tail and an un-annotated entity mention in the context, therefore misinterpreting one annotated argument. (3) Linguistic distractor: the example contains words or phrases related to the predicted relation, however they do not connect to any of the arguments in a way justifying the prediction. (4) Factuality: the model ignores negation, speculation, future tense markers, etc. (5) Context ignored: the example does not contain sufficient linguistic evidence for the predicted relation except for the matching entity types. (6) Relation definition: the predicted relation could be inferred from the context using common sense or world knowledge, however the inference is prohibited by the guidelines (e.g. the spokesperson of an organization is not a top member/employee, or a work location is not a pointer to the employee's residence). 7No Relation: the model incorrectly predicts no relation even though there is sufficient linguistic evidence for the relation in the sentential context.

metadata.title: TACRED Revisited: A Thorough Evaluation of the TACRED Relation Extraction Task

text: We begin by presenting the outcomes of our manual misclassification study (Section 5.1), and then turn to the results from the automatic analysis (Section 5.2). Type annotation errors were consistently marked, whereas minor span annotation mistakes were accepted provided they did not alter the interpretation of the entity or the relation. The category context misinterpretation captures instances where the model fails to correctly understand the sentential context surrounding the arguments. We distinguish the following context-related issues: (1) Inverted arguments: the predicted relation is the reverse of the true relation—that is, the model’s output would be accurate if the head and tail entities were exchanged. (2) Wrong arguments: the model erroneously predicts a relation that actually holds between either the head or tail and some other, un-annotated entity mention in the context, thereby misreading one of the annotated arguments. (3) Linguistic distractor: the sentence contains lexical items or phrases associated with the predicted relation, yet these cues are not linked to any argument in a manner that supports the prediction. (4) Factuality: the model disregards indicators of negation, speculation, future tense, and similar signals. (5) Context ignored: apart from matching entity types, the example offers no substantial linguistic evidence for the predicted relation. (6) Relation definition: although the predicted relation could be inferred through common sense or world knowledge, such an inference is disallowed by the annotation guidelines (e.g., a spokesperson is not considered a top member/employee, or a work location does not imply the employee’s residence). (7) No Relation: the model incorrectly outputs no relation even when the sentential context provides adequate linguistic support for one.

In order to better understand the question generation quality, we manually check the sampled outputs, and list the 5 main error sources in Table 3 . Among them, "Semantic Error", "Redundant", and "Unanswerable" are noticeable errors for all models. However, we find that baselines have more unreasonable subject-predicate-object collocations (semantic errors) than our model. Especially, CGC-QG (B6) has the largest semantic error rate of 26.4% among the three methods; it tends to copy irrelevant contents from the input document. Our model greatly reduces such semantic errors to 8.3%, as we explicitly model the semantic relations between entities by introducing typed semantic graphs. The other noticeable error type is "Unanswerable"; i.e., the question is correct itself but cannot be answered by the passage. Again, CGC-QG remarkably produces more unanswerable questions than the other two models, and our model achieves comparable results with S2sa-at-mp-gsa (B4), likely due to the fact that answerability requires a deeper understanding of the document as well as commonsense knowledge. These issues cannot be fully addressed by incorporating semantic relations. Examples of questions generated by different models are shown in Figure 3 .

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

text: To better understand question generation quality, we manually inspect the sampled outputs and summarize the five main error sources in Table 3. Among these, “Semantic Error,” “Redundant,” and “Unanswerable” are prominent across all models. However, we find that the baselines produce more implausible subject-predicate-object collocations (semantic errors) than our model. In particular, CGC-QG (B6) exhibits the highest semantic error rate, at 26.4% among the three methods; it also tends to copy irrelevant content from the input document. Our model substantially reduces such semantic errors to 8.3%, as we explicitly model semantic relations between entities by introducing typed semantic graphs. The other salient error type is “Unanswerable,” that is, the question is linguistically well formed but cannot be answered from the passage. Again, CGC-QG generates notably more unanswerable questions than the other two models, while our model achieves results comparable to S2sa-at-mp-gsa (B4), likely because answerability requires a deeper understanding of the document as well as commonsense knowledge. These issues cannot be fully resolved simply by incorporating semantic relations. Examples of questions generated by different models are shown in Figure 3.

“Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “Invented a’ apple-tree out of his own head, which is called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know ’em, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted on a Tom Putt, and a Rathe-ripe upon top o’ that again. ’Tis trew ’a used to bide about in a public-house wi’ a ’ooman in a way he had no business to by rights, but there—’a were a clever man in the sense of the term.”

“Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “He invented an apple tree entirely from his own head, and it’s still called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know the sort, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted onto a Tom Putt, with a Rathe-ripe on top of that. It’s true he used to linger about in a public house with a woman in a way he really had no business doing, by rights, but there—he was a clever man, in the proper sense of the word.”

The young man glanced at the clock again and frowned darkly. When his gaze strayed from the glowing horologue of time it rested intently upon a four-story red brick house in the row of dwellings opposite to where he sat. The shades were drawn, and the lights in many rooms shone dimly through them.

The young man looked at the clock once more, his brow furrowing with a deep scowl. As his eyes drifted from the luminous timepiece, they fixed themselves keenly on a four-storey red-brick house among the row of residences across from where he sat. The blinds were lowered, and through them the lights in several rooms glowed faintly.

“He is not ungrateful for winning her,” whispered Eustacia, “and in that respect he is a good man. Many women would go far for such a husband. But do I desire unreasonably much in wanting what is called life—music, poetry, passion, war, and all the beating and pulsing that are going on in the great arteries of the world? That was the shape of my youthful dream; but I did not get it. Yet I thought I saw the way to it in my Clym.”

“He doesn’t take the blessing of winning her for granted,” Eustacia whispered, “and in that regard, he is a good man. Many women would go to great lengths for a husband like him. But am I craving something unreasonably excessive when I long for what is called true life—music, poetry, burning passion, the fire of grand struggle, all the thrumming, surging pulse that beats in the great arteries of the wider world? That was the exact shape of my youthful dream; I never got to hold it, yet I once thought I saw the path to it in my Clym.”

It was the stubborn unreasoning of the time. It was useless to argue with her. Arguments have no chance against petrified training; they wear it as little as the waves wear a cliff. And her training was everybody's. The brightest intellect in the land would not have been able to see that her position was defective.

That age was marked by an obstinate irrationality. There was no point in reasoning with her. Reason stands powerless against hardened conditioning, just as the sea makes no impression on a cliff. And her conditioning was shared by all. Even the most brilliant mind in the country would have failed to recognize the flaw in her stance.

He sat thinking for an interval, and then went on, "The reading and writing alone! I taught him to read myself--because his first governess, you see, wasn't very clever. She was a very good methodical sort, but she had no inspiration. So I got up all sorts of methods for teaching him to read. But it wasn't necessary. He seemed to leap all sorts of difficulties. He leapt to what one was trying to teach him. It was as quick as the movement of some wild animal....

“Urged by this impulse, I seized on the boy as he passed and drew him towards me. As soon as he beheld my form, he placed his hands before his eyes and uttered a shrill scream; I drew his hand forcibly from his face and said, ‘Child, what is the meaning of this? I do not intend to hurt you; listen to me.’

Driven by a sudden compulsion, I caught the lad mid-stride and pulled him close. The moment his gaze fell upon my figure, he clapped both palms over his eyes and let out a piercing cry; I wrenched his hands away from his countenance and spoke, "Boy, why do you shrink so? I mean you no harm; hear what I have to say."

“I will,” she answered triumphantly. “When you went out this evening I thought that as baby was asleep I would see where you were going to so mysteriously without telling me. So I ran out and followed behind you. You stopped at the place where the road forks, looked round at the bonfires, and then said, ‘Damn it, I’ll go!’ And you went quickly up the left-hand road. Then I stood and watched you.”

“I did,” she declared with a victorious lilt. “After you slipped away tonight, I reckoned that with the little one slumbering I’d discover where you were heading so secretively, without a word to me. So I darted out and trailed behind you. You paused at the fork in the lane, glanced back at the blaze of the bonfires, and then muttered, ‘To hell with it, I’m going!’ And you hurried off along the rightward path. Then I stopped and kept my eyes on you.”

"I'm going out--anywhere." She turned, coat in hand. "Can't I go out if I like?" she asked. "It's a beautiful day.... Mustn't I go out?... I suppose you think I ought to take in what you have told me in a moment. Just smile and say '_Indeed!_' ... Abandoned!--while his men retreated! How jolly! And then not think of it any more.... Besides, I must go out. You two want to be left together. You want to canoodle. Do it while you can!"

“I’m leaving—any place but here.” She spun on her heel, wool coat slung over one arm. “What, I can’t step out whenever I feel like it?” she snapped. “The sun’s shining, for Christ’s sake… Am I not allowed to breathe fresh air?… I guess you expect me to swallow everything you just dumped on me all at once, right? Just bat my eyes and chirp ‘How fascinating!’ Deserted his post! Left his troops to fall back alone! Isn’t that just darling? And then I’m supposed to wipe it from my mind like it never happened… And anyway, I need to get out. You two want the place to yourselves. Want to make goo-goo eyes at each other. Go ahead, enjoy it while it lasts!”

Had only Yeobright’s own future been involved he would have proposed to Thomasin with a ready heart. He had nothing to lose by carrying out a dead mother’s hope. But he dreaded to contemplate Thomasin wedded to the mere corpse of a lover that he now felt himself to be. He had but three activities alive in him. One was his almost daily walk to the little graveyard wherein his mother lay; another, his just as frequent visits by night to the more distant enclosure which numbered his Eustacia among its dead; the third was self-preparation for a vocation which alone seemed likely to satisfy his cravings—that of an itinerant preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was difficult to believe that Thomasin would be cheered by a husband with such tendencies as these.

If Clym’s own destiny were the only thing at stake, he would have asked Thomasin to marry him without hesitation. He had nothing to lose by honoring his late mother’s final wish. Yet, he was troubled by the idea of Thomasin being tied to the emotional shell of a man he felt himself to be. Only three interests still flickered within him. One was his nearly daily pilgrimage to the small graveyard where his mother rested; another was his equally regular nighttime visits to the more distant burial ground where Eustacia lay; and the last was his preparation for the only calling that seemed capable of quieting his restless spirit—serving as a wandering preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was hard to imagine that Thomasin would find any comfort in a husband defined by such grim preoccupations.

In the morning he borrowed ten cents from Maria. He would have borrowed it, by preference, from Brissenden, but that erratic individual had disappeared. Two weeks had passed since Martin had seen him, and he vainly cudgelled his brains for some cause of offence. The ten cents carried Martin across the ferry to San Francisco, and as he walked up Market Street he speculated upon his predicament in case he failed to collect the money. There would then be no way for him to return to Oakland, and he knew no one in San Francisco from whom to borrow another ten cents.

At dawn he asked Maria for a dime. He’d rather have gotten it from Brissenden, but that unpredictable man had vanished. Two weeks had slipped by since Martin last encountered him, and he racked his mind in vain for any slight he might have given. The dime took Martin on the ferry to San Francisco, and as he strode up Market Street he considered what would become of him if he couldn’t obtain the money. He’d have no means to get back to Oakland, and he was acquainted with no one in San Francisco who could lend him another dime.

While he waved his hand and muttered that he had done nothing at all, he was obeying her behest by trying to get into a chair. He found time to admire the ease with which she sat down, then lurched toward a chair facing her, overwhelmed with consciousness of the awkward figure he was cutting. This was a new experience for him. All his life, up to then, he had been unaware of being either graceful or awkward. Such thoughts of self had never entered his mind. He sat down gingerly on the edge of the chair, greatly worried by his hands. They were in the way wherever he put them. Arthur was leaving the room, and Martin Eden followed his exit with longing eyes. He felt lost, alone there in the room with that pale spirit of a woman. There was no bar-keeper upon whom to call for drinks, no small boy to send around the corner for a can of beer and by means of that social fluid start the amenities of friendship flowing.

As he dismissed her gratitude with a clumsy gesture and a few swallowed words, Elias obeyed her silent instruction to sit. He paused to marvel at the effortless fluidity of her movements before lurching toward a seat across from her, suddenly and painfully aware of his own bulk. This sensation was entirely novel. Throughout his years, he had never once considered whether he moved with elegance or ineptitude; such vanity had never breached his consciousness. He lowered himself tentatively onto the cushion's edge, tormented by the sudden prominence of his hands. No matter where he rested them, they felt intrusive. Julian was withdrawing from the study, and Elias tracked his retreat with a look of profound longing. He felt abandoned, cast away in this room with a woman who seemed more a ghost than a person. There was no publican here to provide a dram, no lad to fetch a flagon of porter—none of the common spirits that typically washed away the friction of a first meeting.

When I was about fifteen years old we had retired to our house near Belrive, when we witnessed a most violent and terrible thunderstorm. It advanced from behind the mountains of Jura, and the thunder burst at once with frightful loudness from various quarters of the heavens. I remained, while the storm lasted, watching its progress with curiosity and delight. As I stood at the door, on a sudden I beheld a stream of fire issue from an old and beautiful oak which stood about twenty yards from our house; and so soon as the dazzling light vanished, the oak had disappeared, and nothing remained but a blasted stump. When we visited it the next morning, we found the tree shattered in a singular manner. It was not splintered by the shock, but entirely reduced to thin ribbons of wood. I never beheld anything so utterly destroyed.

In my sixteenth year, during our retreat to the family estate overlooking the lake, I bore witness to a tempest of unparalleled ferocity. It gathered behind the dark crags of the Jura, and the thunder erupted with a sudden, deafening roar that seemed to shake the very foundations of the world. I stood upon the threshold, captivated by the chaotic splendor of the elements. Without warning, a pillar of celestial fire descended upon a majestic elm that had long graced our lawn; once the brilliance faded, the great tree had vanished, leaving only a scorched and skeletal ruin. The following morning revealed a strange sight: the trunk had not merely split, but had been teased into a thousand silken filaments of timber. Never before had I seen such total annihilation.

The door of the Doctor’s room opened, and he came out with Charles Darnay. He was so deadly pale--which had not been the case when they went in together--that no vestige of colour was to be seen in his face. But, in the composure of his manner he was unaltered, except that to the shrewd glance of Mr. Lorry it disclosed some shadowy indication that the old air of avoidance and dread had lately passed over him, like a cold wind.

The door to the doctor’s chambers swung open, and he stepped out alongside Charles Darnay. His face was ghastly pale, drained of every last trace of color—a stark change from how he’d looked when the two of them had gone inside together only moments earlier. His steady, unruffled manner was otherwise entirely unchanged, save for a faint, fleeting tell that only Mr. Lorry’s shrewd, practiced eye caught: the old, familiar air of avoidance and dread had just passed over him, sharp and quick as a gust of icy wind.

He drew up the other chair. The tea brightened the girl’s eyes and brought back some of her colour. She began to eat with a sort of dainty ferocity like some starved wild animal. She seemed to regard the young man’s presence and the aid he had rendered her as a natural thing—not as though she undervalued the conventions; but as one whose great stress gave her the right to put aside the artificial for the human. But gradually, with the return of strength and comfort, came also a sense of the little conventions that belong; and she began to tell him her little story. It was one of a thousand such as the city yawns at every day—the shop girl’s story of insufficient wages, further reduced by “fines” that go to swell the store’s profits; of time lost through illness; and then of lost positions, lost hope, and—the knock of the adventurer upon the green door.

He pulled the second empty chair across the scuffed linoleum and settled into it. The steaming chamomile tea gilded the young woman’s irises and painted faint rose back into her sallow, hollowed cheeks. She picked at the bread and jam first, then tore into it with a delicate, ravenous hunger, sharp as a starved wren that’s stumbled across a forgotten windowsill feeder. She treated his presence and the kindness he’d extended as something unremarkable, expected even—not that she dismissed ordinary social niceties; but when one has been stretched thin past breaking point, the unspoken rules of politeness fall away to make room for raw, unvarnished need. But as warmth seeped back into her frozen fingertips and the tight knot of hunger in her stomach unclenched, the weight of those small, unwritten social rules crept back in, and she began to haltingly share what had led her there. It was the kind of tale the great gray city swallowed by the dozen each day, no more notable than a raindrop hitting the sidewalk: a sales clerk’s account of wages too meager to cover rent, whittled even thinner by arbitrary “infraction fees” that padded the department store’s annual profits; weeks missed from work after a bout of winter flu that left her too weak to stand; then a pink termination slip, no savings left, no prospects, and finally, the desperate rap of someone with nowhere else to turn on his forest-green apartment door.

“Hate you—no,” said Thomasin soothingly. “It is only that he loves her too well. Look at it quietly—do. It is not so very bad of him. Do you know, I thought it not the worst match he could have made. Miss Vye’s family is a good one on her mother’s side; and her father was a romantic wanderer—a sort of Greek Ulysses.”

“Hate you—no,” Thomasin said in a gentle tone. “It’s simply that he cares for her too deeply. Consider it calmly—please do. It’s not such a terrible fault in him. You know, I didn’t think it was the poorest choice he could have made. Miss Vye’s lineage is respectable through her mother, and her father was a fanciful rover—a kind of Grecian Ulysses.”

One day when Mr. Seeders came in to dinner he had been drinking beer. There were only two or three customers in the restaurant. When Mr. Seeders had finished his weakfish he got up, put his arm around Tildy’s waist, kissed her loudly and impudently, walked out upon the street, snapped his fingers in the direction of the laundry, and hied himself to play pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

One afternoon, Mr. Seeders arrived for his meal with the flush of beer already upon him. The restaurant was nearly empty, holding just a couple of other patrons. Once he had polished off his weakfish, he rose, slid an arm around Tildy’s waist, and planted a loud, brazen kiss on her. Then he sauntered out to the street, snapped his fingers toward the laundry, and headed off to lose pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

At 6 o’clock the waiter brought her dinner and carried away the typewritten bill of fare. When Sarah ate she set aside, with a sigh, the dish of dandelions with its crowning ovarious accompaniment. As this dark mass had been transformed from a bright and love-indorsed flower to be an ignominious vegetable, so had her summer hopes wilted and perished. Love may, as Shakespeare said, feed on itself: but Sarah could not bring herself to eat the dandelions that had graced, as ornaments, the first spiritual banquet of her heart’s true affection.

As the clock struck six, the waiter delivered her evening meal and retrieved the typed menu. Sarah began to eat, but with a heavy sigh, she pushed aside the plate of dandelion greens and its crowning garnish of egg. Just as these once-vibrant blossoms, symbols of romance, had been reduced to a lowly vegetable, the hopes she had nurtured all summer had similarly withered and died. Though poets claim that love can sustain itself, Sarah could not bring herself to consume the very flowers that had decorated the first sacred feast of her heart’s true devotion.

Soon parting from Gabriel, the lonely and reserved man returned to his house to breakfast—feeling twinges of shame and regret at having so far exposed his mood by those fevered questions to a stranger. He again placed the letter on the mantelpiece, and sat down to think of the circumstances attending it by the light of Gabriel’s information.

Soon after leaving Gabriel, the solitary, reserved man went back home to breakfast, troubled by pangs of shame and regret for having so openly revealed his feelings in those heated questions to a stranger. He once more set the letter on the mantelpiece and sat down to consider the circumstances surrounding it in the light of Gabriel’s information.

“I fancy I heard something outside the shed,” said Christian. “I wish troubles would come in the daytime, for then a man could show his courage, and hardly beg for mercy of the most broomstick old woman he should see, if he was a brave man, and able to run out of her sight!”

“I could’ve sworn I heard a faint stir just outside the lean-to,” said Tobin. “I wish all troubles showed up in the daylight, when a man can stand firm and show his mettle, and would never have to beg mercy even of the scruffiest broom-brandishing old crone he stumbled across, so long as he was bold enough, and quick enough to dart out of her line of sight.”

He got up abruptly and tried to see himself in the dirty looking-glass over the wash-stand. He passed a towel over it and looked again, long and carefully. It was the first time he had ever really seen himself. His eyes were made for seeing, but up to that moment they had been filled with the ever changing panorama of the world, at which he had been too busy gazing, ever to gaze at himself. He saw the head and face of a young fellow of twenty, but, being unused to such appraisement, he did not know how to value it. Above a square-domed forehead he saw a mop of brown hair, nut-brown, with a wave to it and hints of curls that were a delight to any woman, making hands tingle to stroke it and fingers tingle to pass caresses through it. But he passed it by as without merit, in Her eyes, and dwelt long and thoughtfully on the high, square forehead,—striving to penetrate it and learn the quality of its content. What kind of a brain lay behind there? was his insistent interrogation. What was it capable of? How far would it take him? Would it take him to her?

He rose suddenly and tried to catch his reflection in the grimy mirror above the washstand. He wiped it with a towel and studied his image again, long and intently. It was the first time he had truly looked at himself. His eyes were designed for observation, but until now they had been absorbed by the shifting spectacle of the world; he had been too preoccupied gazing outward to ever examine his own face. He saw the head and features of a young man of twenty, but, unaccustomed to such scrutiny, he had no idea how to judge them. Above a broad, squared forehead, he noticed a thick shock of brown hair—nut-brown, with a gentle wave and subtle curls that any woman would find captivating, her hands itching to smooth it, her fingers longing to weave through it. Yet he dismissed it as worthless in her eyes and lingered instead, deep in thought, on the tall, squared forehead—trying to see through it and discern the nature of what lay within. What kind of mind resided there? was his persistent question. What could it achieve? How far could it carry him? Could it carry him to her?

“What he says is quite correct,” observed Blathers, nodding his head in a confirmatory way, and playing carelessly with the handcuffs, as if they were a pair of castanets. “Who is the boy? What account does he give of himself? Where did he come from? He didn’t drop out of the clouds, did he, master?”

“He’s absolutely right,” remarked Blathers, bobbing his head in agreement while idly flipping the handcuffs like they were a set of castanets. “Who’s the lad? What story does he tell? Where’s he from? He didn’t just fall from the sky, did he, sir?”

“We’re lucky that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come on and meet them. Stevens isn’t around, I hear. I’m going to get them started on monism if I can. Wait till they get a few jolts in them and they’ll warm up.”

“We’re fortunate that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come along and meet them. I hear Stevens isn’t around. I’m going to see if I can get them started on monism. Just wait until they’ve had a few jolts and you’ll see them begin to thaw.”

Since he lived six times as many working-days as Sundays, Oak’s appearance in his old clothes was most peculiarly his own—the mental picture formed by his neighbours in imagining him being always dressed in that way. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, spread out at the base by tight jamming upon the head for security in high winds, and a coat like Dr. Johnson’s; his lower extremities being encased in ordinary leather leggings and boots emphatically large, affording to each foot a roomy apartment so constructed that any wearer might stand in a river all day long and know nothing of damp—their maker being a conscientious man who endeavoured to compensate for any weakness in his cut by unstinted dimension and solidity.

Because Oak spent six times as many days laboring as he did resting, his working attire became his truest signature—the image his neighbors conjured whenever they thought of him. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, its brim splayed wide from being jammed down firmly against the wind, and a coat reminiscent of Dr. Johnson’s. His legs were sheathed in ordinary leather gaiters, and his feet in boots of such emphatic size that each one formed a roomy chamber, so stoutly built that a man could stand in a river all day and never feel the damp—their maker being a scrupulous craftsman who made up for any shortcomings in his pattern with unsparing dimension and heft.

“And then I went into Griffin’s to hae my boots hobbed, and then I went to Riggs’s batty-cake shop, and asked ’em for a penneth of the cheapest and nicest stales, that were all but blue-mouldy, but not quite. And whilst I was chawing ’em down I walked on and seed a clock with a face as big as a baking trendle—”

“And then I went into Griffin’s to have my boots hobbed, and after that I went to Riggs’s batty-cake shop, and asked them for a penn’orth of the cheapest and nicest stales, that were all but blue-mouldy, though not quite. And while I was chawing them down I walked on and saw a clock with a face as big as a baking-trindle—”

“Your manner, no less than your actions, assures me that you have never forgotten it, or ceased to think of it with bitterness,” returned Mr. Brownlow. “I speak of fifteen years ago, when you were not more than eleven years old, and your father but one-and-thirty—for he was, I repeat, a boy, when _his_ father ordered him to marry. Must I go back to events which cast a shade upon the memory of your parent, or will you spare it, and disclose to me the truth?”

“The set of your features, every bit as much as your recent actions, makes plain you have never wiped that time from your mind, nor ceased to regard it with sharp bitterness,” Mr. Hale returned. “I speak of fifteen years past, when you were barely eleven years old, and your father only thirty-one—for he was scarcely more than a boy himself, I remind you, when his own father ordered him into that arranged marriage. Must I dredge up events that would cast a permanent shadow over your father’s memory in your eyes, or will you spare me that unkindness, and reveal the full truth to me now?”

In great families, when an advantageous place cannot be obtained, either in possession, reversion, remainder, or expectancy, for the young man who is growing up, it is a very general custom to send him to sea. The board, in imitation of so wise and salutary an example, took counsel together on the expediency of shipping off Oliver Twist, in some small trading vessel bound to a good unhealthy port. This suggested itself as the very best thing that could possibly be done with him: the probability being, that the skipper would flog him to death, in a playful mood, some day after dinner, or would knock his brains out with an iron bar; both pastimes being, as is pretty generally known, very favourite and common recreations among gentleman of that class. The more the case presented itself to the board, in this point of view, the more manifold the advantages of the step appeared; so, they came to the conclusion that the only way of providing for Oliver effectually, was to send him to sea without delay.

In prominent households, when no suitable position—whether immediate, future, contingent, or anticipated—can be secured for a boy coming of age, it is quite customary to pack him off to sea. The board, following this prudent and time-honored precedent, deliberated on the wisdom of dispatching Oliver Twist aboard some modest merchant vessel bound for a reliably insalubrious port. The idea presented itself as the finest possible solution for the boy: the odds being that the captain, in a fit of whimsy after dinner one day, would either whip him to death or stove in his skull with an iron rod—both diversions being, as is widely acknowledged, especially cherished and habitual among gentlemen of that profession. The more the board turned the matter over from this angle, the more numerous the benefits of the measure appeared; thus they resolved that the sole effective means of settling Oliver's future was to send him to sea forthwith.

From a pedigreed yellow pup I grew up to be an anonymous yellow cur looking like a cross between an Angora cat and a box of lemons. But my mistress never tumbled. She thought that the two primeval pups that Noah chased into the ark were but a collateral branch of my ancestors. It took two policemen to keep her from entering me at the Madison Square Garden for the Siberian bloodhound prize.

I began life as a yellow puppy of distinguished lineage, only to mature into a nameless yellow mongrel resembling something between an Angora cat and a crate of lemons. Yet my mistress never caught on. She believed those two primordial hounds that Noah herded onto the ark were merely a distant offshoot of my family tree. It required two officers to restrain her from entering me in the Madison Square Garden show for the Siberian bloodhound award.

“There she died,” said Monks, “after a lingering illness; and, on her death-bed, she bequeathed these secrets to me, together with her unquenchable and deadly hatred of all whom they involved—though she need not have left me that, for I had inherited it long before. She would not believe that the girl had destroyed herself, and the child too, but was filled with the impression that a male child had been born, and was alive. I swore to her, if ever it crossed my path, to hunt it down; never to let it rest; to pursue it with the bitterest and most unrelenting animosity; to vent upon it the hatred that I deeply felt, and to spit upon the empty vaunt of that insulting will by dragging it, if I could, to the very gallows-foot. She was right. He came in my way at last. I began well; and, but for babbling drabs, I would have finished as I began!”

“That’s where she died,” Monks said, “wasting away after a long, drawn-out illness. On her deathbed, she passed all those secrets down to me, along with her ravenous, poisonous hatred for everyone tangled up in them—though she didn’t even need to leave that part to me. I’d inherited that same hatred deep in my bones years before she was even dying. She refused to believe the girl had killed herself and taken the baby with her, not for a second. She was completely convinced a baby boy had been born, and that he was still alive out there somewhere. I swore to her if that boy ever crossed my path, I’d hunt him down without mercy. I’d never let him rest, hound him with the most bitter, unforgiving malice I possessed. I’d pour every drop of my deep-seated hatred out on him, and tear down that empty, arrogant brag in that offensive will by dragging him straight to the gallows steps if I could manage it. She was right. He finally turned up in my life. I got off to a perfect start, too, and if it wasn’t for those chattering, loose-lipped whores running their mouths, I’d have finished the job just as clean as I started it!”

I fear, my friend, that I shall render myself tedious by dwelling on these preliminary circumstances; but they were days of comparative happiness, and I think of them with pleasure. My country, my beloved country! who but a native can tell the delight I took in again beholding thy streams, thy mountains, and, more than all, thy lovely lake!

I suspect, my dear companion, that I grow wearisome by lingering upon these opening scenes; yet they were days of relative peace, and I recall them with joy. My homeland, my adored home! Who but a native can know the rapture of beholding again your rivers, your peaks, and, above all, your exquisite lake!

We apply ROBT to multilingual models by finetuning them for an extra 100k steps with the same batch size as for training. Table 6 shows that ROBT substantially improves ACC zero by 35%∼50%, reaching 85%∼87% under different model settings. The multilingual Transformer with ROBT achieves a translation improvement of up to 10.11 BLEU zero ( 2 w/o ROBT→ 7 w/ ROBT), outperforming the bilingual baseline by 1.1 BLEU zero ( 1 w/o ROBT→ 7 w/ ROBT) and approaching the pivotbased multilingual baseline (-0.63 BLEU zero , 8 w/o ROBT→ 7 w/ ROBT). 11 The strong Pearson correlation between the accuracy and BLEU (0.92 on average, significant at p < 0.01) suggests that the improvement on the off-target translation issue explains the increased translation performance to a large extent.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: We apply ROBT to multilingual models by fine-tuning them for an additional 100k steps using the same batch size as in the original training. Table 6 shows that ROBT markedly improves ACC zero by 35%–50%, attaining 85%–87% across different model configurations. The multilingual Transformer with ROBT achieves a translation gain of up to 10.11 BLEU zero (2 w/o ROBT → 7 w/ ROBT), surpassing the bilingual baseline by 1.1 BLEU zero (1 w/o ROBT → 7 w/ ROBT) and approaching the pivot-based multilingual baseline (−0.63 BLEU zero, 8 w/o ROBT → 7 w/ ROBT). The strong Pearson correlation between accuracy and BLEU (0.92 on average, significant at p < 0.01) indicates that the mitigation of the off-target translation issue largely accounts for the enhanced translation performance.

He wandered over miles and miles of ground, and still came back to the old place. Morning and noon had passed, and the day was on the wane, and still he rambled to and fro, and up and down, and round and round, and still lingered about the same spot. At last he got away, and shaped his course for Hatfield.

He roamed across vast stretches of land, yet always found himself returning to that familiar spot. Morning turned to noon, the day began to fade, and still he drifted back and forth, circling and retracing his steps, unable to leave that one place behind. Finally he broke free and set his path toward Hatfield.

It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils. With an anxiety that almost amounted to agony, I collected the instruments of life around me, that I might infuse a spark of being into the lifeless thing that lay at my feet. It was already one in the morning; the rain pattered dismally against the panes, and my candle was nearly burnt out, when, by the glimmer of the half-extinguished light, I saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open; it breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs.

It was a grim, dreary November night when I finally stood witness to the fruit of all my grueling toil. I was twisted up with an anxiety so sharp it edged close to outright agony as I arranged my life-giving instruments around me, ready to breathe a spark of being into the lifeless form stretched out on the floor at my feet. It was already one in the morning; rain pattered miserably against the windowpanes, and my candle was guttering down to its final dregs, when in the faint, wavering glow of the half-extinguished light, I saw the creature’s murky yellow eye flutter open. It pulled in a harsh, ragged breath, and a sharp convulsive tremor shook through all of its limbs.

All our pilgrims looked on and commented--on the expert way in which the whip was handled. They were too much hardened by lifelong everyday familiarity with slavery to notice that there was anything else in the exhibition that invited comment. This was what slavery could do, in the way of ossifying what one may call the superior lobe of human feeling; for these pilgrims were kind-hearted people, and they would not have allowed that man to treat a horse like that.

Every pilgrim among us watched and remarked on the deftness with which the whip was wielded. Their sensibilities had been so calloused by a lifetime of daily, unthinking exposure to slavery that nothing else in the scene seemed to them worthy of note. Such was slavery’s power to petrify what might be called the higher chambers of human sympathy; for these were, at heart, kindly souls, and they would never have stood by while a man treated a horse in such a fashion.

She had to be in the picture, and so she came in as though she were the central figure, as though she were the quintessential England. There she was, the type, the blood, the likeness, of no end of Massachusetts families, the very same stuff indeed, and yet she was different....

There was no question she belonged in the frame, so she stepped into it like she was its entire focal point, like she was the living embodiment of quintessential Englishness. There she stood, carrying all the hallmarks, the lineage, the familiar bearing you’d spot in countless longstanding Massachusetts families—cut from exactly the same stock, by all appearances, and yet she was still unmistakably different.

The only signs of the terrible sorrow Boldwood had been combating through the night, and was combating now, were the want of colour in his well-defined face, the enlarged appearance of the veins in his forehead and temples, and the sharper lines about his mouth. The horse bore him away, and the very step of the animal seemed significant of dogged despair. Gabriel, for a minute, rose above his own grief in noticing Boldwood’s. He saw the square figure sitting erect upon the horse, the head turned to neither side, the elbows steady by the hips, the brim of the hat level and undisturbed in its onward glide, until the keen edges of Boldwood’s shape sank by degrees over the hill. To one who knew the man and his story there was something more striking in this immobility than in a collapse. The clash of discord between mood and matter here was forced painfully home to the heart; and, as in laughter there are more dreadful phases than in tears, so was there in the steadiness of this agonized man an expression deeper than a cry.

The only hints of the crippling grief Boldwood had fought all night, and was still fighting in that moment, showed in the drained, pale cast of his sharp-featured face, the bulging veins standing out across his forehead and temples, and the newly harsh edges of the lines framing his mouth. The horse carried him off, and even the creature’s plodding gait felt heavy with the same stubborn, unyielding despair clinging to its rider. For a few seconds, Gabriel set his own heartache aside to watch Boldwood’s distress unfold. He tracked the broad, square-shouldered form sitting rigidly upright in the saddle, head held straight without glancing left or right, elbows fixed firmly at his sides, the brim of his hat sitting perfectly even as he glided forward, until the sharp, distinct outline of Boldwood’s figure slowly dipped out of sight over the crest of the hill. For anyone who knew Boldwood and what he’d been through, this rigid stillness hit far harder than any public breakdown would have. The jarring mismatch between his outward composure and inner turmoil cut straight to the chest with a sharp, painful clarity; just as there are strains of laughter far more terrible than sobs, the unshakable calm of this man raw with agony held a weight of emotion far more profound than any screamed outburst.

It may have been observed that there is no regular path for getting out of love as there is for getting in. Some people look upon marriage as a short cut that way, but it has been known to fail. Separation, which was the means that chance offered to Gabriel Oak by Bathsheba’s disappearance, though effectual with people of certain humours, is apt to idealize the removed object with others—notably those whose affection, placid and regular as it may be, flows deep and long. Oak belonged to the even-tempered order of humanity, and felt the secret fusion of himself in Bathsheba to be burning with a finer flame now that she was gone—that was all.

It is often observed that while there is a clear road for falling into love, no such defined path exists for getting out of it. Some regard marriage as a shortcut to that destination, yet it is a cure known to fail. Separation, the remedy chance offered Gabriel Oak upon Bathsheba’s disappearance, might be effective for certain personalities, but it often serves to idealize the absent person for others—specifically those whose love, however quiet and steady, runs deep. Oak belonged to this even-tempered class of men, and he found the secret union of his soul with Bathsheba’s to be burning with a more refined intensity now that she was gone.

And yet, somehow, he had a feeling that Brissenden had been right, after all. “The Shame of the Sun” had been the cause of his success more than the stuff he had written. That stuff had been merely incidental. It had been rejected right and left by the magazines. The publication of “The Shame of the Sun” had started a controversy and precipitated the landslide in his favor. Had there been no “Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide, and had there been no miracle in the go of “The Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide. Singletree, Darnley & Co. attested that miracle. They had brought out a first edition of fifteen hundred copies and been dubious of selling it. They were experienced publishers and no one had been more astounded than they at the success which had followed. To them it had been in truth a miracle. They never got over it, and every letter they wrote him reflected their reverent awe of that first mysterious happening. They did not attempt to explain it. There was no explaining it. It had happened. In the face of all experience to the contrary, it had happened.

Still, he couldn’t shake the sense that Brissenden had been correct all along. The triumph belonged less to his own writing than to “The Shame of the Sun.” His work had merely tagged along. Magazine after magazine had turned it down. It was the publication of “The Shame of the Sun” that ignited a firestorm and set off the avalanche in his favor. Without that piece, there would have been no avalanche; without the inexplicable magic of its release, no avalanche either. Singletree, Darnley & Co. could vouch for that magic. They had printed a cautious first run of fifteen hundred copies, doubtful they’d ever sell them. Seasoned publishers, they were more stunned than anyone by the ensuing success. To them it was a genuine marvel. They never fully recovered from the shock, and every letter they sent him quivered with reverent wonder at that first uncanny event. They offered no explanation. There was none to give. It had simply occurred. Against every known precedent, it had occurred.

Then, after a pause, "The Treaty of Bucharest was an evil treaty. It must be undone. Whatever this German King of Bulgaria does, that treaty must be undone and the Bulgarians united again into one people. They must have themselves, whatever punishment they deserve, they must have nothing more, whatever reward they win."

Then, after a pause, he said, “The Treaty of Bucharest was a wicked treaty. It must be undone. Whatever this German King of Bulgaria does, that treaty must be undone, and the Bulgarians must be brought together again as one people. They must have to answer for themselves, whatever punishment they deserve; they must have nothing more, whatever reward they earn.”

He said this to himself, and they all distinctly heard it. Boldwood remained silent after that, and the noise from indoors was again just audible, until, a few minutes later, light wheels could be distinguished coming down the hill. They drew nearer, and ceased at the gate. Boldwood hastened back to the door, and opened it; and the light shone upon Bathsheba coming up the path.

The words slipped out half under his breath, yet every person lingering on the porch caught them clear as a bell. Elias Thorne made no reply after that, and the faint, muffled din of chatter from inside the house drifted back to their ears, until a few minutes had passed and the soft rattle of light carriage wheels rose above it, winding down the oak-dusted slope above the farm. The clatter grew steadily louder, then cut off sharp right at the white picket gate. Elias hurried back to the front door and tugged it open; warm golden lamplight spilled out across the gravel walk, gilding Elowen Voss as she climbed the path toward them.

Thus situated, employed in the most detestable occupation, immersed in a solitude where nothing could for an instant call my attention from the actual scene in which I was engaged, my spirits became unequal; I grew restless and nervous. Every moment I feared to meet my persecutor. Sometimes I sat with my eyes fixed on the ground, fearing to raise them lest they should encounter the object which I so much dreaded to behold. I feared to wander from the sight of my fellow creatures lest when alone he should come to claim his companion.

So placed, trapped in the most loathsome of tasks, sunk in a loneliness that offered not a single moment’s reprieve from the scene before me, my mood grew fractured; I turned edgy and unsettled. At every instant I dreaded encountering my tormentor. At times I sat staring rigidly at the ground, afraid to lift my gaze lest it meet the very thing I most feared to see. I grew afraid to stray from the presence of other people, lest in solitude he should appear to reclaim his companion.

And then out I came. But I didn't get any shout. There was a wondering and eloquent silence for a moment, then a great wave of laughter began to sweep along that human sea, but a warning bugle-blast cut its career short. I was in the simplest and comfortablest of gymnast costumes--flesh-colored tights from neck to heel, with blue silk puffings about my loins, and bareheaded. My horse was not above medium size, but he was alert, slender-limbed, muscled with watchsprings, and just a greyhound to go. He was a beauty, glossy as silk, and naked as he was when he was born, except for bridle and ranger-saddle.

I stepped out then. But no roar of welcome met me. Instead, a stunned and expressive silence lingered for a beat, followed by a massive wave of laughter that began to roll through the sea of people, only to be cut dead by a sharp bugle blast. I was dressed in the most basic and practical of athletic outfits—flesh-toned tights from my throat to my heels, with blue silk ruffles around my waist, and nothing on my head. My horse was only average-sized, but he was sharp and lean, his muscles like coiled springs, and he was a pure racer. He was a gorgeous creature, his coat as glossy as satin, and he was as unadorned as the day he was born, except for his bridle and a ranger-saddle.

The better generalization of models trained on ADS may be due to ADS having higher lexical and semantic variability, reflected in the larger vocabulary and higher number of words per utterance. Since there is simply more to learn, learning to perform the task is more difficult on ADS, but it leads to more valuable knowledge. It is also possible that SBERT is better suited to encode the semantic content of ADS, as ADS uterrances are likely to be more similar to the sentences SBERT was trained on than CDS utterances are.

metadata.title: Learning to Understand Child-directed and Adult-directed Speech

text: The superior generalization of models trained on ADS may stem from the greater lexical and semantic variability of ADS, evidenced by its larger vocabulary and higher mean words per utterance. Because there is more to learn, mastering the task on ADS is more challenging, yet it yields more transferable knowledge. Another possibility is that SBERT is better equipped to encode the semantic content of ADS, since ADS utterances likely resemble the sentences on which SBERT was trained more closely than CDS utterances do.

She had been pitiless towards Cissie in her grief. She had, in the egotism of her sorrow, treated Cissie as she might have treated a chair or a table, with no thought that Cissie might be weary, might dream of happiness still to come. Cissie had still to play the lover, and her man was already in khaki. There would be no such year as Letty had had in the days before the war darkened the world. Before Cissie's marrying the peace must come, and the peace was still far away. And Direck too would have to take his chances....

She must go into the cottage and kiss Nell, and tuck the parcel away somewhere out of sight until she could discover some poor fellow in uniform to whom she might send it. In her sorrow she had been merciless to Nell. In the selfishness of grief she had handled Nell as carelessly as though she were a chair or a table, forgetting that Nell too might be tired, might still be dreaming of happiness ahead. Nell still had the lover’s part to play, and her man was already in khaki. There could be no year for her such as Elsie had known in those days before the war laid its shadow over the earth. Before Nell was married peace must come, and peace was still very far off. And Derry too would have to take his chance....

Fair or not," Sikes snapped, "just hand it over! Do you think Nancy and I have nothing better to do with our time than spend it tracking down and snatching back every kid who gets caught because of you? Hand it over, you greedy old bag of bones—give it here!

Yeobright did not interrupt the preparations, and went home again. The next morning, when Thomasin withdrew the curtains of her bedroom window, there stood the Maypole in the middle of the green, its top cutting into the sky. It had sprung up in the night, or rather early morning, like Jack’s bean-stalk. She opened the casement to get a better view of the garlands and posies that adorned it. The sweet perfume of the flowers had already spread into the surrounding air, which, being free from every taint, conducted to her lips a full measure of the fragrance received from the spire of blossom in its midst. At the top of the pole were crossed hoops decked with small flowers; beneath these came a milk-white zone of Maybloom; then a zone of bluebells, then of cowslips, then of lilacs, then of ragged-robins, daffodils, and so on, till the lowest stage was reached. Thomasin noticed all these, and was delighted that the May revel was to be so near.

Clym did not pause the bustle, and returned to his dwelling. At dawn, when Tamsin drew back the linen from her chamber casement, the Maypole already pierced the common’s heart, its crown needling the heavens. It had risen in the darkness—or earliest morning—like a stalk from a fairy tale. She pushed the lattice wide to study the wreaths and nosegays that clothed it. The honeyed breath of the blossoms had already seeped into the unstained air, which bore to her mouth an undiluted draught of the scent cascading from that floral steeple. At the summit, crossed hoops wore a fretwork of tiny blooms; below them hung a creamy band of hawthorn; then a girdle of harebells, then cowslips, then lilac, then ragged-robin, narcissus, and onward, until the lowest tier was met. Tamsin marked each ring, and rejoiced that the Maying would unfold so close at hand.

Venn was loth to depart, for all on earth that interested him lay under this roof. As nobody in the house had any more sleep that night, except the two who slept for ever, there was no reason why he should not remain. So he retired into the niche of the fireplace where he had used to sit, and there he continued, watching the steam from the double row of banknotes as they waved backwards and forwards in the draught of the chimney till their flaccidity was changed to dry crispness throughout. Then the woman came and unpinned them, and, folding them together, carried the handful upstairs. Presently the doctor appeared from above with the look of a man who could do no more, and, pulling on his gloves, went out of the house, the trotting of his horse soon dying away upon the road.

Venn was reluctant to go, for everything on earth that stirred his interest was under that roof. Since no one in the house would sleep again that night, save the two who slept forever, there was no reason he should not stay. So he withdrew to the niche in the fireplace where he had once sat, and remained there, watching the steam rise from the double row of banknotes as they swayed to and fro in the chimney draft, until their limp softness had given way entirely to a dry, crisp stiffness. Then the woman came to unpin them, and, gathering them together, carried the little stack upstairs. Soon after, the doctor came down from above with the air of a man who had done all he could, and, drawing on his gloves, left the house, the quick sound of his horse’s trotting soon fading along the road.

I never truly believed they were serious, but I see now that they were. This arrogant buildup and constant drilling, forty years of nationalistic posturing—sooner or later, it was bound to spill over into actual war.

In a manual analysis on the zero-shot NMT outputs, we found many instances of off-target translation (Table 1) . We use translation-language accuracy to measure the proportion of translations that are in the correct target language. Results in Table 6 show that there is a huge accuracy gap between the multilingual and the pivot-based method (-48.83% ACC zero , 1 → 2 , w/o ROBT), from which we conclude that the off-target translation issue is one source of the poor zero-shot performance.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: In a manual inspection of the zero-shot NMT outputs, we observed numerous cases of off-target translation (Table 1). We adopt translation-language accuracy to quantify the fraction of outputs produced in the correct target language. The results reported in Table 6 reveal a substantial accuracy gap between the multilingual and pivot-based methods (-48.83% ACC zero , 1 → 2 , w/o ROBT), leading us to conclude that off-target translation is one contributor to the poor zero-shot performance.

The cloth was laid for supper; the table was covered with bread and butter, plates and glasses; a porter-pot and a wine-bottle. At the upper end of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole lolled negligently in an easy-chair, with his legs thrown over one of the arms: an open clasp-knife in one hand, and a mass of buttered bread in the other. Close beside him stood Charlotte, opening oysters from a barrel: which Mr. Claypole condescended to swallow, with remarkable avidity. A more than ordinary redness in the region of the young gentleman’s nose, and a kind of fixed wink in his right eye, denoted that he was in a slight degree intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the intense relish with which he took his oysters, for which nothing but a strong appreciation of their cooling properties, in cases of internal fever, could have sufficiently accounted.

The supper cloth was spread ready for the meal, the table loaded with bread and butter, plates and glasses, a stout porter pot and a bottle of wine. At the head of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole slouched lazily in an easy chair, his legs slung carelessly over one armrest: an open pocket knife in one hand, a thick chunk of buttered bread in the other. Standing just beside him was Charlotte, prying oysters open from a barrel: morsels Mr. Claypole deigned to swallow with sharp, greedy enthusiasm. An unusual bright redness across the young gentleman’s nose, and a sort of fixed, half-winking twitch in his right eye, marked that he was slightly intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the fierce relish with which he ate his oysters, a preference that could only be adequately explained by a strong awareness of their cooling qualities, perfect for soothing the internal fever brought on by drink.

Wildeve had just cast his lot, but had not yet lifted the box to discover what he had thrown; and now it was impossible.

She stepped over the threshold indoors, her cheeks flushed bright, and faint traces of her recent flurry of excitement still glinting in her eyes. Clym Yeobright looked up, astonished; he had never caught even a hint of this kind of unguarded, heightened state in her before. She slipped past him, ready to climb the stairs without drawing notice, but Clym was so thoroughly concerned that he rose immediately and followed close at her heels.

In all weathers, in the snow and frost of winter, in the bitter winds of spring, in the hot sunshine of summer, in the rains of autumn, and again in the snow and frost of winter, Lucie passed two hours of every day at this place; and every day on leaving it, she kissed the prison wall. Her husband saw her (so she learned from her father) it might be once in five or six times: it might be twice or thrice running: it might be, not for a week or a fortnight together. It was enough that he could and did see her when the chances served, and on that possibility she would have waited out the day, seven days a week.

Through biting winter frosts and drifting snows, through the raw gales of spring, under the blazing summer sun, amid the autumn downpours, and once more through the biting frosts and snows of winter, Elara kept her daily vigil of two hours at that spot; and each day, as she departed, she pressed her lips to the stone wall. Her beloved saw her (so her father told her) perhaps once in five or six visits, perhaps two or three times in succession, perhaps not at all for a week or even a fortnight together. It sufficed that he could and did behold her whenever fortune permitted, and on that slender chance she would have lingered the whole day long, every day of the week.

Liddy, elevating her feelings to the occasion from a sense of grandeur, floated off behind Bathsheba with a milder dignity not entirely free from travesty, and the door was closed.

At that, the petite magistrate rose from the table and swept out of the hall, her black silk gown catching stray bits of straw and pulling them across the floor with a rasping whisper. Lifting her mood to match the moment out of a feeling of importance, Liddy drifted after Bathsheba with a softer stateliness that bordered on parody, and the door shut behind them.

“Justine, you may remember, was a great favourite of yours; and I recollect you once remarked that if you were in an ill humour, one glance from Justine could dissipate it, for the same reason that Ariosto gives concerning the beauty of Angelica—she looked so frank-hearted and happy. My aunt conceived a great attachment for her, by which she was induced to give her an education superior to that which she had at first intended. This benefit was fully repaid; Justine was the most grateful little creature in the world: I do not mean that she made any professions I never heard one pass her lips, but you could see by her eyes that she almost adored her protectress. Although her disposition was gay and in many respects inconsiderate, yet she paid the greatest attention to every gesture of my aunt. She thought her the model of all excellence and endeavoured to imitate her phraseology and manners, so that even now she often reminds me of her.

You surely recall Elara, who was always a particular favorite of yours; I remember your saying that if ever you fell into a melancholy, a single glimpse of her would suffice to dispel it. She possessed that quality of candid cheerfulness which the poets often ascribe to their most radiant muses. My mother became so deeply fond of her that she saw fit to provide the girl with an education far more sophisticated than was first planned. This generosity was met with the quietest, most sincere devotion; Elara never resorted to empty protestations of thanks, yet one could read a world of adoration in her eyes. Though her spirit was naturally buoyant and sometimes heedless, she remained keenly observant of my mother’s every nuance. Viewing her as the absolute standard of grace, she strove to adopt her very way of speaking and carrying herself, so that even now, Elara’s movements frequently bring my mother back to my mind.

I found several letters, and, among others, one which I discovered from its commencement to be from your father. I instantly wrote to Geneva; nearly two months have elapsed since the departure of my letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are unfit for agitation of any kind.”

“The moment you were seized with illness, all the papers found upon you were brought to me, and I examined them in the hope of discovering some clue by which I might inform your relations of your misfortune and sickness. I found several letters, and among them one which I knew at once, from its opening lines, to be from your father. I wrote immediately to Geneva; nearly two months have passed since I sent that letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are in no state for any disturbance.”

“No, Miss Manette; all through it, I have known myself to be quite undeserving. And yet I have had the weakness, and have still the weakness, to wish you to know with what a sudden mastery you kindled me, heap of ashes that I am, into fire--a fire, however, inseparable in its nature from myself, quickening nothing, lighting nothing, doing no service, idly burning away.”

“No, Miss Manette. All this while, I have known full well that I am entirely unworthy of your regard. Even so, I have had this weakness, and carry it still: I want you to understand how suddenly, how completely you kindled a flame in me, heap of cold ash that I am. It is a fire, though, inextricable from my very nature. It rouses nothing, lights no dark space, does no good to anyone. It only burns idle, wasting itself slowly away.”

“Well, never mind arguing—never mind. One thing is sure: you were all but mine, and now you are not nearly mine. Everything is changed, and that by you alone, remember. You were nothing to me once, and I was contented; you are now nothing to me again, and how different the second nothing is from the first! Would to God you had never taken me up, since it was only to throw me down!”

“Well, let us say no more of it—no more. One thing is certain: you were almost mine, and now you are scarcely mine at all. All is altered, and altered by you alone, remember that. Once you meant nothing to me, and I was content; now you mean nothing to me again, and how unlike this second nothing is to the first! Would to God you had never raised me up, if only to cast me down!”

And then they rode to the damsels, and either saluted other, and the eldest had a garland of gold about her head, and she was threescore winter of age or more--”

—“Never yet did a knight arrive but that he found some uncouth adventure, by my troth. In very sooth it is even so, my gracious lord, though indeed it be exceeding hard to declare; and yet, for all that, the thing may not linger, but rather prosper the more through custom. So they rode on toward the maidens, and each gave the other greeting; and the eldest wore a circlet of gold upon her brow, and she was of threescore winters and more—”

"I'd stop his _Daily Mail_," said Raeburn. "I'd leave _The Times_, but I'd stop the _Daily Mail_ on the score of its placards alone. It overdoes Northcliffe. It translates him into the shrieks and yells of underlings. The plain fact is that Northcliffe is scared out of his wits by German efficiency--and in war time when a man is scared out of his wits, whether he is honest or not, you put his head in a bag or hold a pistol to it to calm him.... What is the good of all this clamouring for a change of government? We haven't a change of government. It's like telling a tramp to get a change of linen. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate men, with the habits of advocates. There is nothing masterful in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything else? But they are doing as well as they can, and there is no way of putting in any one else now, and there you are."

I'd cancel his _Daily Mail_," Raeburn said. "I'd keep _The Times_, but I'd drop the _Daily Mail_ for its placards alone. It exaggerates Northcliffe. It turns him into the screeching and howling of subordinates. The simple truth is that Northcliffe is terrified of German efficiency—and in wartime, when a man is terrified, whether he's honest or not, you put a bag over his head or hold a pistol to it to steady him.... What's the use of all this noise about changing the government? We haven't got a change of government. It's like telling a beggar to get a change of clothes. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate, with the instincts of lawyers. There's nothing commanding in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything different? But they're doing the best they can, and there's no way of putting anyone else in now, and that's how it stands.

“We must try to track ’em, since we can’t hear ’em,” said Jan, fumbling in his pockets. He struck a light, and held the match to the ground. The rain had been heavier here, and all foot and horse tracks made previous to the storm had been abraded and blurred by the drops, and they were now so many little scoops of water, which reflected the flame of the match like eyes. One set of tracks was fresh and had no water in them; one pair of ruts was also empty, and not small canals, like the others. The footprints forming this recent impression were full of information as to pace; they were in equidistant pairs, three or four feet apart, the right and left foot of each pair being exactly opposite one another.

“We’ve got to follow their trail now that we can’t pick up any sound of them,” Jan said, fumbling through his pockets. He struck a match and held its wavering flame low to the earth. The rain had fallen harder in this spot, so every footprint and horse hoof print left before the storm had been eroded and blurred by the falling drops, reduced to tiny shallow pools of water that glinted back the match’s flame like watching eyes. Only one set of tracks was fresh, with no water pooled inside its indentations; a single pair of wagon ruts was also dry, rather than the narrow little water channels the older ruts had turned into. Those recent footprints held clear clues about the pace of the person who’d left them: they fell in evenly spaced pairs, three or four feet between each set, the right and left foot of each pair perfectly aligned side by side.
