text: Due to the unique style of the summary explained in Sec. 2, the scarcity of training data makes it hard to train an end-to-end seq2seq (Sutskever et al., 2014) model, as is typical for abstractive summarization. Also, this technique would only offer limited control over the output. Hence, we opt for an extractive method, leveraging OpenIE (Banko et al., 2007) and a BERT-based (Devlin et al., 2019) sentence compressor (both illustrated in Fig. 3) to surface a pool of sub-sentence-level candidate summaries from the headline and the body, which are then scored by a ranker.

metadata.title: NSTM: Real-Time Query-Driven News Overview Composition at Bloomberg

text: Because the summary style described in Section 2 is distinctive, the limited availability of training data makes it difficult to train an end-to-end sequence-to-sequence model (Sutskever et al., 2014) in the manner commonly used for abstractive summarization. Moreover, such an approach would afford only restricted control over the generated output. We therefore adopt an extractive strategy that employs OpenIE (Banko et al., 2007) and a BERT-based (Devlin et al., 2019) sentence compressor (both depicted in Fig. 3) to extract a set of sub-sentential candidate summaries from the headline and body, which are subsequently scored by a ranking component.

“The same reasons which influenced me then, will influence me now,” said Rose firmly. “If I ever owed a strict and rigid duty to her, whose goodness saved me from a life of indigence and suffering, when should I ever feel it, as I should tonight? It is a struggle,” said Rose, “but one I am proud to make; it is a pang, but one my heart shall bear.”

“The motives that swayed me then shall sway me still,” Clara declared with quiet resolve. “If ever I owed an unyielding obligation to the woman whose mercy rescued me from a life of penury and wretchedness, surely that debt claims me most tonight. It is a conflict,” she said, “but one I meet with pride; it is a grief, but one my spirit shall endure.”

Mrs. Morse drove by Martin on the street one day, and smiled and nodded. He smiled back and lifted his hat. The episode did not affect him. A month before it might have disgusted him, or made him curious and set him to speculating about her state of consciousness at that moment. But now it was not provocative of a second thought. He forgot about it the next moment. He forgot about it as he would have forgotten the Central Bank Building or the City Hall after having walked past them. Yet his mind was preternaturally active. His thoughts went ever around and around in a circle. The centre of that circle was “work performed”; it ate at his brain like a deathless maggot. He awoke to it in the morning. It tormented his dreams at night. Every affair of life around him that penetrated through his senses immediately related itself to “work performed.” He drove along the path of relentless logic to the conclusion that he was nobody, nothing. Mart Eden, the hoodlum, and Mart Eden, the sailor, had been real, had been he; but Martin Eden! the famous writer, did not exist. Martin Eden, the famous writer, was a vapor that had arisen in the mob-mind and by the mob-mind had been thrust into the corporeal being of Mart Eden, the hoodlum and sailor. But it couldn’t fool him. He was not that sun-myth that the mob was worshipping and sacrificing dinners to. He knew better.

Mrs. Leland passed Julian one afternoon in the street, smiled, and dipped her head. He answered with a smile and a lift of his hat. It meant nothing to him. A month earlier it might have repelled him, or at least stirred his curiosity and sent him wondering what went on behind her eyes at that instant. Now it provoked no second reflection. The next moment it was gone from his mind. He forgot it as he might have forgotten the granite post office or the courthouse after walking by them. And yet his mind was in unnatural motion. His thoughts wheeled and circled without rest. At the center of that circle was “work accomplished”; it gnawed at his brain like an immortal worm. He woke to it in the morning. It haunted his sleep at night. Everything in life that came to him through the senses was instantly referred to “work accomplished.” He followed the pitiless chain of logic to its end and found himself reduced to no one, to nothing. Jules Eden, the rough, and Jules Eden, the seaman, had been real, had been himself; but Julian Eden, the celebrated author, did not exist. Julian Eden, the celebrated author, was a mist rising in the public mind and by that public mind imposed upon the flesh of Jules Eden, rough and sailor. But it could not deceive him. He was not that sun-born idol to whom the crowd prayed and for whom it sacrificed its dinners. He knew better.

“Frighten him!” echoed Sikes. “It’ll be no sham frightening, mind you. If there’s anything queer about him when we once get into the work; in for a penny, in for a pound. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think of that, before you send him. Mark my words!” said the robber, poising a crowbar, which he had drawn from under the bedstead.

“Scare him?” Sikes echoed. “This won’t be some harmless act, believe me. If he shows any sign of trouble once the job begins, I’m going all the way. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think long and hard about that before you send him out. Remember what I’ve told you!” the thief said, balancing a crowbar he had pulled from beneath the bed frame.

Mr. Bumble grasped the undertaker by the arm, and led him into the building. Mr. Sowerberry was closeted with the board for five minutes; and it was arranged that Oliver should go to him that evening “upon liking”—a phrase which means, in the case of a parish apprentice, that if the master find, upon a short trial, that he can get enough work out of a boy without putting too much food into him, he shall have him for a term of years, to do what he likes with.

Mr. Bumble took the undertaker by the arm and guided him inside. Mr. Sowerberry conferred privately with the board for five minutes, and it was settled that Oliver would be sent to him that very evening "on approval"—a term which, for a parish apprentice, signifies that if the master discovers, after a brief trial, that he can extract sufficient labor from a boy without feeding him too generously, he may keep him for a number of years, to treat as he pleases.

Mrs. Teddy went about the village in a coloured dress bearing herself confidently. Teddy had been listed now as "missing, since reported killed," and she had had two letters from his comrades. They said Teddy had been left behind in the ruins of a farm with one or two other wounded, and that when the Canadians retook the place these wounded had all been found butchered. None had been found alive. Afterwards the Canadians had had to fall back. Mr. Direck had been at great pains to hunt up wounded men from Teddy's company, and also any likely Canadians both at the base hospital in France and in London, and to get what he could from them. He had made it a service to Cissie. Only one of his witnesses was quite clear about Teddy, but he, alas! was dreadfully clear. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and obviously that must have been Teddy. "He had been prodded in half-a-dozen places. His head was nearly severed from his body."

Mrs. Haviland moved through the village in a bright patterned frock, carrying herself with a bold, almost triumphant air. By then Stephen had been entered on the lists as “missing, reported killed,” and she had received two letters from his comrades. They said that Stephen had been left behind in the shattered shell of a farmhouse with one or two other wounded men, and that when the Canadians took the position back these wounded had been discovered slaughtered. Not one had been found alive. Afterward the Canadians had been compelled to withdraw. Mr. Loring had taken immense pains to trace wounded men from Stephen’s company, and also any likely Canadians, both in the base hospital in France and in London, and to draw from them whatever information he could. He had made the matter a service to Elsie. Only one of his witnesses was entirely certain about Stephen, but, unhappily, he was certain in the worst way. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and plainly that must have been Stephen. “He had been stabbed in half a dozen places. His head was almost cut off his body.”

“All thinkers on general subjects, the greatest minds in the world, in fact, rely on the specialists. Herbert Spencer did that. He generalized upon the findings of thousands of investigators. He would have had to live a thousand lives in order to do it all himself. And so with Darwin. He took advantage of all that had been learned by the florists and cattle-breeders.”

Every philosopher of broad themes—the most profound intellects on earth—actually depends upon the specialist. Herbert Spencer followed this model. He drew his universal principles from the raw data of countless individual researchers. He would have required a thousand lifetimes to gather such evidence personally. Darwin was no different; he relied on the practical insights already gained by horticulturalists and livestock farmers.

While she remained thus, overcome by her forebodings, the old clock indoors whizzed forth twelve strokes. Soon after, faint sounds floated to her ear from afar over the hills. The breeze came from that quarter, and it had brought with it the notes of distant bells, gaily starting off in a peal: one, two, three, four, five. The ringers at East Egdon were announcing the nuptials of Eustacia and her son.

As she stood transfixed, weighed down by her grim premonitions, the old clock inside the house whirred out twelve sharp strokes. Moments later, faint sounds drifted to her from across the distant hills. The breeze blew in from that direction, carrying the clear notes of far-off bells, gaily bursting into a peal: one, two, three, four, five. The ringers at East Egdon were announcing the wedding of Eustacia and her son.

Behind the luminous streams of this _aurora militaris_, she could see the hue of Troy’s sword arm, spread in a scarlet haze over the space covered by its motions, like a twanged harpstring, and behind all Troy himself, mostly facing her; sometimes, to show the rear cuts, half turned away, his eye nevertheless always keenly measuring her breadth and outline, and his lips tightly closed in sustained effort. Next, his movements lapsed slower, and she could see them individually. The hissing of the sword had ceased, and he stopped entirely.

Through the shimmering trails of this military dawn-light, she glimpsed the tint of Troy's blade-hand, diffused in a crimson blur across the arc of its swings, much like a plucked harp string vibrating, and beyond it all Troy himself, mainly turned toward her; occasionally, to demonstrate the backhand strokes, half pivoted away, yet his gaze perpetually sharp, gauging her form and silhouette, his mouth pressed firm with steady exertion. Then his motions decelerated, and she could discern each one distinctly. The sword’s whisper faded, and he came to a full halt.

On Friday night he finished the serial, twenty-one thousand words long. At two cents a word, he calculated, that would bring him four hundred and twenty dollars. Not a bad week’s work. It was more money than he had ever possessed at one time. He did not know how he could spend it all. He had tapped a gold mine. Where this came from he could always get more. He planned to buy some more clothes, to subscribe to many magazines, and to buy dozens of reference books that at present he was compelled to go to the library to consult. And still there was a large portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars unspent. This worried him until the thought came to him of hiring a servant for Gertrude and of buying a bicycle for Marian.

By Friday evening, he had completed the twenty-one-thousand-word serial. He calculated that at two cents per word, the piece would earn him four hundred and twenty dollars. It was an impressive return for a single week’s labor. This sum exceeded any amount of money he had ever held at once, and he was unsure how he could possibly exhaust it. He had struck a gold mine, and he knew he could return to the source for more whenever he wished. His plans involved upgrading his wardrobe, subscribing to numerous periodicals, and purchasing the dozens of reference volumes he currently had to visit the library to use. Even so, a significant portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars remained untouched. This surplus troubled him until he hit upon the idea of employing a domestic worker for Gertrude and getting a bicycle for Marian.

The strange luminous semi-opacities of fine autumn afternoons and eves intensified into Rembrandt effects the few yellow sunbeams which came through holes and divisions in the canvas, and spirted like jets of gold-dust across the dusky blue atmosphere of haze pervading the tent, until they alighted on inner surfaces of cloth opposite, and shone like little lamps suspended there.

The inside of the tent was dim, steeped in a strange, distinctive soft shadow. The odd, glowing half-translucence of crisp autumn late afternoons and early evenings turned the handful of pale gold sunbeams slipping through tears and gaps in the canvas into dramatic Rembrandt-style light effects: they streaked like sprays of fine gold dust across the hazy, dusky blue air that filled the entire space, before landing on the opposite inner canvas lining, where they glowed like tiny, suspended lamps.

The authorship process is by nature iterative, and we recognize the importance of facilitating the editing of existing content. In particular, we anticipate a feedback loop in which the author observes or imagines reader turns and wishes to test them and tune the semantic matching model based on the results. For example, an author might want to verify that some reader turn matches, or does not match, a particular edge and add it to that edge as either a positive or anti-example if the desired behavior is not observed.

metadata.title: Usnea: An Authorship Tool for Interactive Fiction using Retrieval Based Semantic Parsing

text: The authorship process is inherently iterative, and we acknowledge the need to support the revision of existing content. Specifically, we envision a feedback loop in which the author observes or anticipates reader turns and seeks to test them, then refines the semantic matching model accordingly. For instance, an author may wish to check whether a given reader turn does or does not match a particular edge and, if the expected behavior does not occur, add it to that edge as either a positive example or a counterexample.

Wildeve had been brooding ever since they started on the mean estimation in which he was held by his wife’s friends; and it cut his heart severely. As the minutes passed he had gradually drifted into a revengeful intention without knowing the precise moment of forming it. This was to teach Mrs. Yeobright a lesson, as he considered it to be; in other words, to show her if he could that her niece’s husband was the proper guardian of her niece’s money.

Ever since their departure, Wildeve had been fixated on the low opinion his wife’s kin harbored toward him, a slight that galled him to his core. With each passing mile, he found himself sliding toward a retaliatory scheme, though he could not have said exactly when the impulse first took root. His aim was to provide Mrs. Yeobright with what he deemed a necessary lesson: to prove that, as her niece’s husband, he was the only rightful custodian of the woman’s fortune.

Fagin laid his hand upon the bundle, and locking it in the cupboard, sat down again without speaking. But he did not take his eyes off the robber, for an instant, during this action; and now that they sat over against each other, face to face, he looked fixedly at him, with his lips quivering so violently, and his face so altered by the emotions which had mastered him, that the housebreaker involuntarily drew back his chair, and surveyed him with a look of real affright.

Fagin rested his hand on the wrapped bundle, stowed it in the cupboard and turned the key in the lock, then settled back into his seat without uttering a sound. Not once during this whole sequence did his gaze stray from the robber’s face, and now that they sat directly across from each other, face to face, he pinned the other man with a fixed stare. His lips quivered so violently, and his features were so warped by the overwhelming emotions that had seized him, that the housebreaker pulled his chair back without thinking, eyeing him with a look of unfeigned, sharp fright.

Now came a diversion. We heard shrieks and yells, and soon a woman came running and crying; and seeing our group, she flung herself into our midst and begged for protection. A mob of people came tearing after her, some with torches, and they said she was a witch who had caused several cows to die by a strange disease, and practiced her arts by help of a devil in the form of a black cat. This poor woman had been stoned until she hardly looked human, she was so battered and bloody. The mob wanted to burn her.

A sudden tumult arose. We detected wails and shouts, and presently a female emerged in a state of frantic flight; upon observing our assembly, she cast herself among us and pleaded for sanctuary. A riotous crowd pursued her closely, brandishing firebrands, and they proclaimed her a conjurer who had cursed the local herds with a mysterious plague, performing her spells with the aid of a fiend in the shape of an ink-colored cat. The wretched creature had been pelted with rocks until her features were scarcely recognizable, so mangled was she by the assault. The rabble intended to consign her to the pyre.

Eustacia remained within the bank looking at the fire, intending to go indoors, yet lingering still. Had she not by her situation been inclined to hold in indifference all things honoured of the gods and of men she would probably have come away. But her state was so hopeless that she could play with it. To have lost is less disturbing than to wonder if we may possibly have won; and Eustacia could now, like other people at such a stage, take a standing-point outside herself, observe herself as a disinterested spectator, and think what a sport for Heaven this woman Eustacia was.

Eustacia stayed inside the bank, gazing at the fire and meaning to go back indoors, yet still lingering there. Had her circumstances not already disposed her to regard with indifference everything revered by gods and men, she would likely have left at once. But her condition was so utterly desperate that she could almost toy with it. Loss is less troubling than the question of whether we may perhaps have won after all; and now Eustacia, like others in such a state, could stand apart from herself, watch herself as though she were a detached observer, and wonder what a jest she made for Heaven, this woman Eustacia.

From that time, in all weathers, she waited there two hours. As the clock struck two, she was there, and at four she turned resignedly away. When it was not too wet or inclement for her child to be with her, they went together; at other times she was alone; but, she never missed a single day.

After that gray, unremarkable Tuesday, no matter how brutal the weather grew, Elara kept her two-hour vigil at the splintered oak fence marking the edge of the depot lot. When the town clock struck two, she was already leaning against its weathered slats, and when it chimed four, she turned for home with a quiet, resigned slump to her shoulders. On days when the rain stayed light and the wind did not bite hard enough to risk her young daughter Mia falling ill, the girl came with her, kicking at loose gravel along the path as they went; on harsher days she made the trip alone, but she never once missed a single visit.

And so the young man has settled in Paris. Manager to a diamond merchant, or some such thing, is he not?”

“A good many of us came to know it just as well as he did before long,” said the captain with a chuckle. “I spent seven years under water because of it when I was a boy—in that cursed surgery on the Triumph, watching men carried down to the cockpit with their arms and legs blown to bits.... So the young fellow has settled in Paris. He’s manager to a diamond merchant, or something of the sort, isn’t he?”

Though Mr. Britling's anxiety about his son was dispelled, his mind remained curiously apprehensive throughout July. He had a feeling that things were not going well with the world, a feeling he tried in vain to dispel by various distractions. Perhaps some subtler subconscious analysis of the situation was working out probabilities that his conscious self would not face. And when presently he bicycled off to Mrs. Harrowdean for flattery, amusement, and comfort generally, he found her by no means the exalting confirmation of everything he wished to believe about himself and the universe, that had been her delightful rôle in the early stages of their romantic friendship. She maintained her hostility to Edith; she seemed bent on making things impossible. And yet there were one or two phases of the old sustaining intimacies.

Even though Mr. Britling no longer felt anxious about his son, a strange, unshakable sense of foreboding stayed with him throughout the whole of July. He carried a quiet certainty that the world was going awry, a feeling he tried and failed to shake off with all manner of trivial diversions. It may well have been that some subtle, subconscious breakdown of the circumstances around him was calculating risks his conscious mind refused to acknowledge. When he eventually bicycled over to Mrs. Harrowdean’s seeking flattery, lighthearted fun, and general comfort, however, he found she was far from the uplifting, approving presence who had validated every one of his beliefs about himself and the wider world back in the giddy early days of their romantic friendship. She still clung to her open hostility toward Edith, and seemed set on making their entire dynamic unworkable. Even so, faint traces of their old, sustaining intimacy still surfaced between them on occasion.

"Nothing changes in England, because the people who want to change things change their minds before they change anything else. I've been in London talking for the last half-year. Studying art they call it. Before that I was a science student, and I want to be one again. Don't you think, Sir, there's something about science--it's steadier than anything else in the world?"

I’ve spent the last six months in London talking. They call it studying art. Before that I was a science student, and I want to be one again. Don’t you think, sir, there’s something about science? The truth was, that as she now stood—excited, wild, and honest as the day—her alluring beauty bore out so fully the epithets he had bestowed upon it that he was quite startled at his temerity in advancing them as false. He said mechanically, “Ah, why?” and continued to look at her.

Another invented answer had risen once more to his lips, but it was once more checked, and he regarded her with a look suddenly arrested. The truth was that, as she stood there now—excited, untamed, and as candid as daylight itself—her irresistible beauty justified so completely the very names he had flung at it that he was almost startled by his own audacity in calling them false. He murmured, almost involuntarily, “Ah, why?” and kept his eyes upon her.

“I have not touched you,” said Troy, quietly. “It was mere sleight of hand. The sword passed behind you. Now you are not afraid, are you? Because if you are I can’t perform. I give my word that I will not only not hurt you, but not once touch you.”

“I never made contact with you,” Troy said in a calm voice. “It was only a trick of the hand. The blade went right past your back. You’re not frightened now, are you? For if you are, I won’t be able to go on. I promise I’ll neither harm you nor so much as brush against you.”

They were happy days, those of Oliver’s recovery. Everything was so quiet, and neat, and orderly; everybody so kind and gentle; that after the noise and turbulence in the midst of which he had always lived, it seemed like Heaven itself. He was no sooner strong enough to put his clothes on, properly, than Mr. Brownlow caused a complete new suit, and a new cap, and a new pair of shoes, to be provided for him. As Oliver was told that he might do what he liked with the old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been very kind to him, and asked her to sell them to a Jew, and keep the money for herself. This she very readily did; and, as Oliver looked out of the parlour window, and saw the Jew roll them up in his bag and walk away, he felt quite delighted to think that they were safely gone, and that there was now no possible danger of his ever being able to wear them again. They were sad rags, to tell the truth; and Oliver had never had a new suit before.

The days of Oliver’s recovery were unreservedly happy. Every part of the house was quiet, neat, and perfectly ordered; everyone around him was so kind and gentle that, after a lifetime spent amid constant noise and turmoil, the place felt like nothing short of heaven. As soon as he was strong enough to dress himself properly, Mr. Brownlow arranged for a full new suit, a new cap, and a new pair of shoes to be made for him. When Oliver was told he could do whatever he liked with his old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been particularly good to him, and asked her to sell them to the Jewish peddler who frequented the area and keep the money for herself. She did so gladly, and as Oliver watched from the parlor window while the peddler rolled the clothes into his bag and walked off, he felt thoroughly delighted to know they were gone for good, and there was no possible chance he would ever have to wear them again. To tell the truth, those clothes were little more than tattered rags, and Oliver had never owned a new suit in his entire life.

He had once before felt in his own person this overpowering of the fervid by the inanimate; but then it had tended to enervate a passion far sweeter than that which at present pervaded him. It was once when he stood parting from Eustacia in the moist still levels beyond the hills.

He had experienced this crushing of human heat by the lifeless material of the earth once before, though in that earlier instance, the sensation had merely dampened a sentiment far gentler than the one now consuming him. It had occurred as he stood taking leave of Elara within the damp, hushed lowlands that lay beyond the crest of the hills.

Earlier work on text simplification define a sentence as simple, if it has more frequent words, shorter length and fewer syllables per word, etc. This motivates a variety of syntactic rule-based methods, such as reducing sentence length (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997; Vickrey and Koller, 2008) , lexical substitution (Glavas and Stajner, 2015; Paetzold and Specia, 2016) or sentence splitting (Woodsend and Lapata, 2011; Sulem et al., 2018b) . Another line of work follows the success of machine translation (MT) (Klein et al., 2017) , and regards TS as a monolingual translation from complex language to simple language (Zhu et al., 2010; Coster and Kauchak, 2011; Wubben et al., 2012) . Zhang and Lapata (2017) incorporate reinforcement learning into the encoder-decoder framework to encourage three types of simplification rewards concerning language simplicity, relevance and fluency, while Shardlow and Nawaz (2019) improve the performance of MT models by introducing explanatory synonyms. To alleviate the heavy burden of parallel training corpora, Surya et al. (2019) propose an unsupervised model via adversarial learning between a shared encoder and separate decoders.

metadata.title: Expertise Style Transfer: A New Task Towards Better Communication between Experts and Laymen

text: Prior research on text simplification characterizes a sentence as simple when it contains more frequent vocabulary, reduced length, and fewer syllables per word. This characterization has inspired a range of syntactic rule-based approaches, including sentence compression (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997; Vickrey and Koller, 2008), lexical substitution (Glavas and Stajner, 2015; Paetzold and Specia, 2016), and sentence splitting (Woodsend and Lapata, 2011; Sulem et al., 2018b). A separate strand of work builds on advances in machine translation (MT) (Klein et al., 2017) and frames text simplification as a monolingual translation task that converts complex language into simpler language (Zhu et al., 2010; Coster and Kauchak, 2011; Wubben et al., 2012). Zhang and Lapata (2017) integrate reinforcement learning into an encoder-decoder architecture to promote three simplification objectives—simplicity, relevance, and fluency—whereas Shardlow and Nawaz (2019) enhance MT model performance by incorporating explanatory synonyms. To reduce the reliance on parallel training data, Surya et al. (2019) introduce an unsupervised approach that employs adversarial learning between a shared encoder and distinct decoders.

Thus has a week passed away, while I have listened to the strangest tale that ever imagination formed. My thoughts and every feeling of my soul have been drunk up by the interest for my guest which this tale and his own elevated and gentle manners have created. I wish to soothe him, yet can I counsel one so infinitely miserable, so destitute of every hope of consolation, to live? Oh, no! The only joy that he can now know will be when he composes his shattered spirit to peace and death. Yet he enjoys one comfort, the offspring of solitude and delirium; he believes that when in dreams he holds converse with his friends and derives from that communion consolation for his miseries or excitements to his vengeance, that they are not the creations of his fancy, but the beings themselves who visit him from the regions of a remote world. This faith gives a solemnity to his reveries that render them to me almost as imposing and interesting as truth.

A full week has slipped by as I have absorbed the most extraordinary story ever spun by mortal fancy. My mind and every pulse of my soul have been consumed by the fascination for my visitor which his narrative and his own lofty, tender bearing have awakened. I long to comfort him, yet dare I urge a being so fathomlessly wretched, so stripped of any hope of solace, to endure? Ah, no! The sole happiness left to him now will arrive when he lays his fractured spirit to rest in stillness and oblivion. Still, he possesses one strange consolation, born of solitude and fevered vision; he trusts that when in sleep he converses with his companions and draws from that fellowship either relief for his sorrows or fuel for his revenge, they are no phantoms of his own invention, but the very souls who visit him from the borders of some distant sphere. This conviction lends his trances a gravity that makes them, to my perception, nearly as majestic and compelling as reality.

The accepted question is mapped into a SQL query through a text-to-SQL model, and finally the SQL execution results are returned to the user. While they have been shown effective in pilot study and production, rule-based approaches are limited in terms of coverage, scalability and naturalnessthey are not robust against the diversity of human language expressions and are difficult to scale across domains.

metadata.title: PHOTON: A Robust Cross-Domain Text-to-SQL System

text: Natural language interfaces to databases (NLIDBs) (Popescu et al., 2003; Li and Jagadish, 2014) facilitate broader access to relational data for end users and have remained a central focus of research for several decades (Hemphill et al., 1990; Dahl et al., 1994; Zelle and Mooney, 1996; Popescu et al., 2003; Bertomeu et al., 2006; Zhong et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018, 2019a). Traditional NLIDB architectures are frequently modular, combining rule-based parsing with components for ambiguity detection and pragmatics modeling (Li and Jagadish, 2014; Setlur et al., 2016, 2019). For instance, as illustrated in the PHOTON workflow in Figure 1, the system utilizes a question corrector to identify untranslatable user queries and pinpoint spans necessitating clarification; once validated, the input is processed by a text-to-SQL model to generate a query, with the resulting data finally returned to the user. While these rule-based frameworks have proven effective in pilot studies and production environments, they remain constrained by limited coverage, scalability issues, and a lack of naturalness. Specifically, such systems lack robustness against the inherent diversity of human expression and present significant challenges when attempting to scale across different domains.

But it was pure, earnest, joyful reality. They drove straight to the door of the chief hotel (which Oliver used to stare up at, with awe, and think a mighty palace, but which had somehow fallen off in grandeur and size); and here was Mr. Grimwig all ready to receive them, kissing the young lady, and the old one too, when they got out of the coach, as if he were the grandfather of the whole party, all smiles and kindness, and not offering to eat his head—no, not once; not even when he contradicted a very old postboy about the nearest road to London, and maintained he knew it best, though he had only come that way once, and that time fast asleep. There was dinner prepared, and there were bedrooms ready, and everything was arranged as if by magic.

Yet it was a sincere, vivid, and joyful reality. They pulled up at the entrance of the town’s principal hotel—a place Oliver had once viewed with such awe that he had mistaken it for a vast palace, though it now appeared curiously shrunken and less imposing. Mr. Grimwig was there to welcome them, greeting both the young lady and the elder woman with a grandfatherly affection as they stepped from the coach. He was the very picture of kindness, never once threatening to eat his own head; he remained remarkably pleasant even while stubbornly correcting an ancient postboy regarding the quickest route to London, insisting he knew the way best despite having only traveled it once while soundly asleep. Dinner was waiting, the bedrooms were ready, and every comfort seemed to have been arranged as if by magic.

This results in the source sentence "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 ", of which s 2 t and s 3 t serve as the anchors which are actually the target language words obtained by translating s 2 and s 3 according to the dictionary, respectively. Through the anchors, some words on the source side share the same word embeddings with the corresponding words on the target side. The AT process will strengthen the consistency of embedding spaces of both languages based on these anchors.

metadata.title: Bilingual Dictionary Based Neural Machine Translation without Using Parallel Sentences

text: As shown in Figure 1 (a), starting from the source sentence “s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 ” in which the words s 2 and s 3 are covered by the bilingual dictionary, we substitute those two words with their dictionary translations. This yields the sentence “s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 ”, where s 2 t and s 3 t function as anchors—target-language words obtained by translating s 2 and s 3 via the dictionary, respectively. By means of these anchors, certain source-side words share the same word embeddings with their corresponding target-side words. The anchor training (AT) procedure then reinforces the consistency of the two languages’ embedding spaces on the basis of these anchors.

Farmer Oak went towards the plantation and pushed through its lower boughs to the windy side. A dim mass under the slope reminded him that a shed occupied a place here, the site being a cutting into the slope of the hill, so that at its back part the roof was almost level with the ground. In front it was formed of board nailed to posts and covered with tar as a preservative. Through crevices in the roof and side spread streaks and dots of light, a combination of which made the radiance that had attracted him. Oak stepped up behind, where, leaning down upon the roof and putting his eye close to a hole, he could see into the interior clearly.

Farmer Oak approached the grove and maneuvered through the low branches until he reached the windward side. A shadowy form nestled against the hill signaled the location of a shed, built into an excavation in the rise so that the rear of its roof rested nearly even with the soil. The front consisted of wooden planks fastened to posts and treated with tar to withstand the elements. Rays and specks of light filtered through gaps in the walls and ceiling, forming the collective glow that had drawn his attention. Oak moved to the back, where he leaned over the roof and peered through a small hole, gaining a clear view of the scene inside.

Intellectual “work” is misnamed; it is a pleasure, a dissipation, and is its own highest reward. The poorest paid architect, engineer, general, author, sculptor, painter, lecturer, advocate, legislator, actor, preacher, singer is constructively in heaven when he is at work; and as for the musician with the fiddle-bow in his hand who sits in the midst of a great orchestra with the ebbing and flowing tides of divine sound washing over him--why, certainly, he is at work, if you wish to call it that, but lord, it's a sarcasm just the same. The law of work does seem utterly unfair--but there it is, and nothing can change it: the higher the pay in enjoyment the worker gets out of it, the higher shall be his pay in cash, also. And it's also the very law of those transparent swindles, transmissible nobility and kingship.

The label "intellectual labor" is a misnomer; it is a delight, an indulgence, and carries its own supreme compensation. The most underpaid architect, engineer, general, writer, sculptor, painter, speaker, lawyer, lawmaker, performer, minister, vocalist is essentially in paradise while engaged in his craft; and as for the musician, bow in hand, seated within a vast orchestra while surging currents of sublime sound cascade over him—well, yes, he is working, if you insist on calling it that, but heavens, the term is a mockery all the same. The principle of work appears profoundly unjust—yet there it stands, immutable: the greater the pleasure a worker derives from his toil, the greater his monetary reward will be as well. And this is precisely the same rule governing those blatant frauds, hereditary aristocracy and monarchy.

Bathsheba’s was an impulsive nature under a deliberative aspect. An Elizabeth in brain and a Mary Stuart in spirit, she often performed actions of the greatest temerity with a manner of extreme discretion. Many of her thoughts were perfect syllogisms; unluckily they always remained thoughts. Only a few were irrational assumptions; but, unfortunately, they were the ones which most frequently grew into deeds.

Marwenna carried an impulsive, fiery streak beneath a cool, deliberative outward bearing. Endowed with the shrewd, strategic mind of Eleanor of Aquitaine and the reckless, romantic spirit of Isabella of France, she often pulled off acts of staggering boldness with a demeanor of quiet, unflappable discretion. Most of her ruminations were perfectly constructed, logically sound syllogisms; unluckily, they never progressed beyond the realm of idle thought. Only a tiny fraction were ungrounded, impulsive leaps of assumption; yet those, unfortunately, were the notions that most often took root and grew into tangible action.

After the jolt of the food panic and a brief, financial scare, the vast inertia of everyday life in England asserted itself. When the public went to the banks for the new paper money, the banks tendered gold--apologetically. The supply of the new notes was very insufficient, and there was plenty of gold. After the first impression that a universal catastrophe had happened there was an effect as if nothing had happened.

Once the initial shock of the food crisis and a fleeting monetary dread had passed, the immense, sluggish routine of daily English life reasserted its hold. When people approached the banks for the freshly issued paper currency, the banks—almost apologetically—offered gold instead. The new notes were in woefully short supply, while gold remained abundant. Following that first sensation of an all-encompassing disaster, an eerie calm settled, as though nothing had occurred at all.

Again she looked. All the centuries of woman since sex began were eloquent in her eyes. And he measured her in a careless way, and knew, bold now, that she would begin to retreat, coyly and delicately, as he pursued, ever ready to reverse the game should he turn fainthearted. And, too, he was human, and could feel the draw of her, while his ego could not but appreciate the flattery of her kindness. Oh, he knew it all, and knew them well, from A to Z. Good, as goodness might be measured in their particular class, hard-working for meagre wages and scorning the sale of self for easier ways, nervously desirous for some small pinch of happiness in the desert of existence, and facing a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of unending toil and the black pit of more terrible wretchedness, the way whereto being briefer though better paid.

Again she looked. In her eyes were all the centuries of womanhood since sex first began to stir. And he measured her with a kind of careless confidence, knowing now, boldly, that as he advanced she would start to retreat, shyly and delicately, always ready to turn the game about again should he grow weak. Yet he was human too, and could not help feeling her attraction, while his vanity could not but relish the tribute of her kindness. Oh, he understood it all, knew them thoroughly from A to Z. Decent, as decency went among people of their sort; laboring hard for small wages and scorning to sell themselves for easier gain; anxiously hungry for some little scrap of happiness in the desert of life, and staring toward a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of endless drudgery and the black abyss of a more dreadful misery, the road to which was shorter, though better paid.

Our conversations are not always confined to his own history and misfortunes. On every point of general literature he displays unbounded knowledge and a quick and piercing apprehension. His eloquence is forcible and touching; nor can I hear him, when he relates a pathetic incident or endeavours to move the passions of pity or love, without tears. What a glorious creature must he have been in the days of his prosperity, when he is thus noble and godlike in ruin! He seems to feel his own worth and the greatness of his fall.

We don’t always limit our conversations to his personal history and the misfortunes he’s endured. On every subject within general literature, he showcases a staggering breadth of knowledge and a sharp, perceptive grasp of nuance. He speaks with such compelling, heartfelt eloquence that I never manage to hold back tears when he recounts a devastating story, or tries to rouse feelings of pity or tenderness in those listening. If he carries himself with this much dignity and almost divine grace even in ruin, I can only fathom how extraordinary he must have been in the height of his success. He seems fully conscious of both his own inherent worth, and the sheer magnitude of how far he has fallen.

Bathsheba loved Troy in the way that only self-reliant women love when they abandon their self-reliance. When a strong woman recklessly throws away her strength she is worse than a weak woman who has never had any strength to throw away. One source of her inadequacy is the novelty of the occasion. She has never had practice in making the best of such a condition. Weakness is doubly weak by being new.

Elspeth adored Torin with the fierce, unmoored devotion that only fiercely independent women know when they cast aside their own hard-won self-sufficiency. When a woman accustomed to holding her own carelessly discards that well-earned fortitude, she fares far worse than the soft, untested woman who never possessed such strength to squander in the first place. A core root of her helplessness lies in how entirely unacquainted she is with this state of being. She has never had cause to practice navigating, let alone making the most of, such vulnerable disarray. Fragility is doubly feeble when it is still new, unrooted in long habit.

“Poor little thing, her feelings got the better of her, no doubt. ’Tis nature. Well, they may call him what they will—he’ve several acres of heth-ground broke up here, besides the public house, and the heth-croppers, and his manners be quite like a gentleman’s. And what’s done cannot be undone.”

The poor girl was simply overcome by her feelings, no doubt. It is only natural. People may say what they wish about him—he has cultivated several acres of heath, owns the local inn, and keeps a stock of ponies. His manners are those of a true gentleman. Besides, what is done is done, and it cannot be altered now.

They entered a dim, cluttered study crammed with books and strewn with papers, where a fire roared in the grate. A kettle puffed steam on the hearth, and amid the chaos of scattered documents a table gleamed, laden with wine, brandy, rum, sugar, and lemons.

While q MT and q LM can, in general, be any two models, we exploit the characteristics of NMT models to provide a more meaningful, model-specific estimate of XMI. NMT architectures typically consist of two components: an encoder that embeds the input text sequence, and a decoder that generates translated output text. The latter acts as a conditional language model, where the source-language sentence embedded by the encoder drives the target-language generation. Hence, we use the decoder of q MT as our q LM to accurately estimate the difficulty of translation for a given architecture in a controlled way.

metadata.title: It's Easier to Translate out of English than into it: Measuring Neural Translation Difficulty by Cross-Mutual Information
metadata.section: Disentangling Translation Difficulty and Monolingual Complexity

text: While q_MT and q_LM can, in principle, be arbitrary models, we leverage the structural properties of neural machine translation architectures to derive a model-specific, interpretable measure of XMI. Standard NMT designs comprise an encoder that maps the source sequence into a representation, and a decoder that produces the target-language output. The decoder operates as a conditional language model, conditioned on the encoder’s representation of the source sentence to steer generation in the target language. Accordingly, we adopt the decoder of q_MT as q_LM, enabling a precise, architecture-aware estimation of translation difficulty under controlled conditions.

“Very well,” said Wildeve, rising. Searching about with the lantern, he found a large flat stone, which he placed between himself and Christian, and sat down again. The lantern was opened to give more light, and its rays directed upon the stone. Christian put down a shilling, Wildeve another, and each threw. Christian won. They played for two, Christian won again.

"Fine," Wildeve replied, getting to his feet. Scouring the area by the lantern’s glow, he located a wide, level stone, positioned it between himself and Christian, and sat back down. He opened the lantern to brighten the area, focusing its beams onto the rock. Christian set down a shilling, Wildeve matched the bet, and each of them threw. Christian won. They played for two shillings, and Christian was victorious again.

Existing semantically-motivated metrics for reference-free evaluation of MT systems have so far displayed rather poor correlation with human estimates of translation quality. In this work, we investigate a range of reference-free metrics based on cutting-edge models for inducing cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual (contextualized) word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We have identified some scenarios in which these metrics fail, prominently their inability to punish literal word-by-word translations (the so-called "translationese"). We have investigated two different mechanisms for mitigating this undesired phenomenon: (1) an additional (weakly-supervised) cross-lingual alignment step, reducing the mismatch between representations of mutual translations, and 2language modeling (LM) on the target side, which is inherently equipped to punish "unnatural" sentences in the target language. We show that the reference-free coupling of cross-lingual similarity scores with the target-side language model surpasses the reference-based BLEU in segment-level MT evaluation. We believe our results have two relevant implications. First, they portray the viability of referencefree MT evaluation and warrant wider research efforts in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation may be the most challenging ("adversarial") evaluation task for multilingual text encoders as it uncovers some of their shortcomings-prominently, the inability to capture semantically non-sensical word-by-word translations or paraphrases-which remain hidden in their common evaluation scenarios.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Existing semantically grounded metrics for reference-free machine translation (MT) system evaluation have thus far exhibited consistently weak correlation with human judgments of translation quality. In the present work, we examine a suite of reference-free metrics built on state-of-the-art models for deriving cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual contextualized word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We identify key failure modes for these metrics, most notably their failure to penalize literal, word-for-word translations, a phenomenon widely referred to as “translationese.” We test two distinct mechanisms to mitigate this undesirable limitation: (1) an auxiliary weakly supervised cross-lingual alignment step that reduces representational mismatch between pairs of mutual translations, and (2) target-side language modeling (LM), which is natively suited to penalizing unnatural phrasing and ungrammatical sentences in the target language. We demonstrate that a reference-free metric combining cross-lingual similarity scores with target-side LM scoring outperforms the reference-based BLEU metric on segment-level MT evaluation tasks. Our findings carry two notable implications. First, they confirm the feasibility of reference-free MT evaluation and justify expanded research investment in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation represents a uniquely challenging “adversarial” evaluation task for multilingual text encoders, as it exposes critical limitations that remain hidden in these encoders’ standard evaluation scenarios—most notably, an inability to detect semantically incoherent word-for-word translations or invalid paraphrases.

Copy Vs. Context We observe that models allowing for augmented input sentences effectively learn to output the target words used as augmented translations. Table 5 illustrates the rates of usage. We compute for each word added in the input sentence as T (part of a lexicalised match), R (not in the match) and E (from an embedding match), how often they appear in the translated sentence. Results show that T words increase their usage rate by more than 10% compared to the corresponding base models. Considering R words, models incorporating fuzzy matches increase their usage rate compared to base models, albeit with lower rates than for T words. Furthermore, the number of R words output by FM + is clearly lower than those output by FM # , demonstrating the effect of marking unrelated matching words. Thus, we can confirm the copy behaviour of the networks with lexicalised matches. Words marked as E (embedding matches) increase their usage rates when compared to base models but are far from the rates of T words. We hypothesize that these sentences are not copied by the translation model, rather they are used to further contextualise translations.

metadata.title: Boosting Neural Machine Translation with Similar Translations
metadata.section: Individual comparison of Matching algorithms and Augmentation methods

text: Copy vs. Context We observe that models that permit augmented input sentences effectively learn to output the target words used as augmented translations. Table 5 illustrates the rates of usage. For each word added to the input sentence as T (part of a lexicalized match), R (not in the match), or E (from an embedding match), we calculate how often it appears in the translated sentence. The results show that T words increase their usage rate by more than 10% relative to the corresponding baseline models. For R words, models incorporating fuzzy matches increase their usage rate compared with the baseline models, although the rates remain lower than those for T words. Moreover, the number of R words output by FM+ is clearly lower than that produced by FM#, demonstrating the effect of marking unrelated matching words. We can therefore confirm the copying behavior of the networks with lexicalized matches. Words labeled as E (embedding matches) also show increased usage rates compared with the baseline models, but these remain far below the rates observed for T words. We hypothesize that these sentences are not copied by the translation model; rather, they are used to further contextualize the translations.

The hill was covered on its northern side by an ancient and decaying plantation of beeches, whose upper verge formed a line over the crest, fringing its arched curve against the sky, like a mane. To-night these trees sheltered the southern slope from the keenest blasts, which smote the wood and floundered through it with a sound as of grumbling, or gushed over its crowning boughs in a weakened moan. The dry leaves in the ditch simmered and boiled in the same breezes, a tongue of air occasionally ferreting out a few, and sending them spinning across the grass. A group or two of the latest in date amongst the dead multitude had remained till this very mid-winter time on the twigs which bore them and in falling rattled against the trunks with smart taps.

The ridge was cloaked on its eastern face by a venerable and moribund grove of oaks, the highest reaches of which etched a dark border over the summit, crowning the pale arc of the firmament like a bristling fringe. This night, the timber served to screen the western incline from the biting gusts that struck the copse and labored through the branches with a heavy muttering, or surged across the topmost limbs in a weary sigh. Down in the trench, the brittle leaves seethed and hissed in the restless air, a sudden draft occasionally prying a few loose to send them skittering wildly over the turf. A final scattering of the autumn’s dead had endured upon their native twigs until this deep mid-winter hour, and in their eventual fall, they struck the boles with a succession of crisp, percussive raps.

The strong tide, so swift, so deep, and certain, was like a congenial friend, in the morning stillness. He walked by the stream, far from the houses, and in the light and warmth of the sun fell asleep on the bank. When he awoke and was afoot again, he lingered there yet a little longer, watching an eddy that turned and turned purposeless, until the stream absorbed it, and carried it on to the sea.--“Like me.”

The powerful current, so rapid, so deep, and so sure, seemed in the morning hush like a kindly companion. He wandered beside the water, far from the dwellings, and in the sun’s light and warmth he fell asleep upon the bank. When he woke and rose again, he stayed there still a while longer, gazing at a whirlpool that spun and spun without purpose, until the stream took it into itself and bore it onward to the sea.—“Like me.”

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: We obtain source sentences, system outputs, and reference translations from the WMT17–19 news translation shared tasks (Bojar et al., 2017b; Ma et al., 2018b, 2019). These tasks provide predictions from 166 translation systems across 16 language pairs in WMT17, 149 systems across 14 pairs in WMT18, and 233 systems across 18 pairs in WMT19. Our evaluation targets X-to-English (X-en) pairs, where X represents a diverse group of 12 languages: German (de), Chinese (zh), Czech (cs), Latvian (lv), Finnish (fi), Russian (ru), Turkish (tr), Gujarati (gu), Kazakh (kk), Lithuanian (lt), and Estonian (et). Each language pair within the WMT17–19 datasets comprises approximately 3,000 source sentences, with each sentence linked to a single reference translation and the automatic translations produced by the participating systems.

The clean, sleek creature arose from its fleece—how perfectly like Aphrodite rising from the foam should have been seen to be realized—looking startled and shy at the loss of its garment, which lay on the floor in one soft cloud, united throughout, the portion visible being the inner surface only, which, never before exposed, was white as snow, and without flaw or blemish of the minutest kind.

The smooth, spotless creature lifted itself free of its fleece—you would have to see the sight to fully grasp how perfectly it mirrored Aphrodite rising from the seafoam—looking startled and timid now that its covering was gone, the garment spread across the floor in a single, soft, unbroken cloud. Only the inner layer of the fleece was visible, a surface that had never been uncovered before, as white as fresh fallen snow, with not even the tiniest speck, scratch, or blemish to be found anywhere.

Venn now bethought himself of his clothes, which were saturated with water to the weight of lead. He changed them, spread them before the fire, and lay down to sleep. But it was more than he could do to rest here while excited by a vivid imagination of the turmoil they were in at the house he had quitted, and, blaming himself for coming away, he dressed in another suit, locked up the door, and again hastened across to the inn. Rain was still falling heavily when he entered the kitchen. A bright fire was shining from the hearth, and two women were bustling about, one of whom was Olly Dowden.

He made his way back across the open heath to his van. The fire had not yet burned out, and every item sat exactly where he had left it. Venn suddenly thought of his clothes, which were so soaked through with rain they felt as heavy as lead. He changed out of the damp garments, spread them flat in front of the fire to dry, and stretched out to sleep. But rest was out of the question: his mind buzzed with sharp, vivid images of the chaos unfolding at the house he had just left. Chiding himself for departing in the first place, he pulled on a different suit, locked the van door, and hurried back across the heath toward the inn. A whole village-full of sensuous emotion, scattered abroad all the year long, surged here in a focus for an hour. The forty hearts of those waving couples were beating as they had not done since, twelve months before, they had come together in similar jollity. For the time paganism was revived in their hearts, the pride of life was all in all, and they adored none other than themselves.

All the rich, sensory feeling stitched through the entire village, scattered thin across every ordinary day of the year, swelled into a single thrumming, concentrated focus here for one short hour. The forty hearts of those swaying, dancing pairs beat faster than they had at any point since they’d gathered for the same giddy, raucous celebration twelve full months prior. For that fleeting stretch, unbridled pagan warmth bloomed again in their chests, the unapologetic joy of being alive was the only thing that mattered, and the only thing they worshipped was the bright, vivid pulse of their own selves.

Thus strangely are our souls constructed, and by such slight ligaments are we bound to prosperity or ruin. When I look back, it seems to me as if this almost miraculous change of inclination and will was the immediate suggestion of the guardian angel of my life—the last effort made by the spirit of preservation to avert the storm that was even then hanging in the stars and ready to envelop me. Her victory was announced by an unusual tranquillity and gladness of soul which followed the relinquishing of my ancient and latterly tormenting studies. It was thus that I was to be taught to associate evil with their prosecution, happiness with their disregard.

How strangely our souls are fashioned, and by what frail threads we are tethered to fortune or disaster. Looking back, I feel as though this nearly wondrous shift in desire and resolve came as a direct whisper from the guardian spirit of my existence—a final effort by the preserving force to turn aside the tempest that already loomed in the constellations, poised to swallow me. Its triumph was heralded by a rare stillness and lightness of heart that arrived the moment I abandoned my long-accursed studies. So I was schooled to link misery with their pursuit, and joy with their release.

A common way to extract word alignments is to train two models, one for the forward direction (source to target) and one for the backward direction (target to source). For each model, one can extract separate word alignments and symmetrize these using heuristics like grow-diagonal (Och and Ney, 2000b; Koehn et al., 2005) . However, this approach uses the hard word alignments of both directions as an input, and does not consider any other information of the forward and backward model. For attention-based neural networks it is possible to adapt attention optimization as described in Section 3.4 to consider two models at the same time. The goal of attention optimization is to find attention activations that lead to the correct prediction of the target sequence for a single neural network. We extend this procedure to optimize the likelihood of the sentence pair jointly under both the forward and the backward model, with the additional bias to favor contiguous alignments. Figure 5 depicts this procedure.

metadata.title: End-to-End Neural Word Alignment Outperforms GIZA++

text: A common approach to extracting word alignments is to train two models, one in the forward direction (source to target) and one in the backward direction (target to source). For each model, separate word alignments can then be extracted and symmetrized using heuristics such as grow-diagonal (Och and Ney, 2000b; Koehn et al., 2005). However, this method relies only on the hard word alignments produced by both directions as input, and it does not take into account any other information from the forward and backward models. For attention-based neural networks, attention optimization as described in Section 3.4 can be adapted to consider both models simultaneously. The objective of attention optimization is to find attention activations that yield the correct prediction of the target sequence for a single neural network. We extend this procedure by optimizing the likelihood of the sentence pair jointly under both the forward and backward models, with the additional bias of favoring contiguous alignments. Figure 5 illustrates this procedure.

The adjustment of the farmer’s hazy conceptions of her charms to the portrait of herself she now presented him with was less a diminution than a difference. The starting-point selected by the judgment was her height. She seemed tall, but the pail was a small one, and the hedge diminutive; hence, making allowance for error by comparison with these, she could have been not above the height to be chosen by women as best. All features of consequence were severe and regular. It may have been observed by persons who go about the shires with eyes for beauty, that in Englishwoman a classically-formed face is seldom found to be united with a figure of the same pattern, the highly-finished features being generally too large for the remainder of the frame; that a graceful and proportionate figure of eight heads usually goes off into random facial curves. Without throwing a Nymphean tissue over a milkmaid, let it be said that here criticism checked itself as out of place, and looked at her proportions with a long consciousness of pleasure. From the contours of her figure in its upper part, she must have had a beautiful neck and shoulders; but since her infancy nobody had ever seen them. Had she been put into a low dress she would have run and thrust her head into a bush. Yet she was not a shy girl by any means; it was merely her instinct to draw the line dividing the seen from the unseen higher than they do it in towns.

The farmer’s vague image of her attractiveness, when measured against the actual figure she now cut, underwent not so much a downgrading as a shift in kind. His eye first seized upon her stature as a reference point. She looked tall, yet the bucket was small and the hedge low; allowing for the distortion these objects introduced, she likely stood no higher than the height most women would consider ideal. Her prominent features were sharp and symmetrical. Those who travel the counties with an eye for beauty may have noted that in English women a classically sculpted face rarely pairs with an equally classical body, the finely drawn features tending to overpower the frame; a graceful, eight-heads-high figure usually dissolves into irregular facial lines. Without draping a milkmaid in nymph-like gauze, let us simply say that here judgment paused as if intruding, and regarded her proportions with a prolonged sense of delight. From the shape of her upper body, she must have possessed a lovely neck and shoulders; but no one had glimpsed them since she was a child. Had she been dressed in a low-cut gown, she would have bolted and buried her head in a bush. Yet she was hardly a timid girl; it was only her instinct to place the boundary between the visible and the hidden higher than town dwellers do.

I began saying that I wanted to ask you something, but I stray from subjects like a drunken man. I am anxious to know what my mother said to you when she last saw you. You talked with her a long time, I think?”

Yeobright passed along specific details of her illness and eventual death to him, then continued speaking: “After all this, no sort of pain will ever register as anything worse than a minor ailment to me. I started out meaning to ask you a question, didn’t I? I wander off subject as easily as a man addled by drink. What I’m eager to know is what my mother said to you the last time you saw each other. You spent a good long while talking with her, if I recall correctly?”

When Mrs. Yeobright approached, Eustacia surveyed her with the calm stare of a stranger.

When Mrs. Yeobright drew near, Eustacia regarded her with the cool, unblinking stare of a total stranger.

Bathsheba was far from dreaming that the dark and silent shape upon which she had so carelessly thrown a seed was a hotbed of tropic intensity. Had she known Boldwood’s moods, her blame would have been fearful, and the stain upon her heart ineradicable. Moreover, had she known her present power for good or evil over this man, she would have trembled at her responsibility. Luckily for her present, unluckily for her future tranquillity, her understanding had not yet told her what Boldwood was. Nobody knew entirely; for though it was possible to form guesses concerning his wild capabilities from old floodmarks faintly visible, he had never been seen at the high tides which caused them.

Bathsheba had no idea that the dark, silent figure into which she had so carelessly cast a seed contained a furnace of tropical intensity. Had she understood Boldwood’s moods, her self-reproach would have been dreadful, and the stain upon her conscience impossible to erase. And had she realized the power she now held, for good or for ill, over this man, she would have shuddered at the weight of such responsibility. Fortunately for her at that moment, though disastrously for her later peace, she had not yet grasped what Boldwood truly was. No one understood him completely; although one could infer something of his wild possibilities from the old floodmarks still faintly visible, he had never been seen in the high tides that had made them.

When trained on a 16th of the dataset (Figure 4) , the difference between the models becomes more pronounced. Most notably, the Span-ConveRT model produces a greater proportion of type 4 errors compared to the V-CNN-CRF model on every slot. This suggests that the errors Span-ConveRT makes, although not precisely correct with its span prediction, are more likely to yield a span that could parse to a correct value. For example, consider the sentence "a table for 8pm this evening". The correct span for the slot time is "8pm", but if a model erroneously predicts "8pm this evening" (a span which overlaps the label span) it will still parse to the same time as the label span. As an accompaniment to the quantitative results, we provide a brief qualitative analysis of errors in the best performing models. Considering only the first name slot, we collect the errors made on the test set that are exclusive to each model. That left 10 errors for Span-ConveRT and 50 for V-CNN-CRF. Along with our analysis based on the full set of 60 errors, we provide a random sample of 5 errors from each model in Tables 8 and 9 . A large portion of the errors exclusively made by V-CNN-CRF were predictions of spans where no name was mentioned. Many words that are not standard to the domain of restaurant booking were, often confidently, wrongly predicted as names. For example, in Table 9 we show that the words "bloody", "web", "animal" and "spread" were all predicted as first names by the baseline model. Employing transferred conversational representations evidently lessens the likelihood of these forms of errors occurring. Also included in the table is an example where the baseline model fails to recognize a name which, when corroborated with similar occurrences in the wider set of errors, suggests that it is less likely to predict spans for out-of-vocabulary names than Span-ConveRT.

text: When trained on one-sixteenth of the dataset (Figure 4), the differences between the models become more pronounced. Most notably, the Span-ConveRT model produces a larger proportion of type 4 errors than the V-CNN-CRF model for every slot. This suggests that the errors made by Span-ConveRT, although not exact span matches, are more likely to produce a span that can be parsed into the correct value. For example, consider the sentence “a table for 8pm this evening.” The correct span for the time slot is “8pm,” but if a model incorrectly predicts “8pm this evening” (a span that overlaps the labeled span), it will still parse to the same time value as the labeled span. In addition to the quantitative results, we provide a brief qualitative analysis of errors in the best-performing models. Considering only the first-name slot, we collect the test-set errors that are exclusive to each model. This leaves 10 errors for Span-ConveRT and 50 for V-CNN-CRF. Alongside our analysis of the full set of 60 errors, we present a random sample of five errors from each model in Tables 8 and 9. A large proportion of the errors made exclusively by V-CNN-CRF were span predictions in which no name was actually mentioned. Many words that are not standard in the restaurant-booking domain were, often with high confidence, incorrectly predicted as names. For instance, Table 9 shows that the words “bloody,” “web,” “animal,” and “spread” were all predicted as first names by the baseline model. Using transferred conversational representations evidently reduces the likelihood of such errors. The table also includes an example in which the baseline model fails to recognize a name, which, together with similar cases in the broader error set, suggests that it is less likely than Span-ConveRT to predict spans for out-of-vocabulary names.

She was manifestly a constitutionally triumphant woman. A certain afternoon lassitude vanished in the swirl of her arrival. Mr. Philbert removed wrappings and handed them to the manservant.

The gaunt oak-cased clock, with the picture of the Ascension on the door panel and the Miraculous Draught of Fishes on the base; his grandmother’s corner cupboard with the glass door, through which the spotted china was visible; the dumb-waiter; the wooden tea trays; the hanging fountain with the brass tap—whither would these venerable articles have to be banished?

While Julian paced the apartments, he was seized by a sharp distaste for the removals necessary to reconcile his forebears’ hallowed furniture with Genevieve’s fashionable whims. The skeletal oak clock, its panel depicting the Assumption and its plinth the Gathering of the Nets; the corner hutch with its glazed door revealing the dapple-patterned china; the rotating server; the heavy wooden salvers; the wall-cistern with its burnished tap—to what exile would these venerable relics be consigned?

If there was going to be a war it would have happened two years ago when the Balkan League fell upon Turkey. Or when Bulgaria attacked Serbia...."
