Never had he been at such an altitude of living, and he kept himself in the background, listening, observing, and pleasuring, replying in reticent monosyllables, saying, “Yes, miss,” and “No, miss,” to her, and “Yes, ma’am,” and “No, ma’am,” to her mother. He curbed the impulse, arising out of his sea-training, to say “Yes, sir,” and “No, sir,” to her brothers. He felt that it would be inappropriate and a confession of inferiority on his part—which would never do if he was to win to her. Also, it was a dictate of his pride. “By God!” he cried to himself, once; “I’m just as good as them, and if they do know lots that I don’t, I could learn ’m a few myself, all the same!” And the next moment, when she or her mother addressed him as “Mr. Eden,” his aggressive pride was forgotten, and he was glowing and warm with delight. He was a civilized man, that was what he was, shoulder to shoulder, at dinner, with people he had read about in books. He was in the books himself, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

He had never before reached such a peak of existence. He lingered on the edges, listening, watching, taking quiet pleasure, and answering in guarded monosyllables—"Yes, miss" and "No, miss" to her, "Yes, ma'am" and "No, ma'am" to her mother. He suppressed the sailor's reflex to say "Yes, sir" and "No, sir" to her brothers. It would strike the wrong note, he thought, a tacit admission of lower standing—and that would never serve if he meant to win her. His pride demanded it too. "By God!" he exclaimed inwardly at one point. "I'm every bit their equal, and if they know plenty I don't, I could teach them a thing or two myself, all the same!" Then, a moment later, when she or her mother called him "Mr. Eden," his bristling pride dissolved, and he glowed, suffused with pleasure. He was a man of cultivation now—that was the truth of it—sitting shoulder to shoulder at dinner with figures he had only met before in books. He himself had stepped into those books, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

where the first term is the auto-encoder reconstruction of x with a cascaded NLU-NLG path. The second term is the KL divergence which regularizes the approximated posterior distribution. Detailed derivations can be found in Appendix. When computing the reconstruction term of x, it requires us to first run through the NLU model to obtain the prediction on y, from which we run through NLG to reconstruct x. The full information flow is (x → z → y → z → x). 3 Connections can be drawn with recent work which uses backtranslation to augment training data for machine translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016) . Unlike back-translation, the presence of latent variable in our model requires us to sample z along the NLU-NLG path. The introduced stochasticity allows the model to explore a larger area of the data manifold.

metadata.title: A Generative Model for Joint Natural Language Understanding and Generation

text: where the first term corresponds to the autoencoder reconstruction of x via a cascaded NLU-NLG pathway. The second term is the KL divergence, which serves to regularize the approximate posterior distribution. Full derivations are provided in the Appendix. To compute the reconstruction term for x, we first pass x through the NLU model to obtain a prediction for y, and then feed this prediction into the NLG model to reconstruct x. The complete information flow is thus (x → z → y → z → x). 3 This formulation is related to recent work that leverages back-translation to expand training data for machine translation (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016). However, unlike back-translation, our model includes a latent variable, which requires sampling z along the NLU-NLG path. This injected stochasticity enables the model to explore a broader region of the data manifold.

He had been a lad of whom something was expected. Beyond this all had been chaos. That he would be successful in an original way, or that he would go to the dogs in an original way, seemed equally probable. The only absolute certainty about him was that he would not stand still in the circumstances amid which he was born.

He was a young man from whom much was anticipated, yet outside of that singular promise, everything remained a muddle. It appeared just as likely that he would achieve success through some unconventional path as it was that he would ruin himself in an equally singular way. The one undeniable truth was that he could never stay stagnant within the station of his birth.

Boldwood looked at her—not slily, critically, or understandingly, but blankly at gaze, in the way a reaper looks up at a passing train—as something foreign to his element, and but dimly understood. To Boldwood women had been remote phenomena rather than necessary complements—comets of such uncertain aspect, movement, and permanence, that whether their orbits were as geometrical, unchangeable, and as subject to laws as his own, or as absolutely erratic as they superficially appeared, he had not deemed it his duty to consider.

Boldwood watched her—not with cunning, judgment, or insight, but with a vacant stare, much like a harvester pausing to watch a steam engine rush past—as a thing entirely outside his world and only vaguely grasped. To him, women were distant curiosities rather than essential partners—celestial bodies of such vague form, motion, and endurance that he had never felt compelled to wonder if their paths were governed by the same rigid principles as his own, or if they were as truly chaotic as they appeared on the surface.

He reached home damp and weary enough after his ten-mile walk. It had hardly been a propitious beginning, but he had chosen his course, and would show no swerving. The evening and the following morning were spent in concluding arrangements for his departure. To stay at home a minute longer than necessary after having once come to his determination would be, he felt, only to give new pain to his mother by some word, look, or deed.

He arrived at his doorstep soaked and spent from the ten-mile tramp. The outset had been anything but auspicious, yet he had fixed his path and resolved not to waver. That night and the next morning passed in tying up final threads for his leaving. Lingering at home even a moment beyond what was needful now that his mind was made up would, he sensed, merely inflict fresh hurt on his mother through some stray phrase, glance, or gesture.

At a table behind him sat the Artful Dodger, Master Charles Bates, and Mr. Chitling: all intent upon a game of whist; the Artful taking dummy against Master Bates and Mr. Chitling. The countenance of the first-named gentleman, peculiarly intelligent at all times, acquired great additional interest from his close observance of the game, and his attentive perusal of Mr. Chitling’s hand; upon which, from time to time, as occasion served, he bestowed a variety of earnest glances: wisely regulating his own play by the result of his observations upon his neighbour’s cards. It being a cold night, the Dodger wore his hat, as, indeed, was often his custom within doors. He also sustained a clay pipe between his teeth, which he only removed for a brief space when he deemed it necessary to apply for refreshment to a quart pot upon the table, which stood ready filled with gin-and-water for the accommodation of the company.

At a scrubbed pine table just over his shoulder sat Cunning Jem, Master Toby Hackett, and Mr. Griggs, all wholly absorbed in a game of whist; Jem held the dummy hand, pitted against the paired play of Hackett and Griggs. The face of the first-named young rascal, sharp and quick-witted even on the slowest of days, took on an extra air of keen absorption as he tracked every turn of the game, his gaze drifting repeatedly to study Mr. Griggs’ fanned-out hand. Whenever the moment suited, he darted a series of shrewd sidelong looks at those exposed cards, carefully calibrating his own moves to what he’d gleaned from his neighbor’s hold. The night carried a sharp bite, so Jem kept his cap pulled down on his head, a habit he indulged even indoors more often than not. A dented clay pipe jutted from between his teeth, which he only removed for a few seconds at a time when he wanted a sip of refreshment from the quart pot resting on the table, brimming full of gin-and-water for the whole party’s use.

He drew near and said, “I must be leaving you.”

She watched him bend toward the turf, gather up the curling strand he had cut from her abundant hair, wind it about his fingers, undo a button at the breast of his coat, and with grave care place it within. She felt no strength left in her to resist or refuse him. He was altogether beyond her, and Clara seemed like one who, meeting a freshening wind, finds it rise so fiercely that it checks the breath. He came closer and said, “I must leave you now.”

I had started a teacher-factory and a lot of Sunday-schools the first thing; as a result, I now had an admirable system of graded schools in full blast in those places, and also a complete variety of Protestant congregations all in a prosperous and growing condition. Everybody could be any kind of a Christian he wanted to; there was perfect freedom in that matter. But I confined public religious teaching to the churches and the Sunday-schools, permitting nothing of it in my other educational buildings. I could have given my own sect the preference and made everybody a Presbyterian without any trouble, but that would have been to affront a law of human nature: spiritual wants and instincts are as various in the human family as are physical appetites, complexions, and features, and a man is only at his best, morally, when he is equipped with the religious garment whose color and shape and size most nicely accommodate themselves to the spiritual complexion, angularities, and stature of the individual who wears it; and, besides, I was afraid of a united Church; it makes a mighty power, the mightiest conceivable, and then when it by and by gets into selfish hands, as it is always bound to do, it means death to human liberty and paralysis to human thought.

I had begun by setting up a teacher-factory and a good many Sunday-schools; as a result, I now had a splendid system of graded schools running at full speed in those places, along with a full range of Protestant congregations, all flourishing and steadily increasing. Anyone was free to be whatever kind of Christian he chose; there was complete liberty in that regard. But I limited public religious instruction to the churches and the Sunday-schools, allowing none of it in my other schools. I could easily have favored my own denomination and made everybody a Presbyterian without any difficulty, but that would have violated a law of human nature: spiritual needs and instincts differ among people just as much as physical appetites, complexions, and features do, and a man is at his moral best only when he is fitted with the religious garment whose color, shape, and size suit as closely as possible the spiritual complexion, odd angles, and stature of the person wearing it; besides, I was wary of a united Church. It is a tremendous power, the greatest imaginable, and once it falls into selfish hands, as it inevitably does, it spells the death of human liberty and the crippling of human thought.

He stopped his car by the roadside, and sat for a long time looking at this and musing. He sensed an urgent need to look upon the ocean. He decided to drive past the Rodwell hamlets, then climb toward the summit of Eastonbury Hill. There he arrived and gazed through a cleft in the lower hills at a wedge of moonlit sea, shimmering yet motionless. He pulled his car to the verge and remained seated for a great while, watching the water and lost in thought. At one moment, he thought he saw three tiny forms, like stubby dark needles, gliding in single file across the liquid silver.

Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) are two fundamental tasks in building task-oriented dialogue systems. In a modern dialogue system, an NLU module first converts a user utterance, provided by an automatic speech recognition model, into a formal representation. The representation is then consumed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to update a belief state which represents an aggregated user goal. Based on the current belief state, a policy network decides the formal representation of the system response. This is finally used by an NLG module to generate the system response (Young et al., 2010) .

metadata.title: A Generative Model for Joint Natural Language Understanding and Generation

text: Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) constitute two core foundational tasks underpinning the development of task-oriented dialogue systems. Within contemporary dialogue system architectures, the NLU module first transforms a user utterance output by an automatic speech recognition model into a structured formal representation. This structured representation is subsequently processed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to revise the system’s belief state, a construct that encodes the cumulative inferred user goal. Using the current belief state as input, a policy network determines the formal representation of the intended system response. This formal response representation is ultimately fed to the NLG module, which produces the final natural language system response (Young et al., 2010).

“I expected this reception,” said the dæmon. “All men hate the wretched; how, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things! Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us. You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace; but if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.”

“I anticipated this kind of welcome,” the creature said. “Society has always detested the miserable; imagine, then, the scorn I must face, being the most wretched of all living things. Even you, my maker, abhor and reject your own creation, though we are bound by a link that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to take my life, but how can you treat the gift of existence so lightly? Fulfill your responsibilities to me, and I will honor my obligations to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and everyone else in peace; however, if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is gorged on the blood of every friend you have left.”

She had spoken frankly, and neither asked nor expected any reply from Gabriel more satisfactory than that she had obtained. Yet in the centremost parts of her complicated heart there existed at this minute a little pang of disappointment, for a reason she would not allow herself to recognize. Oak had not once wished her free that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the insect sting. Not that she would have listened to any such hypothesis. O no—for wasn’t she saying all the time that such thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man to speak sentiment to her? Yet he might have just hinted about that old love of his, and asked, in a playful off-hand way, if he might speak of it. It would have seemed pretty and sweet, if no more; and then she would have shown how kind and inoffensive a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to give such cool advice—the very advice she had asked for—it ruffled our heroine all the afternoon.

She had spoken plainly, and she did not ask or expect any answer from Gabriel more satisfying than the one she had received. And yet, at the very center of her complicated heart, there was at that moment a small stab of disappointment for a reason she refused to admit even to herself. Oak had not once wished her free so that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the little sting. Not that she would have listened to any such notion. Oh no—for wasn’t she saying all the while that thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man for sentiment with her? And yet he might at least have hinted at that old love of his, and asked, in some lightly teasing way, whether he might mention it. It would have been touching and sweet, if nothing else; and then she would have shown how gentle and harmless a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to offer such cool advice—the very advice she had sought—kept our heroine unsettled all afternoon.

How Mr. Brownlow went on, from day to day, filling the mind of his adopted child with stores of knowledge, and becoming attached to him, more and more, as his nature developed itself, and showed the thriving seeds of all he wished him to become—how he traced in him new traits of his early friend, that awakened in his own bosom old remembrances, melancholy and yet sweet and soothing—how the two orphans, tried by adversity, remembered its lessons in mercy to others, and mutual love, and fervent thanks to Him who had protected and preserved them—these are all matters which need not to be told. I have said that they were truly happy; and without strong affection and humanity of heart, and gratitude to that Being whose code is Mercy, and whose great attribute is Benevolence to all things that breathe, happiness can never be attained.

How Mr. Harcourt passed from day to day, storing the mind of his adopted boy with knowledge, and growing more and more fond of him as his character unfolded itself, and revealed the flourishing seeds of all he would fain have him become; how he discerned in him fresh lineaments of his earliest friend, and these recalled to his own breast memories long past, sad indeed, yet gentle and consoling; how the two children of misfortune, taught by suffering, remembered its lesson in kindness to others, in tenderness for one another, and in grateful praise to Him who had watched over and preserved them—these are matters there is no need to tell. I have said that they were truly happy; and without deep affection and a humane heart, and thankfulness to that Being whose law is Mercy, and whose chief attribute is goodness toward all that lives and breathes, happiness can never be won.

In response, we propose cross-mutual information (XMI), a new metric towards cross-linguistic comparability in NMT. In contrast to BLEU, this information-theoretic quantity no longer explicitly depends on language, model, and tokenization choices. It does, however, require that the models under consideration are probabilistic. As an initial starting point, we perform a case study with a controlled experiment on 21 European languages. Our analysis showcases XMI's potential for shedding light on the difficulties of translation as an effect of the properties of the source or target language. We also perform a correlation analysis in an attempt to further explain our findings. Here, in contrast to the general wisdom, we find no significant evidence that translating into a morphologically rich language is harder than translating into a morphologically impoverished one. In fact, the only significant correlate of MT difficulty we find is source-side type-token ratio.

metadata.title: It's Easier to Translate out of English than into it: Measuring Neural Translation Difficulty by Cross-Mutual Information

text: We introduce cross-mutual information (XMI), a novel metric designed to enable cross-linguistic comparability in neural machine translation. Unlike BLEU, this information-theoretic measure does not rely explicitly on language, model architecture, or tokenization scheme; it does, however, presuppose that the evaluated models are probabilistic. As an initial exploration, we conduct a case study through a controlled experiment spanning 21 European languages. Our results illustrate XMI’s capacity to illuminate translation difficulty as a function of source- or target-language properties. We further carry out a correlation analysis to better interpret these outcomes. Contrary to prevailing assumptions, we find no significant indication that translating into a morphologically rich language is inherently more challenging than translating into a morphologically poor one. Instead, the sole meaningful predictor of MT difficulty we identify is the type-token ratio on the source side.

Of all things in the world Hugh was the one that could most easily rouse Mr. Britling's unhappy aptitude for distressing imaginations. Hugh was nearer by far to his heart and nerves than any other creature. In the last few years Mr. Britling, by the light of a variety of emotional excursions in other directions, had been discovering this. The boy resided closer to the marrow of his being and the sensitivity of his nerves than any other living soul. Over recent years, illuminated by the flickering torches of several romantic diversions, Sterling had gradually come to recognize this truth. Whatever Sterling perceived, he felt compelled to articulate; from this profound well of affection—an effortless warmth that surpassed the choreographed passions of his past—he had forged the philosophy he once shared with Mr. Thorne: that only through one’s offspring can a man touch the hem of selfless, unadulterated devotion. Yet this doctrine failed to account for the visceral, singular grip Julian held over him, a bond far more potent than the cheerful regard he felt for his younger step-sons. It was a discrepancy that Sterling, with a deliberate and practiced caution, chose never to scrutinize too closely.

This was his first meal in a private household in England, and for obscure reasons he had expected something very stiff and formal with "spotless napery." He had also expected a very stiff and capable service by implacable parlourmaids, and the whole thing indeed highly genteel. And while the maids at this migratory sideboard carved and opened bottles and so forth, the small boy and a slightly larger brother, assisted a little by two young men of no very defined position and relationship, served the company. Instead, two smiling women servants emerged from what was presumably the kitchen side, pushing a strange wicker contrivance, which his little guide told him was called Aunt Clatter—an apt name, plainly—and which carried the meal upon its shelves. It came from the room into which Elizabeth had retired. This state lasted but for an instant; the scream was repeated, and I rushed into the room.

After she went inside, I spent some time pacing the hallways of the house, searching every corner that might offer a hiding place for my enemy. It came from the room where Elizabeth had gone to rest. The moment I heard it, the full horror of the situation crashed down on me; my arms fell limp, and every muscle in my body froze. I could feel the blood crawling through my veins, a cold tingling sensation reaching my very fingertips. This paralysis lasted only a second; the scream echoed again, and I bolted toward the room.

An important feature of the dependency analysis in UD is that content words are considered the principal components of dependency relations. Within this framework, function words are generally dependents of the content word they relate to most closely. The primacy of content words brings out cross-linguistic parallels that would be harder to detect with other annotation frameworks since function words are highly variable across languages. Importantly, dependency paths between content words do not generally contain function words. As a result, by comparing paths across languages, differences in the surface realization are often masked, and argument structure and linkage differences emphasized.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: A core design property of Universal Dependencies (UD) dependency annotation schemas is the designation of content words as the head units of all dependency relations. Under this framework, function words are uniformly marked as dependents of the content word with which they share the closest syntactic and semantic association. This prioritization of content words surfaces cross-linguistic structural parallels that are far less detectable in alternative annotation schemes, as function word inventories and syntactic behavior exhibit extreme typological variability across languages. Critically, dependency paths connecting pairs of content words almost never include intervening function word nodes as a result of this design choice. Accordingly, cross-linguistic comparison of these content-word dependency paths naturally obscures variation in surface morphosyntactic realization, while highlighting divergent properties of argument structure and inter-constituent linkage across languages.

• Increasing the capacity of multilingual NMT yields large improvements and narrows the performance gap with bilingual models. Lowresource translation benefits more from the increased capacity. • Language-specific modeling and deep NMT architectures can slightly improve zero-shot translation, but fail to alleviate the off-target translation issue. • Finetuning multilingual NMT with ROBT substantially reduces the proportion of offtarget translations (by ∼50%) and delivers an improvement of ∼10 BLEU in zero-shot settings, approaching the conventional pivotbased method. We show that finetuning with ROBT converges within a few thousand steps.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: Increasing the capacity of multilingual NMT leads to substantial gains and reduces the performance gap relative to bilingual systems, with low-resource translation benefiting the most from this added capacity. Language-specific modeling and deeper NMT architectures can provide modest improvements for zero-shot translation, but they do not resolve the off-target translation problem. By contrast, finetuning multilingual NMT with ROBT markedly decreases the share of off-target outputs by approximately 50% and yields an improvement of about 10 BLEU in zero-shot scenarios, bringing performance close to that of the conventional pivot-based approach. We further show that finetuning with ROBT converges after only a few thousand steps.

The afternoon drew on apace, and, looking to the right towards the sea as he walked beside the horse, Poorgrass saw strange clouds and scrolls of mist rolling over the long ridges which girt the landscape in that quarter. They came in yet greater volumes, and indolently crept across the intervening valleys, and around the withered papery flags of the moor and river brinks. Then their dank spongy forms closed in upon the sky. It was a sudden overgrowth of atmospheric fungi which had their roots in the neighbouring sea, and by the time that horse, man, and corpse entered Yalbury Great Wood, these silent workings of an invisible hand had reached them, and they were completely enveloped, this being the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the series.

The afternoon waned swiftly, and as Poorgrass trudged beside the horse, he glanced seaward to the right. There he saw queer clouds and trailing ribbons of mist unfurling over the long ridges that hemmed the land in that direction. They swelled into thicker volumes, drifting lazily across the hollows between and curling about the dry, paper-like reeds along the moor and riverbanks. Soon their clammy, porous masses sealed off the sky. It was a swift efflorescence of vaporous growths rooted in the nearby sea, and by the time horse, man, and corpse reached Yalbury Great Wood, these mute machinations of an unseen agency had overtaken them, wrapping them entirely—the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the season.

Label smoothing has led to state-of-the-art models in image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016) , language translation (Vaswani et al., 2017) and speech recognition (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017) . Prior attempts in label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Müller et al., 2019) focus on categorical labels to prevent the network from becoming overconfident while we apply label smoothing on ordinal labels (i.e., Likert scores) to prevent the network from overfitting on noisy ordinal labels.

text: Label smoothing has yielded state-of-the-art models for image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016), language translation (Vaswani et al., 2017), and speech recognition (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017). While existing applications of label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Müller et al., 2019) center on categorical labels to prevent networks from developing excessive predictive confidence, the present work implements label smoothing for ordinal labels (i.e., Likert scores) to prevent networks from overfitting to noisy ordinal label data.

Some half-dozen times a year, at most, he claimed his privilege of coming in uninvited, and would sit among them through the evening, as he had once done often. He never came there heated with wine. And one other thing regarding him was whispered in the echoes, which has been whispered by all true echoes for ages and ages.

The murmurs seldom stirred at Sydney Carton’s own footfalls. At most, on five or six occasions each year, he exercised his right to appear without summons, settling among them for the night as he had habitually done in former days. He never arrived flushed with drink. And one further secret about him was breathed through the murmurs—a secret that every faithful murmur has carried across the centuries.

When she registered that she felt exactly as well as she had a moment prior, however, she eased her eyes open again.

He was not bragging, not showing off. His sole motive was to make her happy, to make her proud of him, to justify her long faith in him. She put the book in the front room on top of the family Bible. A sacred thing was this book her lodger had made, a fetich of friendship. It softened the blow of his having been a laundryman, and though she could not understand a line of it, she knew that every line of it was great. She was a simple, practical, hard-working woman, but she possessed faith in large endowment.

He wasn’t boasting or trying to impress. All he wanted was to bring her joy, to make her proud, to prove her years of belief in him had been worth it. She placed the book in the front room, resting it on the family Bible. This volume her boarder had created was something hallowed, a token of friendship. It eased the sting of his past as a laundry worker, and even though she couldn’t grasp a single sentence, she understood that every sentence was important. She was a plain, pragmatic, industrious woman, yet she carried an abundant gift of faith.

As apparent from the previous section, a crucial aspect for applying our methodology is the design of the train-dev-test split of the Source because it directly impacts the amount of words we can test our lexicons on during gold evaluation. In line with these considerations, we choose the lexical items which are already present in ANEW (Bradley and Lang, 1999) as Source-test set. ANEW is the precursor to the version later distributed by Warriner et al. (2013) ; it is widely used and has been adapted to a wide range of languages. With this choice, it is likely that a resulting TargetPred-test set has a large overlap with the respective TargetGold lexicon. As for the TargetGold lexicons, we included every VA(D) and BE5 lexicon we could get hold of with more than 500 entries. This resulted in 26 datasets covering 12 quite diverse languages (see Table 2 ). Note that we also include English lexicons in the gold evaluation. In these cases, no translation will be carried out (Source is identical to TargetMT) so that only the expansion step is validated. Appendix A.1 gives further details on data preparation.

metadata.title: Learning and Evaluating Emotion Lexicons for 91 Languages

text: As the preceding section indicates, a key factor in applying our methodology is the design of the train–dev–test split for the Source, since this split directly determines the number of words available for evaluating our lexicons during gold evaluation. In accordance with these considerations, we select the lexical items already contained in ANEW (Bradley and Lang, 1999) as the Source-test set. ANEW is the precursor to the version later disseminated by Warriner et al. (2013); it is extensively used and has been adapted to numerous languages. This choice makes it probable that a resulting TargetPred-test set will overlap substantially with the corresponding TargetGold lexicon. Regarding the TargetGold lexicons, we included every VA(D) and BE5 lexicon we could obtain that contains more than 500 entries. This yielded 26 datasets spanning 12 highly diverse languages (see Table 2). Notably, we also incorporate English lexicons in the gold evaluation. In these instances, no translation is performed (Source is identical to TargetMT), so that only the expansion step is validated. Appendix A.1 provides further details on data preparation.

“Oh, it is true enough. I may as well be hung for a sheep as a lamb (an old country saying, not of much account, but it will do for a rough soldier), and so I will speak my mind, regardless of your pleasure, and without hoping or intending to get your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this manner that your good looks may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant looked down the mead in critical abstraction. “Probably some one man on an average falls in love with each ordinary woman. She can marry him: he is content, and leads a useful life. Such women as you a hundred men always covet—your eyes will bewitch scores on scores into an unavailing fancy for you—you can only marry one of that many. Out of these say twenty will endeavour to drown the bitterness of despised love in drink; twenty more will mope away their lives without a wish or attempt to make a mark in the world, because they have no ambition apart from their attachment to you; twenty more—the susceptible person myself possibly among them—will be always draggling after you, getting where they may just see you, doing desperate things. Men are such constant fools! The rest may try to get over their passion with more or less success. But all these men will be saddened. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are saddened with them. There’s my tale. That’s why I say that a woman so charming as yourself, Miss Everdene, is hardly a blessing to her race.”

“Oh, it’s quite true enough. I may as well be hanged for a sheep as for a lamb—an old country saying, not worth much, but it suits a rough soldier—and so I’ll say what I think, without regard to whether it pleases you, and without expecting or meaning to win your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this way that your beauty may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant gazed down the meadow in reflective disapproval. “As a rule, perhaps one man falls in love with every ordinary woman. She can marry him; he is satisfied, and goes on to lead a useful life. But women such as you are desired by a hundred men at once—your eyes will cast scores and scores into hopeless admiration for you—you can marry only one of them. Of those, let us say twenty will try to drown the pain of rejected love in drink; twenty more will waste their lives in idleness, with no desire and no effort to make any mark in the world, because they have no ambition beyond their devotion to you; twenty more—the impressionable sort, myself perhaps among them—will be forever trailing after you, going wherever they can merely catch sight of you, doing desperate things. Men are such hopeless fools! The rest may manage, with greater or less success, to get over their passion. But all these men will be made unhappy. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are made unhappy too. There’s my story. That is why I say that a woman as lovely as you, Miss Everdene, is scarcely a blessing to her kind.”

Once they were seated in the living-room, he began to get on easily—more easily by far than he had expected. She made it easy for him; and the gracious spirit with which she did it made him love her more madly than ever. They talked first of the borrowed books, of the Swinburne he was devoted to, and of the Browning he did not understand; and she led the conversation on from subject to subject, while she pondered the problem of how she could be of help to him. She had thought of this often since their first meeting. She wanted to help him. He made a call upon her pity and tenderness that no one had ever made before, and the pity was not so much derogatory of him as maternal in her. Her pity could not be of the common sort, when the man who drew it was so much man as to shock her with maidenly fears and set her mind and pulse thrilling with strange thoughts and feelings. The old fascination of his neck was there, and there was sweetness in the thought of laying her hands upon it. It seemed still a wanton impulse, but she had grown more used to it. She did not dream that in such guise new-born love would epitomize itself. Nor did she dream that the feeling he excited in her was love. She thought she was merely interested in him as an unusual type possessing various potential excellencies, and she even felt philanthropic about it.

Once they’d settled into the faded velvet armchairs of her front parlor, the conversation unfolded far more easily than he’d dared to anticipate. She met him halfway at every turn, the unforced warmth of her manner stoking his adoration to a giddy, dizzying pitch he’d never thought possible. They first chatted about the stack of books he’d borrowed the week prior: the wild, throbbing verse of Dowson he’d memorized half of by heart, and the layered, allusive poems of Rossetti he still couldn’t parse. She steered the talk gently from topic to topic, all while turning over the question of how she might lift him up, a problem she’d mulled constantly since their first meeting by the town’s frozen mill pond. She ached to help him. He tugged at a vein of pity and tenderness no other person had ever reached in her, and that softness was not belittling to him so much as it was gently maternal, rooted in care, not condescension. It could never be the ordinary sort of pity, not when the man who inspired it was so unapologetically vividly male that he roused fluttering, girlish alarms in her, sent her thoughts spinning and her pulse thrumming with odd, unrecognizable feelings. She was still drawn to the strong, sun-browned slope of his neck, and the thought of pressing her palms flat to it hummed with quiet sweetness. It still felt like a bold, unruly impulse, but she’d grown far more comfortable with the thought of it lately. She never guessed that new, fledgling love so often dressed itself in exactly these small, unnameable, half-wanton urges. She never guessed that the warm, thrumming feeling he roused in her was love at all, instead convincing herself she was merely curious about him, an unusual, rough-edged figure brimming with untapped potential, and she even framed the whole interest as a sort of quiet, philanthropic project.

In addition, 50 random HSs from NICHE-training are utilized with verbatim repetition as HS-HS to discourage the same text for both HS and CN in a pair, and 50 random HSs are paired with other random HSs simulating the condition of inappropriate CNs with hateful text. metadata.title: Generating Counter Narratives against Online Hate Speech: Data and Strategies

text: The negative pairs are drawn from instances labeled under the human reviewer condition that fall into the “at least one 0” bin. Additionally, 50 randomly selected HSs from NICHE-training are reused with exact repetition as HS–HS pairs to discourage identical text for both HS and CN within a pair, and another 50 random HSs are paired with distinct random HSs to simulate the condition of inappropriate CNs containing hateful content. For the test data, we assembled a balanced test set comprising 101 positive and 101 negative pairs. Both positive and negative examples were constructed by replicating the non-expert reviewer annotation procedure described in Section 7.1, generating new CNs with the NICHE test set using the author model GPT‑niche.

PASCAL layer When we introduced our model, we motivated our design choice of placing PASCAL heads in the first layer in order to enrich the representations of words from their isolated embeddings by introducing contextualization from their parents. We ran an ablation study on the NC11 data in order to verify our hypothesis. As shown in Table 3a , the performance of our model on the validation sets is lower when placing Pascal heads in upper layers; a trend that we also observed with the LISA mechanism. These results corroborate the findings of Raganato and Tiedemann (2018) who noticed that, in the first layer, more attention heads solely focus on the word to be translated itself rather than its context. We can then deduce that enforcing syntactic dependencies in the first layer effectively leads to better word representations, which further enhance the translation accuracy of the Transformer model. Investigating the performance of multiple syntax-aware layers is left as future work.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: We introduced our model by motivating the decision to place PASCAL heads in the first layer, arguing that this design enriches isolated word embeddings with contextualization drawn from their syntactic parents. To test this hypothesis, we conducted an ablation study on the NC11 dataset. As reported in Table 3a, model performance on the validation sets declines when PASCAL heads are situated in higher layers—a pattern we also observed with the LISA mechanism. These results align with the findings of Raganato and Tiedemann (2018), who noted that in the first layer a greater proportion of attention heads focus on the word being translated rather than on its surrounding context. We therefore infer that enforcing syntactic dependencies in the first layer yields improved word representations, which in turn boost the translation accuracy of the Transformer model. We leave the investigation of multiple syntax-aware layers for future work.

Until just a short while before, that spot had been left forgotten, heaped with piles of loose stone and thick alder bushes, but it had recently been cleared and straightened out to accommodate new burials, as the rest of the cemetery grounds were filling up at a rapid clip.

Freshness was exhaled in an essence from the varied contours of bank and hollow, as if the earth breathed maiden breath; and the pleased birds were hymning to the scene. Before her, among the clouds, there was a contrast in the shape of lairs of fierce light which showed themselves in the neighbourhood of a hidden sun, lingering on to the farthest north-west corner of the heavens that this midsummer season allowed.

After charging Gabriel and Maryann with the task of securing the house for the night, she stepped outdoors just as a brief thunderstorm concluded. The rain had cleansed the atmosphere and lightly dampened the earth's surface, though the ground underneath remained entirely dry. A crisp vitality radiated from every ridge and valley, as if the soil itself were exhaling a pure, sweet breath, and the joyful birds offered a chorus to the landscape. In front of her, the sky displayed a striking contrast: patches of violent light flickered around a veiled sun, which drifted toward the furthest northwestern edge of the horizon that the midsummer solstice would permit.

Thus, our evaluation methodology needs to fulfill the following two requirements: (1) evaluation must not be performed on translational equivalents of the Source entries to which the model already had access during training (e.g., Sonnenschein and nuklear in our example from Figure 1) ; but, on the other hand, (2) a reasonable number of instances must be available for evaluation (ideally, as many as possible to increase reliability). The intricate cross-lingual train-dev-test set assignment of our generation methodology is in place so that we meet these two requirements. In particular, for our silver evaluation, we intersect TargetMT-test with TargetPred-test and compute the correlation of these two sets individually for each emotion variable. Pearson's r will be used as correlation measure throughout this paper. Establishing a test set at the very start of our workflow, Source-test, assures that there is a relatively large overlap between the two sets and, by extension, that our requirements for the evaluation are met.

metadata.title: Learning and Evaluating Emotion Lexicons for 91 Languages
metadata.section: A Novel Approach to Lexicon Creation

text: Consequently, our evaluation framework is designed to satisfy two primary criteria: first, assessment must exclude any translational counterparts of Source entries utilized during the training phase (e.g., *Sonnenschein* and *nuklear* in Figure 1); second, the evaluation set must comprise a sufficient number of instances to ensure statistical reliability. Our generation methodology utilizes a sophisticated cross-lingual data partitioning strategy to reconcile these requirements. Specifically, for our silver evaluation, we determine the intersection of TargetMT-test and TargetPred-test and calculate the correlation for each emotion variable within this subset. We employ Pearson’s $r$ as the standard correlation metric throughout this work. By establishing the Source-test set at the beginning of our workflow, we ensure a substantial overlap between these sets, thereby fulfilling our evaluation objectives.

He found himself jostled among a crowd of people, chiefly women, who were huddled together in a dirty frowsy room, at the upper end of which was a raised platform railed off from the rest, with a dock for the prisoners on the left hand against the wall, a box for the witnesses in the middle, and a desk for the magistrates on the right; the awful locality last named, being screened off by a partition which concealed the bench from the common gaze, and left the vulgar to imagine (if they could) the full majesty of justice.

He found himself pressed in among a crowd, mostly women, who were packed together in a filthy, squalid room, at the far end of which stood a raised platform shut off from the rest by a railing, with a prisoners’ dock on the left against the wall, a witnesses’ box in the center, and a magistrates’ desk on the right; the last and most formidable of these places was screened by a partition that hid the bench from ordinary view and left the common people to imagine, if they could, the full majesty of justice.

The young tenant in the chair allowed these thoughts to file, soft-shod, through his mind, while there drifted into the room furnished sounds and furnished scents. He heard in one room a tittering and incontinent, slack laughter; in others the monologue of a scold, the rattling of dice, a lullaby, and one crying dully; above him a banjo tinkled with spirit. Doors banged somewhere; the elevated trains roared intermittently; a cat yowled miserably upon a back fence. And he breathed the breath of the house—a dank savour rather than a smell—a cold, musty effluvium as from underground vaults mingled with the reeking exhalations of linoleum and mildewed and rotten woodwork.

The young man seated in the chair let these reflections pass through his mind with silent, padded steps, as the room filled with borrowed noises and borrowed odors. From one chamber came a giggling, loose and unrestrained laughter; from others, a scold’s endless harangue, the clatter of dice, a cradle song, and a muffled weeping; overhead, a banjo twanged with energy. Doors slammed somewhere in the distance; elevated trains thundered at intervals; a cat wailed wretchedly on a back fence. And he inhaled the house’s exhalation—a damp essence more than a smell—a chill, stale vapor as from subterranean crypts blended with the pungent emissions of linoleum and decaying, moldy timber.

Results: We report automatic metrics averaged across three random initializations for all learned models, and use bootstrap tests (Berg-Kirkpatrick et al., 2012) for statistical significance. Table 2 presents the results. While reranking using C old appears to help the generation model, it still substantially underperforms all other models, across all metrics. Although this model is trained on considerably more data, it does not have access to C old during training and uses fewer inputs and consequently has less context than the edit model. Reranking slightly deteriorates the edit model's performance with respect to SARI; however, it provides statistically significant improvements on most other metrics. Although two of the baselines achieve slightly higher BLEU-4 scores than our best model, these differences are not statistically significant, and our model is better at editing comments, as shown by the results on exact match, SARI, and GLEU. In particular, our edit models beat all other models with wide, statistically significant, margins on SARI, which explicitly measures performance on edit operations. Furthermore, merely copying C old , yields a relatively high BLEU-4 score of 46.218. The return type substitution and return type substitution w/ null handling baselines produce predictions that are identical to C old for 74.73% and 65.76% of the test examples, respectively, while it is only 9.33% for the reranked edit model. In other words, the baselines attain high scores on automatic metrics and even beat our model on BLEU-4, without actually performing edits on the majority of examples. This further underlines the shortcomings of some of these metrics and the importance of conducting human evaluation for this task.

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

text: Results: We report automatic evaluation metrics averaged over three random random initializations for all learned models, and assess statistical significance using bootstrap tests (Berg-Kirkpatrick et al., 2012). The results are shown in Table 2. Although reranking with \(C_{\text{old}}\) seems to benefit the generation model, it still lags far behind all other models on every metric. Despite being trained on substantially more data, this model does not have access to \(C_{\text{old}}\) at training time and relies on fewer inputs, and therefore has less contextual information than the edit model. Reranking causes a slight drop in the edit model’s SARI score, but yields statistically significant gains on most of the remaining metrics. While two baselines obtain marginally higher BLEU-4 scores than our best model, these differences are not statistically significant, and our model is superior at comment editing, as reflected by exact match, SARI, and GLEU. In particular, our edit models outperform all other systems by wide and statistically significant margins on SARI, which directly evaluates edit operations. Moreover, simply copying \(C_{\text{old}}\) already gives a relatively strong BLEU-4 score of 46.218. The return type substitution and return type substitution w/ null handling baselines produce outputs identical to \(C_{\text{old}}\) for 74.73% and 65.76% of the test examples, respectively, whereas this occurs for only 9.33% of the reranked edit model’s predictions. In other words, these baselines achieve high automatic scores and even surpass our model on BLEU-4 without actually editing the majority of examples. This further highlights the limitations of these metrics and the need for human evaluation on this task.

His mind took no note of the fact that Heinrich was an enemy, that by the reckoning of a "war of attrition" his death was balance and compensation for the death of Hugh. He went straight to the root fact that they had been gallant and kindly beings, and that the same thing had killed them both....

He caught himself reflecting on young Heinrich much as he did on his own boy, though with a shade less anguish—both of them, he felt, were bright futures pointlessly cut short. His thoughts brushed aside the reality that Heinrich was a foe, that in the cold arithmetic of a grinding war his death evened the scales for Hugh’s. He cut directly to the core truth: they had been brave and gentle souls, and the same force had taken them both.
