Whenever these home supplies were exhausted he would go to the Quiet Woman, and, standing with his back to the fire, grog in hand, tell remarkable stories of how he had lived seven years under the waterline of his ship, and other naval wonders, to the natives, who hoped too earnestly for a treat of ale from the teller to exhibit any doubts of his truth.

Old Master Thorne had come home, and was occupied with decanting several gallons of fresh-delivered spirits into the cubic flasks of his heavy sideboard. When these private stores ran dry, he would betake himself to The Mute Virgin, where, warming his coat-tails at the hearth with a steaming glass in his grip, he would recount extraordinary fables of surviving a full decade beneath the keel of a man-of-war, along with various other maritime miracles, to the local laborers, who were far too eager for a free draught of bitter to voice the slightest skepticism regarding his veracity.

Eustacia turned, entered the house, and ascended to the front bedroom, where a shaded light was burning. In the bed lay Clym, pale, haggard, wide awake, tossing to one side and to the other, his eyes lit by a hot light, as if the fire in their pupils were burning up their substance.

Eustacia turned around, went inside, and climbed the stairs to the front bedroom, where a lamp glowed behind a shade. On the bed rested Clym, pallid, worn, fully conscious, shifting restlessly from side to side, his eyes gleaming with a fierce brightness, as though the blaze within them was consuming his very being.

We propose a language-neutral, fine-grained definition of cross-linguistic morphosyntactic divergences (CLMD) that allows for their extraction using a syntactically annotated, content-wordaligned parallel corpus. Concretely, we classify CLMD based on the edge labels on the dependency paths between corresponding pairs of content words ( § 3.2). See Figure 1 for an example. 1 We further conduct a detailed corpus study, manually aligning content words in a subset of the PUD corpus (Zeman et al., 2017) over five language pairs-English-French (En-Fr), English-Russian (En-Ru), English-Chinese (En-Zh), English-Korean (En-Ko), and English-Japanese (En-Jp) ( § 3.1)-and analyze the prevalence of divergences by types ( § 4). The resulting resource can be useful for MT research, by guiding the creation of challenge sets focusing on particular constructions known to be cross-linguistically divergent, as well as by guiding preprocessing of source-side sentences for better MT performance. 2 The emerging CLMD provide information not only on the macro-structure of the grammar (e.g., whether the language is pro-dropping), but also on parameters specific to certain lexical classes (e.g., modal verbs) and probabilistic tendencies (e.g., Japanese tends to translate sequences of events, expressed in English using coordinating conjunctions, with subordinate clauses). See § 5.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: We introduce a language-agnostic, fine-grained characterization of cross-linguistic morphosyntactic divergences (CLMD) that enables their extraction from syntactically parsed, content-word-aligned parallel corpora. Specifically, we categorize CLMD according to the edge labels along dependency paths linking aligned content-word pairs ( § 3.2); Figure 1 provides an illustration. We further perform an extensive corpus analysis, manually aligning content words across five language pairs—English–French (En–Fr), English–Russian (En–Ru), English–Chinese (En–Zh), English–Korean (En–Ko), and English–Japanese (En–Jp)—in a subset of the PUD corpus (Zeman et al., 2017) ( § 3.1), and examine the distribution of divergence types ( § 4). The resulting dataset can inform machine translation research, both by steering the construction of challenge sets that target constructions known to diverge cross-linguistically, and by directing source-side preprocessing strategies to improve MT output. The derived CLMD reveal not only macro-grammatical properties (e.g., whether a language is pro-drop), but also parameters tied to particular lexical classes (e.g., modal verbs) and probabilistic patterns (e.g., Japanese preferentially renders event sequences—expressed in English via coordinating conjunctions—using subordinate clauses). See § 5.

Since then the appellation which I had thought to reserve to the horizons and landscapes of a merely realistic dream-country, has become more and more popular as a practical definition; and the dream-country has, by degrees, solidified into a utilitarian region which people can go to, take a house in, and write to the papers from. But I ask all good and gentle readers to be so kind as to forget this, and to refuse steadfastly to believe that there are any inhabitants of a Victorian Wessex outside the pages of this and the companion volumes in which they were first discovered.

Since that time, the name I once meant to keep for the distant views and countryside of a purely imagined realm has steadily gained currency as a handy label; and that dream-realm has gradually hardened into a practical district where folk can settle, lease a cottage, and send letters to the press. Yet I entreat every kind and courteous reader to put this out of mind, and to resist with firm conviction the notion that any denizens of a Victorian Wessex exist beyond the covers of this book and the companion volumes where they first came to light.

"You see that old felled tree there. I was sitting on it the day before yesterday and thinking of you. Will you come there and sit with me on it? When you sit on it you get a view, oh! a perfectly lovely English view, just a bit of the house and those clumps of trees and the valley away there with the lily pond. I'd love to have you in my memory of it...."

Do you see that fallen timber over there? I rested upon it only the day before yesterday, my mind filled with thoughts of you. Won't you accompany me to that spot and sit a while? From that seat, one is granted a view—oh, a most charming English prospect!—offering a glimpse of the manor, those stands of trees, and the valley stretching toward the lily pond. I should so like to include you in my remembrance of it.

The text memories can be computed by Sentence Encoder at the beginning of the whole parsing while the graph memories are constructed by Graph Encoder incrementally as the parsing progresses. During the iterative inference, a semantic representation of current state is used to attend to both graph and text memories (blue and red arrows) in order to locate the new concept and obtain its relations to the existing graph, both of which subsequently refine each other. Intuitively, after a first glimpse of the input sentence and the current graph, specific sub-areas of both sequence and graph are revisited to obtain a better understanding of the current situation. Later steps typically read the text in detail with specific learning aims, either confirming or overturning a previous hypothesis. Finally, after several iterations of reasoning steps, the refined sequence/graph decisions are used for graph expansion.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: Text memories are computed via a Sentence Encoder at the onset of the full parsing pipeline, while graph memories are constructed incrementally by a Graph Encoder as parsing progresses. During iterative inference, a semantic representation of the current system state is applied to attend to both graph and text memories (marked by blue and red arrows) to locate new concepts and derive their relational connections to the existing graph, with these two information streams subsequently undergoing mutual refinement. Intuitively, following an initial pass over the input sentence and current graph state, targeted subregions of both the text sequence and graph structure are revisited to build a more robust understanding of the current parsing context. Subsequent inference steps typically perform fine-grained, targeted reads of the input text guided by explicit operational objectives, either confirming or overturning previously formulated hypotheses. Finally, after several iterations of these reasoning steps, the refined sequence and graph parsing decisions are leveraged to support graph expansion.

And so Oliver remained all that day, and for the greater part of many subsequent days, seeing nobody, between early morning and midnight, and left during the long hours to commune with his own thoughts. Which, never failing to revert to his kind friends, and the opinion they must long ago have formed of him, were sad indeed.

And so Oliver remained there all that first day, and for the greater part of many days that came after, seeing not a single soul between the break of dawn and the deep of midnight, left to occupy the long, empty hours in conversation with his own thoughts. Those thoughts never strayed long from his kind old friends, and the poor opinion they must surely have formed of him by now, which made them bitter with sadness indeed.

It sounded good! In this atmosphere of telephones and lightning communication with distant regions, I was breathing the breath of life again after long suffocation. I realized, then, what a creepy, dull, inanimate horror this land had been to me all these years, and how I had been in such a stifled condition of mind as to have grown used to it almost beyond the power to notice it.

It was a welcome sound! Amidst the ring of telephones and the rush of instant communication with the world, I was finally drawing breath after a long period of choking. I realized then what a ghostly, stagnant, and lifeless nightmare this land had been to me, and how my mind had become so suppressed that I had grown used to the gloom almost beyond the point of noticing it.

Then he went to a locked closet, and took from a locked drawer therein a small circular case the size of a pillbox, and was about to put it into his pocket. But he lingered to open the cover and take a momentary glance inside. It contained a woman’s finger-ring, set all the way round with small diamonds, and from its appearance had evidently been recently purchased. Boldwood’s eyes dwelt upon its many sparkles a long time, though that its material aspect concerned him little was plain from his manner and mien, which were those of a mind following out the presumed thread of that jewel’s future history.

Then he crossed to a fastened cupboard, and from a fastened drawer within it took a little round case, no larger than a pillbox, and was on the point of slipping it into his pocket. Yet he paused, detaining it just long enough to lift the lid and cast a fleeting look within. There lay a woman’s ring, encircled all around with small diamonds, and from its look it had plainly been but lately bought. His eyes rested for a long while upon its many glances, though that the thing itself had little hold upon his thoughts was evident from his bearing and countenance, which were those of a man whose mind was tracing, as he supposed, the future history of that jewel.

His two days in London had filled him with matter, and he was glad to have something more than Hugh and Teddy and Mrs. Britling to talk it upon. What was happening now in Great Britain, he declared, was _adjustment_. It was an attempt on the part of a great unorganised nation, an attempt, instinctive at present rather than intelligent, to readjust its government and particularly its military organisation to the new scale of warfare that Germany had imposed upon the world. For two strenuous decades the British navy had been growing enormously under the pressure of German naval preparations, but the British military establishment had experienced no corresponding expansion. It was true there had been a futile, rather foolishly conducted agitation for universal military service, but there had been no accumulation of material, no preparation of armament-making machinery, no planning and no foundations for any sort of organisation that would have facilitated the rapid expansion of the fighting forces of a country in a time of crisis. Such an idea was absolutely antagonistic to the mental habits of the British military caste. The German method of incorporating all the strength and resources of the country into one national fighting machine was quite strange to the British military mind--still. Even after a month of war. War had become the comprehensive business of the German nation; to the British it was an incidental adventure. In Germany the nation was militarised, in England the army was specialised. The nation for nearly every practical purpose got along without it. Just as political life had also become specialised.... Now suddenly we wanted a government to speak for every one, and an army of the whole people. How were we to find it?

The two days he’d spent in London had given him no end of material to unpack, and he was relieved to have a broader circle than just Hugh, Teddy, and Mrs. Britling to hash it all out with. What was unfolding across Great Britain right now, he argued, was *adjustment*. It was an effort from a vast, loosely structured nation—still more instinctual than deliberate at this stage—to refashion its government, and its military organization most of all, to fit the unprecedented scale of warfare Germany had forced on the entire world. For two grueling decades, the British navy had ballooned in size in direct response to German naval build-up, but the country’s land military establishment had seen no corresponding growth. It was true there had been a fruitless, rather haphazardly run campaign pushing for universal military service, but there had been no stockpiling of supplies, no investment in arms manufacturing infrastructure, no advance planning or foundational systems laid to ease the rapid expansion of the nation’s fighting forces when a crisis hit. That kind of forward thinking was fundamentally at odds with the ingrained mindsets of Britain’s military caste. The German model of folding every last bit of the country’s strength and resources into a single, unified national war machine was still entirely alien to British military thinking—even after a full month of war. For Germany, war had become the all-consuming business of the entire nation; for the British, it still read as an incidental, offhand adventure. In Germany, the whole population was militarized; in England, the army was a narrow, specialized institution. For nearly every practical, day-to-day purpose, the country ran perfectly well without it, just as political life had also become a separate, specialized sphere… Now all at once, the public wanted a government that spoke for every citizen, and an army drawn from the whole of the people. How were they ever supposed to build that?

But Martin went no further into the matter. He led the conversation off into other channels. They had lunch in the restaurant, where he ordered wine and expensive delicacies and afterward he danced with her and with no one but her, till she was tired. He was a good dancer, and she whirled around and around with him in a heaven of delight, her head against his shoulder, wishing that it could last forever. Later in the afternoon they strayed off among the trees, where, in the good old fashion, she sat down while he sprawled on his back, his head in her lap. He lay and dozed, while she fondled his hair, looked down on his closed eyes, and loved him without reserve. Looking up suddenly, he read the tender advertisement in her face. Her eyes fluttered down, then they opened and looked into his with soft defiance.

Julian allowed the subject to drift away, steering their talk into lighter currents. They dined in the hotel’s hall, where he indulged in vintage wines and rare delicacies; afterward, he claimed her for every dance, ignoring the room until her strength began to fail. He moved with an effortless grace, and she spun in a dizzying paradise, her forehead resting against his jacket, wishing the world might stop spinning right there. Later, they wandered deep into the grove where, in the timeless way of lovers, she sat upon the moss and he stretched out, his head heavy in her lap. He dozed fitfully while she ran her fingers through his hair, watching his quiet face and loving him with a total, unshielded heart. When he opened his eyes suddenly, he saw the naked devotion written in her gaze. Her lashes brushed her cheeks for a second, then her eyes met his again with a gentle, stubborn pride.

“As the night advanced, a fierce wind arose from the woods and quickly dispersed the clouds that had loitered in the heavens; the blast tore along like a mighty avalanche and produced a kind of insanity in my spirits that burst all bounds of reason and reflection. I lighted the dry branch of a tree and danced with fury around the devoted cottage, my eyes still fixed on the western horizon, the edge of which the moon nearly touched. A part of its orb was at length hid, and I waved my brand; it sank, and with a loud scream I fired the straw, and heath, and bushes, which I had collected. The wind fanned the fire, and the cottage was quickly enveloped by the flames, which clung to it and licked it with their forked and destroying tongues.

As the night wore on, a brutal, howling wind gusted up from the deep woods, scattering the slow-loitering clouds that had hung heavy across the sky in seconds. The gale tore through the air like a crashing avalanche, tipping my mind into a rabid frenzy that shattered every last boundary of reason and careful thought. I lit a dry, gnarled tree branch and danced in a wild rage around the cottage I’d marked for destruction, my eyes locked tight to the western horizon where the moon hovered just a hair’s breadth above the edge. When the first sliver of its disc dipped out of view, I swung my burning torch high; the moment it sank fully out of sight, I let out a deafening, ragged scream and touched the flame to the pile of straw, heath, and dry brush I’d stashed beforehand. The wind fanned the fire to a roar, and the cottage was quickly wrapped in roiling flames, which coiled tight to its walls and lapped at every inch of it with their sharp, forked, ravenous tongues.

Leaving her and Oliver to compare notes at leisure, Mr. Brownlow led the way into another room; and there, heard from Rose a full narration of her interview with Nancy, which occasioned him no little surprise and perplexity. Rose also explained her reasons for not confiding in her friend Mr. Losberne in the first instance. The old gentleman considered that she had acted prudently, and readily undertook to hold solemn conference with the worthy doctor himself. To afford him an early opportunity for the execution of this design, it was arranged that he should call at the hotel at eight o’clock that evening, and that in the meantime Mrs. Maylie should be cautiously informed of all that had occurred. These preliminaries adjusted, Rose and Oliver returned home.

After Rose and Oliver were left to share their thoughts freely, Mr. Brownlow went ahead into another room. There, Rose gave him a complete account of her meeting with Nancy, which left him deeply surprised and puzzled. She also told him why she had not at first trusted her friend Mr. Losberne with the matter. The old man judged that she had behaved wisely and promptly agreed to have a serious talk with the good doctor himself. To give him an early chance to carry out this plan, they decided he would visit the hotel at eight that evening, and in the meanwhile Mrs. Maylie would be carefully told everything that had happened. Once these details were settled, Rose and Oliver went home.

There was a slight noise from the direction of the dim corner where the ladder was. It was the king descending. I could see that he was bearing something in one arm, and assisting himself with the other. He came forward into the light; upon his breast lay a slender girl of fifteen. She was but half conscious; she was dying of smallpox. Here was heroism at its last and loftiest possibility, its utmost summit; this was challenging death in the open field unarmed, with all the odds against the challenger, no reward set upon the contest, and no admiring world in silks and cloth of gold to gaze and applaud; and yet the king's bearing was as serenely brave as it had always been in those cheaper contests where knight meets knight in equal fight and clothed in protecting steel. He was great now; sublimely great. The rude statues of his ancestors in his palace should have an addition--I would see to that; and it would not be a mailed king killing a giant or a dragon, like the rest, it would be a king in commoner's garb bearing death in his arms that a peasant mother might look her last upon her child and be comforted.

A faint rustle emanated from the gloom near the wooden rungs. It was the sovereign making his descent. I watched as he climbed down, one arm occupied by a heavy weight, the other guiding his passage. When he stepped into the pale light, I saw a girl of tender years resting against his chest, her consciousness fading as the pestilence claimed her. Here was the pinnacle of human courage; a confrontation with the reaper in the starkest of terms, where no prizes were offered and no banners waved. There were no throngs in velvet to cheer this lonely struggle, yet his resolve was as unshakable as when he faced an armed peer in the safety of his harness. In this hour, he reached a height of grandeur beyond any crown. I determined then that the hall of his forefathers needed a new monument—not another iron-clad warrior triumphing over a mythic foe, but a king in humble wool, carrying mortality itself so a mother’s heart might be stilled by one last glimpse of her child.

It is not improbable that if Mr. Bumble had been possessed of this information at an earlier period of the interview, he might have imparted a very different colouring to his little history. It was too late to do it now, however; so he shook his head gravely, and, pocketing the five guineas, withdrew.

It is quite conceivable that had Mr. Grimshaw acquired such knowledge sooner in their conversation, he would have cast an altogether different hue upon his modest account. But the moment for that had passed; thus he gave a solemn shake of his head, slipped the five sovereigns into his pocket, and took his leave.

For, the time was to come, when the gaunt scarecrows of that region should have watched the lamplighter, in their idleness and hunger, so long, as to conceive the idea of improving on his method, and hauling up men by those ropes and pulleys, to flare upon the darkness of their condition. But, the time was not come yet; and every wind that blew over France shook the rags of the scarecrows in vain, for the birds, fine of song and feather, took no warning.

The hour had not yet arrived when the starved effigies of that countryside, hollow-eyed and motionless, would study the lamplighter so obsessively in their barren waiting that they would dream of refining his craft—of hoisting men by those same cords and wheels to blaze against the blackness of their plight. But the hour was still distant, and every gust that swept across the land stirred the scarecrows’ tatters uselessly, for the birds, elegant in plume and melody, heeded nothing.

Structured prediction has played a key role in the history of natural language processing. Example methods include techniques for sequence labeling and segmentation (Lafferty et al., 2001; Sarawagi and Cohen, 2005) , discriminative dependency and constituency parsing (Finkel et al., 2008; McDonald et al., 2005) , unsupervised learning for Figure 1 : Distribution of binary trees over an 1000token sequence. Coloring shows the marginal probabilities of every span. Torch-Struct is an optimized collection of common CRF distributions used in NLP that is designed to integrate with deep learning frameworks. labeling and alignment (Vogel et al., 1996; Goldwater and Griffiths, 2007) , approximate translation decoding with beam search (Tillmann and Ney, 2003) , among many others.

metadata.title: Torch-Struct: Deep Structured Prediction Library

text: Structured prediction has played a central role in the history of natural language processing. Representative methods include techniques for sequence labeling and segmentation (Lafferty et al., 2001; Sarawagi and Cohen, 2005), discriminative dependency and constituency parsing (Finkel et al., 2008; McDonald et al., 2005), unsupervised learning for labeling and alignment (Vogel et al., 1996; Goldwater and Griffiths, 2007), approximate translation decoding with beam search (Tillmann and Ney, 2003), among many others. Figure 1: Distribution of binary trees over a 1000-token sequence. Coloring indicates the marginal probabilities of each span. Torch-Struct is an optimized collection of common CRF distributions used in NLP, designed to integrate with deep learning frameworks.

"But I suppose all sorts of people could write an Irish pamphlet. Nobody but you could write 'The Silent Places.' Oh, _why_ don't you finish that great beautiful thing, and leave all this world of reality and newspapers, all these Crude, Vulgar, Quarrelsome, Jarring things to other people? You have the magic gift, you might be a poet, you can take us out of all these horrid things that are, away to Beautyland, and you are just content to be a critic and a disputer. It's your surroundings. It's your sordid realities. It's that Practicality at your elbow. You ought never to see a newspaper. You ought never to have an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on bowls of milk drunk in valleys of asphodel."

“But I daresay any number of people could contrive an Irish pamphlet. Only you could have written ‘The Still Places.’ Oh, why don’t you bring that great, lovely thing to its end, and leave all this world of fact and journals, all these coarse, noisy, contentious, discordant affairs, to other people? You have the enchanted faculty; you might be a poet. You can carry us away from all these dreadful realities to Beauty-land, and instead you are content to be merely a critic and a quarreller. It is your company about you. It is your wretched realities. It is that Practicality always at your elbow. You ought never to catch sight of a newspaper. You ought never to let an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on dishes of milk sipped in valleys of asphodel.”

But now the easy-fitting discipline of the Dower House style of hockey became apparent. Mr. Direck had last observed the tall young Indian gentleman, full of vitality and anxious for destruction, far away in the distance on the opposing right wing. But now, regardless of the more formal methods of the game, this young man had resolved, without further delay and at any cost, to hit the ball hard, and he was travelling like some Asiatic typhoon with an extreme velocity across the remonstrances of Mr. Britling and the general order of his side. Mr. Direck became aware of him just before his impact. There was a sort of collision from which Mr. Direck emerged with a feeling that one side of his face was permanently flattened, but still gallantly resolved to hit the comparatively lethargic ball. He and the staggered but resolute Indian clashed sticks again. And Mr. Direck had the best of it. Years of experience couldn't have produced a better pass to the captain....

But now the casual, free-flowing nature of hockey as played at the Dower House revealed itself. Mr. Direck had last glimpsed the tall young Indian gentleman, brimming with energy and bent on havoc, stationed far off on the opposite right flank. Yet now, ignoring the game's more conventional tactics, this young man had decided, without a moment's hesitation and no matter the consequences, to strike the ball with force, and he was hurtling forward like some Asian typhoon at tremendous speed, cutting through the protests of Mr. Britling and the entire formation of his team. Mr. Direck registered his approach only an instant before the crash. There was a kind of impact from which Mr. Direck recovered with the sensation that one cheek had been permanently compressed, though he remained bravely determined to hit the relatively sluggish ball. He and the reeling but steadfast Indian clashed sticks once more. And Mr. Direck came out on top. A lifetime of practice could not have delivered a finer pass to the captain.

Martin’s alienation from his family continued. Following upon Mr. Higginbotham’s unprovoked attack, Mr. Hermann von Schmidt showed his hand. The fortunate sale of several storiettes, some humorous verse, and a few jokes gave Martin a temporary splurge of prosperity. Not only did he partially pay up his bills, but he had sufficient balance left to redeem his black suit and wheel. The latter, by virtue of a twisted crank-hanger, required repairing, and, as a matter of friendliness with his future brother-in-law, he sent it to Von Schmidt’s shop.

Edgar’s estrangement from his family went on unabated. After Mr. Kettlewell’s gratuitous assault, Mr. Otto von Riedel revealed his true colors. The lucky disposal of a handful of short sketches, a few comic verses, and several jests brought Edgar a brief spell of affluence. Not only was he able to pay off part of his obligations, but he had enough remaining to reclaim his black suit and bicycle. The latter, because of a bent crank hanger, needed mending, and, as a gesture of cordiality toward his prospective brother-in-law, he sent it to von Riedel’s establishment.

Thomasin came out of the tree, shook from her hair and dress the loose berries which had fallen thereon, and went down the hill with her aunt, each woman bearing half the gathered boughs. It was now nearly four o’clock, and the sunlight was leaving the vales. When the west grew red the two relatives came again from the house and plunged into the heath in a different direction from the first, towards a point in the distant highway along which the expected man was to return.

Thomasin emerged from the tree, shaking from her hair and dress the loose berries that had lodged there, and went down the hill with her aunt, each woman carrying half the gathered branches. It was now nearly four o’clock, and the sunlight was withdrawing from the valleys. As the west turned red, the two relations came once more out from the house and plunged into the heath in a different direction from before, toward a point on the distant highway by which the expected man was to return.

It was the rush and roar of rain that he typified, and it stopped him, for no voice could be heard in it. A memorable storm of thunder and lightning broke with that sweep of water, and there was not a moment’s interval in crash, and fire, and rain, until after the moon rose at midnight.

He became the very image of the downpour’s clamour and surge, and it brought him to a halt, for no sound could rise above it. With that deluge came an unforgettable tempest of thunderbolts and blinding flashes, and there was no pause in the cracking, the blazing, and the torrent until the moon climbed into the sky at midnight.

Prince Michael’s shoes were wrecked far beyond the skill of the carefullest cobbler. The ragman would have declined any negotiations concerning his clothes. The two weeks’ stubble on his face was grey and brown and red and greenish yellow—as if it had been made up from individual contributions from the chorus of a musical comedy. No man existed who had money enough to wear so bad a hat as his.

Prince Adrian’s shoes were ruined beyond the repair of the most meticulous cobbler. The ragpicker would have refused all discussion of his garments. The fortnight’s stubble on his face was grey and brown and rusty red and a sickly yellow, as though it had been assembled from separate offerings by the chorus of a comic opera. No man alive possessed sufficient means to wear a hat as dreadful as his.

Notwithstanding the most superlative, and, I may say, supernat’ral exertions on the part of this parish,” said Bumble, “we have never been able to discover who is his father, or what was his mother’s settlement, name, or condition.”

“And despite a promised bounty of fifteen sovereigns, which was thereafter increased to thirty; despite the most exhaustive and, I might say, preternatural labors on the part of this vestry,” declared Grimshaw, “we have never managed to uncover the identity of his sire, or his mother’s legal residence, name, or social standing.”

“I’ll tell you,” said Sydney. “I lighted on you, Mr. Barsad, coming out of the prison of the Conciergerie while I was contemplating the walls, an hour or more ago. You have a face to be remembered, and I remember faces well. Made curious by seeing you in that connection, and having a reason, to which you are no stranger, for associating you with the misfortunes of a friend now very unfortunate, I walked in your direction. I walked into the wine-shop here, close after you, and sat near you. I had no difficulty in deducing from your unreserved conversation, and the rumour openly going about among your admirers, the nature of your calling. And gradually, what I had done at random, seemed to shape itself into a purpose, Mr. Barsad.”

“Let me enlighten you,” Julian said softly. “My gaze chanced upon you, Mr. Vane, as you departed the shadows of the Châtelet while I stood observing its heavy walls some time ago. Your features possess a distinct quality, and I have a keen faculty for recollection. Spurred by the sight of you at that particular threshold, and mindful of your past link to the calamities of a friend who now languishes in sorrow, I took it upon myself to trail you. I followed you into this very cellar, keeping close, and found a place beside you. From your own reckless speech and the common talk of the ruffians who surround you, your true profession was easily unmasked. And so, what started as a wandering impulse has begun to take the form of a design, Mr. Vane.”

The training corpus seems to play an important role in the performance of a downstream task. Both HerBERT and ELMo models were trained mainly on web crawled texts and they excel at tasks from an online domain (CBD, PolEmo-IN, PolEmo-OUT, and AR). On the other hand, the other Transformerbased models are superior on the Czy wiesz? task. It can be related to the fact that it is a Wikipedia-based question-answering task and the aforementioned models were trained mainly on Wikipedia corpus. Interestingly, the Slavic-BERT, which was additionally trained on Russian News corpus, has a lower score on the Czy wiesz? task than Multi-BERT and XLM-17.

metadata.title: KLEJ: Comprehensive Benchmark for Polish Language Understanding

text: The composition of the training corpus appears to significantly influence downstream task performance. Both HerBERT and ELMo, which were predominantly trained on web-crawled texts, achieve strong results on tasks originating from online domains—namely CBD, PolEmo-IN, PolEmo-OUT, and AR. In contrast, the other Transformer-based models perform better on the Czy wiesz? task. This outcome may stem from the fact that Czy wiesz? is a Wikipedia-based question-answering benchmark, and those models were primarily trained on Wikipedia corpora. Notably, Slavic-BERT, which received additional training on the Russian News corpus, attains a lower score on Czy wiesz? than both Multi-BERT and XLM-17.

It turned out to be one of those rich and rare fall days which is just a June day toned down to a degree where it is heaven to be out of doors. Toward noon the guests arrived, and we assembled under a great tree and were soon as sociable as old acquaintances. Even the king's reserve melted a little, though it was some little trouble to him to adjust himself to the name of Jones along at first. I had asked him to try to not forget that he was a farmer; but I had also considered it prudent to ask him to let the thing stand at that, and not elaborate it any. Because he was just the kind of person you could depend on to spoil a little thing like that if you didn't warn him, his tongue was so handy, and his spirit so willing, and his information so uncertain.

It ended up being one of those lush, uncommon autumn days—exactly the kind of sun-kissed June day softened just enough that being outside feels like pure bliss. By midday, the guests started showing up, and we all gathered beneath a massive old tree, chatting easily with one another like we’d been close friends for years. Even the king’s usual standoffishness thawed a little, though he stumbled at first when he had to get used to answering to the plain, ordinary name of Jones. I’d told him to do his best to remember he was supposed to be a regular farmer, but I’d also thought it was smart to warn him not to add any extra details beyond that basic backstory. He was exactly the sort of person who’d absolutely ruin a small, simple setup like that if you didn’t lay down ground rules first: he talked too quickly, was too eager to jump into any conversation, and half the facts he spouted were unreliable at best.

text: This section evaluates the performance of models trained on Conceptual Captions, as detailed in Section 4. Table 2 presents the results on the OID1k test set for side-by-side (SxS) human evaluations, comparing the PLuGS model against the TGT model (upper half) and the TTG model (lower half). Across all five languages, the results demonstrate that the PLuGS model consistently outperforms the TGT model on both the Gain SxS and Gain Accept metrics. Specifically, the absolute Gain SxS improvements for PLuGS relative to TGT range from 3% to 5%, while the gains relative to TTG range from 1% to 3%, with similar trends reflected in the Gain Accept metric. Table 3 reports the CIDEr scores for the Conceptual Captions v1.1 (CC-1.1) validation set. Notably, the CIDEr metric fails to exhibit any meaningful correlation with the results of the SxS human evaluations.

Mrs. Maylie took up her abode with her son and daughter-in-law, to enjoy, during the tranquil remainder of her days, the greatest felicity that age and worth can know—the contemplation of the happiness of those on whom the warmest affections and tenderest cares of a well-spent life, have been unceasingly bestowed.

Mr. Bumble emerged at early morning from the workhouse-gate, and walked with portly carriage and commanding steps, up the High Street. He was in the full bloom and pride of beadlehood; his cocked hat and coat were dazzling in the morning sun; he clutched his cane with the vigorous tenacity of health and power. Mr. Bumble always carried his head high; but this morning it was higher than usual. There was an abstraction in his eye, an elevation in his air, which might have warned an observant stranger that thoughts were passing in the beadle’s mind, too great for utterance.

Mr. Bumble stepped out through the workhouse gate at dawn and strode up the High Street with a stately bearing and authoritative tread. He stood at the very peak of his beadle’s glory; his tricorne hat and coat gleamed brilliantly in the early sunlight, and he gripped his walking stick with the firm vigor of robust vitality. Mr. Bumble habitually held his chin aloft, but this morning it rose even higher than normal. A faraway look in his gaze and a loftiness in his demeanor might have signaled to any perceptive passerby that ideas were stirring in the beadle’s head—thoughts far too momentous to be spoken aloud.

His wilful hands and feet began to beat and churn about, spasmodically and feebly. But he had fooled them and the will to live that made them beat and churn. He was too deep down. They could never bring him to the surface. He seemed floating languidly in a sea of dreamy vision. Colors and radiances surrounded him and bathed him and pervaded him. What was that? It seemed a lighthouse; but it was inside his brain—a flashing, bright white light. It flashed swifter and swifter. There was a long rumble of sound, and it seemed to him that he was falling down a vast and interminable stairway. And somewhere at the bottom he fell into darkness. That much he knew. He had fallen into darkness. And at the instant he knew, he ceased to know.

His rebellious limbs thrashed weakly, twitching in erratic spasms. Yet he had outwitted them, and the stubborn life force that drove their flailing. He had sunk too far. They could never haul him back up. He drifted as if suspended in a drowsy sea of hallucinatory light. Hues and luminous glows wrapped around him, soaked through him, saturated his being. What was that? It looked like a lighthouse, but it pulsed inside his skull—a flickering, brilliant white beam. It blinked faster and faster. A long, rolling thunder of noise swelled, and he felt himself tumbling down an immense, never-ending staircase. And somewhere at its base he plunged into blackness. That much he grasped. He had plunged into blackness. And the moment he grasped it, he grasped nothing at all.

The farmers were bound to throw in something, to sort of offset my liberality, whether I would or no; so I let them give me a flint and steel; and as soon as they had comfortably bestowed Sandy and me on our horse, I lit my pipe. When the first blast of smoke shot out through the bars of my helmet, all those people broke for the woods, and Sandy went over backwards and struck the ground with a dull thud. They thought I was one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had infinite trouble to persuade those people to venture back within explaining distance. Then I told them that this was only a bit of enchantment which would work harm to none but my enemies. And I promised, with my hand on my heart, that if all who felt no enmity toward me would come forward and pass before me they should see that only those who remained behind would be struck dead. The procession moved with a good deal of promptness. There were no casualties to report, for nobody had curiosity enough to remain behind to see what would happen.

The farmers felt compelled to contribute something, as a sort of return for my generosity, whether I wanted it or not; so I allowed them to give me a flint and steel. And as soon as they had snugly seated Sandy and me on the horse, I struck a light for my pipe. When the first plume of smoke burst out through the bars of my helmet, the whole crowd bolted for the woods, and Sandy toppled backward and hit the ground with a heavy thump. They took me for one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had endless difficulty persuading them to come back within speaking range. At last I told them this was merely a bit of enchantment that would harm no one except my enemies. Then I declared, hand over my heart, that if all who bore me no ill will would come forward and pass in front of me, they should see that only those who stayed behind would be struck dead. The procession got under way with surprising speed. There were no losses to report, for no one was curious enough to linger and see what would happen.

The whole series of my life appeared to me as a dream; I sometimes doubted if indeed it were all true, for it never presented itself to my mind with the force of reality.

I turned away in revulsion from the woman who could utter such a cold, unfeeling remark to someone who’d just been pulled back from the very brink of death; but I was so weak and listless I could barely parse the full weight of all that had unfolded. The entire arc of my life felt like a hazy, unmoored dream; sometimes I doubted if any of it had actually been real, for none of it ever settled in my mind with the sharp, tangible force of lived truth.

(1) Word-level accuracy: for a given word 8 , we consider the prediction correct if and only if all the diacritic marks on the word are correct, including gemination and the 'shin' dot, with all matres lectionis removed. 2Character-level accuracy: For each Hebrew letter in the input text we check if the model predicted the correct set of diacritic marks for the letter (and, for matres lectionis, we check that the model predicted their removal).

metadata.title: Nakdan: Professional Hebrew Diacritizer

text: Quantitative Evaluation We utilize two distinct quantitative metrics to evaluate the performance of our proposed model. (1) Word-level accuracy: For a given target word, a prediction is deemed correct if and only if all diacritic marks associated with the word are fully accurate, including gemination markers and the “shin” dot, with all matres lectionis properly excluded from the output. (2) Character-level accuracy: For each individual Hebrew letter present in the input text, we validate whether the model generates the correct complete set of diacritic marks for that character; for matres lectionis specifically, we separately verify that the model correctly predicts their removal as required.

The persistent torrent from the gurgoyle’s jaws directed all its vengeance into the grave. The rich tawny mould was stirred into motion, and boiled like chocolate. The water accumulated and washed deeper down, and the roar of the pool thus formed spread into the night as the head and chief among other noises of the kind created by the deluging rain. The flowers so carefully planted by Fanny’s repentant lover began to move and writhe in their bed. The winter-violets turned slowly upside down, and became a mere mat of mud. Soon the snowdrop and other bulbs danced in the boiling mass like ingredients in a cauldron. Plants of the tufted species were loosened, rose to the surface, and floated off.

The unrelenting downpour spewing from the gargoyle’s mouth poured all its unbridled force straight down into the open grave. The deep, warm brown loam churned and roiled, bubbling thick as melted chocolate. Water kept piling up, carving deeper into the earth as it settled, and the low, thunderous roar of the growing pool cut through the dark, louder than any other sound the drenching storm had stirred up across the grounds. The blooms Fanny’s regretful former lover had tended so gently started to twist and thrash in their planting bed. The winter violets flipped slowly root-over-bloom, dissolving into nothing but a sodden, muck-covered mat. Before long, snowdrops and other bulb flowers bounced and tossed in the churning mire like bits of seasoning tossed into a bubbling cauldron. Clumping, tufted ground cover plants were torn free from the soil, bobbed up to the top of the pool, and drifted away on the murky water.

By degrees, he grew more calm, and besought, in a low and broken voice, that he might be rescued from his present dangers; and that if any aid were to be raised up for a poor outcast boy who had never known the love of friends or kindred, it might come to him now, when, desolate and deserted, he stood alone in the midst of wickedness and guilt.

He implored that if any mercy remained for a lonely orphan who had never known the love of kin or companions, it might reach him now, as he stood forsaken and isolated in the heart of such pervasive evil and guilt.

And with that, she slipped from his thoughts once more beneath the weight of more pressing concerns....

All the while that Mr. Britling had been indulging in these imaginative slaughterings and spending the tears and hate that had gathered in his heart, his reason had been sitting apart and above the storm, like the sun waiting above thunder, like a wise nurse watching and patient above the wild passions of a child. And all the time his reason had been maintaining silently and firmly, without shouting, without speech, that the men who had made this hour were indeed not devils, were no more devils than Mr. Britling was a devil, but sinful men of like nature with himself, hard, stupid, caught in the same web of circumstance. "Kill them in your passion if you will," said reason, "but understand. This thing was done neither by devils nor fools, but by a conspiracy of foolish motives, by the weak acquiescences of the clever, by a crime that was no man's crime but the natural necessary outcome of the ineffectiveness, the blind motives and muddleheadedness of all mankind."

All the time Mr. Britling had been lost in these mental massacres, pouring out the grief and fury that had pooled inside him, his reason had hovered detached above the turmoil—like the sun poised beyond a storm, like a calm nurse watching over a child's wild tantrums with patient stillness. And through it all, that reason had been holding its ground, quietly and unwaveringly, without outcry or declaration, insisting that the men who had brought about this moment were not fiends, no more fiends than Mr. Britling himself was a fiend, but flawed human beings of the same kind as he—stubborn, obtuse, tangled in the same nets of circumstance. "Rage against them, kill them in your heart if you must," reason seemed to say, "but see clearly. This was not the work of demons or fools, but of foolish impulses conspiring together, of clever people yielding weakly, of a crime that belonged to no single person but sprang inevitably from the fecklessness, the blind drives, the muddled thinking of all humanity."

“Well, now, you’d hardly believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s father—was one of the ficklest husbands alive, after a while. Understand? ’a didn’t want to be fickle, but he couldn’t help it. The pore feller were faithful and true enough to her in his wish, but his heart would rove, do what he would. He spoke to me in real tribulation about it once. ‘Coggan,’ he said, ‘I could never wish for a handsomer woman than I’ve got, but feeling she’s ticketed as my lawful wife, I can’t help my wicked heart wandering, do what I will.’ But at last I believe he cured it by making her take off her wedding-ring and calling her by her maiden name as they sat together after the shop was shut, and so ’a would get to fancy she was only his sweetheart, and not married to him at all. And as soon as he could thoroughly fancy he was doing wrong and committing the seventh, ’a got to like her as well as ever, and they lived on a perfect picture of mutel love.”

Oh, you’d scarce believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s own father—turned out to be one of the most fickle husbands walking the earth, after a while. Mark this: he never meant to be unsteady, he just couldn’t help himself. Poor fellow, every part of him wanted to be loyal and true to his wife, but his heart would wander no matter how hard he tried to rein it in. He poured out his trouble to me once, proper wretched over the whole mess. “Coggan,” he said, “I could never wish for a handsomer woman than the one I’ve got, but the second I remember she’s my lawfully wedded wife, my wicked heart drifts off, no matter what I do to stop it.” But I think he cured himself of it in the end, you see: he had her take off her wedding ring, and he’d call her by her maiden name every night once the shop was shut and they were sitting together alone. That way, he could fool himself into thinking she was only his sweetheart, not his spouse at all. The minute he could fully believe he was doing wrong and breaking the seventh commandment, he grew as fond of her as he’d ever been, and they went on to live as perfect a picture of quiet, devoted love as you could ever find.

Our baselines fall into three categories: retrievalbased, generative-based and pretrain-finetunebased models. Among the retrieval-based baselines, KV Profile Memory (Zhang et al., 2018b) was the official baseline which employed the memory network along with profile information, and Dually Interactive Matching Network (Gu et al., 2019) proposed a dual matching architecture to match between the responses and their corresponding contexts. Language Model, Generative Profile Memory (Zhang et al., 2018b) and SEQ2SEQ with attention mechanism (Bahdanau et al., 2015) were implemented as generative baselines for dialogue generation. The remaining methods were all pretrain-finetune-based. Transfertransfo (Wolf et al., 2019b) 3 achieved the state-of-the-art performance on automatic metrics, while Lost In Conversation 4 topped the human evaluations (Dinan et al., 2019) . Analogous to our approach, they employed the pretrained language model GPT to initialize their models, and then fine-tuned it on the dataset. Table 1 shows the experimental results on automatic metrics. Following Zhang et al. 2018b, we reported the official automatic metrics to evaluate the methods: Hits@1, Perplexity (ppl) and F1. Given 20 response candidates, Hits@1 is the probability that the real response ranks the highest according to the model. Perplexity measures the negative log likelihood of the correct sequence output by the model, lower values indicating better performance. F1 is the harmonic mean of word-level precision and recall. As observed, our approach outperforms almost all baselines and achieves new state-of-the-art performance on ppl and F1, with highly competitive performance on Hits@1. In the revised mode, our approach still achieves the best performance, obtaining a relative improvement of 13.4% on F1 against the strongest baseline. It is worth noting that we also tried to employ F1 as the reward, but the result is far from satisfactory.

metadata.title: You Impress Me: Dialogue Generation via Mutual Persona Perception

text: Our baselines can be grouped into three categories: retrieval-based, generative, and pretrain-finetune-based models. For the retrieval-based setting, KV Profile Memory (Zhang et al., 2018b) served as the official baseline, using a memory network augmented with profile information, and Dually Interactive Matching Network (Gu et al., 2019) introduced a dual matching architecture to align responses with their respective contexts. As generative baselines for dialogue generation, we implemented Language Model, Generative Profile Memory (Zhang et al., 2018b), and SEQ2SEQ with an attention mechanism (Bahdanau et al., 2015). The remaining approaches were all based on pretrain-finetune learning. Transfertransfo (Wolf et al., 2019b) achieved the strongest results on automatic metrics, while Lost In Conversation 4 ranked first in human evaluation (Dinan et al., 2019). Similar to our method, both models used the pretrained GPT language model as initialization and then fine-tuned it on the dataset. Table 1 reports the experimental results on automatic metrics. Following Zhang et al. 2018b, we adopted the official automatic metrics for evaluation: Hits@1, Perplexity (ppl), and F1. Given 20 candidate responses, Hits@1 denotes the probability that the ground-truth response is ranked highest by the model. Perplexity captures the negative log-likelihood of the correct sequence produced by the model, with lower values indicating better performance. F1 is the harmonic mean of word-level precision and recall. As shown, our method surpasses nearly all baselines and establishes new state-of-the-art results on ppl and F1, while remaining highly competitive on Hits@1. In the revised mode, our method continues to perform best, yielding a 13.4% relative improvement in F1 over the strongest baseline. Notably, we also attempted to use F1 as the reward, but the outcome was far from satisfactory.

text: In Wikipedia, each page belongs to zero, one, or multiple categories, such as 20th-century women scientists, Naturalized citizens of France, or People from Warsaw, to mention a few of the 58 categories associated with the Marie Curie page. These categories establish a hierarchy that lies at the core of knowledge bases such as (Suchanek et al., 2007) and YAGO2 (Hoffart et al., 2013), as well as taxonomies such as WikiTaxonomy (Ponzetto and Strube, 2011) and WikiNet (Nastase et al., 2010). creation, (7) the number of translations of the page in other versions of Wikipedia, (8) whether the title contains the name of a month, (9) whether the title is a date (matching some regular expression), and (10) whether the title contains foreign characters. With the exception of the last three, which are binary, each feature is represented as a real value and then linearly transformed so that the corresponding distribution over all pages has mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
