text: Additional analysis We also present an analysis to show how Shapley denoising works as shown in Figure 2 . We examine the Shapley value for each training datum in Stage 3. We first show an example dialog with a negative Shapley value in Table 5 . According to the Shapley value, we remove data points one by one starting from the least valuable to the most valuable. Each time, after the point is removed, we create new KNN regressor models K = 1, 5, 25, 50, 100 on the remaining dialogs and labels and evaluate them on the test set with expert annotations. We extract the features of the remaining dialogs using the dialog encoder φ tuned by Stage 1 and Stage 2. As shown in Figure 2 , removing training data with low Shapley values increases the performance to a certain point before convergence for K of all choices. We observe a similar trend when re-training a model on the remaining data. In contrast, removing data randomly decreases the performance on the test set. In addition, larger K has a better performance, which validates the denoising effect of KNN with large K.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: Supplementary analysis To further elucidate the mechanism of Shapley denoising, we provide an illustrative investigation in Figure 2. Specifically, we compute the Shapley value for every training instance from Stage 3. Table 5 presents a representative dialogue that obtains a negative Shapley value. Guided by these values, we iteratively discard data points in ascending order of their Shapley contributions, starting from the least beneficial. After each removal step, we construct fresh KNN regressors with K = 1, 5, 25, 50, 100 on the retained dialogues and labels, then assess them on the test set using expert annotations. The features of the surviving dialogues are extracted via the dialog encoder φ fine-tuned in Stages 1 and 2. As depicted in Figure 2, excising training samples with low Shapley values consistently improves performance up to a saturation point across all choices of K. A similar pattern emerges when retraining a model on the filtered data. Conversely, random removal degrades test-set performance. Moreover, larger K yields stronger results, corroborating the denoising benefit of KNN with a substantial neighborhood size.

where W, G i are the input sequence and the current semantic graph respectively. g(•), f (•) seek where to construct (edge prediction) and what to abstract (node prediction) respectively, and x i t , y i t are the t-th graph hypothesis (where to construct) and t-th sequence hypothesis (what to abstract) for the i-th expansion step respectively. For clarity, we may drop the superscript i in the following descriptions. Figure 2 depicts an overview of the graphsequence iterative inference process. Our model has four main components: (1) Sequence Encoder, which generates a set of text memories (per token) to provide grounding for concept alignment and abstraction; (2) Graph Encoder, which generates a set of graph memories (per node) to provide grounding for relation reasoning; (3) Concept Solver, where a previous graph hypothesis is used for concept prediction; and (4) Graph Solver, where a previous concept hypothesis is used for relation prediction. The last two components correspond to the reasoning functions g(•) and f (•) respectively.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: where W and G_i denote the input sequence and the current semantic graph, respectively. g(•) and f(•) aim to determine where to construct (edge prediction) and what to abstract (node prediction), respectively, and x_it and y_it denote the t-th graph hypothesis (where to construct) and the t-th sequence hypothesis (what to abstract) for the i-th expansion step, respectively. For clarity, we may omit the superscript i in the following description. Figure 2 presents an overview of the iterative graph-sequence inference process. Our model consists of four main components: (1) a Sequence Encoder, which produces a set of text memories (one per token) to ground concept alignment and abstraction; (2) a Graph Encoder, which produces a set of graph memories (one per node) to ground relation reasoning; (3) a Concept Solver, in which a previous graph hypothesis is used for concept prediction; and (4) a Graph Solver, in which a previous concept hypothesis is used for relation prediction. The last two components correspond to the reasoning functions g(•) and f(•), respectively.

“Just look at it!” he cried. “An’ it’s all mine! It’s free. I can lie down under them trees an’ sleep for a thousan’ years if I want to. Aw, come on, Mart, let’s chuck it. What’s the good of waitin’ another moment. That’s the land of nothin’ to do out there, an’ I got a ticket for it—an’ it ain’t no return ticket, b’gosh!”

“Look at that!” he shouted. “And it’s all mine! It’s wide open. I could lie down under those trees and sleep for a thousand years if I felt like it. Come on, Mart, let's just leave it all behind. What’s the point in waiting another minute? That’s the land of leisure out there, and I’ve got my ticket in hand—and believe me, I’m never coming back!”

“These were the reflections of my hours of despondency and solitude; but when I contemplated the virtues of the cottagers, their amiable and benevolent dispositions, I persuaded myself that when they should become acquainted with my admiration of their virtues they would compassionate me and overlook my personal deformity. Could they turn from their door one, however monstrous, who solicited their compassion and friendship? I resolved, at least, not to despair, but in every way to fit myself for an interview with them which would decide my fate. I postponed this attempt for some months longer, for the importance attached to its success inspired me with a dread lest I should fail. Besides, I found that my understanding improved so much with every day’s experience that I was unwilling to commence this undertaking until a few more months should have added to my sagacity.

Such were the musings of my bleak and lonely hours; yet when I dwelled upon the cottagers’ goodness—their gentle, generous natures—I convinced myself that once they perceived how deeply I revered their kindness, they would pity me and disregard my monstrous form. Could they really spurn a creature, however hideous, who came begging only for their sympathy and fellowship? I determined, at least, not to surrender to despair, but to prepare myself by every means for the meeting that would seal my destiny. I deferred the attempt some months longer, for the gravity of its outcome filled me with a terror of failing. Moreover, I sensed my understanding sharpening with each day’s experience, and I was loath to begin the venture until a few more months had seasoned my wisdom.

The sun went down almost blood-red that night, and a livid cloud received its rays in the east. Up against this dark background the west front of the church tower—the only part of the edifice visible from the farm-house windows—rose distinct and lustrous, the vane upon the summit bristling with rays. Hereabouts, at six o’clock, the young men of the village gathered, as was their custom, for a game of Prisoners’ base. The spot had been consecrated to this ancient diversion from time immemorial, the old stocks conveniently forming a base facing the boundary of the churchyard, in front of which the ground was trodden hard and bare as a pavement by the players. She could see the brown and black heads of the young lads darting about right and left, their white shirt-sleeves gleaming in the sun; whilst occasionally a shout and a peal of hearty laughter varied the stillness of the evening air. They continued playing for a quarter of an hour or so, when the game concluded abruptly, and the players leapt over the wall and vanished round to the other side behind a yew-tree, which was also half behind a beech, now spreading in one mass of golden foliage, on which the branches traced black lines.

That evening, the sun set in a deep, blood-like crimson, casting its final glow onto a dark, bruised cloud in the east. Against this somber backdrop, the western face of the church tower—the only part of the building visible from the farmhouse windows—stood out with sharp clarity, its weathercock atop the spire shimmering in the fading light. At six o’clock, the village youth gathered here as they always did for a game of Prisoners’ Base. This location had been used for the ancient game since time immemorial; the old stocks served as a convenient base near the churchyard boundary, where the ground had been beaten as hard and smooth as pavement by the players' feet. She watched the dark-haired boys darting to and fro, their white shirt-sleeves catching the sun, while the quiet evening air was occasionally broken by a shout or a burst of spirited laughter. After about fifteen minutes, the game came to a sudden halt, and the players vaulted over the wall, vanishing behind a yew tree. This tree was partially hidden by a beech that had turned into a mass of golden leaves, its branches forming dark patterns against the bright foliage.

They reached the place where the hollies grew, which was in a conical pit, so that the tops of the trees were not much above the general level of the ground. Thomasin stepped up into a fork of one of the bushes, as she had done under happier circumstances on many similar occasions, and with a small chopper that they had brought she began to lop off the heavily berried boughs.

They came at last to the hollow where the ilex trees clustered, sunken in a funnel-shaped depression so that their crowns rose only a little above the surrounding earth. Into a crotch of one of the shrubs Tamsin climbed, as she had done in brighter days on countless such errands, and with the little billhook they had fetched she set to work severing the branches freighted with crimson fruit.

About two hundred yards off, in the flat, we built a pen of scantlings, about four feet high, and laid planks on it, and so made a platform. We covered it with swell tapestries borrowed for the occasion, and topped it off with the abbot's own throne. When you are going to do a miracle for an ignorant race, you want to get in every detail that will count; you want to make all the properties impressive to the public eye; you want to make matters comfortable for your head guest; then you can turn yourself loose and play your effects for all they are worth. I know the value of these things, for I know human nature. You can't throw too much style into a miracle. It costs trouble, and work, and sometimes money; but it pays in the end. Well, we brought the wires to the ground at the chapel, and then brought them under the ground to the platform, and hid the batteries there. We put a rope fence a hundred feet square around the platform to keep off the common multitude, and that finished the work. My idea was, doors open at 10:30, performance to begin at 11:25 sharp. I wished I could charge admission, but of course that wouldn't answer. I instructed my boys to be in the chapel as early as 10, before anybody was around, and be ready to man the pumps at the proper time, and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.

About two hundred yards distant, upon the level ground, we constructed an enclosure of slender timbers, rising some four feet, and laid planks across it to form a stage. This we draped with sumptuous tapestries borrowed for the purpose, and crowned it with the abbot’s own throne. When one means to perform a miracle before an unlettered people, every detail that tells must be attended to; the properties must strike the public gaze, and the chief guest must be seated in comfort; only then can you give yourself free rein and work your effects for all they are worth. I understand such things, for I understand human nature. You cannot invest a miracle with too much ceremony. It costs exertion, labour, and sometimes coin; but it repays itself in the end. We ran the wires down to the chapel wall, then carried them underground to the platform, and concealed the batteries there. Around the stage we set a rope barrier a hundred feet square, to hold back the common crowd, and with that the preparations were complete. My design was to open the doors at half past ten, the performance to begin precisely at twenty-five minutes past eleven. I wished I might charge admission, but naturally that would not serve. I directed my lads to be in the chapel by ten, before any spectators gathered, ready to work the pumps at the appointed moment and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.

As it grew later the fire was made up in the large long hall into which the staircase descended, and all encumbrances were cleared out for dancing. The log which was to form the back-brand of the evening fire was the uncleft trunk of a tree, so unwieldy that it could be neither brought nor rolled to its place; and accordingly two men were to be observed dragging and heaving it in by chains and levers as the hour of assembly drew near.

As the shadows lengthened, the hearth was built up within the vast, low-beamed parlor where the main stairs terminated, and the floor was stripped of its furnishings to accommodate the feet of the dancers. The centerpiece of the night’s fire—a gargantuan, unsplit length of timber—proved so burdensome that no man could lift or roll it; consequently, as the guests’ arrival neared, a pair of workers could be seen laboring to drag it into position, plying levers and rattling chains to force the great weight home.

Strangely enough he began to feel now that it would not be so hard to persuade her who was his best friend that comparative poverty was essentially the higher course for him, as to reconcile to his feelings the act of persuading her. From every provident point of view his mother was so undoubtedly right, that he was not without a sickness of heart in finding he could shake her.

Curiously, it dawned on him at last that the true difficulty lay not in convincing his dearest ally that a modest station was, for him, the nobler path, but in making peace with his own conscience for having swayed her. By every prudent measure her judgment was so faultless that a quiet ache settled in his chest when he realized he possessed the power to unsettle it.

And now night drooped slowly upon the wide watery levels in front; and at no great distance from them, where the shoreline curved round, and formed a long riband of shade upon the horizon, a series of points of yellow light began to start into existence, denoting the spot to be the site of Budmouth, where the lamps were being lighted along the parade. The cluck of their oars was the only sound of any distinctness upon the sea, and as they laboured amid the thickening shades the lamp-lights grew larger, each appearing to send a flaming sword deep down into the waves before it, until there arose, among other dim shapes of the kind, the form of the vessel for which they were bound.

Evening settled slowly over the vast, watery expanse ahead. Nearby, where the coast curved to trace a dark ribbon against the horizon, a row of amber sparks began to flicker into view, signaling the location of Budmouth as the lamps were lit along the promenade. The rhythmic thud of their oars was the only distinct sound on the water, and as they rowed through the deepening shadows, the lights grew brighter, each casting a fiery blade deep into the waves. Finally, among the other hazy outlines, the silhouette of the ship they were heading for loomed into sight.

"The old system of life was organisation. That is where Germany is still the most ancient of European states. It's a reversion to a tribal cult. It's atavistic.... To organise or discipline, or mould characters or press authority, is to assume that you have reached finality in your general philosophy. It implies an assured end. Heinrich has his assured end, his philological professorship or thereabouts as a part of the Germanic machine. And that too has its assured end in German national assertion. Here, we have none of those convictions. We know we haven't finality, and so we are open and apologetic and receptive, rather than wilful.... You see all organisation, with its implication of finality, is death. We feel that. The Germans don't. What you organise you kill. Organised morals or organised religion or organised thought are dead morals and dead religion and dead thought. Yet some organisation you must have. Organisation is like killing cattle. If you do not kill some the herd is just waste. But you musn't kill all or you kill the herd. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the continuation; the unorganised side of life is the real life. The reality of life is adventure, not performance. What isn't adventure isn't life. What can be ruled about can be machined. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats get hold of life and try to make it _all_ rules, _all_ etiquette and regulation and correctitude.... And parents and the love of parents make for the same thing. It is all very well to experiment for oneself, but when one sees these dear things of one's own, so young and inexperienced and so capable of every sort of gallant foolishness, walking along the narrow plank, going down into dark jungles, ah! then it makes one want to wrap them in laws and foresight and fence them about with 'Verboten' boards in all the conceivable aspects...."

The old way of life was built on structure. That’s why Germany remains the most archaic of European nations—a throwback to tribal worship, a kind of atavism. To organise, to discipline, to shape character or impose authority, is to act as if you’ve arrived at a final truth in your overall philosophy. It presupposes a fixed destination. Heinrich has his fixed destination, his professorship in philology or something like it, slotted into the Germanic machinery. And that, in turn, has its fixed destination in German national self-assertion. Over here, we hold no such certainties. We know we haven’t reached finality, so we stay open, apologetic, receptive, instead of willful. You see, all organisation, because it implies finality, is death. We sense that. The Germans don’t. Whatever you organise, you kill. Organised morals, organised religion, organised thought—these are dead morals, dead religion, dead thought. And yet some organisation is necessary. Organisation is like slaughtering cattle. If you don’t kill some, the herd simply goes to waste. But you mustn’t kill them all, or you destroy the herd itself. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the ongoing life; the unorganised side of existence is the real life. The essence of life is adventure, not achievement. What isn’t adventure isn’t life. What can be governed can be mechanised. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats seize hold of life and try to turn it entirely into rules, entirely into etiquette and regulation and correctness. And parents, and parental love, push toward the same end. It’s all very well to experiment on one’s own, but when you watch those dear creatures of your own, so young and untried and so ready for every kind of splendid folly, stepping along the narrow plank, descending into dark jungles—ah, then you long to swaddle them in laws and foresight and fence them round with “Verboten” signs on every conceivable side.

Mr. Bumble walked on with long strides; little Oliver, firmly grasping his gold-laced cuff, trotted beside him, inquiring at the end of every quarter of a mile whether they were “nearly there.” To these interrogations Mr. Bumble returned very brief and snappish replies; for the temporary blandness which gin-and-water awakens in some bosoms had by this time evaporated; and he was once again a beadle.

Master Harrow strode forward in wide, swinging steps; young Pip clung tightly to the braided hem of his sleeve, scurrying to keep pace and asking after every few hundred yards if they had “almost arrived.” To these questions Master Harrow gave only curt and waspish answers, for the fleeting warmth that a draught of brandy kindles in certain hearts had now faded entirely, and he was once more a parish officer.

Twice the long hands of the clocks went round, Royalties thinned from their _al fresco_ thrones, and buzzed or clattered away in their vehicles of state. The music retired into cases of wood and bags of leather and baize. Waiters removed cloths pointedly near the plain figure sitting almost alone.

Twice the long hands of the clocks made their circuit, and the royalties dwindled on their _al fresco_ thrones, then buzzed and clattered away in their state carriages. The music was packed back into its wooden cases and leather and baize bags. The waiters drew away the cloths with marked deliberation near the plain figure seated almost alone.

In practice, most fixed embeddings are used as feature-based models. The most notable examples are word2vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) , GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) and fastText . All of them are extensively used in a variety of applications nowadays. On the other hand, contextualized word representations and language models have been developed using both featurebased architectures, the most notable examples being ELMo and Flair (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2018) , and transformer based architectures, that are commonly used in a fine-tune setting, as is the case of GPT-1, GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018 (Radford et al., , 2019 , BERT and its derivatives Lan et al., 2019) and more recently T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) . All of them have repeatedly improved the state-of-the art in many downstream NLP tasks over the last year.

metadata.title: A Monolingual Approach to Contextualized Word Embeddings for Mid-Resource Languages

text: In practice, most fixed embeddings are used as feature-based models. The most prominent examples are word2vec (Mikolov et al., 2013), GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014), and fastText. All of these are now extensively used across a wide range of applications. By contrast, contextualized word representations and language models have been developed using both feature-based architectures, most notably ELMo and Flair (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2018), and transformer-based architectures, which are commonly used in fine-tuning settings, as in GPT-1, GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018, 2019), BERT and its derivatives (Lan et al., 2019), and more recently T5 (Raffel et al., 2019). Over the past year, all of these have repeatedly advanced the state of the art on many downstream NLP tasks.

Anyone who had stood by now would have pitied her, not so much on account of her exposure to weather, and isolation from all of humanity except the mouldered remains inside the tumulus; but for that other form of misery which was denoted by the slightly rocking movement that her feelings imparted to her person. Extreme unhappiness weighed visibly upon her. Between the drippings of the rain from her umbrella to her mantle, from her mantle to the heather, from the heather to the earth, very similar sounds could be heard coming from her lips; and the tearfulness of the outer scene was repeated upon her face. The wings of her soul were broken by the cruel obstructiveness of all about her; and even had she seen herself in a promising way of getting to Budmouth, entering a steamer, and sailing to some opposite port, she would have been but little more buoyant, so fearfully malignant were other things. She uttered words aloud. When a woman in such a situation, neither old, deaf, crazed, nor whimsical, takes upon herself to sob and soliloquize aloud there is something grievous the matter.

Any onlooker lingering at the spot would have felt their heart twist for her, not first for the lash of the cold rain on her skin or her utter solitude, broken only by the decayed remains tucked inside the ancient barrow at her back; but for the slow, unsteady sway of her frame, a physical echo of the agony churning beneath her skin. A suffocating weight of despair hung off her, impossible to miss even through the fog of falling rain. Rain pattered off the edge of her oilskin umbrella to her thick wool cloak, dripped from her cloak to the gorse and heather at her feet, seeped from the heather down into the dark loam below; and between those soft, pattering beats came the faint, hitching sound of her sobs, the damp grief of the storm mirrored in the streaks of tears carving tracks through the rain on her cheeks. The very wings of her spirit had been snapped by the cold, unyielding cruelty of every turn her life had taken lately; even if she’d suddenly found herself on the clear road to Brineport, stepping onto a steam packet bound for some distant shore across the channel, she would not have felt a scrap lighter, so heavy and unforgiving was the weight of the wrongs that had been done to her. She spoke fragments of sentences out loud to the empty air. When a woman in such straits, who is neither elderly, hard of hearing, unwell in the mind, nor given to fanciful displays, weeps and murmurs to herself in the open wild, you know her suffering is beyond bearing.

Sunday he had intended to devote to studying for the high school examination, but the pearl-diving article lured him away, and he spent the day in the white-hot fever of re-creating the beauty and romance that burned in him. The fact that the _Examiner_ of that morning had failed to publish his treasure-hunting article did not dash his spirits. He was at too great a height for that, and having been deaf to a twice-repeated summons, he went without the heavy Sunday dinner with which Mr. Higginbotham invariably graced his table. To Mr. Higginbotham such a dinner was advertisement of his worldly achievement and prosperity, and he honored it by delivering platitudinous sermonettes upon American institutions and the opportunity said institutions gave to any hard-working man to rise—the rise, in his case, which he pointed out unfailingly, being from a grocer’s clerk to the ownership of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

Sunday was meant for cramming for the high school exam, but the article on pearl diving seduced him, and he surrendered the hours to the white-hot thrill of reshaping the beauty and romance blazing inside him. That morning’s _Examiner_ hadn’t printed his treasure-hunting piece, yet his mood stayed aloft, untouched. Ignoring two calls to the table, he skipped the hefty Sunday dinner Mr. Higginbotham always laid on as a trophy of his worldly success. To Mr. Higginbotham, that meal proclaimed his prosperity, and he sanctified it with trite little sermons on American institutions and the chances they offered any industrious man to climb—his own climb, as he never failed to note, having lifted him from grocery clerk to proprietor of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

When she had disappeared Charley, with misgiving in his eyes, slowly came from the stable door, and going to another point in the bank he looked over. Eustacia was leaning against it on the outside, her face covered with her hands, and her head pressing the dewy heather which bearded the bank’s outer side. She appeared to be utterly indifferent to the circumstance that her bonnet, hair, and garments were becoming wet and disarranged by the moisture of her cold, harsh pillow. Clearly something was wrong.

Once she had vanished entirely from view, Charley, his eyes heavy with unease, moved slowly away from the stable doorway, then picked a different spot along the bank to peer over its edge. Eustacia was leaning against the bank’s outer face, her face buried in her hands, her head pressed into the dewy heather that bristled along the slope’s outer edge. She seemed completely unconcerned that her bonnet, hair, and clothing were growing damp and rumpled from the moisture seeping out of that cold, scratchy makeshift pillow. It was obvious something was deeply amiss.

Upon reaching a particular junction, Elias paused, deliberating whether to pivot back toward the ornate and prestigious dining room where he was accustomed to feasting during his moments of deliberate extravagance. At that precise heartbeat, a slip of a girl rounded the brickwork at a trot, lost her purchase on a crust of frosted rime, and collapsed abruptly onto the paving stones.

The idea took hold of Mr. Direck's mind that until they got through the park gate it would be quite out of order to say anything. The lane and the road and the stile and the gate were all so much preliminary stuff to be got through before one could get to business. But after the little white gate the way was clear, the park opened out and one could get ahead without bothering about the steering. And Mr. Direck had, he felt, been diplomatically involved in lanes and by-ways long enough.

Mr. Direck was fully settled in the belief that bringing up what he needed to say before they passed through the park gate would be entirely out of line. The winding lane, the open road, the wooden stile, and the entry gate all felt like nothing more than formalities to work through before he could get to the actual point. Once they stepped past that small white gate, though, the path would clear, the whole park would open up around them, and they could move forward without having to fuss over navigating every tricky little turn. And Mr. Direck felt he’d already spent far too long tiptoeing around diplomatic niceties, stuck in those meandering, roundabout lead-up moments.

“My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking _like_ a gentleman, “my name, sir, is Brownlow. Permit me to inquire the name of the magistrate who offers a gratuitous and unprovoked insult to a respectable person, under the protection of the bench.” Saying this, Mr. Brownlow looked around the office as if in search of some person who would afford him the required information.

“My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking like a gentleman, “is Brownlow. Allow me to ask the name of the magistrate who is ready to offer an unprovoked and gratuitous insult to a respectable man, while sheltering himself behind the bench.” As he said this, Mr. Brownlow glanced about the office, as though looking for someone who could give him the information he wanted.

Martin had enjoyed the fight, with a recrudescence of the old fighting thrills. But they quickly died away, and he was oppressed by a great sadness. He felt very old—centuries older than those careless, care-free young companions of his others days. He had travelled far, too far to go back. Their mode of life, which had once been his, was now distasteful to him. He was disappointed in it all. He had developed into an alien. As the steam beer had tasted raw, so their companionship seemed raw to him. He was too far removed. Too many thousands of opened books yawned between them and him. He had exiled himself. He had travelled in the vast realm of intellect until he could no longer return home. On the other hand, he was human, and his gregarious need for companionship remained unsatisfied. He had found no new home. As the gang could not understand him, as his own family could not understand him, as the bourgeoisie could not understand him, so this girl beside him, whom he honored high, could not understand him nor the honor he paid her. His sadness was not untouched with bitterness as he thought it over.

Martin relished the conflict, feeling a brief revival of his former combative spirit. However, the sensation faded rapidly, leaving him burdened by a profound melancholy. He felt incredibly old—eons older than the reckless, happy-go-lucky friends of his youth. He had moved too far along his path to ever retreat. Their lifestyle, which he once embraced, now seemed repulsive to him. He felt let down by everything; he had become a total outsider. Just as the local beer tasted harsh, their presence felt unrefined. The distance between them was immense, an abyss created by the countless books he had read. He was an exile of his own making, having ventured so far into the intellectual wilderness that his old home was unreachable. Yet, being human, his natural desire for community remained starving. He hadn't found a new sanctuary. His old friends, his family, and the social elite all failed to understand him, and even the girl at his side—the one he placed on a pedestal—could not grasp his true self or the depth of his respect for her. A touch of bitterness colored his sorrow as he dwelled on his isolation.

I was at first touched by the expressions of his misery; yet, when I called to mind what Frankenstein had said of his powers of eloquence and persuasion, and when I again cast my eyes on the lifeless form of my friend, indignation was rekindled within me. “Wretch!” I said. “It is well that you come here to whine over the desolation that you have made. You throw a torch into a pile of buildings, and when they are consumed, you sit among the ruins and lament the fall. Hypocritical fiend! If he whom you mourn still lived, still would he be the object, again would he become the prey, of your accursed vengeance. It is not pity that you feel; you lament only because the victim of your malignity is withdrawn from your power.”

At first I was moved by the signs of his distress; yet when I remembered what Frankenstein had told me of his gift for eloquence and persuasion, and when I looked once more upon the lifeless body of my friend, my anger flared again. “Wretch!” I cried. “It is fitting that you should come here to whimper over the ruin you have made. You cast a torch into a heap of buildings, and when they are burned down, you sit among the wreckage and mourn their destruction. Hypocritical fiend! If the one you now lament were still alive, he would still be the target, still once more the victim, of your cursed vengeance. What you feel is not pity; you grieve only because the prey of your malice has been taken beyond your reach.”

“Stop!” he said imperiously, putting his hand upon Mrs. Yeobright’s arm. “We are regularly besieged. There are fifty of them out there if there’s one. You stay in this room with Thomasin; I’ll go out and face them. You must stay now, for my sake, till they are gone, so that it may seem as if all was right. Come, Tamsie dear, don’t go making a scene—we must marry after this; that you can see as well as I. Sit still, that’s all—and don’t speak much. I’ll manage them. Blundering fools!”

“Stop!” he said commandingly, laying his hand on Mrs. Yeobright’s arm. “We’re positively besieged. There must be fifty of them outside, if there’s one. You stay here with Thomasin; I’ll go out and face them. You must remain now, for my sake, until they’re gone, so that everything may appear right. Come, Tamsie, dear, don’t be making a scene—we’ve got to be married after this; you can see that as well as I can. Just sit still, that’s all—and don’t talk too much. I’ll deal with them. Blundering fools!”

Mr. Giles directed an appealing glance at the tinker; but he had suddenly fallen asleep. The women were out of the question.

As he spoke, Mr. Hale glanced over at Toby, but that soft-spoken young man, naturally modest to a fault, likely considered himself far too unimportant to be the intended recipient of the question, so he assumed the inquiry could not possibly be meant for him; in any case, he offered no reply whatsoever. Mr. Hale then turned an imploring look toward the itinerant tinker, but the man had suddenly fallen fast asleep. Asking any of the women in the room was entirely out of the question.

In the room from which the conversation proceeded Bathsheba and her servant-companion, Liddy Smallbury, were to be discovered sitting upon the floor, and sorting a complication of papers, books, bottles, and rubbish spread out thereon—remnants from the household stores of the late occupier. Liddy, the maltster’s great-granddaughter, was about Bathsheba’s equal in age, and her face was a prominent advertisement of the light-hearted English country girl. The beauty her features might have lacked in form was amply made up for by perfection of hue, which at this winter-time was the softened ruddiness on a surface of high rotundity that we meet with in a Terburg or a Gerard Douw; and, like the presentations of those great colourists, it was a face which kept well back from the boundary between comeliness and the ideal. Though elastic in nature she was less daring than Bathsheba, and occasionally showed some earnestness, which consisted half of genuine feeling, and half of mannerliness superadded by way of duty.

Inside the room where the voices originated, Bathsheba and her companion-servant, Liddy Smallbury, could be found seated on the floor. They were sorting through a chaotic jumble of documents, books, vials, and discarded items—the leftover belongings of the former resident. Liddy, the great-granddaughter of the maltster, was nearly the same age as Bathsheba, her face a striking portrait of a spirited English country girl. Any lack of refinement in her features was easily overlooked because of her magnificent complexion; in the winter air, her full, round face held a gentle flush similar to those seen in the works of Terburg or Gerard Douw. Like the figures in those old Dutch paintings, her beauty was grounded and pleasant, stopping well short of the sublime. Though she was resilient, she wasn't as adventurous as Bathsheba, and she occasionally exhibited a seriousness that was equal parts genuine sentiment and a dutiful sense of decorum.

He then went to the stable and coach-house to assure himself that the horse, gig, and harness were in a fit condition for a long drive. Nearly half an hour was spent thus, and on returning to the house Wildeve had no thought of Thomasin being anywhere but in bed. He had told the stable lad not to stay up, leading the boy to understand that his departure would be at three or four in the morning; for this, though an exceptional hour, was less strange than midnight, the time actually agreed on, the packet from Budmouth sailing between one and two.

He made his way next to the carriage-house and stalls, wanting to be certain the mare, the trap, and all the tack were ready for a lengthy journey. A full thirty minutes passed in this inspection, and when he came back into the house Wildeve never imagined Thomasin would be anywhere save her bedroom. He had instructed the groom not to remain awake, giving the lad the impression they would set out at three or four in the morning—an odd hour, to be sure, yet far less peculiar than midnight, which was the hour truly fixed, for the Budmouth vessel sailed between one and two.

Thus it had come to pass, that Tellson’s was the triumphant perfection of inconvenience. After bursting open a door of idiotic obstinacy with a weak rattle in its throat, you fell into Tellson’s down two steps, and came to your senses in a miserable little shop, with two little counters, where the oldest of men made your cheque shake as if the wind rustled it, while they examined the signature by the dingiest of windows, which were always under a shower-bath of mud from Fleet-street, and which were made the dingier by their own iron bars proper, and the heavy shadow of Temple Bar. If your business necessitated your seeing “the House,” you were put into a species of Condemned Hold at the back, where you meditated on a misspent life, until the House came with its hands in its pockets, and you could hardly blink at it in the dismal twilight. Your money came out of, or went into, wormy old wooden drawers, particles of which flew up your nose and down your throat when they were opened and shut. Your bank-notes had a musty odour, as if they were fast decomposing into rags again. Your plate was stowed away among the neighbouring cesspools, and evil communications corrupted its good polish in a day or two. Your deeds got into extemporised strong-rooms made of kitchens and sculleries, and fretted all the fat out of their parchments into the banking-house air. Your lighter boxes of family papers went up-stairs into a Barmecide room, that always had a great dining-table in it and never had a dinner, and where, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty, the first letters written to you by your old love, or by your little children, were but newly released from the horror of being ogled through the windows, by the heads exposed on Temple Bar with an insensate brutality and ferocity worthy of Abyssinia or Ashantee.

So it was that Tellson’s had reached a state of absolute, triumphant inconvenience. Once you had wrenched open a door that resisted with a stubborn, rattling gasp, you tumbled down two steps into the bank, finding yourself in a cramped and wretched shop. At two small counters, ancient clerks caused your check to flutter as if caught in a draft while they scrutinized your signature by the filthiest windows imaginable. These panes were constantly pelted by the mud of Fleet Street and further obscured by thick iron bars and the oppressive shadow of Temple Bar. Should your affairs require an audience with "the House," you were confined to a sort of death-row cell in the rear; there, you might ponder a life of regret until the management arrived, hands in pockets, barely discernible in the murky twilight. Your cash was pulled from or deposited into decaying wooden drawers, which released clouds of fine rot into your lungs every time they were handled. The banknotes emitted a stale, damp scent, as though they were rapidly reverting to the shredded rags from which they were born. Any silver you left for safekeeping was stored near the local sewers, where the foul vapors stripped away its luster within forty-eight hours. Your legal deeds were shoved into makeshift vaults that had once been kitchens, where the humid air seemed to draw the very oil from the parchment. Lighter crates of family records were carried to an upstairs room—a hollow dining hall containing a massive table that never saw a feast. There, even in 1780, the tenderest letters from lost loves or small children were kept in the grim shadow of the severed heads displayed on Temple Bar, a display of mindless ferocity that would have seemed barbaric in the most savage of lands.

“There’s one fellow—Stevens—a theosophist. Makes a pretty tangle when he gets going. Just now he’s dish-washer in a restaurant. Likes a good cigar. I’ve seen him eat in a ten-cent hash-house and pay fifty cents for the cigar he smoked afterward. I’ve got a couple in my pocket for him, if he shows up.”

There’s one fellow, Stevens, a theosophist, who rambles into the most tangled, convoluted tangents once he gets going on a topic. Just now he’s working as a dishwasher in a local restaurant, and he’s deeply partial to a high-quality cigar. I once saw him eat his fill at a grubby ten-cent hash house, then pay fifty cents for the cigar he lit up and smoked right after finishing his meal. I’ve got a couple of nice ones tucked in my pocket for him, if he happens to show up.

We have two observations from the aspects of model and evaluation. For models, there is a huge gap between all of the above models and human references. MSD is indeed challenging to conduct language modifications considering both knowledge and structures. Most of the time, these models basically output the original sentences without any modifications, or simply cut off the complex long sentence. Therefore, it is exciting to combine the techniques in TS, such as syntactic revisions including sentence splitting and lexical substitutions, with the techniques in ST: style and content disentanglement or the unsupervised idea of alleviating the lack of parallel training data. For evaluation, human checking is necessary in the current stage, even though SARI seems to offer a good start for automatic evaluation. Based on our observations, it is actually easy to fool the three ST metrics simultaneously via a trick: output sentences by adding style-related words before the original inputs. This is demonstrated by a variant of ControlledGen. We incorporate into the generator an extra knowledge encoder, which encodes the definition of concepts in each sentence (as mentioned in Section 3.1). Surprisingly, such a simple model achieves a very high style accuracy (over 90%) and good BLEU scores (around 20). But the model does not succeed in the style transfer task, and simply learns to add the word doctors into layman sentences while almost keeping the other words unchanged; and adding the word eg into the expertise sentences. Thus, it achieves good performance on all of the three ST measures, but makes little useful modifications.

metadata.title: Expertise Style Transfer: A New Task Towards Better Communication between Experts and Laymen

text: We draw two observations regarding models and evaluation. On the modeling side, there is a substantial gap between all of the above models and the human references. MSD is indeed challenging, because it requires making language modifications that involve both knowledge and structure. In most cases, these models simply reproduce the original sentence without change, or else merely truncate the complex long sentence. It is therefore promising to combine techniques from TS, such as syntactic revisions including sentence splitting and lexical substitution, with techniques from ST, such as style–content disentanglement or unsupervised methods that alleviate the scarcity of parallel training data. On the evaluation side, human assessment remains necessary at the current stage, even though SARI appears to provide a useful starting point for automatic evaluation. Our observations suggest that the three ST metrics can in fact be deceived fairly easily by a simple trick: generating sentences that add style-related words before the original input. This is illustrated by a variant of ControlledGen. We augment the generator with an additional knowledge encoder that encodes the definitions of the concepts in each sentence, as described in Section 3.1. Surprisingly, this simple model attains very high style accuracy (over 90%) and strong BLEU scores (around 20). However, it does not actually perform style transfer; instead, it merely learns to insert the word doctors into layman sentences while leaving almost all other words unchanged, and to insert eg into expertise sentences. As a result, it scores well on all three ST metrics while making very little meaningful modification.

"We have to put an end to the folly and vanity of kings, and to any people ruling any people but themselves. There is no convenience, there is no justice in any people ruling any people but themselves; the ruling of men by others, who have not their creeds and their languages and their ignorances and prejudices, that is the fundamental folly that has killed Teddy and Hugh--and these millions. To end that folly is as much our duty and business as telling the truth or earning a living...."

We must put a stop to the folly and empty vanity of kings, and do away with any system where one people rules over another instead of every community governing itself. There is no practical benefit, no shred of justice, in one group holding power over another that does not rule itself; when people are governed by outsiders who share none of their creeds, their languages, their unexamined ignorances and their particular prejudices, that is the foundational foolishness that killed Teddy and Hugh—and all those millions of other lives too. Ending that senseless wrong is as much our core duty and personal business as speaking the truth or earning a living....

When the waggon had passed on, Gabriel withdrew from his point of espial, and descending into the road, followed the vehicle to the turnpike-gate some way beyond the bottom of the hill, where the object of his contemplation now halted for the payment of toll. About twenty steps still remained between him and the gate, when he heard a dispute. It was a difference concerning twopence between the persons with the waggon and the man at the toll-bar.

Once the wagon had rolled onward, Gabriel pulled back from his hidden vantage point, climbed down to the road, and followed the vehicle to the turnpike gate a short way past the hill’s foot, where the figure he’d been intently observing had now stopped to pay toll. He was still roughly twenty paces from the gate when the sound of a dispute reached his ears. The disagreement, over a mere twopence, was between the people accompanying the wagon and the man manning the toll bar.

Not a single bleat. He went to the hedge; a gap had been broken through it, and in the gap were the footprints of the sheep. Rather surprised to find them break fence at this season, yet putting it down instantly to their great fondness for ivy in winter-time, of which a great deal grew in the plantation, he followed through the hedge. They were not in the plantation. He called again: the valleys and farthest hills resounded as when the sailors invoked the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; but no sheep. He passed through the trees and along the ridge of the hill. On the extreme summit, where the ends of the two converging hedges of which we have spoken were stopped short by meeting the brow of the chalk-pit, he saw the younger dog standing against the sky—dark and motionless as Napoleon at St. Helena.

There was not a single bleat to be heard. He walked over to the hedgerow, where a gap had been torn through the thick, tangled growth, and sheep hoof prints were clearly pressed into the soft earth at the opening. He was rather surprised that the flock would break through fencing at this time of year, but he instantly chalked the behavior up to their well-documented fondness for ivy in the cold winter months, and a great deal of the vine grew thick in the plantation just beyond the hedge. He clambered through the gap. The sheep were nowhere to be seen in the plantation. He called out for them again, his voice bouncing off the valley walls and the farthest hilltops, ringing bright as the cries of sailors invoking the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; still there was no sign of the flock, no answering bleat. He pushed through the tree line and tramped along the hill’s ridge. At the very summit, where the two converging hedges we have spoken of stop short where they meet the steep brow of the chalk pit, he spotted the younger dog silhouetted against the open sky—dark, utterly motionless, as stoic and still as Napoleon stranded on St. Helena.

"You were so good as to ask me, Mr. Britling, a little while ago, what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, my first impression of England that seems to me to matter in the least is this: that it looks and feels more like the traditional Old England than any one could possibly have believed, and that in reality it is less like the traditional Old England than any one would ever possibly have imagined."

A little while ago, Mr. Britling, you were kind enough to ask me what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, the only first impression of the country that feels even remotely worth mentioning is this: it looks and feels far more like that iconic traditional Old England than anyone could ever have believed, but in reality, it’s less like that long-held vision of Old England than anyone would possibly be able to imagine.

“Be happy, my dear Victor,” replied Elizabeth; “there is, I hope, nothing to distress you; and be assured that if a lively joy is not painted in my face, my heart is contented. Something whispers to me not to depend too much on the prospect that is opened before us, but I will not listen to such a sinister voice. Observe how fast we move along and how the clouds, which sometimes obscure and sometimes rise above the dome of Mont Blanc, render this scene of beauty still more interesting. Look also at the innumerable fish that are swimming in the clear waters, where we can distinguish every pebble that lies at the bottom. What a divine day! How happy and serene all nature appears!”

“Try to be at peace, my dearest Victor,” Elizabeth said. “I trust there is nothing left to trouble your mind. Please believe that even if my face doesn't radiate a visible joy, I am truly content in my heart. A small voice inside warns me not to place too much faith in the future we've envisioned, yet I refuse to listen to such dark premonitions. Notice how quickly we are traveling and how the clouds, drifting around the summit of Mont Blanc, make this beautiful landscape even more captivating. Watch the countless fish darting through the transparent water; it is so clear we can see every single stone on the lakebed. What a glorious day this is! The whole natural world seems so joyful and calm.”

In five or ten minutes there was another tap at the door. Liddy reappeared, and coming in a little way stood hesitating, until at length she said, “Maryann has just heard something very strange, but I know it isn’t true. And we shall be sure to know the rights of it in a day or two.”

Five or ten minutes later, another knock sounded at the door. Liddy returned, stepping a short distance into the room and pausing with uncertainty until she finally spoke. “Maryann has just heard a most peculiar rumor, though I am certain it is false. We will surely learn the truth of the matter in a day or two.”

“I stand aside, messieurs, by my heap of stones, to see the soldiers and their prisoner pass (for it is a solitary road, that, where any spectacle is well worth looking at), and at first, as they approach, I see no more than that they are six soldiers with a tall man bound, and that they are almost black to my sight--except on the side of the sun going to bed, where they have a red edge, messieurs. Also, I see that their long shadows are on the hollow ridge on the opposite side of the road, and are on the hill above it, and are like the shadows of giants. Also, I see that they are covered with dust, and that the dust moves with them as they come, tramp, tramp! But when they advance quite near to me, I recognise the tall man, and he recognises me. Ah, but he would be well content to precipitate himself over the hill-side once again, as on the evening when he and I first encountered, close to the same spot!”

I step aside, gentlemen, beside my pile of stones, to watch the soldiers and their captive go by (for this is a lonely road, where any sight is worth observing), and at first, as they draw near, I see only that they are six soldiers escorting a tall man in restraints, and they appear nearly black to my eyes—except along the edge where the sun is setting, where they glow with a crimson rim, gentlemen. I also notice their stretched shadows falling across the sunken ridge on the far side of the road, and climbing the slope beyond it, resembling the shadows of giants. I notice too that they are coated in dust, and the dust travels with them as they advance, tramp, tramp! But when they come quite close to me, I know the tall man, and he knows me. Ah, how gladly he would fling himself down the hillside once more, just as on that evening when he and I first met, near this very place!

As if endowed with the spirit of prophecy, Bathsheba gasped out, “No, it is not true; it cannot be true!” Then she said and heard no more. The ice of self-command which had latterly gathered over her was broken, and the currents burst forth again, and overwhelmed her. A darkness came into her eyes, and she fell.

As though seized by a flash of foresight, Bathsheba choked out, “No, it isn’t true; it can’t be true!” After that, she neither spoke nor perceived anything more. The frozen composure that had lately formed around her shattered, and a flood of emotion broke loose and swept her away. A blackness clouded her vision, and she dropped to the ground.

If any one had told him he was going to be stunned for five or six minutes, cut about the brow and face and have a bone in his wrist put out, and that as a consequence he would find himself pleased and exhilarated, he would have treated the prophecy with ridicule; but here he was lying stiffly on his back with his wrist bandaged to his side and smiling into the darkness even more brightly than he had smiled at the Essex landscape two days before. The fact is pain hurts or irritates, but in itself it does not make a healthily constituted man miserable. The expectation of pain, the certainty of injury may make one hopeless enough, the reality rouses our resistance. Nobody wants a broken bone or a delicate wrist, but very few people are very much depressed by getting one. People can be much more depressed by smoking a hundred cigarettes in three days or losing one per cent. of their capital.

Had someone prophesied that he would shortly be dazed for several minutes, slashed across the forehead and cheeks, suffer a dislocated wrist-bone, and emerge from the ordeal oddly content and buoyant, he would have scoffed at the forecast. Yet there he was, stretched rigidly upon his back, his injured wrist strapped tightly against his ribs, beaming into the blackness with a radiance keener than the smile he had bestowed upon the Essex countryside two mornings earlier. Pain, in truth, stings or vexes, but by itself it does not render a soundly built man wretched. The dread of agony, the certitude of harm may breed despair; the actual event summons our defiance. No one seeks a fractured bone or a fragile joint, yet scarcely anyone sinks into gloom upon acquiring one. A man can be far more dejected by smoking a hundred cigarettes over three days or by forfeiting one per cent of his fortune.

As the resting man looked at the barrow he became aware that its summit, hitherto the highest object in the whole prospect round, was surmounted by something higher. It rose from the semiglobular mound like a spike from a helmet. The first instinct of an imaginative stranger might have been to suppose it the person of one of the Celts who built the barrow, so far had all of modern date withdrawn from the scene. It seemed a sort of last man among them, musing for a moment before dropping into eternal night with the rest of his race.

Watching the ancient mound, the reclining man saw that its summit, formerly the highest mark on the horizon, was crowned by something taller. It projected from the rounded earth like a crest upon a casque. A dreamer might have mistaken the silhouette for one of the prehistoric builders, so far had the modern world retreated from the view. He seemed a final sentinel of his people, lingering a moment in thought before falling into the same eternal shadow as the rest of his vanished kin.

Christian pulled on his boots, and with heavy breathings, which could be heard to some distance, dragged his limbs together, arose, and tottered away out of sight. Wildeve set about shutting the lantern to return to the house, for he deemed it too late to go to Mistover to meet his wife, who was to be driven home in the captain’s four-wheel. While he was closing the little horn door a figure rose from behind a neighbouring bush and came forward into the lantern light. It was the reddleman approaching.

Christian dragged his boots over his feet, and with labored gasps that carried far across the stillness, hauled his frame upright, staggered upright, and stumbled away until the darkness swallowed him. Wildeve began fastening the lantern, intending to go back indoors, for he judged the hour too advanced to set out for Mistover and meet his wife, who was expected to return in the captain’s carriage. As he latched the small horn panel, a shape lifted itself from the shadow of a nearby thicket and stepped into the circle of light. The reddleman was drawing near.

They talked of Hugh for a time, but Mr. Britling was fitful and irritable and quite prepared to hold Mr. Direck accountable for the laxity of the War Office, the treachery of Bulgaria, the ambiguity of Roumania or any other barb that chanced to be sticking into his sensibilities. They lunched precariously. Then they went into the study to smoke.

For a period they spoke of Hugh, yet Mr. Britling remained restless and ill-tempered, seemingly ready to make Mr. Direck answer for the War Office’s failures, the betrayal by Bulgaria, the evasiveness of Romania, or any other frustration currently vexing his spirit. They shared an uneasy, hurried lunch. Afterward, they withdrew to the study to smoke.

“No, no, now—that’s too much!” said the timid man, becoming a man of brazen courage all of a sudden. “I didn’t say _sir_. I’ll take my oath I didn’t say ‘Joseph Poorgrass o’ Weatherbury, sir.’ No, no; what’s right is right, and I never said sir to the bird, knowing very well that no man of a gentleman’s rank would be hollering there at that time o’ night. ‘Joseph Poorgrass of Weatherbury,’—that’s every word I said, and I shouldn’t ha’ said that if ’t hadn’t been for Keeper Day’s metheglin.... There, ’twas a merciful thing it ended where it did.”

“No, no, now—that’s going too far!” cried the shrinking fellow, who had all at once grown as bold as brass. “I never said _sir_. I’ll swear I never called out, ‘Abel Mugford o’ Nethercombe, sir.’ No, no; fairness is fairness, and I never put the title on the bird, being well aware that no gentleman of standing would be bawling there at that hour o’ the night. ‘Abel Mugford of Nethercombe’—that was the whole of it, every syllable; and I’d never ha’ spoken so much, if it hadn’t been for old Gaffin’s barley-wine.... There now, it was a merciful thing the matter stopped where it did.”

“Then come with me,” said Mrs. Sowerberry: taking up a dim and dirty lamp, and leading the way upstairs; “your bed’s under the counter. You don’t mind sleeping among the coffins, I suppose? But it doesn’t much matter whether you do or don’t, for you can’t sleep anywhere else. Come; don’t keep me here all night!”

“Come along with me, then,” said Mrs. Sowerberry, picking up a dim, grimy lamp and leading the way up the stairs. “Your bed’s stowed under the counter. I suppose you don’t mind sleeping surrounded by coffins, do you? But it hardly makes a difference whether you mind or not, because there’s nowhere else for you to sleep. Hurry up now—don’t make me hang around here all night!”

“Sometimes,” she said, a little wearily, “Clementina tries me. I’m afraid she doesn’t practise enough, and I have to tell her the same things so often. And then she always dresses entirely in white, and that does get monotonous. But Gen. Pinkney is the dearest old man! I wish you could know him, Joe. He comes in sometimes when I am with Clementina at the piano—he is a widower, you know—and stands there pulling his white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and the demisemiquavers progressing?’ he always asks.

“Every now and then,” she said, with a soft note of weariness lacing her tone, “Clementina really tries my patience. I’m afraid she never puts in nearly enough practice, so I end up going over the exact same corrections with her time and time again. What’s more, she wears nothing but head-to-toe white, which does get dreadfully monotonous after a while. But General Pinkney is the dearest old soul! I so wish you could know him, Joe. He stops by sometimes when I’m sitting with Clementina at the piano—he’s a widower, you know—and stands there plucking at his neat white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and demisemiquavers coming along?’ he always asks.”

“Called up the Morses. Miss Morse answered the ’phone. And here I am.” He tugged at his coat pocket and flung a thin volume on the table. “There’s a book, by a poet. Read it and keep it.” And then, in reply to Martin’s protest: “What have I to do with books? I had another hemorrhage this morning. Got any whiskey? No, of course not. Wait a minute.”

“Rang the Halsteads earlier. Miss Halstead picked up the receiver, so now I’m here.” He fished in the depths of his overcoat pocket and tossed a slim volume onto the scuffed tabletop. “That’s a collection, by a little-known poet. Read through it, you can keep it.” Then, cutting off Elias’s fumbling protest: “What use would I have for books anyway? I had another bleeding spell this morning. You got any whiskey stashed somewhere? No, of course you don’t. Hang on a minute.”

I sat reflecting upon my evident cosmopolite and wondering how the poet had managed to miss him. He was my discovery and I believed in him. How was it? “The men that breed from them they traffic up and down, but cling to their cities’ hem as a child to the mother’s gown.”

I sat mulling over the unfeigned, far-roaming cosmopolitan I’d crossed paths with, wondering how generations of poets had never stumbled on his type to immortalize. He was my own unshared discovery, and I held full belief in his singular character. How had he slipped through the cracks? “The men born of that stock drift back and forth trading across borders, yet cling to the frayed edge of their home city’s skirt as a small child clutches at the fabric of its mother’s gown.”

“We workfolk shall have some lordly junketing to-night,” said Cainy Ball, casting forth his thoughts in a new direction. “This morning I see ’em making the great puddens in the milking-pails—lumps of fat as big as yer thumb, Mister Oak! I’ve never seed such splendid large knobs of fat before in the days of my life—they never used to be bigger then a horse-bean. And there was a great black crock upon the brandish with his legs a-sticking out, but I don’t know what was in within.”

“Us workfolk are in for a proper grand feast tonight,” said Cainy Ball, veering his thoughts to an entirely new topic. “I caught ’em mixing up great big puddings in the milking pails first thing this morning—lumps of fat as big as your thumb, Mister Oak! I’ve never laid eyes on such splendid large chunks of fat in all my born days; before now, they were never any bigger than a horse bean. And there was a great big black crock perched on the cooking stand with its legs sticking out, though I’ve no notion what was inside it.”

He stopped as if in doubt whether this waiting vehicle could have anything to do with the flight of his wife or not. The sight of Yeobright at once banished Wildeve’s sober feelings, who saw him again as the deadly rival from whom Eustacia was to be kept at all hazards. Hence Wildeve did not speak, in the hope that Clym would pass by without particular inquiry.

Silas hesitated, struck by the suspicion that this stationary coach might be the very instrument of his wife’s disappearance. The appearance of Silas instantly dispelled Julian’s calm, as he once again perceived the man as the mortal adversary from whom Elara must be sequestered by any means. Julian, therefore, maintained a guarded silence, praying that Silas would pass along the road without turning his attention toward the shadows.

Dogs are not generally apt to revenge injuries inflicted upon them by their masters; but Mr. Sikes’s dog, having faults of temper in common with his owner, and labouring, perhaps, at this moment, under a powerful sense of injury, made no more ado but at once fixed his teeth in one of the half-boots. Having given in a hearty shake, he retired, growling, under a form; just escaping the pewter measure which Mr. Sikes levelled at his head.

Dogs are not often disposed to avenge the wrongs done them by their masters; yet Mr. Vane’s hound, inheriting, as it were, the ill-humour of his keeper, and perhaps at that instant oppressed by a very lively consciousness of being ill-used, wasted no time in resentment, but at once clamped his teeth upon one of the worn half-boots. After administering to it a vigorous shake, he withdrew beneath a settle, still growling, and so narrowly escaped the pewter measure which Mr. Vane flung at his head.

He walked at the other’s heels with a swing to his shoulders, and his legs spread unwittingly, as if the level floors were tilting up and sinking down to the heave and lunge of the sea. The wide rooms seemed too narrow for his rolling gait, and to himself he was in terror lest his broad shoulders should collide with the doorways or sweep the bric-a-brac from the low mantel. He recoiled from side to side between the various objects and multiplied the hazards that in reality lodged only in his mind. Between a grand piano and a centre-table piled high with books was space for a half a dozen to walk abreast, yet he essayed it with trepidation. His heavy arms hung loosely at his sides. He did not know what to do with those arms and hands, and when, to his excited vision, one arm seemed liable to brush against the books on the table, he lurched away like a frightened horse, barely missing the piano stool. He watched the easy walk of the other in front of him, and for the first time realized that his walk was different from that of other men. He experienced a momentary pang of shame that he should walk so uncouthly. The sweat burst through the skin of his forehead in tiny beads, and he paused and mopped his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

He tramped at the other man’s heels, shoulders swinging loosely, his legs unconsciously splayed as if the flat floorboards were rolling and dipping with the ocean’s heave and surge. The spacious rooms felt far too cramped for his rolling gait, and he was privately terrified his broad shoulders would bash against doorframes or send the bric-a-brac clattering off the low mantelpiece. He flinched left and right around assorted furnishings, inflating risks that existed entirely in his own head. The gap between a grand piano and a central table stacked high with books was wide enough for half a dozen people to walk side by side, yet he edged through it with skittish dread. His heavy arms hung limp at his sides; he had no idea what to do with those gangly limbs, and when his overwrought gaze spotted his arm brushing perilously close to the volumes stacked on the table, he lurched away like a spooked horse, barely clearing the piano stool. He watched the easy, unselfconscious stride of the man ahead, and for the first time it hit him that his walk was nothing like other men’s. A sharp, fleeting stab of shame pricked him at how uncouth his gait was. Tiny beads of sweat burst across his forehead, and he stopped short, dabbing at his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

Last, we conduct a correlation study between the translation difficulties as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of and . Table 2 lists Pearson's and Spearman's correlation coefficients for data-dependent metrics, where bold values indicate statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction (p < 0.0029). Interestingly, the only features that significantly correlate with our metric are related to the type-to-token ratio (TTR) for the source language and the distance between source and target TTRs. This implies that a potential explanation for the differences in translation difficulty lies in lexical variation. For full correlation results, refer to App. D.

metadata.title: It's Easier to Translate out of English than into it: Measuring Neural Translation Difficulty by Cross-Mutual Information
metadata.section: Correlations with linguistic and data features

text: Finally, we conduct a correlation analysis examining the relationship between translation difficulty as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of  and . Table 2 reports Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients for all data-dependent metrics, with bolded values indicating statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (adjusted threshold p < 0.0029). Notably, the only features that exhibit a significant correlation with our difficulty metric are source language type-to-token ratio (TTR) and the pairwise distance between source and target language TTR values. This finding implies that lexical variation offers a potential explanatory mechanism for observed cross-task differences in translation difficulty. Full correlation results are provided in Appendix D.

About noon next day, when the Dodger and Master Bates had gone out to pursue their customary avocations, Mr. Fagin took the opportunity of reading Oliver a long lecture on the crying sin of ingratitude; of which he clearly demonstrated he had been guilty, to no ordinary extent, in wilfully absenting himself from the society of his anxious friends; and, still more, in endeavouring to escape from them after so much trouble and expense had been incurred in his recovery. Mr. Fagin laid great stress on the fact of his having taken Oliver in, and cherished him, when, without his timely aid, he might have perished with hunger; and he related the dismal and affecting history of a young lad whom, in his philanthropy, he had succoured under parallel circumstances, but who, proving unworthy of his confidence and evincing a desire to communicate with the police, had unfortunately come to be hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Mr. Fagin did not seek to conceal his share in the catastrophe, but lamented with tears in his eyes that the wrong-headed and treacherous behaviour of the young person in question, had rendered it necessary that he should become the victim of certain evidence for the crown: which, if it were not precisely true, was indispensably necessary for the safety of him (Mr. Fagin) and a few select friends. Mr. Fagin concluded by drawing a rather disagreeable picture of the discomforts of hanging; and, with great friendliness and politeness of manner, expressed his anxious hopes that he might never be obliged to submit Oliver Twist to that unpleasant operation.

Around noon the following day, once the Dodger and Master Bates had departed to attend to their usual trades, Mr. Fagin seized the chance to deliver Oliver a lengthy sermon on the grievous vice of ingratitude. He made it abundantly plain that the boy had been guilty of no small measure of it—first by deliberately withdrawing from the company of his solicitous friends, and worse, by attempting to flee them after they had gone to such pains and expense to reclaim him. Fagin placed particular emphasis on the fact that he had taken Oliver in and cared for him when, without that timely intervention, the child might well have starved; and he recounted the pitiful, moving tale of another young lad whom, out of pure benevolence, he had aided in nearly identical straits, but who—proving unworthy of his trust and showing a regrettable inclination to parley with the police—had unhappily ended up hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Fagin made no effort to disguise his own part in that catastrophe, yet lamented, with tears standing in his eyes, that the perverse and treacherous conduct of the youth in question had forced the necessity of his becoming the subject of certain Crown evidence—which, if not strictly accurate, was absolutely essential to the security of himself (Mr. Fagin) and a small circle of select companions. He closed by painting a rather disagreeable portrait of the unpleasantness of hanging, and with great cordiality and courteousness of manner expressed his fervent hope that he might never be obliged to subject Oliver Twist to that disagreeable procedure.
