Our final opportunity has vanished, and it was a slim one at best. Yes, it is better that he be brought to her now. But before you depart, I need you to give me your full attention for a moment. Do not question the conditions I am about to set or the pledge I am going to demand; I have my reasons, and they are sound ones.

Dulcie went up to her room—the third floor back in a West Side brownstone-front. She lit the gas. Scientists tell us that the diamond is the hardest substance known. Their mistake. Landladies know of a compound beside which the diamond is as putty. They pack it in the tips of gas-burners; and one may stand on a chair and dig at it in vain until one’s fingers are pink and bruised. A hairpin will not remove it; therefore let us call it immovable.

Dulcie climbed the stairs to her room, the rear unit on the third floor of a West Side brownstone. She turned on the gas. Any scientist will tell you diamond is the hardest substance known to humankind. They’re wrong. Landladies are familiar with a compound next to which diamond is as soft as putty. They pack this stuff right into the tips of gas burners; you can stand on a chair and dig at it fruitlessly until your fingers are pink and bruised. Not even a hairpin can pry it loose, so we might as well call it entirely immovable.

“I have not fallen in love with you, Mr. Boldwood—certainly I must say that.” She allowed a very small smile to creep for the first time over her serious face in saying this, and the white row of upper teeth, and keenly-cut lips already noticed, suggested an idea of heartlessness, which was immediately contradicted by the pleasant eyes.

“I haven’t fallen for you, Mr. Boldwood—I have to make that perfectly clear.” As she spoke, a tiny smile tugged across her usually solemn face for the very first time. The flash of her even white upper teeth, paired with the sharply defined lips everyone had already taken note of, gave a fleeting impression of cold heartlessness, an impression that was immediately erased by the warm, pleasant glow of her eyes.

“It was not enchanted aforetime,” she said in a musing fashion, as if to herself. “And how strange is this marvel, and how awful --that to the one perception it is enchanted and dight in a base and shameful aspect; yet to the perception of the other it is not enchanted, hath suffered no change, but stands firm and stately still, girt with its moat and waving its banners in the blue air from its towers. And God shield us, how it pricks the heart to see again these gracious captives, and the sorrow deepened in their sweet faces! We have tarried along, and are to blame.”

“It bore no spell in former days,” she murmured, half lost in reverie, as though speaking to her own soul. “And what a strange and fearsome wonder this is—that to one beholder it appears bewitched and cloaked in a mean and shameful guise; while to the other’s sight it remains unaltered, steadfast and proud, encircled by its waters and flying its pennons from the towers against the high, blue sky. And Heaven preserve us, how it wounds the spirit to look once more upon these gentle prisoners, with grief etched deeper in their tender faces! We have lingered too long, and the fault is ours.”

It tickled Marco to the marrow to hear about such an odd character; but it also prepared him for accidents; and in my experience when you travel with a king who is letting on to be something else and can't remember it more than about half the time, you can't take too many precautions.

It amused Silas to his very soul to learn of so peculiar a figure; nevertheless, the news also served to arm him against the coming calamities. In my own experience, when one wanders the roads with a monarch who is feigning a lowlier state—and who manages to recall the deception only half the time—no degree of caution can ever be considered excessive.

The performer seemed quite at home anywhere between a horse’s head and its tail, and the necessity for this abnormal attitude having ceased with the passage of the plantation, she began to adopt another, even more obviously convenient than the first. She had no side-saddle, and it was very apparent that a firm seat upon the smooth leather beneath her was unattainable sideways. Springing to her accustomed perpendicular like a bowed sapling, and satisfying herself that nobody was in sight, she seated herself in the manner demanded by the saddle, though hardly expected of the woman, and trotted off in the direction of Tewnell Mill.

The rider appeared perfectly at ease on any part of her horse’s back. Once the plantation was behind her and there was no longer a need for her strange posture, she transitioned into a much more sensible stance. She lacked a side-saddle, and it was obvious that she could never maintain a secure grip on the polished leather by sitting sideways. Straightening up like a resilient young tree and ensuring she was unobserved, she took her seat astride the animal as the saddle required, despite the social impropriety, and trotted away toward Tewnell Mill.

For there can be no denying that Mr. Britling was, in a sense, distinguished. The hero and subject of this novel was at its very beginning a distinguished man. He was in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. In the last few years he had grown with some rapidity into a writer recognised and welcomed by the more cultivated sections of the American public, and even known to a select circle of British readers. To his American discoverers he had first appeared as an essayist, a serious essayist who wrote about aesthetics and Oriental thought and national character and poets and painting. He had come through America some years ago as one of those Kahn scholars, those promising writers and intelligent men endowed by Auguste Kahn of Paris, who go about the world nowadays in comfort and consideration as the travelling guests of that original philanthropist--to acquire the international spirit. Previously he had been a critic of art and literature and a writer of thoughtful third leaders in the London _Times_. He had begun with a Pembroke fellowship and a prize poem. He had returned from his world tour to his reflective yet original corner of _The Times_ and to the production of books about national relationships and social psychology, that had brought him rapidly into prominence.

There is no question that Mr. Britling possessed a certain distinction. At the outset of this story, the man who stands at its center was already a figure of note. His name appeared in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. Over the preceding few years, he had swiftly emerged as an author recognized and embraced by the more discerning American readership, and even known among a small, selective audience in Britain. To those who first discovered him across the Atlantic, he had presented himself as a serious essayist, one who explored aesthetics, Eastern philosophy, national identity, poets, and the visual arts. Years before, he had traveled through America as a Kahn fellow—one of those gifted writers and thinkers funded by the Parisian philanthropist Auguste Kahn, who sends such promising individuals around the globe in comfort and esteem to cultivate an international outlook. Earlier still, he had served as a critic of art and literature and composed reflective op-ed pieces for the London _Times_. His career had begun with a fellowship at Pembroke and a prize-winning poem. After returning from his global journey, he settled back into his contemplative yet distinctive niche at _The Times_ and began producing books on national relations and social psychology, which quickly propelled him into the limelight.

Notwithstanding the evidently useless nature of their search, they did not desist until the coming on of night rendered its further prosecution hopeless; and even then, they gave it up with reluctance. Giles was dispatched to the different ale-houses in the village, furnished with the best description Oliver could give of the appearance and dress of the strangers. Of these, the Jew was, at all events, sufficiently remarkable to be remembered, supposing he had been seen drinking, or loitering about; but Giles returned without any intelligence, calculated to dispel or lessen the mystery.

Even as it grew clearer their hunt was going nowhere, they refused to call it off until dusk draped the lanes so thick that pushing on would have been utterly fruitless; even at that point, abandoning the search felt like a defeat they resented deeply. Tompkins was dispatched to every tavern and public house scattered through the hamlet, armed with the most detailed account Luka could muster of the unknown men’s features and clothing. Of the group, the haggard, silver-bearded ringleader was distinct enough that anyone who’d spotted him lingering over a pint or loitering on the tavern thresholds would have surely recalled him; yet Tompkins returned with not a single scrap of intelligence that might cut through or even soften the edges of the unyielding mystery.

“His rooms we’re going to. One time professor—fired from university—usual story. A mind like a steel trap. Makes his living any old way. I know he’s been a street fakir when he was down. Unscrupulous. Rob a corpse of a shroud—anything. Difference between him and the bourgeoisie is that he robs without illusion. He’ll talk Nietzsche, or Schopenhauer, or Kant, or anything, but the only thing in this world, not excepting Mary, that he really cares for, is his monism. Haeckel is his little tin god. The only way to insult him is to take a slap at Haeckel.”

We’re heading to his place right now. He used to be a professor, got canned from the university, same old run-of-the-mill story. He’s got a mind as sharp as a steel trap, never lets a single detail slip past him. He scrapes by doing whatever odd work he can scare up, and I know for a fact he worked as a cheap street con artist when he hit rock bottom. The man’s completely unscrupulous—would steal the burial shroud right off a corpse if it suited him, no questions asked, no guilt whatsoever. The only real difference between him and your typical bourgeois type is that he doesn’t hide behind self-serving illusions when he takes what he wants. He’ll hold forth for hours about Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Kant, any philosopher you bring up, but the only thing in the entire world he actually cares about—not even Mary is an exception—is his monist philosophy. Haeckel is his personal little tin god, the one figure he reveres without question. The only surefire way to insult him is to talk trash about Haeckel.

Letty came through the little wood and over the stile that brought her into sight of the cottage. The windows of the cottage as she saw it under the bough of the big walnut tree, were afire from the sun. The crimson rambler over the porch that she and Teddy had planted was still bearing roses. The door was open and people were moving in the porch.

Letty tramped through the small patch of woods and clambered over the stile, and the cottage popped immediately into her line of sight. Tucked under the thick, overhanging bough of the massive walnut tree, the cottage windows blazed bright, lit up golden by the full force of the sun. The crimson rambler twining over the front porch—the one she and Teddy had planted together—was still heavy with open, blushing roses. The door stood wide open, and she could see people moving to and fro just inside the entryway.

“An’ next week we got to do it all over again,” he said sadly. “An’ what’s the good of it all, hey? Sometimes I wish I was a hobo. They don’t work, an’ they get their livin’. Gee! I wish I had a glass of beer; but I can’t get up the gumption to go down to the village an’ get it. You’ll stay over, an’ send your books down by express, or else you’re a damn fool.”

“And next week we’ll have to go through the whole grind once more,” he said with a mournful note. “What’s the point of any of it, eh? There are moments I wish I were a tramp. They don’t lift a finger and still scrape by. Lord, I’d give anything for a glass of beer, but I just can’t muster the will to walk down to the village and fetch one. You’ll spend the night, and ship your books later by express—unless you’re a damned fool.”

I closed not my eyes that night. My internal being was in a state of insurrection and turmoil; I felt that order would thence arise, but I had no power to produce it. By degrees, after the morning’s dawn, sleep came. I awoke, and my yesternight’s thoughts were as a dream. There only remained a resolution to return to my ancient studies and to devote myself to a science for which I believed myself to possess a natural talent. On the same day I paid M. Waldman a visit. His manners in private were even more mild and attractive than in public, for there was a certain dignity in his mien during his lecture which in his own house was replaced by the greatest affability and kindness. I gave him pretty nearly the same account of my former pursuits as I had given to his fellow professor. He heard with attention the little narration concerning my studies and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt that M. Krempe had exhibited. He said that “These were men to whose indefatigable zeal modern philosophers were indebted for most of the foundations of their knowledge. They had left to us, as an easier task, to give new names and arrange in connected classifications the facts which they in a great degree had been the instruments of bringing to light. The labours of men of genius, however erroneously directed, scarcely ever fail in ultimately turning to the solid advantage of mankind.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any presumption or affectation, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists; I expressed myself in measured terms, with the modesty and deference due from a youth to his instructor, without letting escape (inexperience in life would have made me ashamed) any of the enthusiasm which stimulated my intended labours. I requested his advice concerning the books I ought to procure.

I didn’t sleep a wink all that night. My whole mind was roiling with chaos and unrest, even as I sensed some kind of clarity would eventually settle out of it—yet I had no power to force that order to come. Slowly, once dawn had broken across the sky, I finally drifted off. When I woke, all the frantic, tangled thoughts I’d had the night before felt like a faded, half-remembered dream. All that remained was a firm resolution to return to my long-held course of study, and devote myself fully to the science I’d always believed I had a natural talent for.

I went to visit Professor Waldman that very same day. He was far gentler and more charming in private than he was in public; the quiet, stately dignity he carried during his lectures softened entirely in his own home, replaced by open, warm affability and kindness. I told him almost the exact same account of my past pursuits that I’d shared with his fellow professor. He listened closely as I walked him through my earlier studies, and smiled when I mentioned the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, with none of the sneering contempt Professor Krempe had shown at those very same names.

“These were men whose relentless, unflagging zeal laid most of the foundation for the knowledge modern scientists build their work on,” he said. “They did the hard work of bringing so many key facts to light; our far simpler task is to give those facts new, standardized names and sort them into connected, logical systems. Even the work of brilliant men, no matter how misdirected it might seem at the time, almost always ends up serving the lasting good of humanity in the end.”

I listened to his words, which he delivered without any trace of arrogance or performative pretension, then told him his lecture had erased all the prejudices I’d held against modern chemists. I spoke in careful, measured terms, with the modesty and deference a young student owes their instructor, careful not to let any of the fiery enthusiasm spurring my planned work slip out; my inexperience with the world would have left me mortified to show that level of unguarded eagerness. I ended by asking him for advice on which books I ought to purchase for my studies.

Oak seized the cut ends of the sheaves, as if he were going to engage in the operation of “reed-drawing,” and digging in his feet, and occasionally sticking in the stem of his sheep-crook, he clambered up the beetling face. He at once sat astride the very apex, and began with his crook to beat off the fiery fragments which had lodged thereon, shouting to the others to get him a bough and a ladder, and some water.

Thorne gripped the blunt ends of the bundles as if preparing for a turn at straw-dressing; then, bracing his heels and jamming his crook into the gaps, he hauled himself up the overhanging wall. He quickly straddled the topmost ridge and began to strike at the smoldering embers with his staff, hollering to the men below for a leafy bough, a ladder, and a dousing of water.

In the morning a regiment of cavalry had left the town, and a sergeant and his party had been beating up for recruits through the four streets. As the end of the day drew on, and he found himself not hired, Gabriel almost wished that he had joined them, and gone off to serve his country. Weary of standing in the market-place, and not much minding the kind of work he turned his hand to, he decided to offer himself in some other capacity than that of bailiff.

By morning a troop of horse had ridden out of the town, and a sergeant with his men had gone searching the four streets for recruits. Toward evening, as the day wore down, Gabriel, still without a place, came near to wishing he had gone with them and taken the King’s shilling. Tired of lingering in the market square, and caring little what sort of labor he might set himself to, he resolved to present himself in some other role than that of a bailiff.

Mrs. Yeobright’s exertions, physical and emotional, had well-nigh prostrated her; but she continued to creep along in short stages with long breaks between. The sun had now got far to the west of south and stood directly in her face, like some merciless incendiary, brand in hand, waiting to consume her. With the departure of the boy all visible animation disappeared from the landscape, though the intermittent husky notes of the male grasshoppers from every tuft of furze were enough to show that amid the prostration of the larger animal species an unseen insect world was busy in all the fullness of life.

Mrs. Yeobright was nearly felled by the toll of her physical efforts and raw emotional strain, yet she pressed on, shuffling forward in tiny bursts, pausing for long stretches between each small advance. The sun had drifted far southwest by now, hanging directly in her line of sight like a ruthless arsonist, a flaming brand clutched in one hand, poised to burn her to ash. When the boy had vanished, every last glimmer of visible life drained from the surrounding landscape, though the rough, stuttering chirps of male grasshoppers rising from every furze tuft made clear that even as all larger creatures lay listless and drained, a hidden world of insects hummed with unflagging, full-bodied vitality.

As I purpose to show in the sequel whether the white waistcoated gentleman was right or not, I should perhaps mar the interest of this narrative (supposing it to possess any at all), if I ventured to hint just yet, whether the life of Oliver Twist had this violent termination or no.

Whether the gentleman in the white waistcoat was correct is something I mean to reveal later in this account, and so—should this story hold even a scrap of interest—I would likely spoil it if I dared suggest, at this early point, whether Oliver Twist’s life came to such a brutal end or not.

It seemed as though Mr. Direck would be unable to write any letters until his wrist had mended. Teddy tried him with a typewriter, but Mr. Direck was very awkward with his left hand, and then Mr. Britling suddenly remembered a little peculiarity he had which it was possible that Mr. Direck might share unconsciously, and that was his gift of looking-glass writing with his left hand. Mr. Britling had found out quite by chance in his schoolboy days that while his right hand had been laboriously learning to write, his left hand, all unsuspected, had been picking up the same lesson, and that by taking a pencil in his left hand and writing from right to left, without watching what he was writing, and then examining the scrawl in a mirror, he could reproduce his own handwriting in exact reverse. About three people out of five have this often quite unsuspected ability. He demonstrated his gift, and then Miss Cecily Corner, who had dropped in in a casual sort of way to ask about Mr. Direck, tried it, and then Mr. Direck tried it. And they could all do it. And then Teddy brought a sheet of copying carbon, and so Mr. Direck, by using the carbon reversed under his paper, was restored to the world of correspondence again.

It looked as if Mr. Direck wouldn’t be able to write any letters until his wrist had fully recovered. Teddy attempted to get him started on a typewriter, but Mr. Direck’s left hand proved far too clumsy. Suddenly, Mr. Britling recalled a strange quirk he possessed, one that Mr. Direck might also have without realizing it: the knack for mirror writing with his left hand. Back in his school days, Mr. Britling had stumbled upon the fact that while his right hand was painstakingly learning to write, his left hand had been secretly absorbing the same lessons. He found that by moving a pencil from right to left with his left hand, without focusing too hard on the page, he could create a perfect reverse of his usual script. Apparently, about three out of five people possess this hidden ability. After he demonstrated the trick, Miss Cecily Corner—who had stopped by casually to check on Mr. Direck—gave it a try, and then Mr. Direck followed suit. They all succeeded. Teddy then fetched a sheet of carbon paper, and by placing it backwards under the stationery, Mr. Direck was finally able to rejoin the world of correspondence.

Ikey Schoenstein was the night clerk of the Blue Light and the friend of his customers. Thus it is on the East Side, where the heart of pharmacy is not glacé. There, as it should be, the druggist is a counsellor, a confessor, an adviser, an able and willing missionary and mentor whose learning is respected, whose occult wisdom is venerated and whose medicine is often poured, untasted, into the gutter. Therefore Ikey’s corniform, be-spectacled nose and narrow, knowledge-bowed figure was well known in the vicinity of the Blue Light, and his advice and notice were much desired.

Ikey Schoenstein served as the night clerk at the Blue Light pharmacy and was a confidant to his patrons. This was the way of the East Side, where the drugstore’s heart hasn’t frozen over. There, as it ought to be, the pharmacist acts as counselor, confessor, guide—a devoted and capable missionary and sage whose learning earns respect, whose mysterious knowledge is revered, and whose remedies are often tipped, unsipped, into the gutter. So Ikey’s horn-rimmed, spectacled nose and his slender, studiously stooped frame were familiar sights around the Blue Light, and both his counsel and his attention were eagerly sought.

The ultimate and saddest singularity of woman’s effort and invention was reached when, with a quickened breathing, she rose to a stooping posture, and, resting her two little arms upon the shoulders of the dog, leant firmly thereon, and murmured stimulating words. Whilst she sorrowed in her heart she cheered with her voice, and what was stranger than that the strong should need encouragement from the weak was that cheerfulness should be so well stimulated by such utter dejection. Her friend moved forward slowly, and she with small mincing steps moved forward beside him, half her weight being thrown upon the animal. Sometimes she sank as she had sunk from walking erect, from the crutches, from the rails. The dog, who now thoroughly understood her desire and her incapacity, was frantic in his distress on these occasions; he would tug at her dress and run forward. She always called him back, and it was now to be observed that the woman listened for human sounds only to avoid them. It was evident that she had an object in keeping her presence on the road and her forlorn state unknown.

The most grievous, singular apex of Elara’s desperate ingenuity arrived when, chest rising and falling fast, she pushed herself up into a hunched stance, rested her two slight forearms on the hound’s broad shoulders, leaned her weight steadily against him, and murmured soft, urging words under her breath. Though her chest ached with unspoken grief, her tone stayed bright and buoyant, and there was no stranger paradox than that a creature so sturdy should draw courage from one so frail, or that warm hope could be kindled by a spirit so crushed by despair. Her companion paced forward at a crawl, and she picked her way beside him in tiny, shuffling steps, half her mass bearing down on his broad frame. Every now and then her knees buckled, just as they had when she’d tried walking unassisted, when she’d relied on crutches, when she’d clung to roadside fence posts for support. The hound, who now fully grasped what she needed and how little she could manage on her own, grew nearly frantic with worry when she stumbled; he would yank at the hem of her skirt and dart a few paces ahead, as if trying to pull her onward. She always called him back to her side, and anyone watching might have noticed she now strained to catch the sound of other people only to veer out of their way. It was clear she was determined to keep her presence on the lonely lane and her wretched condition hidden from all passersby.

Question Candidate Generation. We fine-tune a GPT2 language model (Radford et al., 2019) on the task of question generation using the SQuAD 2.0 dataset (Rajpurkar et al., 2018) . At training, the model reads a paragraph from the training set, and learns to generate a question associated with the paragraph. For each paragraph in each article of the story (the paragraph set), we use beam search to generate K candidate questions. In our experience, using a large beam size (K=20) is important, as one paragraph can yield several valid questions. Beam search enforces exploration, with the first step of beam search often containing several interrogative words (what, where...).

metadata.title: What's The Latest? A Question-driven News Chatbot

text: We fine-tune a GPT2 language model (Radford et al., 2019) for question generation on the SQuAD 2.0 dataset (Rajpurkar et al., 2018). During training, the model ingests a paragraph from the training set and learns to produce a corresponding question. For every paragraph in each article of the story (the paragraph set), we apply beam search to generate K candidate questions. In our experience, employing a large beam size (K=20) proves essential, since a single paragraph can support multiple valid questions. Beam search promotes exploration, and its initial step frequently yields diverse interrogative words (what, where…).

The horse was seen descending the hill, and the wearisome series had to be repeated in reverse order: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had had presence of mind enough to give the mare up to Gabriel, and return himself on foot. The rider neared them. It was Tall.

The horse was now visible as it came down the hill, and the tedious procession had to be gone through again in reverse: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had been sensible enough to leave the mare with Gabriel and come back on foot himself. The rider drew closer. It was Tall.

Mr. Oak carried about him, by way of watch, what may be called a small silver clock; in other words, it was a watch as to shape and intention, and a small clock as to size. This instrument being several years older than Oak’s grandfather, had the peculiarity of going either too fast or not at all. The smaller of its hands, too, occasionally slipped round on the pivot, and thus, though the minutes were told with precision, nobody could be quite certain of the hour they belonged to. The stopping peculiarity of his watch Oak remedied by thumps and shakes, and he escaped any evil consequences from the other two defects by constant comparisons with and observations of the sun and stars, and by pressing his face close to the glass of his neighbours’ windows, till he could discern the hour marked by the green-faced timekeepers within. It may be mentioned that Oak’s fob being difficult of access, by reason of its somewhat high situation in the waistband of his trousers (which also lay at a remote height under his waistcoat), the watch was as a necessity pulled out by throwing the body to one side, compressing the mouth and face to a mere mass of ruddy flesh on account of the exertion required, and drawing up the watch by its chain, like a bucket from a well.

Mr. Vale wore, for a watch, what might more properly have been called a little silver clock; that is to say, it was a watch in shape and purpose, and a clock only in its smallness. This piece of mechanism was older than his grandfather by several years, and possessed the singular habit of either running far too quickly or else refusing to run at all. The lesser hand, moreover, had a trick of sometimes slipping upon its pivot; so that while the minutes were shown with exactness, no one could be altogether sure to what hour they belonged. Vale cured the stopping by means of knocks and jolts, and escaped the inconvenience of the other two imperfections by perpetual reference to the sun and stars, together with such observations as he could make by pressing his face close against his neighbours’ panes, until he could distinguish the hour displayed in the green faces of the clocks within. It should be added that, his fob being hard of access by reason of its rather lofty position in the waistband of his trousers, which itself lay at a yet more remote elevation beneath his waistcoat, the watch had to be produced, of necessity, by throwing the body over to one side, compressing mouth and countenance into a mere crimson lump under the strain, and hauling the watch up by its chain as one might draw a bucket from a well.

And here was the man Spencer, organizing all knowledge for him, reducing everything to unity, elaborating ultimate realities, and presenting to his startled gaze a universe so concrete of realization that it was like the model of a ship such as sailors make and put into glass bottles. There was no caprice, no chance. All was law. It was in obedience to law that the bird flew, and it was in obedience to the same law that fermenting slime had writhed and squirmed and put out legs and wings and become a bird.

Spencer stood as the architect of all understanding, weaving every fragment of knowledge into a singular, cohesive system. He defined the fundamental nature of reality, revealing a cosmos so tangible and structured that it resembled those intricate model ships that sailors carefully assemble inside glass bottles. There was no room for randomness or caprice; everything was governed by law. It was these immutable rules that allowed a bird to take flight, and the very same principles that had once driven primordial ooze to churn and evolve, eventually sprouting the limbs and feathers required to transform into a creature of the air.

“The grand aristocrats,” Harry answered, “by which term I gather you mean my exceedingly dignified uncle, have sent me no word at all since I arrived; and at this season, it’s hardly probable that any matter would arise requiring my prompt presence in their company.”

"Mr. Sterling," she breathed, her composure shattered, "how long have you been watching me?"

“Indeed, we shall hear it again,” the boy sighed. “That same old, tiresome story he has told a thousand times in exactly the same words, and will go on telling until the day he dies, every time he gets his barrel filled and feels his exaggeration machine set to work. God grant I had died, or that I had never lived to see this day!”

In order to speed up the annotation process of our data, we decided to create a pre-annotation morphosyntactic and syntactic annotator trained on quasi-synthetic data obtained by "transliterating" a pre-existing Arabic (MSA) treebank, the Prague Arabic Dependency Treebank (PADT), into the NArabizi Latin script, together with data from the French GSD UD treebank. Both are taken from the UD treebank collection (Nivre et al., 2018). Before it can be used as training data, the PADT needs to first be transformed into a form similar to NArabizi. Since the PADT corpus is a collection of MSA sentences with no diacritics, it is impossible to directly "transliterate" into NArabizi. We first diacritized it, in order to add short-vowel information, and then "translitterated" it into an Arabizi-like corpus. We describe this process in this Section. The results of the pseudo-NArabizi parser trained on the "translitterated" corpus are then presented in Section 6.2.

metadata.title: Building a User-Generated Content North-African Arabizi Treebank: Tackling Hell
metadata.section: Pre-annotation Tool Development via Noisy Transliteration of an Arabic UD Treebank

text: To accelerate the annotation of our data, we developed a morphosyntactic and syntactic pre-annotation model trained on quasi-synthetic data. These data were produced by transliterating an existing Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) treebank—the Prague Arabic Dependency Treebank (PADT)—into the NArabizi Latin script, and combining it with data from the French GSD Universal Dependencies treebank. Both resources are drawn from the UD treebank collection (Nivre et al., 2018). Before the PADT can serve as training data, it must first be reshaped into a form resembling NArabizi. Because the PADT corpus consists of undiacritized MSA sentences, direct transliteration into NArabizi is not feasible. We therefore diacritized the corpus to restore short-vowel information, and subsequently transliterated it into an Arabizi-like corpus. This process is detailed in the present section. The performance of the pseudo-NArabizi parser trained on the transliterated corpus is then reported in Section 6.2.

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, stopping pretty often at ale-houses by the way to drink, but evincing a tendency to keep his own counsel, and to keep his hat cocked over his eyes. He had eyes that assorted very well with that decoration, being of a surface black, with no depth in the colour or form, and much too near together--as if they were afraid of being found out in something, singly, if they kept too far apart. They had a sinister expression, under an old cocked-hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and over a great muffler for the chin and throat, which descended nearly to the wearer’s knees. When he stopped for drink, he moved this muffler with his left hand, only while he poured his liquor in with his right; as soon as that was done, he muffled again.

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, pausing often at alehouses along the way to drink, yet seeming determined to keep his own counsel and to wear his hat pulled low over his eyes. Those eyes suited that fashion well: black on the surface, with no depth of color or expression, and set much too close together, as if they feared being detected in some misdeed if they kept too far apart. They gave him a sinister look beneath an old cocked hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and above a huge muffler for his chin and throat that fell almost to his knees. Whenever he stopped to drink, he lifted the muffler with his left hand only long enough to pour the liquor in with his right; once he had done so, he drew it up again.

“I’m Mister Noah Claypole,” said the charity-boy, “and you’re under me. Take down the shutters, yer idle young ruffian!” With this, Mr. Claypole administered a kick to Oliver, and entered the shop with a dignified air, which did him great credit. It is difficult for a large-headed, small-eyed youth, of lumbering make and heavy countenance, to look dignified under any circumstances; but it is more especially so, when superadded to these personal attractions are a red nose and yellow smalls.

“Name’s Noah Claypole—Mister Claypole, to you,” the charity ward said, “and you answer directly to me. Get those shutters pulled down, you lazy little lout!” With that, Claypole landed a sharp kick to Oliver’s leg, then marched into the shop with a lofty, dignified bearing he clearly thought suited him perfectly. It’s hard enough for a big-headed, beady-eyed, clumsily built, thick-featured teen to pull off dignity at the best of times, but the task becomes outright impossible when you add a blotchy red nose and scuffed yellow knee-breeches to that already unflattering list of traits.

"No one in England really seems to have any suspicion what is going on in Belgium. You hear stories--People tell them in Holland. It takes your breath away. They have set out just to cow those Belgians. They have started in to be deliberately frightful. You do not begin to understand.... Well.... Outrages. The sort of outrages Americans have never heard of. That one doesn't speak of.... Well.... Rape.... They have been raping women for disciplinary purposes on tables in the market-place of Liège. Yes, sir. It's a fact. I was told it by a man who had just come out of Belgium. Knew the people, knew the place, knew everything. People over here do not seem to realise that those women are the same sort of women that you might find in Chester or Yarmouth, or in Matching's Easy for the matter of that. They still seem to think that Continental women are a different sort of women--more amenable to that sort of treatment. They seem to think there is some special Providential law against such things happening to English people. And it's within two hundred miles of you--even now. And as far as I can see there's precious little to prevent it coming nearer...."

Hardly a soul in Britain seems to harbor any inkling of the nightmare unfolding across the Channel. One hears the accounts—they circulate in the Dutch borderlands—and they leave one reeling. The invaders have embarked upon a campaign to systematically terrorize the populace; they have embraced a philosophy of cold, calculated horror. You cannot conceive of it... Truly... Violations. Cruelties of a nature that would stun a New Yorker. Things one simply does not mention... Well... Defilement. They have been despoiling women as a method of discipline, out in the open on the very merchant stalls of Louvain. It is an absolute fact. I had it from a gentleman who fled the region himself; he knew the families, he knew the terrain, he saw the wreckage. Our neighbors here fail to recognize that those victims are the same flesh and blood as the women in York or Hastings, or even here in Little Waltham for that matter. There remains this stubborn belief that the women of the Continent are of a separate, more resilient cast—better suited to endure such savagery. They cling to the notion that some celestial decree renders the English immune to such calamity. And yet, it is occurring not sixty leagues from where we stand. As far as I can tell, there is little enough to stop that shadow from stretching toward our own threshold.

"They were bound to one another, heart and soul; it is only right they should depart as one," she murmured. "I was a ghost to their union, an intruder; why then should I cling to this remnant of her?" She gathered the lock in her palm and held it poised above the hungry grate. "Yet—I shall not burn it. I will preserve it in remembrance of her, the wretched girl!" she added, jerking her hand back from the heat.

“The short and the long of what you mean,” said Nancy: speaking very emphatically, and slightly frowning at Oliver as if to bespeak his serious attention to her words: “is, that if you’re crossed by him in this job you have on hand, you’ll prevent his ever telling tales afterwards, by shooting him through the head, and will take your chance of swinging for it, as you do for a great many other things in the way of business, every month of your life.”

“The short and the long of what you mean,” said Nancy, speaking with great emphasis and frowning a little at Oliver, as though to demand his full attention to what she said, “is that if he gets in your way in this job you’ve got in hand, you’ll stop him from ever telling tales afterward by shooting him through the head, and you’ll take your chance of being hanged for it, just as you do for a great many other things in the course of business, every month of your life.”

I let it go at that. Then I read in a low voice, to as many as could crowd their shaven heads within hearing distance, part of the account of the miracle of the restoration of the well, and was accompanied by astonished and reverent ejaculations all through: “Ah-h-h!” “How true!” “Amazing, amazing!” “These be the very haps as they happened, in marvelous exactness!” And might they take this strange thing in their hands, and feel of it and examine it?--they would be very careful. Yes. So they took it, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it had been some holy thing come from some supernatural region; and gently felt of its texture, caressed its pleasant smooth surface with lingering touch, and scanned the mysterious characters with fascinated eyes. These grouped bent heads, these charmed faces, these speaking eyes --how beautiful to me! For was not this my darling, and was not all this mute wonder and interest and homage a most eloquent tribute and unforced compliment to it? I knew, then, how a mother feels when women, whether strangers or friends, take her new baby, and close themselves about it with one eager impulse, and bend their heads over it in a tranced adoration that makes all the rest of the universe vanish out of their consciousness and be as if it were not, for that time. I knew how she feels, and that there is no other satisfied ambition, whether of king, conqueror, or poet, that ever reaches half-way to that serene far summit or yields half so divine a contentment.

I didn’t push the matter further. Then I read aloud softly, pitched to carry only to the cluster of people who’d pressed in close enough to fit their shaved heads within earshot, a section of the account of the well’s miraculous restoration. The entire reading was punctuated by shocked, reverent exclamations: “Ohhhh!” “That’s exactly how it went!” “Incredible, just incredible!” “These are the actual events, word for word, perfectly spot-on!”

When they asked if they could hold this strange, precious object in their own hands to feel and examine it—promising they would be as careful as could be—I said yes. They passed it between them, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it were a sacred relic sent down from some otherworldly realm. They ran their fingers lightly over its texture, lingered to stroke its warm, smooth surface, and stared at the mysterious printed characters with rapt, fascinated eyes.

All those huddled, bowed heads, those entranced faces, those bright, expressive eyes—they struck me as so deeply beautiful. Wasn’t this little volume my most cherished darling, after all? And wasn’t all that quiet awe, eager curiosity, and unprompted respect the most eloquent, sincere tribute and unforced compliment it could ever receive?

In that moment, I understood exactly how a mother feels when other women, be they strangers or friends, crowd around to hold her newborn baby, leaning in all at once out of pure, urgent affection, their heads bowed over the infant in a rapt, adoring haze that wipes the entire rest of the universe from their consciousness, like nothing else exists for those few seconds. I knew that quiet, soaring joy, and I knew no other fulfilled ambition—whether that of a king, a conqueror, or a celebrated poet—ever comes even halfway to that serene, distant high, or delivers half as much divine, unshakable contentment.

Of course, it would have been best, all round, for Merlin to waive etiquette and quit and call it half a day, since he would never be able to start that water, for he was a true magician of the time; which is to say, the big miracles, the ones that gave him his reputation, always had the luck to be performed when nobody but Merlin was present; he couldn't start this well with all this crowd around to see; a crowd was as bad for a magician's miracle in that day as it was for a spiritualist's miracle in mine; there was sure to be some skeptic on hand to turn up the gas at the crucial moment and spoil everything. But I did not want Merlin to retire from the job until I was ready to take hold of it effectively myself; and I could not do that until I got my things from Camelot, and that would take two or three days.

It would have been far more sensible, certainly, for Valerius to set aside his dignity and abandon the field at once, for he was never going to coax a drop from that dry earth. He was a quintessential sorcerer of his era; which is to say, those legendary feats that secured his fame were invariably performed when no one but Valerius was there to witness them. He could hardly command the spring to flow before such a vast assembly; a throng of spectators was as fatal to a medieval wonder as a skeptic is to a modern séance. There is always some doubter present, ready to strike a match at the critical moment and expose the fraud. Yet, I had no wish for Valerius to retire until I was prepared to seize the stage with proper effect myself. That remained impossible until my apparatus arrived from the Citadel, a journey that would require at least two or three days.

I have other cares.”

Never going to happen! I’ll keep my mouth clamped shut as tight as the grave I don’t even fear facing, even though speaking up right now would clear me of half the garbage you buy into about me. That’s right, I mean it. What person with an ounce of dignity would bother scraping the cobwebs out of some unreasonable brute’s head after he’s spewed language like that? No. Let him keep right on, cling to his narrow, small-minded thoughts, and run his own head straight into the mire for all I care. I’ve got far more important things to tend to.

I must look in at Tellson’s; so I will go there at once and come back presently. Then, we will take him a ride into the country, and dine there, and all will be well.”

“I think,” he whispered to Mrs. Baines, after a troubled pause of thought, “I think it would be wiser not to address him at present, nor in any way stir him. I must step into Harwood’s; so I shall go there immediately and return in a little while. Then we will carry him out for a drive into the country, dine there, and everything will be right.”

As my sickness quitted me, I was absorbed by a gloomy and black melancholy that nothing could dissipate. The image of Clerval was for ever before me, ghastly and murdered. More than once the agitation into which these reflections threw me made my friends dread a dangerous relapse. Alas! Why did they preserve so miserable and detested a life? It was surely that I might fulfil my destiny, which is now drawing to a close. Soon, oh, very soon, will death extinguish these throbbings and relieve me from the mighty weight of anguish that bears me to the dust; and, in executing the award of justice, I shall also sink to rest. Then the appearance of death was distant, although the wish was ever present to my thoughts; and I often sat for hours motionless and speechless, wishing for some mighty revolution that might bury me and my destroyer in its ruins.

When my illness had at last departed, I became possessed by a heavy and funereal melancholy from which nothing could deliver me. The figure of Hartwell was ever before my eyes, pale, distorted, and slain. More than once the turmoil into which such meditations drove me caused my companions to fear a fatal relapse. Alas! Why had they spared so wretched and loathed a life? Surely only that I might complete the fate appointed me, which now seemed near its end. Soon, ah, very soon, death would extinguish these palpitations and release me from the vast burden of sorrow that pressed me to the earth; and, while the sentence of justice was fulfilled, I too should descend into repose. At that time death still appeared remote, though the desire for it was never absent from my mind; and I would often remain for hours without motion or speech, longing for some mighty convulsion that might bury both me and my persecutor beneath its ruins.

We concluded to make a tour between the inner fences. We elected to walk upright, for convenience's sake; we argued that if discerned, we should be taken for friends rather than enemies, and in any case we should be out of reach of swords, and these gentry did not seem to have any spears along. Well, it was a curious trip. Everywhere dead men were lying outside the second fence--not plainly visible, but still visible; and we counted fifteen of those pathetic statues--dead knights standing with their hands on the upper wire.

We resolved to venture along the path between the inner barriers. We chose to move erect, for ease of motion; we reasoned that if spotted, we would be mistaken for allies rather than foes, and besides, we would remain beyond the sweep of blades, and these fellows appeared to carry no lances with them. It was, I must say, a strange journey. On every side fallen men lay scattered beyond the second paling—not starkly exposed, yet still discernible; and we tallied fifteen of those sorrowful figures—slain soldiers upright with their fingers clutching the topmost strand.

I noted that the two figures were muffled in heavy capes, seemingly anxious to escape recognition. As they stood together by the coach’s entrance, I perceived that they were roughly my own age, or perhaps a trifle younger; they were, moreover, singularly alike in height, in gait, in the very pitch of their voices, and, so far as I could judge, in countenance also.
