The remainder of the dinner passed like a funeral, the judge and Mr. Morse confining their talk to each other, and the rest of the conversation being extremely desultory. Then afterward, when Ruth and Martin were alone, there was a scene.

Martin cut off mid-sentence sharply, the room falling completely still. Every member of Ruth’s family held Judge Blount in high regard as a formidable, highly accomplished man, so they were all appalled by Martin’s sudden outburst. The rest of the dinner dragged on with the stifling, somber energy of a funeral; the judge and Mr. Morse kept their conversation exclusively between themselves, and any other scraps of chat were scattered, half-hearted, and entirely aimless. Later, once the meal ended and Ruth and Martin were left alone together, a tense, explosive scene unfolded.

As soon as the tent was completed the band struck up highly stimulating harmonies, and the announcement was publicly made, Black Bess standing in a conspicuous position on the outside, as a living proof, if proof were wanted, of the truth of the oracular utterances from the stage over which the people were to enter. These were so convinced by such genuine appeals to heart and understanding both that they soon began to crowd in abundantly, among the foremost being visible Jan Coggan and Joseph Poorgrass, who were holiday keeping here to-day.

As soon as the tent was finished, the band broke into lively, stirring music, and an announcement was made aloud. Black Bess stood prominently outside as living proof, if any proof were needed, of the truth of the prophetic words spoken from the stage beneath which the crowd was to pass inside. The people were so persuaded by these sincere appeals to both feeling and reason that they quickly began streaming in thickly; among the first to be seen were Jan Coggan and Joseph Poorgrass, who were taking their holiday here that day.

Heaven opened then, indeed. The flash was almost too novel for its inexpressibly dangerous nature to be at once realized, and they could only comprehend the magnificence of its beauty. It sprang from east, west, north, south, and was a perfect dance of death. The forms of skeletons appeared in the air, shaped with blue fire for bones—dancing, leaping, striding, racing around, and mingling altogether in unparalleled confusion. With these were intertwined undulating snakes of green, and behind these was a broad mass of lesser light. Simultaneously came from every part of the tumbling sky what may be called a shout; since, though no shout ever came near it, it was more of the nature of a shout than of anything else earthly. In the meantime one of the grisly forms had alighted upon the point of Gabriel’s rod, to run invisibly down it, down the chain, and into the earth. Gabriel was almost blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm tremble in his hand—a sensation novel and thrilling enough; but love, life, everything human, seemed small and trifling in such close juxtaposition with an infuriated universe.

The sky truly split open right then. The burst of light was so startlingly unfamiliar that they didn’t immediately register its unspeakable peril, all they could grasp first was the staggering, unearthly beauty of it. It arced out from every point of the compass—east, west, north, south—a flawless, terrible dance of death. Skeleton shapes flickered into view in the open air, their bones forged from searing blue flame: dancing, leaping, striding, darting in circles, all blending together into a chaos no one could have imagined. Wriggling, coiling streaks of green lightning wove through them, and behind the whole swirling display loomed a wide, dimmer bank of diffused light. At the exact same moment, a sound tore out of every corner of the roiling sky that could only be called a shout; no human yell could ever match its power, but it aligned more with the raw, feral energy of a shout than any other earthly phenomenon. Right as that sound hit, one of those ghoulish, glowing shapes landed on the tip of Gabriel’s rod, snaking invisibly down its length, along the chain at its base, and straight into the soil. Gabriel was nearly blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm trembling where he clutched it, a jolt of something new and sharp and thrilling shooting through him at the contact; but love, life, every small, human concern felt trivial and tiny, pressed up so close against the unbridled rage of the universe itself.

Information Extraction (IE), and specifically Relation Extraction (RE), can improve the access to central information for downstream tasks (Santos et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2016; Miwa and Bansal, 2016; Luan et al., 2018a) . However, the focus of current RE systems and datasets is either too narrow, i.e., a handful of semantic relations, such as 'USED-FOR' and 'SYNONYMY', or too broad, i.e., an unbounded number of generic relations extracted from large, heterogeneous corpora (Niklaus et al., 2018) , referred to as Open IE (OIE) (Etzioni et al., 2005; Banko et al., 2007) . Narrow approaches to IE from scientific text (Augenstein et al., 2017; Gábor et al., 2018; Luan et al., 2018a) cover only a fraction of the information captured in a paper -usually what is within an abstract. It has been shown that scientific texts contain many unique relation types and, therefore, it is not feasible to create separate narrow IE classifiers for these (Groth et al., 2018) . On the other hand, OIE systems are primarily developed for the Web and news-wire domain and have been shown to perform poorly on scientific texts. What laymen really need is a bit of both: the accuracy of narrow RE systems to extract central relations from scientific texts and the flexibility of an OIE system to capture a much larger fraction of the possible relations expressed in scientific texts.

metadata.title: In Layman's Terms: Semi-Open Relation Extraction from Scientific Texts

text: Information Extraction (IE), and in particular Relation Extraction (RE), can enhance access to central information for downstream tasks (Santos et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2014; Jiang et al., 2016; Miwa and Bansal, 2016; Luan et al., 2018a). However, the current emphasis of RE systems and datasets is either too narrow, that is, limited to a small set of semantic relations such as 'USED-FOR' and 'SYNONYMY', or too broad, that is, restricted to an unbounded collection of generic relations extracted from large, heterogeneous corpora (Niklaus et al., 2018), commonly referred to as Open IE (OIE) (Etzioni et al., 2005; Banko et al., 2007). Narrow approaches to IE from scientific text (Augenstein et al., 2017; Gábor et al., 2018; Luan et al., 2018a) capture only a portion of the information contained in a paper—typically what appears in an abstract. It has been demonstrated that scientific texts include many unique relation types and, consequently, it is not practical to build separate narrow IE classifiers for them (Groth et al., 2018). By contrast, OIE systems are mainly designed for the Web and news-wire domain and have been shown to perform poorly on scientific texts. What laymen really need is a combination of both: the precision of narrow RE systems for extracting central relations from scientific texts and the flexibility of an OIE system to capture a much larger fraction of the possible relations expressed in scientific texts.

Oliver’s ailings were neither slight nor few. In addition to the pain and delay attendant on a broken limb, his exposure to the wet and cold had brought on fever and ague: which hung about him for many weeks, and reduced him sadly. But, at length, he began, by slow degrees, to get better, and to be able to say sometimes, in a few tearful words, how deeply he felt the goodness of the two sweet ladies, and how ardently he hoped that when he grew strong and well again, he could do something to show his gratitude; only something, which would let them see the love and duty with which his breast was full; something, however slight, which would prove to them that their gentle kindness had not been cast away; but that the poor boy whom their charity had rescued from misery, or death, was eager to serve them with his whole heart and soul.

Elias’s maladies were no trivial, short-lived affair. Beyond the throbbing ache and slow, cumbersome healing of his fractured leg, the hours he’d spent exposed to lashing rain and bitter frost had stirred up a raging fever and shaking ague that clung to his system for weeks, wasting his small frame to a gaunt, listless shadow. But at length, he began to mend by almost imperceptible degrees, now and then able to murmur a few wobbly, tear-thick words about how deeply he cherished the kindness of the two gentle women who’d taken him in, and how ardently he hoped that once he regained his full strength and health, he might find some small way to show his gratitude. It only needed to be some small act, something that would lay bare the overflowing devotion and sense of duty swelling in his breast; no matter how slight the effort, it would prove that their soft, unhesitating goodness had not been cast aside for nothing, that the ragged, destitute boy their charity had pulled from misery, and almost certain death, ached to devote every beat of his heart and every scrap of his strength to their care.

Main technical challenges: 1) There is no public dataset corresponding to this overview composition problem with all the requirements set above, so we were required to either define new (sub-)tasks and collect new annotations, or select techniques by intuition, implement them, and iterate on feedback; 2) Generating summaries which are simultaneously accurate, informational, fluent, and highly concise necessitates careful and innovative choices of summarization techniques; 3) Supporting arbitrary user searches in real-time places significant performance requirements on the system whilst also setting a high bar for its robustness.

metadata.title: NSTM: Real-Time Query-Driven News Overview Composition at Bloomberg

text: Main technical challenges: 1) There is no public dataset for this overview-composition problem that satisfies all of the requirements stated above, so we had to either define new (sub-)tasks and collect new annotations, or select techniques based on intuition, implement them, and refine them through feedback; 2) Producing summaries that are simultaneously accurate, informative, fluent, and highly concise requires careful and novel choices of summarization techniques; 3) Supporting arbitrary user searches in real time places substantial performance demands on the system while also imposing a high standard for robustness.

At four o’clock there was a gentle knock at the door. It was from Charley, who had been sent by Captain Vye to inquire if anything had been heard of Eustacia. The girl who admitted him looked in his face as if she did not know what answer to return, and showed him in to where Venn was seated, saying to the reddleman, “Will you tell him, please?”

At four o’clock there came a soft tap at the door. It was from Ned, who had been sent by Captain Waye to learn whether any news had reached them of Selina. The maid who admitted him looked up into his face as though at a loss for what reply to make, and ushered him in to where Durnford was seated, saying to the younger man, “Will you tell him, please?”

So Miss Corner went off to get two sticks and a ball and came back to instruct Mr. Direck. She said he had a good eye. The two small boys scenting play in the air got sticks and joined them. The overnight visitor's wife appeared from the house in abbreviated skirts, and wearing formidable shin-guards. With her abundant fair hair, which was already breaking loose, so to speak, to join the fray, she looked like a short stout dismounted Valkyr. Her gaze was clear and firm.

Miss Corner stepped off to fetch two sticks and a ball, then returned to teach Mr. Direck how to play, noting he had a sharp, capable eye for the game. The two small boys, catching wind of play nearby, grabbed their own sticks and darted over to join in. The wife of the overnight guest emerged from the house in cropped skirts, a set of formidable shin guards fastened to her legs. Her thick fair hair was already slipping free of its fastenings, as if itching to throw itself into the fray, and she looked for all the world like a short, stout dismounted Valkyrie, her gaze clear and unshakably steady.

Then—oh, then—if you still stood on one foot, with your hot hand clutching the three moist dollars in your pocket, and hoarsely proclaimed your hideous and culpable poverty, nevermore would Mrs. Parker be cicerone of yours. She would honk loudly the word “Clara,” she would show you her back, and march downstairs. Then Clara, the coloured maid, would escort you up the carpeted ladder that served for the fourth flight, and show you the Skylight Room. It occupied 7×8 feet of floor space at the middle of the hall. On each side of it was a dark lumber closet or storeroom.

And then—if you still wavered there, your feverish hand clutching three soggy dollars as you rasped out the truth of your miserable poverty—Mrs. Parker would no longer be your guide. She would bray a loud call for “Clara,” present you with her back, and march down the stairs. Then Clara, the maid, would lead you up the steep, carpeted ladder of the fourth floor to reveal the Skylight Room. It took up a mere seven-by-eight feet in the middle of the landing, squeezed between two pitch-black storage closets.

“But it is! ’Tis a thing which is everybody’s business,” said Samway. “We know very well that master’s on a wrong tack, and that she’s quite in the dark, and we should let ’em know at once. Laban, you know her best—you’d better go and ask to speak to her.”

“But it really is! This is something that concerns everyone,” Samway insisted. “We know for a fact that the master is on the wrong path and she’s completely unaware of the truth. We ought to tell them immediately. Laban, you're the one who knows her best—you should be the one to go and ask for a word with her.”

She trembled, turned, and said “Good morning.” His tone was so utterly removed from all she had expected as a beginning. It was lowness and quiet accentuated: an emphasis of deep meanings, their form, at the same time, being scarcely expressed. Silence has sometimes a remarkable power of showing itself as the disembodied soul of feeling wandering without its carcase, and it is then more impressive than speech. In the same way, to say a little is often to tell more than to say a great deal. Boldwood told everything in that word.

She shivered, turned, and murmured a greeting. His voice was so completely different from the start she had imagined. It was a quality of profound, hushed stillness—an emphasis on deep significance that remained barely articulated. Silence sometimes possesses a striking ability to manifest as the naked spirit of emotion, wandering free from its physical shell, and it is then more powerful than any spoken word. In this manner, brevity often reveals more than an abundance of speech. Boldwood had conveyed everything in that single greeting.

“I can cure it, I think, and without drugs,” was Martin’s answer. “I am not sure, of course, but I’d like to try. It’s simply massage. I learned the trick first from the Japanese. They are a race of masseurs, you know. Then I learned it all over again with variations from the Hawaiians. They call it _lomi-lomi_. It can accomplish most of the things drugs accomplish and a few things that drugs can’t.”

“I believe I can remedy this without resorting to medicine,” Martin replied. “I can’t be certain, naturally, but I’m eager to give it a go. It is a matter of massage, really. I first picked up the technique from the Japanese—they are a people gifted in the art of bodywork, you know. Later, I refined the practice through the Hawaiians, who refer to it as *lomi-lomi*. It achieves much of what pharmaceuticals can do, along with several things they simply cannot.”

“I heard your dog howling and scratching at the door of the hut when I came to the milking (it was so lucky, Daisy’s milking is almost over for the season, and I shall not come here after this week or the next). The dog saw me, and jumped over to me, and laid hold of my skirt. I came across and looked round the hut the very first thing to see if the slides were closed. My uncle has a hut like this one, and I have heard him tell his shepherd not to go to sleep without leaving a slide open. I opened the door, and there you were like dead. I threw the milk over you, as there was no water, forgetting it was warm, and no use.”

“I heard your hound wailing and clawing at the cabin door when I arrived for the evening milking—such a mercy that Daisy’s flow is nearly done for the year, and I shan’t need to come this way beyond a week or two more. The beast caught sight of me, bounded over, and seized the hem of my dress. I crossed the yard at once and circled the hut, my first thought being to check whether the vents were shut. My own uncle keeps a cabin much like this one, and I’ve heard him warn his shepherd never to sleep without a vent cracked open. I drew back the door, and there you lay as still as a corpse. I dashed the milk over you, there being no water to hand, forgetting in my haste that it was warm and would do nothing.”

“‘I have done— Put by the lute. Song and singing soon are over As the airy shades that hover In among the purple clover. I have done— Put by the lute. Once I sang as early thrushes Sing among the dewy bushes; Now I’m mute. I am like a weary linnet, For my throat has no song in it; I have had my singing minute. I have done. Put by the lute.’”

“I have finished—set the lute aside. Song and singing pass away quickly, like the airy shadows that drift among the purple clover. I have finished—set the lute aside. Once I sang like the early thrushes singing in the dewy bushes; now I am silent. I am like an exhausted linnet, for there is no song left in my throat; my moment for singing has come and gone. I have finished. Set the lute aside.”

The reddleman looked hopeful; after these words from her his third attempt seemed promising. “As we have now opened our minds a bit, miss,” he said, “I’ll tell you what I have got to propose. Since I have taken to the reddle trade I travel a good deal, as you know.”

The red-dye peddler appeared optimistic; following her remark, his third effort felt as though it might actually succeed. "Since we’ve finally started to speak more candidly, miss," he said, "I’ll tell you what I’m prepared to suggest. As you know, I’m on the road quite often now that I’ve taken up this trade."

Mr. Giles walked into the corner with much importance, and some wonder, and was honoured with a short whispering conference with the doctor, on the termination of which, he made a great many bows, and retired with steps of unusual stateliness. The subject matter of this conference was not disclosed in the parlour, but the kitchen was speedily enlightened concerning it; for Mr. Giles walked straight thither, and having called for a mug of ale, announced, with an air of majesty, which was highly effective, that it had pleased his mistress, in consideration of his gallant behaviour on the occasion of that attempted robbery, to deposit, in the local savings-bank, the sum of five-and-twenty pounds, for his sole use and benefit. At this, the two women-servants lifted up their hands and eyes, and supposed that Mr. Giles, pulling out his shirt-frill, replied, “No, no”; and that if they observed that he was at all haughty to his inferiors, he would thank them to tell him so. And then he made a great many other remarks, no less illustrative of his humility, which were received with equal favour and applause, and were, withal, as original and as much to the purpose, as the remarks of great men commonly are.

Mr. Giles approached the corner with significant gravity and a touch of intrigue, where he was favored with a brief, whispered dialogue with the doctor. Once the exchange concluded, he performed a series of elaborate bows and departed with a stride of remarkable stateliness. Though the nature of this conversation was kept secret from those in the parlor, the kitchen was quickly informed of its contents. Mr. Giles headed there at once, and after calling for a mug of ale, declared with an effective air of grandeur that his mistress, moved by his brave defense during the attempted burglary, had deposited twenty-five pounds into the local savings bank for his exclusive use. At this news, the two maidservants raised their hands in wonder. Mr. Giles, smoothing his shirt-front, demurred with a "No, no," requesting that they inform him if he ever appeared too arrogant toward his social inferiors. He then offered several other comments intended to showcase his humility; these were received with great acclaim and were, in truth, about as insightful and relevant as the pronouncements of great men typically tend to be.

Too long had British life been corrupted by the fictions of loyalty to an uninspiring and alien Court, of national piety in an official Church, of freedom in a politician-rigged State, of justice in an economic system where the advertiser, the sweater and usurer had a hundred advantages over the producer and artisan, to maintain itself now steadily at any high pitch of heroic endeavour. It had bought its comfort with the demoralisation of its servants. It had no completely honest organs; its spirit was clogged by its accumulated insincerities. Brought at last face to face with a bitter hostility and a powerful and unscrupulous enemy, an enemy socialistic, scientific and efficient to an unexampled degree, it seemed indeed to be inspired for a time by an unwonted energy and unanimity. Youth and the common people shone. The sons of every class went out to fight and die, full of a splendid dream of this war. Easy-going vanished from the foreground of the picture. But only to creep back again as the first inspiration passed. Presently the older men, the seasoned politicians, the owners and hucksters, the charming women and the habitual consumers, began to recover from this blaze of moral exaltation. Old habits of mind and procedure reasserted themselves. The war which had begun so dramatically missed its climax; there was neither heroic swift defeat nor heroic swift victory. There was indecision; the most trying test of all for an undisciplined people. There were great spaces of uneventful fatigue. Before the Battle of the Yser had fully developed the dramatic quality had gone out of the war. It had ceased to be either a tragedy or a triumph; for both sides it became a monstrous strain and wasting. It had become a wearisome thrusting against a pressure of evils....

For too long, the British character had been hollowed out by myths: a feigned devotion to a distant, unmoving monarchy, a hollow religiosity within the state church, and a charade of liberty in a government controlled by party hacks. Economic justice was a farce in a system where the promoter, the exploiter, and the loan shark held every advantage over the laborer and the craftsman; such a society could not long sustain a truly heroic fervor. It had purchased its ease at the cost of its people’s integrity. Every institution was tainted, the national soul stifled by a mountain of pretenses. Yet, when finally confronted by a ruthless and formidable foe—an adversary characterized by an unprecedented level of social organization and scientific precision—the nation seemed briefly galvanized by a rare spirit of purpose. The common folk and the young were radiant. Men of every rank marched away to perish, fueled by a magnificent vision of the conflict. The old lethargy retreated from view. But as that first wave of passion ebbed, the complacency returned. Soon, the elder statesmen, the merchants, the socialites, and the idle wealthy began to shake off their brief moral awakening. The familiar patterns of thought and conduct resumed. The war, which had opened with such theatrical force, failed to reach a resolution; there was no swift, glorious triumph and no sudden, noble collapse. Instead came the agony of stalemate, the harshest trial for a people lacking discipline. Long stretches of monotonous exhaustion set in. Even before the struggle at the Yser reached its peak, the war’s sense of narrative had dissolved. It was no longer a story of victory or defeat, but a colossal burden of attrition. It had devolved into a grueling, stagnant resistance against an overwhelming tide of misery.

“Pretty fair--pretty fair. Chin a little lower, please--there, very good. Eyes too high; pray don't look at the horizon, look at the ground, ten steps in front of you. Ah--that is better, that is very good. Wait, please; you betray too much vigor, too much decision; you want more of a shamble. Look at me, please--this is what I mean.... Now you are getting it; that is the idea--at least, it sort of approaches it.... Yes, that is pretty fair. _But!_ There is a great big something wanting, I don't quite know what it is. Please walk thirty yards, so that I can get a perspective on the thing.... Now, then--your head's right, speed's right, shoulders right, eyes right, chin right, gait, carriage, general style right--everything's right! And yet the fact remains, the aggregate's wrong. The account don't balance. Do it again, please.... _Now_ I think I begin to see what it is. Yes, I've struck it. You see, the genuine spiritlessness is wanting; that's what's the trouble. It's all _amateur_--mechanical details all right, almost to a hair; everything about the delusion perfect, except that it don't delude.”

“Not bad, really not bad at all. Tuck your chin just a little lower, please—there, that’s perfect. Your eyes are tilted too far up, c’mon, don’t stare off at the horizon. Fix your gaze on the ground ten steps ahead of you, yeah? Ah, that’s way better, that works great. Hold on a second, though—you’re coming off too energized, too sure of yourself. You need to shamble more, drag your feet a little. Watch me, okay? This is what I’m talking about… Oh, you’re getting the hang of it now, that’s exactly the vibe—well, it’s close enough, anyway… Yeah, that’s pretty decent. But! There’s still something big missing, I can’t quite put my finger on it yet. Walk thirty yards for me, would you? So I can get a proper perspective on how it reads from a distance… Okay, let’s break this down—your head position’s right, pace is right, shoulders are right, eyes are right, chin is right, your gait, your posture, the whole general energy checks out—every last little detail is correct! But the fact remains, the full package is off. It doesn’t add up at all. Do it again, please… Now I think I finally see what the issue is. Yeah, I figured it out. Look, you don’t have that real, bone-deep lack of spirit, that’s what’s wrong. This is all total amateur hour—all the mechanical details are almost perfect, down to the smallest tiny thing, every part of the act is flawless, except for the fact that it doesn’t fool anyone for a second.”

Within a few minutes, Miss Pross stood whispering at his side. If he had had any particle of doubt left, her talk would of necessity have resolved it; but he was by that time clear-headed, and had none. He advised that they should let the time go by until the regular breakfast-hour, and should then meet the Doctor as if nothing unusual had occurred. If he appeared to be in his customary state of mind, Mr. Lorry would then cautiously proceed to seek direction and guidance from the opinion he had been, in his anxiety, so anxious to obtain.

A moment later, Miss Pross was murmuring quietly beside him. Any lingering uncertainty he might have harbored would certainly have been dispelled by her words, but his mind was already lucid and free of doubt. He suggested they allow the hours to pass until the usual breakfast time, then greet the Doctor as though the morning were entirely ordinary. Should the Doctor show his typical frame of mind, Mr. Lorry would delicately attempt to draw out the counsel he had so desperately been hoping to secure.

The skewness, noisiness and insensitivity of the self-reported Likert scale rating make it a suboptimal dialog evaluation metric. In practice, we find that directly training a classifier (even for pre-trained BERT-based model) on the noisy selfreported Likert scale ratings suffers from underfitting. One of the Alexa Price Challenge team, Alana (Papaioannou et al., 2017 ) train a binaryclassifier between successful dialogs (human rating 4 or 5) and unsuccessful dialogs (rating 1 or 2) with heavy hand-engineered features. They reach 69.40% accuracy on this binary classification problem, which is far from usable in real-world settings.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: The skewness, noise, and insensitivity of self-reported Likert scale ratings make them a suboptimal metric for dialog evaluation. In practice, we find that directly training a classifier—even a pre-trained BERT-based model—on noisy self-reported Likert scale ratings leads to underfitting. One of the Alexa Prize Challenge teams, Alana (Papaioannou et al., 2017), trained a binary classifier to distinguish successful dialogs (human ratings of 4 or 5) from unsuccessful dialogs (ratings of 1 or 2) using heavily hand-engineered features. They achieved 69.40% accuracy on this binary classification task, which is far from usable in real-world settings.

Throughout the week-end Mr. Britling had kept this trouble well battened down. He had written to Mrs. Harrowdean a brief ambiguous note saying, "I am thinking over all that you have said," and after that he had scarcely thought about her at all. Or at least he had always contrived to be much more vividly thinking about something else. But now in these night silences the suppressed trouble burst hatches and rose about him.

Over the weekend, Mr. Britling had succeeded in keeping his distress tightly contained. He had dispatched a short, noncommittal note to Mrs. Harrowdean, telling her he was reflecting on her words, and from that moment on, she had rarely entered his mind. More precisely, he had ensured that his attention was always occupied by more vivid interests. Now, however, in the hush of the late hours, the buried tension forced its way out and surged up to confront him.

“Way behind,” he said. “Got to work after supper.” And after supper they worked until ten o’clock, under the blazing electric lights, until the last piece of under-clothing was ironed and folded away in the distributing room. It was a hot California night, and though the windows were thrown wide, the room, with its red-hot ironing-stove, was a furnace. Martin and Joe, down to undershirts, bare armed, sweated and panted for air.

“We’re way behind on orders,” he grunted. “Have to put in hours after supper.” Once the meal was cleared away, they labored straight through to ten o’clock beneath the searing glow of overhead electric bulbs, not pausing until the final piece of underclothing had been pressed crisp and tucked away in the distribution room. It was a scorching California night, and though every window had been flung wide to the dark, the small workroom, heated by the red-glowing ironing stove, was nothing short of a furnace. Leo and Rafe, stripped down to thin cotton undershirts, bare-armed, dripped with sweat and panted for even the smallest wisp of cool air.

“He is tall,” said the girl, “and a strongly made man, but not stout; he has a lurking walk; and as he walks, constantly looks over his shoulder, first on one side, and then on the other. Don’t forget that, for his eyes are sunk in his head so much deeper than any other man’s, that you might almost tell him by that alone. His face is dark, like his hair and eyes; and, although he can’t be more than six or eight and twenty, withered and haggard. His lips are often discoloured and disfigured with the marks of teeth; for he has desperate fits, and sometimes even bites his hands and covers them with wounds—why did you start?” said the girl, stopping suddenly.

“He’s tall,” the girl said, “solidly built, but not heavy. He walks like he’s trying not to draw attention, always glancing over his shoulder as he moves, first one side, then the other. Don’t miss that detail—his eyes are set so much deeper in his skull than most people’s, you could practically spot him by that feature alone. He’s dark-skinned, same as his hair and eyes, and even though he can’t be more than twenty-six or twenty-eight, his face looks worn and gaunt. His lips are usually discolored and chewed raw with tooth marks, too; he gets these terrible, desperate fits, and sometimes he even bites his own hands so hard they’re covered in wounds—why did you start?” the girl asked, cutting herself off abruptly.

It was a perfectly appointed, small steam laundry, wherein the most modern machinery did everything that was possible for machinery to do. Martin, after a few instructions, sorted the great heaps of soiled clothes, while Joe started the masher and made up fresh supplies of soft-soap, compounded of biting chemicals that compelled him to swathe his mouth and nostrils and eyes in bath-towels till he resembled a mummy. Finished the sorting, Martin lent a hand in wringing the clothes. This was done by dumping them into a spinning receptacle that went at a rate of a few thousand revolutions a minute, tearing the water from the clothes by centrifugal force. Then Martin began to alternate between the dryer and the wringer, between times “shaking out” socks and stockings. By the afternoon, one feeding and one stacking up, they were running socks and stockings through the mangle while the irons were heating. Then it was hot irons and underclothes till six o’clock, at which time Joe shook his head dubiously.

The laundry was a compact, immaculate establishment, fitted with the latest machines to perform every task a machine could undertake. After a brief orientation, Martin set to sorting the mountainous piles of soiled linen, while Joe fired up the masher and prepared a fresh batch of soft-soap—a brew so acrid with harsh chemicals that he had to wrap his mouth, nose, and eyes in bath-towels until he looked like a mummy. Once the sorting was done, Martin helped with the wringing. They heaved the wet garments into a spinning drum that whirled at several thousand revolutions per minute, centrifugal force slinging the water out of the cloth. From then on, Martin shuttled between the dryer and the wringer, pausing now and then to “shake out” socks and stockings. By afternoon, one man feeding and the other stacking, they were running hosiery through the mangle while the irons heated. Then came hot irons and undergarments until six o’clock, when Joe gave a doubtful shake of his head.

Oh! No mortal could support the horror of that countenance. A mummy again endued with animation could not be so hideous as that wretch. I had gazed on him while unfinished; he was ugly then, but when those muscles and joints were rendered capable of motion, it became a thing such as even Dante could not have conceived.

Oh! No mortal could have borne the horror of that face. Not even a mummy restored to life could have been as ghastly as that miserable creature. I had looked at him when he was still unfinished; he had been ugly enough then, but once those muscles and joints were made capable of movement, he became a being such as even Dante could never have imagined.

It was very early the next morning—a time of sun and dew. The confused beginnings of many birds’ songs spread into the healthy air, and the wan blue of the heaven was here and there coated with thin webs of incorporeal cloud which were of no effect in obscuring day. All the lights in the scene were yellow as to colour, and all the shadows were attenuated as to form. The creeping plants about the old manor-house were bowed with rows of heavy water drops, which had upon objects behind them the effect of minute lenses of high magnifying power.

It was very early the next morning, in that hour of sun and dew. The broken first notes of many birds widened through the fresh air, and the pale blue sky was here and there veiled by thin webs of cloud so insubstantial they did nothing to dim the day. Every light in the scene had a yellow cast, and every shadow was drawn out and slight. The climbing plants around the old manor-house bent under rows of heavy droplets, each one acting on the objects behind it like a tiny lens of great magnifying power.

“In the name of the assembled companions in misfortune,” said a gentleman of courtly appearance and address, coming forward, “I have the honour of giving you welcome to La Force, and of condoling with you on the calamity that has brought you among us. May it soon terminate happily! It would be an impertinence elsewhere, but it is not so here, to ask your name and condition?”

“In the name of this company of fellow sufferers,” said a gentleman of polished bearing and manner, stepping a little in advance, “I have the honour to bid you welcome to La Force, and to offer you my sympathy in the misfortune that has conducted you hither. May it end speedily and well! It might elsewhere seem an impertinence, but it is not so here, to inquire your name and station?”

“I do, and I do not,” said he mischievously. “That is, I have my times and my seasons. One moment you are too tall, another moment you are too do-nothing, another too melancholy, another too dark, another I don’t know what, except—that you are not the whole world to me that you used to be, my dear. But you are a pleasant lady to know and nice to meet, and I dare say as sweet as ever—almost.”

“I do, and I do not,” he said with a mischievous air. “That is to say, I have my times and seasons. At one moment you are too tall, at another too idle; one time too melancholy, another too dark, and another I scarcely know what, except that you are not the whole world to me that you once were, my dear. But you are a pleasant lady to know and agreeable to meet, and I dare say as sweet as ever—almost.”

In this paper we offer initial answers to these questions, systematically assessing the syntactic generalization abilities of neural language models on 34 targeted test suites (33 adapted from previously published work, and 1 novel) covering a wide range of syntactic phenomena. Test suites are written using a standard format that allows for flexible predictions which more closely resemble those used in psycholinguistic studies, specifically allowing for predictions about interactions among multiple testing conditions. Performance on each test suite is reported as a Syntactic Generalization (SG) score. We group test suites into six syntactic circuits based on the linguistic representations needed to achieve high performance on each suite.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: This paper presents preliminary responses to these questions by systematically evaluating the syntactic generalization capacities of neural language models across 34 targeted test suites—33 adapted from prior published research and one newly introduced—spanning a broad spectrum of syntactic phenomena. The test suites employ a standardized format that enables flexible predictions more akin to those utilized in psycholinguistic experiments, notably permitting predictions concerning interactions among multiple experimental conditions. Results for each test suite are expressed as a Syntactic Generalization (SG) score. We organize the test suites into six syntactic circuits according to the linguistic representations required to attain strong performance on each suite.

Having disposed of these evil-minded persons for the night, Mr. Bumble sat himself down in the house at which the coach stopped; and took a temperate dinner of steaks, oyster sauce, and porter. Putting a glass of hot gin-and-water on the chimney-piece, he drew his chair to the fire; and, with sundry moral reflections on the too-prevalent sin of discontent and complaining, composed himself to read the paper.

Having settled the matter of these ill-intentioned folk for the evening, Mr. Bumble installed himself in the inn where the coach had halted and partook of a modest repast of steaks, oyster sauce, and porter. After placing a glass of hot gin-and-water on the mantelpiece, he pulled his chair closer to the fire and, interspersing the act with sundry moralisings on the pervasive vice of discontent and grumbling, settled down to peruse the newspaper.

“And so,” he concluded, in a swift résumé, “no state composed of the slave-types can endure. The old law of development still holds. In the struggle for existence, as I have shown, the strong and the progeny of the strong tend to survive, while the weak and the progeny of the weak are crushed and tend to perish. The result is that the strong and the progeny of the strong survive, and, so long as the struggle obtains, the strength of each generation increases. That is development. But you slaves—it is too bad to be slaves, I grant—but you slaves dream of a society where the law of development will be annulled, where no weaklings and inefficients will perish, where every inefficient will have as much as he wants to eat as many times a day as he desires, and where all will marry and have progeny—the weak as well as the strong. What will be the result? No longer will the strength and life-value of each generation increase. On the contrary, it will diminish. There is the Nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves—of, by, and for, slaves—must inevitably weaken and go to pieces as the life which composes it weakens and goes to pieces.

“And so,” he concluded, in a sharp, rapid summary, “no state built of people with a slave’s mindset can ever endure. The ancient law of development still holds unshaken. In the struggle for existence, as I have demonstrated, the strong and their descendants are disposed to survive, while the weak and their children are crushed and fated to perish. The outcome is that the strong and their line persist, and as long as that struggle remains in place, each generation grows more capable than the last. That is the very essence of development. But you slaves— I will grant that it is a miserable thing to be a slave— but you cling to a fantasy of a society where the law of development is struck down, where no weak, unfit person ever perishes, where every incompetent person gets all the food they crave whenever they want it, and where everyone may marry and have children—the weak just as freely as the strong. What will come of this? The strength and inherent vitality of each generation will no longer rise. On the contrary, it will dwindle. That is the nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves, by slaves, and for slaves, will inevitably weaken and crumble just as the lives that form it weaken and are snuffed out.”

The tranquillity which I now enjoyed did not endure. Memory brought madness with it, and when I thought of what had passed, a real insanity possessed me; sometimes I was furious and burnt with rage, sometimes low and despondent. I neither spoke nor looked at anyone, but sat motionless, bewildered by the multitude of miseries that overcame me.

The brief peace I had found soon withered away. My memories ushered in a state of frenzy, and whenever I reflected on what had transpired, a true delirium seized my mind. I swung between fits of searing rage and depths of hollow despair. I became silent and unseeing, sitting in a frozen stupor, paralyzed by the overwhelming weight of the tragedies that had befallen me.

There was, however, gradually evolved from its transformation scenes a less extravagant episode, in which the heath dimly appeared behind the general brilliancy of the action. She was dancing to wondrous music, and her partner was the man in silver armour who had accompanied her through the previous fantastic changes, the visor of his helmet being closed. The mazes of the dance were ecstatic. Soft whispering came into her ear from under the radiant helmet, and she felt like a woman in Paradise. Suddenly these two wheeled out from the mass of dancers, dived into one of the pools of the heath, and came out somewhere into an iridescent hollow, arched with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice by her side, and blushingly looking up she saw him removing his casque to kiss her. At that moment there was a cracking noise, and his figure fell into fragments like a pack of cards.

Out of the shifting pageant, a quieter vision slowly took shape: the heath emerged in shadowy outline behind the brilliant foreground. She was dancing to unearthly music, partnered by the silver-armored man who had moved beside her through all the earlier fantastic transformations, his helmet’s visor shut. The dance’s winding steps were rapturous. A soft whisper reached her ear from beneath the gleaming helmet, and she felt herself a woman in Paradise. Suddenly the two of them spun away from the crowd of dancers, plunged into one of the heath’s dark pools, and surfaced in a shimmering hollow vaulted with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice at her side, and when she looked up, blushing, she saw him lifting his helmet to kiss her. In that instant came a sharp crack, and his figure shattered like a pack of cards.

"Our officers are beastly good chaps, nearly all of them. That's where the perplexity of the whole thing comes in. If only they weren't such good chaps! If only they were like the Prussian officers to their men, then we'd just take on a revolution as well as the war, and make everything tidy at once. But they are decent, they are charming.... Only they do not think hard, and they do not understand that doing a job properly means doing it as directly and thought-outly as you possibly can. They won't worry about things. If their tempers were worse perhaps their work might be better. They won't use maps or timetables or books of reference. When we move to a new place they pick up what they can about it by hearsay; not one of our lot has the gumption to possess a contoured map or a Michelin guide. They have hearsay minds. They are fussy and petty and wasteful--and, in the way of getting things done, pretentious. By their code they're paragons of honour. Courage--they're all right about that; no end of it; honesty, truthfulness, and so on--high. They have a kind of horsey standard of smartness and pluck, too, that isn't bad, and they have a fine horror of whiskers and being unbuttoned. But the mistake they make is to class thinking with whiskers, as a sort of fussy sidegrowth. Instead of classing it with unbuttonedupness. They hate economy. And preparation....

Nearly every last one of our officers is a total stand-up guy. That’s exactly what makes this whole situation so baffling. If only they weren’t such decent blokes! If they treated their men the way Prussian officers treat theirs, we could stage a revolution right alongside fighting the war, and sort every last thing out all in one go. But they’re kind, they’re endlessly charming… they just don’t put any real work into thinking things through, and they don’t grasp that doing a job properly means tackling it as intentionally and deliberately as you possibly can. They refuse to fuss over the important details. If they had worse tempers, their work might actually be far stronger. They won’t bother with maps, timetables, or reference books. When we move to a new posting, they only pick up whatever information they can secondhand, from gossip and casual chatter; not a single one of them has the good sense to carry a topographical map or a Michelin guide. They operate entirely on hearsay. They’re nitpicky, small-minded, and wasteful—and when it comes to actually getting tasks completed, they’re full of empty posturing. By their own internal code, they’re perfect paragons of honor. Courage? They’ve got more than enough of that, no argument there. Honesty, trustworthiness, all those values? They rank incredibly high on all of them. They also have that sort of equestrian-adjacent standard of sharpness and pluck that’s not half bad, and they’re properly horrified by unkempt facial hair and walking around with their uniforms half-unbuttoned. But the massive mistake they make is lumping critical thinking in with scraggly beards, writing it off as some fussy, unnecessary side thing, instead of grouping it with being slovenly and unbuttoned, something that should be avoided at all costs. They loathe being economical. And they hate advance planning even more.

GSL: The F 1 score of our model using the GSL module is 48.91%, higher than the performance of Baseline. In addition, compared with ELMo, although the performance of GSL does not exceed ELMo's, GSL obtain comparable results. This manifests that the two modules (GSL and SFL) could reinforce with each other, which utilizes the geometric structure information by the algebraic operation. Moreover, we exploit the geometric structure clues to augment the semantic understanding of discourse from a new aspect, which is different from the ELMo only focusing on the semantic information of the text itself.

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: GSL: Our model equipped with the GSL module achieves an F1 score of 48.91%, outperforming the baseline model. While GSL does not surpass the performance of ELMo, it delivers results that are fully comparable to those of ELMo. This observation indicates that the GSL and SFL modules are mutually reinforcing, as they utilize geometric structure information via algebraic operations. Furthermore, we leverage geometric structure cues to enhance discourse semantic understanding from a novel angle, a key difference from ELMo, which exclusively focuses on the inherent semantic information of the input text itself.

The trees beneath which she sat were singularly battered, rude, and wild, and for a few minutes Mrs. Yeobright dismissed thoughts of her own storm-broken and exhausted state to contemplate theirs. Not a bough in the nine trees which composed the group but was splintered, lopped, and distorted by the fierce weather that there held them at its mercy whenever it prevailed. Some were blasted and split as if by lightning, black stains as from fire marking their sides, while the ground at their feet was strewn with dead fir-needles and heaps of cones blown down in the gales of past years. The place was called the Devil’s Bellows, and it was only necessary to come there on a March or November night to discover the forcible reasons for that name. On the present heated afternoon, when no perceptible wind was blowing, the trees kept up a perpetual moan which one could hardly believe to be caused by the air.

The trees under which she rested were singularly rough, battered, and savage, and for a few moments Mrs. Ashdown forgot her own storm-worn weariness to regard theirs. Not a branch in the little company of nine but had been splintered, lopped, or twisted awry by the violence of the weather, which, whenever it rose, held the spot in its cruel grasp. Some were scorched and riven as though by lightning, with dark stains running down their trunks like fire-marks, while the earth below was littered with dead pine-needles and drifts of cones flung down by the gales of former years. The place was known as the Devil’s Bellowing, and one had only to visit it on a March or November night to understand the strength of the name. Yet on the present sultry afternoon, when no breeze was perceptible, the trees maintained a continual lament that could scarcely be thought the mere voice of the air.

The plan seemed unfeasible at first; the journey was a grueling trek even for a sturdy horse, and she had significantly underestimated the distance. It was a remarkably bold venture for a woman to undertake alone in the dark.

Inside the blue door, open half-way down, were to be seen at this time the backs and tails of half-a-dozen warm and contented horses standing in their stalls; and as thus viewed, they presented alternations of roan and bay, in shapes like a Moorish arch, the tail being a streak down the midst of each. Over these, and lost to the eye gazing in from the outer light, the mouths of the same animals could be heard busily sustaining the above-named warmth and plumpness by quantities of oats and hay. The restless and shadowy figure of a colt wandered about a loose-box at the end, whilst the steady grind of all the eaters was occasionally diversified by the rattle of a rope or the stamp of a foot.

His house stood back from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, lay behind it, their lower parts hidden among laurel bushes. Through the blue door, opened halfway, one could then see the backs and tails of half a dozen warm, contented horses standing in their stalls; viewed in this way, they showed alternate patches of roan and bay, shaped like a Moorish arch, with the tail forming a streak down the center of each. A restless, shadowy colt moved about a loose box at the far end, while the steady grinding of the feeders was at intervals broken by the rattle of a rope or the stamping of a hoof.

"Ah! your troubles in life haven't begun yet. Wait till you're a father. That cuts to the bone. You have the most delicate thing in the world in hand, a young kindred mind. You feel responsible for it, you know you are responsible for it; and you lose touch with it. You can't get at it. Nowadays we've lost the old tradition of fatherhood by divine right--and we haven't got a new one. I've tried not to be a cramping ruler, a director, a domestic tyrant to that lad--and in effect it's meant his going his own way.... I don't dominate. I hoped to advise. But you see he loves my respect and good opinion. Too much. When things go well I know of them. When the world goes dark for him, then he keeps his trouble from me. Just when I would so eagerly go into it with him.... There's something the matter now, something--it may be grave. I feel he wants to tell me. And there it is!--it seems I am the last person to whom he can humiliate himself by a confession of blundering, or weakness.... Something I should just laugh at and say, 'That's in the blood of all of us, dear Spit of myself. Let's see what's to be done.'..."

“Ah, your real troubles have scarcely begun. Wait until you have a child of your own. That is what goes to the quick. You are given the most fragile thing in existence, a young mind of your own blood. You know you are answerable for it, you feel the burden of that responsibility—and yet you lose hold of it. You cannot reach it. We have, nowadays, let slip the old notion of fatherhood by divine right, and nothing has come to take its place. I have tried not to be a narrowing master, a taskmaker, a household despot to that boy—only it has come to mean his taking his own path.... I do not impose myself. I had hoped to counsel. But you see, he values my regard and approval. Too much. When all goes well, I hear of it. When the sky lowers for him, he keeps his sorrow to himself. Just when I would most eagerly have gone into it with him.... Something is wrong now, something serious perhaps. I can feel that he wants to speak. And yet there it is—it seems I am the very last person before whom he could bring himself to confess folly, or frailty.... Something I ought only to laugh over and say, ‘That is in all our blood, my dear little replica. Let us see what can be done about it.’...”

What then became of me? I know not; I lost sensation, and chains and darkness were the only objects that pressed upon me. Sometimes, indeed, I dreamt that I wandered in flowery meadows and pleasant vales with the friends of my youth, but I awoke and found myself in a dungeon. Melancholy followed, but by degrees I gained a clear conception of my miseries and situation and was then released from my prison. For they had called me mad, and during many months, as I understood, a solitary cell had been my habitation.

What then became of me? I cannot say; I lost all feeling, and nothing but chains and darkness seemed to bear upon me. At times, indeed, I dreamed that I roamed through flowery meadows and pleasant valleys with the friends of my youth, yet I awoke to find myself in a dungeon. A melancholy mood came over me, but little by little I recovered a clear sense of my sufferings and my condition, and was then released from my prison. They had, it seems, declared me mad, and for many months, as I afterward learned, a solitary cell had been my dwelling.

Human Evaluation. We conduct the human evaluation on 25 random samples extracted from each of Zh2EnSum and En2ZhSum, respectively. We compare the summaries generated by ATS (Adapt strategy) with other methods (including TNCLS, CLSMS, and CLSMT). Three graduate students are recruited to rate the generated summaries according to the references. Each summary is assessed from the three independent aspects: (1) How informative is the summary? (2) How concise is the summary? (3) How fluent and grammatical is the summary? Each aspect is scored from 1 (worst) to 5 (Best). The average results are given in Table 7 . We can find that the informativeness score, conciseness score, and fluency score of ATS-A are significantly better than those of the baseline TNCLS, which further demonstrates the effectiveness of our method. In Zh2En task, ATS-A outperforms CLSMT from all three aspects. The conciseness score of ATS-A is comparable with that of CLSMS, but ATS can generate more informative and fluent summaries. In En2Zh task, ATS-A outperforms CLSMS from all three aspects as well. The informativeness score and conciseness score of CLSMT are comparable with those of ATS-A, but ATS-A can generate more fluent summaries. To sum up, ATS-A can outperform CLSMS and CLSMT in Zh2En task, and ATS-A can outperform CLSMS while performing comparably with CLSMT in En2Zh task.

metadata.title: Attend, Translate and Summarize: An Efficient Method for Neural Cross-Lingual Summarization

text: Human Evaluation. We carried out a human evaluation using 25 randomly selected samples from each of the Zh2EnSum and En2ZhSum datasets. We compared summaries produced by ATS (Adapt strategy) with those generated by other methods—specifically TNCLS, CLSMS, and CLSMT. Three graduate students were recruited to rate the summaries against the references. Each summary was assessed along three independent dimensions: (1) How informative is the summary? (2) How concise is the summary? (3) How fluent and grammatical is the summary? Each dimension was scored on a scale from 1 (worst) to 5 (best). The averaged results are presented in Table 7. The findings show that the informativeness, conciseness, and fluency scores of ATS-A are significantly higher than those of the TNCLS baseline, further confirming the effectiveness of our method. In the Zh2En task, ATS-A outperforms CLSMT across all three dimensions. Its conciseness score is comparable to that of CLSMS, yet ATS-A yields more informative and fluent summaries. In the En2Zh task, ATS-A again outperforms CLSMS on all three dimensions. While the informativeness and conciseness scores of CLSMT are comparable to those of ATS-A, ATS-A produces more fluent summaries. Overall, ATS-A surpasses both CLSMS and CLSMT in the Zh2En task, and in the En2Zh task it outperforms CLSMS while performing on par with CLSMT.

But heartless circumstance could not leave entire Gabriel’s happiness of this morning. The rams, old ewes, and two-shear ewes had duly undergone their stripping, and the men were proceeding with the shearlings and hogs, when Oak’s belief that she was going to stand pleasantly by and time him through another performance was painfully interrupted by Farmer Boldwood’s appearance in the extremest corner of the barn. Nobody seemed to have perceived his entry, but there he certainly was. Boldwood always carried with him a social atmosphere of his own, which everybody felt who came near him; and the talk, which Bathsheba’s presence had somewhat suppressed, was now totally suspended.

Yet the austerity of fortune would not suffer Silas’s morning contentment to remain whole. The tups, the matronly ewes, and the twice-shorn stock had already been denuded of their wool, and the hands were just commencing upon the yearlings and wethers, when Thorne’s fancy that Elara might remain to witness his skill through a further labor was sharply broken by the manifestation of Master Sterling in the barn's remotest alcove. No eye had marked his approach, yet his presence was now an established fact. Sterling moved within a singular aura of solemnity that cast a shadow over all in his vicinity; and the conversation, which Elara’s arrival had merely tempered, was now utterly extinguished.

Had Juliet so seen her love tokens dishonoured the sooner would she have sought the lethean herbs of the good apothecary.

Had Juliet watched her love tokens so dishonored, she would have sought the good apothecary’s lethean herbs all the sooner.

But she was too busy in her mind, carving out a career for him that would at least be possible, to ask what the ultimate something was which he had hinted at. There was no career for him in literature. Of that she was convinced. He had proved it to-day, with his amateurish and sophomoric productions. He could talk well, but he was incapable of expressing himself in a literary way. She compared Tennyson, and Browning, and her favorite prose masters with him, and to his hopeless discredit. Yet she did not tell him her whole mind. Her strange interest in him led her to temporize. His desire to write was, after all, a little weakness which he would grow out of in time. Then he would devote himself to the more serious affairs of life. And he would succeed, too. She knew that. He was so strong that he could not fail—if only he would drop writing.

She was too preoccupied with mapping out a feasible path for him to bother asking about that vague ambition he’d alluded to. Literature held no future for him; of that, she felt certain. His clumsy, adolescent efforts earlier that day had only confirmed it. Though he was a gifted talker, he lacked the ability to translate those thoughts into a literary form. When she measured him against the likes of Tennyson, Browning, or her most beloved prose stylists, he fell embarrassingly short. Still, she held back her full judgment. Her unusual fascination with him prompted her to stall for time. This urge to write was merely a trivial flaw, a phase he would eventually move past. Once he did, he could focus on the more substantial pursuits of the world. She was confident he would thrive then. His inner strength was so profound that failure seemed impossible—provided he abandoned his pen.

And you will see how truly I spoke of myself just now, in saying I had no feelings, and that all the relations I hold with my fellow-creatures are mere business relations, when you reflect that I have never seen you since. No; you have been the ward of Tellson’s House since, and I have been busy with the other business of Tellson’s House since. Feelings! I have no time for them, no chance of them. I pass my whole life, miss, in turning an immense pecuniary Mangle.”

“Miss Hale, that was indeed I. No; you have been the ward of Hargrove & Sons Merchant Bank all this while, and I have been swamped with the rest of Hargrove & Sons’ affairs ever since. Attachments? I have no time to spare for them, no opportunity to nurture them. I spend my entire existence, miss, turning the crank of a colossal, coin-clogged mangle.”

And so Martin’s rôle was arranged for him, while he, led on by Arthur and Norman, was meditating an extravagance. They were going out for a ride into the hills Sunday morning on their wheels, which did not interest Martin until he learned that Ruth, too, rode a wheel and was going along. He did not ride, nor own a wheel, but if Ruth rode, it was up to him to begin, was his decision; and when he said good night, he stopped in at a cyclery on his way home and spent forty dollars for a wheel. It was more than a month’s hard-earned wages, and it reduced his stock of money amazingly; but when he added the hundred dollars he was to receive from the _Examiner_ to the four hundred and twenty dollars that was the least _The Youth’s Companion_ could pay him, he felt that he had reduced the perplexity the unwonted amount of money had caused him. Nor did he mind, in the course of learning to ride the wheel home, the fact that he ruined his suit of clothes. He caught the tailor by telephone that night from Mr. Higginbotham’s store and ordered another suit. Then he carried the wheel up the narrow stairway that clung like a fire-escape to the rear wall of the building, and when he had moved his bed out from the wall, found there was just space enough in the small room for himself and the wheel.

Elias’s part in the weekend plan was already settled for him, even as he, egged on by Finn and Jasper, was plotting a little splurge of his own. The two boys had planned a Sunday morning bicycle ride out into the foothills, a plan that had not piqued Elias’s interest at all until he heard Lila would be joining them, and that she rode a bike herself. Elias had never learned to ride, nor did he own a bicycle, but if Lila rode, he decided, there was no question he would learn too. He said his goodnights to the group, then detoured to a bike shop on his walk home and dropped forty dollars on a new model. That sum was more than he earned in a full month of backbreaking work, and it cut his available cash sharply, but when he tallied the hundred dollars coming to him from the _Herald_ alongside the minimum four hundred twenty he would get from _Young Folks' Quarterly_, he was relieved to whittle down the awkward surplus of money that had been weighing on him as of late. He didn’t even care that he tore and stained his good suit terribly while fumbling to learn to ride the bike the rest of the way home. That same night, he called his tailor from the pay phone at Mr. Hollister’s general store and put in an order for a new suit. After that, he hauled the bicycle up the narrow staircase bolted like a fire escape to the back of his apartment building, and once he shifted his bed a few inches away from the wall, he found his tiny room had just enough floor space left for both him and his new purchase.

He danced like a faun; he introduced manner and style and atmosphere; his words came trippingly upon his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in succession with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan brought.

So she had her point; but for Terry O’Sullivan the evening’s honours came one after another in quick succession. He tossed back his curls, smiled, and moved with effortless ease through the seven exercises for gaining grace in your own room before an open window, ten minutes a day. He danced like a faun; he brought in elegance and style and atmosphere; the words danced lightly on his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in a row with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan had brought.

“While I was overcome by these feelings, I left the spot where I had committed the murder, and seeking a more secluded hiding-place, I entered a barn which had appeared to me to be empty. A woman was sleeping on some straw; she was young, not indeed so beautiful as her whose portrait I held, but of an agreeable aspect and blooming in the loveliness of youth and health. Here, I thought, is one of those whose joy-imparting smiles are bestowed on all but me. And then I bent over her and whispered, ‘Awake, fairest, thy lover is near—he who would give his life but to obtain one look of affection from thine eyes; my beloved, awake!’

While I was still swayed by these emotions, I left the place where I had committed the murder, and, in search of a more hidden refuge, entered a barn that had seemed to me deserted. There, upon a bed of straw, a woman was sleeping. She was young, not indeed so lovely as the one whose portrait I held, yet pleasing in appearance and radiant with the freshness of youth and health. Here, I thought, was one of those to whom those joyful smiles are granted that are denied to me. Then I bent over her and murmured, “Awake, fairest; thy lover is at hand—he who would give his life to win but one glance of affection from thine eyes. My beloved, awake!”

Mr. Direck changed again, and after using some embrocation that Mrs. Britling recommended very strongly, came down in a black jacket and a cheerfully ample black tie. He had a sense of physical well-being such as he had not experienced since he came aboard the liner at New York. The curious thing was that it was not quite the same sense of physical well-being that one had in America. That is bright and clear and a little dry, this was--humid. His mind quivered contentedly, like sunset midges over a lake--it had no hard bright flashes--and his body wanted to sit about. His sense of intimacy with Cecily increased each time he looked at her. When she met his eyes she smiled. He'd caught her style now, he felt; he attempted no more compliments and was frankly her pupil at hockey and Badminton. After supper Mr. Britling renewed his suggestion of an automobile excursion on the Monday.

Mr. Sterling emerged transformed, having applied a pungent liniment at Mrs. Thorne’s insistent suggestion, now attired in a somber dinner jacket and a generously knotted silk tie. He was suffused with a bodily contentment he had not tasted since departing the New York docks. Yet, this vigor possessed a quality distinct from the transatlantic variety. Where the American spirit felt crystalline and parched, this English vitality was—moist. His thoughts hovered with a quiet restlessness, like evening flies above a pool—devoid of sharp brilliance—and his limbs craved only repose. His bond with Elara matured with every shared look. Whenever their gazes locked, she offered a grin. He had grasped her tempo at last; abandoning his clumsy flatteries, he became her humble apprentice in the arts of the court and green. Following the evening meal, Mr. Thorne revisited his proposal for a motorcar outing on Monday.

Monseigneur, one of the great lords in power at the Court, held his fortnightly reception in his grand hotel in Paris. Monseigneur was in his inner room, his sanctuary of sanctuaries, the Holiest of Holiests to the crowd of worshippers in the suite of rooms without. Monseigneur was about to take his chocolate. Monseigneur could swallow a great many things with ease, and was by some few sullen minds supposed to be rather rapidly swallowing France; but, his morning’s chocolate could not so much as get into the throat of Monseigneur, without the aid of four strong men besides the Cook.

Monseigneur, a powerful lord of the Court, was holding his fortnightly levee at his opulent Parisian mansion. He remained sequestered in his private suite, a sanctum sanctorum to the fawning masses gathered in the adjoining rooms. Monseigneur was about to consume his chocolate. While he was a man who could swallow much with little effort—and was even rumored by a few bitter critics to be busily devouring the wealth of France—his morning beverage could not reach his throat without the assistance of four robust attendants working alongside the Chef.

“I know you cannot. But he stole in in my absence and robbed me. Why didn’t he win you away before, when nobody would have been grieved?—when nobody would have been set tale-bearing. Now the people sneer at me—the very hills and sky seem to laugh at me till I blush shamefully for my folly. I have lost my respect, my good name, my standing—lost it, never to get it again. Go and marry your man—go on!”

“I understand you can’t. But he crept in while I was gone and plundered me. Why didn’t he take you earlier, when no one would have mourned?—when no one would have whispered stories. Now the folk mock me—the very hills and heavens appear to jeer at me until I flush with disgrace at my own foolishness. I’ve forfeited my honor, my reputation, my place—lost it, and I’ll never reclaim it. Go and wed your man—go ahead!”

He took the ferry across to San Francisco and headed straight for a hiring hall. “Anything at all—I’ve got no skilled trade,” he said to the clerk, just as another man walked in, turned out with a touch of dandyism, the way certain laborers dress when they’ve an inborn taste for something finer. The clerk gave a dispirited shake of his head.

This obscure, obsolete, superseded country figures in Domesday. Its condition is recorded therein as that of heathy, furzy, briary wilderness—“Bruaria.” Then follows the length and breadth in leagues; and, though some uncertainty exists as to the exact extent of this ancient lineal measure, it appears from the figures that the area of Egdon down to the present day has but little diminished. “Turbaria Bruaria”—the right of cutting heath-turf—occurs in charters relating to the district. “Overgrown with heth and mosse,” says Leland of the same dark sweep of country.

This forgotten, outworn, bypassed tract of earth is entered in the Great Survey. Its character is preserved there as a scrubby, thorn-choked, desolate wasteland—“Erica.” Then follow its dimensions in archaic leagues; and, though the true scale of those ancient units remains a matter of some debate, the figures indicate that the reach of Blackwood Moor has scarcely contracted since that distant era. “Privilegium Fodiendi”—the entitlement to harvest peat—is cited in old scrolls pertaining to the region. “Clothed in bracken and gray lichen,” noted Camden of this same brooding expanse of land.

I say by me—I see that your cunning associate suppressed my name, although for aught he knew, it would be quite strange to your ears. When he was rescued by me, then, and lay recovering from sickness in my house, his strong resemblance to this picture I have spoken of, struck me with astonishment. Even when I first saw him in all his dirt and misery, there was a lingering expression in his face that came upon me like a glimpse of some old friend flashing on one in a vivid dream. I need not tell you he was snared away before I knew his history—”

“By me,” said Mr. Brownlow. “I told you before long I should interest you. By me, I mean—I could see that your crafty companion kept my name back, though for all he knew it might have sounded entirely unfamiliar to you. When I rescued him, and he was afterward lying ill and recovering in my house, the likeness he bore to the portrait I have mentioned astonished me. Even when I first laid eyes on him, filthy and wretched as he was, there was still something in his face that seemed to me like the sudden sight of an old friend in the midst of a vivid dream. I need hardly tell you that he was spirited away before I had learned his story—”

He followed mechanically to the end of the dock, and sat down on a chair. The man pointed it out, or he would not have seen it.

He gazed longingly at their faces as they exited, one after the other, as if trying to gauge which way the consensus was tilting; but the effort was futile. The guard touched his shoulder. He moved like an automaton toward the end of the dock and sat down. He wouldn't have even seen the chair if the man hadn't gestured toward it.

metadata.title: SyntaxGym: An Online Platform for Targeted Evaluation of Language Models

text: SyntaxGym facilitates the seamless construction and execution of controlled experiments regarding the syntactic proficiency of language models for linguists, while simultaneously enabling NLP researchers to benchmark their models against established standards. Upon submitting targeted syntactic evaluation suites to the platform, users receive automated assessments based on the language models integrated within the Gym. The platform further processes and graphically represents these evaluative outcomes. It serves to bridge the technical requirements of (a) deploying large-scale neural network language models with (b) the specialized linguistic expertise of researchers necessary for designing rigorous, theoretically substantive psycholinguistic experiments.
