text: In pursuance of this determination, little Oliver, to his excessive astonishment, was released from bondage, and ordered to put himself into a clean shirt. He had hardly achieved this very unusual gymnastic performance, when Mr. Bumble brought him, with his own hands, a basin of gruel, and the holiday allowance of two ounces and a quarter of bread. At this tremendous sight, Oliver began to cry very piteously: thinking, not unnaturally, that the board must have determined to kill him for some useful purpose, or they never would have begun to fatten him up in that way.

Following through on this decision, little Oliver, to his utter amazement, was freed from his confinement and told to change into a fresh shirt. He had barely completed this remarkably unfamiliar physical feat when Mr. Bumble personally delivered a bowl of gruel and the special holiday portion of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Confronted by this overwhelming sight, Oliver began to weep most sorrowfully, convinced, not unreasonably, that the board must have resolved to dispatch him for some practical end—otherwise they would never have started to fatten him up in such a manner.

The unseen force was drawing him fast to itself, now, and all the tides and winds were setting straight and strong towards it. He left his two letters with a trusty porter, to be delivered half an hour before midnight, and no sooner; took horse for Dover; and began his journey. “For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name!” was the poor prisoner’s cry with which he strengthened his sinking heart, as he left all that was dear on earth behind him, and floated away for the Loadstone Rock.

A hidden power now pulled him swiftly toward its core, every current and gust aligning with fierce purpose in that single direction. He entrusted his two letters to a reliable porter, with strict orders they be handed over no earlier than half an hour before midnight; then he mounted his horse at Dover and set out. “For the love of Heaven, for justice, for mercy, for the honour of your noble name!”—that desperate prisoner’s plea was what he used to steady his faltering spirit as he abandoned everything precious in this world and drifted toward the Loadstone Rock.

It was at this time, at the lowest ebb, that the smooth-running editorial machine broke down. A cog must have slipped or an oil-cup run dry, for the postman brought him one morning a short, thin envelope. Martin glanced at the upper left-hand corner and read the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart gave a great leap, and he suddenly felt faint, the sinking feeling accompanied by a strange trembling of the knees. He staggered into his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that moment came understanding to him how people suddenly fall dead upon receipt of extraordinarily good news.

It was then, at the very bottom of the trough, that the smooth-working editorial machine finally failed. Some gear must have slipped, or an oil cup must have gone dry, for one morning the postman handed him a short, narrow envelope. Martin looked at the upper left corner and saw the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart leaped violently, and at once he felt faint, that dropping sensation accompanied by an odd trembling in his knees. He made his way unsteadily to his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that instant he understood how people can suddenly die on receiving news that is extraordinarily good.

Meantime, it was getting hotter and hotter in there. You see, the sun was beating down and warming up the iron more and more all the time. Well, when you are hot, that way, every little thing irritates you. When I trotted, I rattled like a crate of dishes, and that annoyed me; and moreover I couldn't seem to stand that shield slatting and banging, now about my breast, now around my back; and if I dropped into a walk my joints creaked and screeched in that wearisome way that a wheelbarrow does, and as we didn't create any breeze at that gait, I was like to get fried in that stove; and besides, the quieter you went the heavier the iron settled down on you and the more and more tons you seemed to weigh every minute. And you had to be always changing hands, and passing your spear over to the other foot, it got so irksome for one hand to hold it long at a time.

Meanwhile, it was growing hotter and hotter inside. The sun was beating down and steadily heating the iron more and more. And when you are that hot, everything begins to fret you. If I trotted, I clattered like a box of dishes, and that vexed me; besides, I could not bear that shield of mine slapping and banging, now against my breast, now about my back. If I fell into a walk, my joints would creak and squeal in that weary way a wheelbarrow does, and since we made no breeze at that pace, I was in danger of being roasted in that furnace. Worse still, the more quietly you went, the more the iron seemed to settle on you, and the more tons you appeared to weigh with every passing minute. And all the while you had to keep shifting your hands and changing your spear from one foot to the other, for it became so tiresome to hold it in one hand very long.

After that, in order to win his favor, she always addressed him first and often gave him a little drink money, which he was quick to accept.

I’m not marrying, and I’m not—well, loving without marrying, though I’ve done my share of that in the past. I’m sorry I came here to-day and met you. But it can’t be helped now, and I never expected it would turn out this way.

“And precisely for that reason,” he said softly, “I’m not going to. You are so broad and magnanimous that you put me to shame and make me try to rise to your generosity. I’m not going to marry, and I’m not going to—well, love without marriage, though I’ve had my own part of that sort of thing in the past. I’m sorry I came here today and found you. But it can’t be helped now, and I never imagined it would end like this.

The maltster, after having lain down in his clothes for a few hours, was now sitting beside a three-legged table, breakfasting off bread and bacon. This was eaten on the plateless system, which is performed by placing a slice of bread upon the table, the meat flat upon the bread, a mustard plaster upon the meat, and a pinch of salt upon the whole, then cutting them vertically downwards with a large pocket-knife till wood is reached, when the severed lump is impaled on the knife, elevated, and sent the proper way of food.

The maltster had stretched out fully dressed for a short rest and was now seated at a rickety three-legged table, making his breakfast of bread and bacon. He followed the plateless method: laying a slice of bread directly on the wood, setting the meat flat atop it, spreading a layer of mustard over the meat, and scattering a pinch of salt across everything. Then, using a hefty pocket-knife, he sliced straight down until the blade met the table, speared the severed morsel on the knife-point, lifted it, and dispatched it along its rightful path.

Gabriel was already among the turgid, prostrate forms. He had flung off his coat, rolled up his shirt-sleeves, and taken from his pocket the instrument of salvation. It was a small tube or trochar, with a lance passing down the inside; and Gabriel began to use it with a dexterity that would have graced a hospital surgeon. Passing his hand over the sheep’s left flank, and selecting the proper point, he punctured the skin and rumen with the lance as it stood in the tube; then he suddenly withdrew the lance, retaining the tube in its place. A current of air rushed up the tube, forcible enough to have extinguished a candle held at the orifice.

Gabriel was already moving among the swollen, prostrate sheep. He’d flung his coat aside, rolled his shirtsleeves up to his elbows, and drawn the life-saving implement from his pocket. It was a small trocar: a hollow tube fitted with a sharp lance running through its center, and Gabriel wielded it with the precise dexterity that would do a seasoned hospital surgeon proud. He ran his hand over the sheep’s left flank to find the correct spot, then drove the lance, still housed in the tube, through the skin and distended rumen. He withdrew the lance in a single sharp movement, leaving the tube fixed in place. A forceful stream of air rushed up through the tube, powerful enough to snuff out a candle held directly at its opening.

Exceedingly red-eyed and grim, as if he had been up all night at a party which had taken anything but a convivial turn, Jerry Cruncher worried his breakfast rather than ate it, growling over it like any four-footed inmate of a menagerie. Towards nine o’clock he smoothed his ruffled aspect, and, presenting as respectable and business-like an exterior as he could overlay his natural self with, issued forth to the occupation of the day.

Jerry Cruncher, his eyes bloodshot and his face severe as though he'd spent the night at a gathering that had soured into something far from festive, merely picked at his breakfast instead of eating it, muttering over the food like some caged beast in a zoo. As nine o'clock drew near, he composed his disheveled demeanor, and, cloaking his true nature beneath the most decent and industrious front he could muster, stepped out to take up the day's work.

“You are a noble heart. Did I say we could depend upon no better man? Tell her, to-night, what you know of her danger as involving her child and her father. Dwell upon that, for she would lay her own fair head beside her husband’s cheerfully.” He faltered for an instant; then went on as before. “For the sake of her child and her father, press upon her the necessity of leaving Paris, with them and you, at that hour. Tell her that it was her husband’s last arrangement. Tell her that more depends upon it than she dare believe, or hope. You think that her father, even in this sad state, will submit himself to her; do you not?”

“You’ve got such an honorable, good heart. Didn’t I say there’s no man we could depend on more than you? Tonight, tell her everything you know about the danger she’s facing, the threat that puts both her child and her father at risk right alongside her. Lean into that point, because she’d cheerfully lay down her own life without hesitation to stay by her husband’s side.” His voice wavered for a quick instant, then he continued just as he had before. “For the sake of her child and her father, make her understand how critical it is that she leave Paris at that exact hour, bringing both of them with you. Tell her this was her husband’s final arrangement for her. Let her know more rides on this than she could ever dare to believe, or even hope for. You think her father, even in this fragile, sorrowful state, will follow her lead, won’t you?”

“Now, gentlemen,” he continued, as the newcomers drew up to the table, “there’s five have entered, and we want four more to make up the number. I think, by the faces of those gentlemen who have just come in, that they are shrewd enough to take advantage of this rare opportunity of beautifying their ladies at a very trifling expense.”

“Observe, my friends,” the speaker resumed as the fresh arrivals gathered round the board. “We have five names inscribed already, and we seek but four more to complete the tally. I perceive in the countenances of these newcomers a certain sagacity—surely they are astute enough to grasp this fleeting chance to adorn their fair companions with finery for a mere pittance.”

“Now, if I had been stung by ten adders I should hardly have lost a day’s work for’t,” said Grandfer Cantle. “Such is my spirit when I am on my mettle. But perhaps ’tis natural in a man trained for war. Yes, I’ve gone through a good deal; but nothing ever came amiss to me after I joined the Locals in four.” He shook his head and smiled at a mental picture of himself in uniform. “I was always first in the most galliantest scrapes in my younger days!”

“If I’d been bitten by a dozen vipers, I doubt I’d have missed even a day’s labor over it,” Grandfer Cantle declared. “That’s the kind of fire I have when my pride’s at stake. Still, I suppose it comes naturally to a man drilled for battle. Lord knows, I’ve weathered plenty—but nothing ever truly rattled me once I signed up with the Local Volunteers in ’04.” He gave a slow shake of his head, smiling as he conjured an image of himself in regimentals. “Back in my youth, I was always the first to leap into the very thickest of the fray!”

He dared not go near Ruth’s neighborhood in the daytime, but night found him lurking like a thief around the Morse home, stealing glimpses at the windows and loving the very walls that sheltered her. Several times he barely escaped being caught by her brothers, and once he trailed Mr. Morse down town and studied his face in the lighted streets, longing all the while for some quick danger of death to threaten so that he might spring in and save her father. On another night, his vigil was rewarded by a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, and her arms raised as she fixed her hair before a mirror. It was only for a moment, but it was a long moment to him, during which his blood turned to wine and sang through his veins. Then she pulled down the shade. But it was her room—he had learned that; and thereafter he strayed there often, hiding under a dark tree on the opposite side of the street and smoking countless cigarettes. One afternoon he saw her mother coming out of a bank, and received another proof of the enormous distance that separated Ruth from him. She was of the class that dealt with banks. He had never been inside a bank in his life, and he had an idea that such institutions were frequented only by the very rich and the very powerful.

He did not venture near Ruth’s neighborhood during the day, but at night he lingered around the Morse house like a prowler, watching the windows and feeling a sense of reverence for the very walls that protected her. He narrowly avoided being spotted by her brothers on several occasions, and once he even followed Mr. Morse into the city, studying the man’s face beneath the streetlamps while secretly hoping for some fatal accident to occur so that he might rush in and save her father’s life. One night, his persistence was rewarded when he caught a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, her arms lifted as she styled her hair in front of a mirror. It lasted only a moment, yet for him, that moment stretched on as his blood turned to wine and raced through his heart. Then she lowered the shade. Now that he knew which room was hers, he returned often, hiding beneath a dark tree on the far side of the street and smoking one cigarette after another. One afternoon, he happened to see her mother leaving a bank, which served as another reminder of the vast social gulf between them. She belonged to the class of people who used banks; he had never once entered such a building, believing they were reserved solely for the most affluent and powerful members of society.

“I am not afraid to die, Citizen Evrémonde, but I have done nothing. I am not unwilling to die, if the Republic which is to do so much good to us poor, will profit by my death; but I do not know how that can be, Citizen Evrémonde. Such a poor weak little creature!”

“I’m not afraid to die, Citizen Evrémonde, but I’ve done nothing wrong. I don’t even mind losing my life, if the Republic that’s supposed to bring so much good to us poor folks actually benefits from my death; but I have no idea how that could possibly be the case, Citizen Evrémonde. I’m just such a poor, weak little creature, after all.”

Its owner went downstairs into the courtyard, got into his carriage, and drove away. Not many people had talked with him at the reception; he had stood in a little space apart, and Monseigneur might have been warmer in his manner. It appeared, under the circumstances, rather agreeable to him to see the common people dispersed before his horses, and often barely escaping from being run down. His man drove as if he were charging an enemy, and the furious recklessness of the man brought no check into the face, or to the lips, of the master. The complaint had sometimes made itself audible, even in that deaf city and dumb age, that, in the narrow streets without footways, the fierce patrician custom of hard driving endangered and maimed the mere vulgar in a barbarous manner. But, few cared enough for that to think of it a second time, and, in this matter, as in all others, the common wretches were left to get out of their difficulties as they could.

The Count descended to the stone quadrangle, stepped into his waiting coach, and sped off. He had found few companions at the gala; he had stood in a pocket of isolation, and the Cardinal’s reception of him had been notably frigid. In light of this, it seemed to afford him a certain grim satisfaction to see the dregs of the city flee before his galloping horses, their lives held by a thread as they scrambled from his path. His driver steered with the ferocity of a cavalryman in mid-charge, yet this lethal speed provoked neither a frown nor a word of caution from the master. A murmur of protest was sometimes heard, even in that unhearing city and muted century, suggesting that in these tight thoroughfares lacking walkways, the haughty tradition of high-speed travel brutally crippled the poor. However, no one of consequence gave the matter a second thought, and in this, as in all things, the wretched populace was left to endure their misery as best they could.

Can’t you see that we’re both Pups in the Wood and the missis is the cruel uncle after you with the dish towel and me with the flea liniment and a pink bow to tie on my tail. Why not cut that all out and be pards forever more?”

You scruffy, woodchuck-hunting old mutt," I said to him. "You moon-howling, rabbit-tracking, egg-thieving beagle, don't you realize I have no desire to leave you? Can't you see we are both like those pups lost in the forest, while the mistress plays the part of the wicked uncle, chasing you with a kitchen towel and coming for me with flea ointment and a pink ribbon for my tail? Why don't we just give all that up and be partners for good?

Out from his tent rode great Sir Sagramor, an imposing tower of iron, stately and rigid, his huge spear standing upright in its socket and grasped in his strong hand, his grand horse's face and breast cased in steel, his body clothed in rich trappings that almost dragged the ground--oh, a most noble picture. A great shout went up, of welcome and admiration.

Out from his pavilion rode the mighty Sir Almeric, a lofty pillar of steel, grave and unbending, with his long lance set upright in its rest and held fast in his powerful hand; his great horse, too, had its face and chest armored in steel, while its body was hung with rich trappings that nearly swept the earth—truly a splendid sight. A loud acclaim arose then, full of greeting and wonder.

“As there is ample time I will tell you, though you know well enough. I mean that it is extraordinary that you should be alone in my absence. Tell me, now, where is he who was with you on the afternoon of the thirty-first of August? Under the bed? Up the chimney?”

“Since the hour permits, I shall be explicit, though you surely grasp my intent. It strikes me as singularly odd that you should be found quite so solitary during my time away. Pray, inform me: where is that gentleman who shared your company on the final afternoon of August? Is he concealed beneath the divan? Or perhaps wedged within the flue?”

He recalled her conduct towards him throughout the last few weeks, when they had often been working together in the garden, just as they had formerly done when they were boy and girl under his mother’s eye. What if her interest in him were not so entirely that of a relative as it had formerly been? To Yeobright any possibility of this sort was a serious matter; and he almost felt troubled at the thought of it. Every pulse of loverlike feeling which had not been stilled during Eustacia’s lifetime had gone into the grave with her. His passion for her had occurred too far on in his manhood to leave fuel enough on hand for another fire of that sort, as may happen with more boyish loves. Even supposing him capable of loving again, that love would be a plant of slow and laboured growth, and in the end only small and sickly, like an autumn-hatched bird.

He thought back to her manner with him over the past several weeks, when they had often laboured side by side in the garden, just as they used to do as boy and girl watched over by his mother. What if her care for him were no longer merely that of a kinswoman, as it had once been? To Yeobright any hint of such a shift was a grave affair, and the notion almost unsettled him. Every pulse of romantic feeling that had not been extinguished while Eustacia lived had been buried with her. His love for her had struck too late in his manhood to leave behind enough tinder for another blaze of that kind, as can occur with younger, more boyish affections. Even if he were capable of loving again, that love would be a slow and toilsome growth, stunted and sickly in the end, like a bird hatched in autumn.

It was entirely against his habits of mind to hide anything--more particularly an entanglement with a difficult proposition--but he perceived quite clearly that neither Cecily nor Mr. Britling were really to be trusted to listen calmly to what, under happier circumstances, might be a profoundly interesting moral complication. Yet it was not in his nature to conceal; it was in his nature to state.

To harbor a secret was contrary to Arthur’s every instinct—especially one involving a complex ethical dilemma—but he recognized that Beatrice and Professor Vance were ill-equipped to entertain with composure a matter that, in a more serene setting, would be a compelling philosophical problem. Yet he was not made for silence; he was made for the explicit declaration.

The havoc continued an hour and a half, and unimaginable was the destruction of substantials. Of the chief feature of the feast --the huge wild boar that lay stretched out so portly and imposing at the start--nothing was left but the semblance of a hoop-skirt; and he was but the type and symbol of what had happened to all the other dishes.

The carnage lasted for an hour and a half, leaving behind a level of culinary devastation that was hard to fathom. Of the banquet’s centerpiece—the massive wild boar that had looked so grand and stout when the meal began—nothing remained but a skeletal frame resembling a hoop-skirt. This ruin was merely a symbol of the wreckage that had overtaken every other dish on the table.

This is rather intuitive considering the synthetic nature of many of the foreign languages in the dataset, wherein a single long word in the foreign language may translate into 4-5 words on the English side, as in this translation from tΛckotoyatih in the Mexican language Zoque to the English only for the tooth.

text: The left panel of Figure 1 presents statistics regarding sentence length. While the majority of training and test pairs are relatively brief, there is significant variation in length. This variability arises because the dataset’s puzzles range from individual word translations to noun-modifier phrases and full sentences. English sentences are generally longer (median of 4) than their translations (median of 2). This discrepancy is intuitive given the synthetic nature of many languages in the dataset, where a single complex word often corresponds to four or five English words; for instance, the Zoque term *tΛckotoyatih* translates to the four-word English phrase "only for the tooth."

“You can resist, and resist again! Instead of hating you I could, I think, mourn for and pity you, if you were contrite, and would confess all. Forgive you I never can. I don’t speak of your lover—I will give you the benefit of the doubt in that matter, for it only affects me personally. But the other—had you half-killed _me_, had it been that you wilfully took the sight away from these feeble eyes of mine, I could have forgiven you. But _that’s_ too much for nature!”

You may fight back, and keep fighting! If you showed remorse and laid everything bare, I believe I could grieve for you and feel compassion rather than hatred. But forgiveness is beyond me. I’ll say nothing of your lover—on that point I’ll grant you the benefit of the doubt, since it touches only my own heart. Yet the other matter—if you had half-murdered me, if you had deliberately stolen the light from these weak eyes of mine, I might have pardoned you. But that deed goes past what nature can endure!

Soapy had confidence in himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was shaven, and his coat was decent and his neat black, ready-tied four-in-hand had been presented to him by a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could reach a table in the restaurant unsuspected success would be his. The portion of him that would show above the table would raise no doubt in the waiter’s mind. A roasted mallard duck, thought Soapy, would be about the thing—with a bottle of Chablis, and then Camembert, a demi-tasse and a cigar. One dollar for the cigar would be enough. The total would not be so high as to call forth any supreme manifestation of revenge from the café management; and yet the meat would leave him filled and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

Soapy had unshakable confidence in every part of himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was clean-shaven, his coat was presentable, and his crisp pre-tied black four-in-hand tie had been a gift from a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could make his way to a table in the restaurant without raising suspicion, his gambit would be a success. Every inch of him visible above the table would give the waitstaff no cause for doubt. A roasted mallard duck, Soapy mused, would be just the thing—paired with a bottle of Chablis, followed by Camembert cheese, a demi-tasse, and a cigar. One dollar would be more than enough for the cigar. The total bill would not be steep enough to provoke any excessive retaliation from the café management, yet the rich meat would leave him sated and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

Charley wiped his own eyes, which were rather blurred with moisture, and stepped closer to the casement. “Mr. Venn is asking Christian Cantle to sing,” he replied, “and Christian is moving about in his chair as if he were much frightened at the question, and his father has struck up a stave instead of him.”

Charley brushed a hand across his damp lashes, the pane before him swimming faintly, and drew nearer to the window. “It’s Mr. Venn,” he said, “putting the same request to Christian Cantle, and Christian is shifting in his seat like a man half-scared out of his wits, while his father has broken into a song in his place.”

“Because I didn’t know,” he answered. “I never dreamed of lepers. When I deserted the schooner and landed on the beach, I headed inland for some place of hiding. For three days I lived off guavas, _ohia_-apples, and bananas, all of which grew wild in the jungle. On the fourth day I found the trail—a mere foot-trail. It led inland, and it led up. It was the way I wanted to go, and it showed signs of recent travel. At one place it ran along the crest of a ridge that was no more than a knife-edge. The trail wasn’t three feet wide on the crest, and on either side the ridge fell away in precipices hundreds of feet deep. One man, with plenty of ammunition, could have held it against a hundred thousand.

I simply wasn't aware," he replied. "The possibility of lepers never entered my mind. After I abandoned the schooner and reached the shore, I struck out for the interior, looking for a place to hide. For three days, I sustained myself on wild guavas, *ohia*-apples, and bananas gathered from the jungle. On the fourth day, I stumbled upon a path—a simple foot-trail. It climbed steadily as it led further inland. This was the direction I intended to take, and the trail showed evidence of recent use. At one point, it followed the spine of a ridge that was as narrow as a knife's edge. The path atop the crest was less than three feet wide, with vertical cliffs on either side dropping hundreds of feet into the depths. A lone man, well-supplied with ammunition, could have defended that position against a hundred thousand.

About this time we retired to our house at Belrive. This change was particularly agreeable to me. The shutting of the gates regularly at ten o’clock and the impossibility of remaining on the lake after that hour had rendered our residence within the walls of Geneva very irksome to me. I was now free. Often, after the rest of the family had retired for the night, I took the boat and passed many hours upon the water. Sometimes, with my sails set, I was carried by the wind; and sometimes, after rowing into the middle of the lake, I left the boat to pursue its own course and gave way to my own miserable reflections. I was often tempted, when all was at peace around me, and I the only unquiet thing that wandered restless in a scene so beautiful and heavenly—if I except some bat, or the frogs, whose harsh and interrupted croaking was heard only when I approached the shore—often, I say, I was tempted to plunge into the silent lake, that the waters might close over me and my calamities for ever. But I was restrained, when I thought of the heroic and suffering Elizabeth, whom I tenderly loved, and whose existence was bound up in mine. I thought also of my father and surviving brother; should I by my base desertion leave them exposed and unprotected to the malice of the fiend whom I had let loose among them?

It was during this season that we sought refuge at our villa in Clairmont. This transition brought a profound relief to my spirit; the rigid locking of the town gates at the stroke of ten and the prohibition against lingering upon the waves past twilight had made our urban dwelling feel like a stifling cage. Now, liberty was mine. Frequently, once my kin had surrendered to sleep, I would unmoor the skiff and spend the dark watches upon the great expanse of the water. On certain nights, I let the canvas catch the breeze to carry me where it would; on others, having pulled the oars until I reached the center of the lake, I allowed the vessel to drift aimlessly while I surrendered to the agony of my own dark reflections. 

Amidst the profound stillness of the world, I felt myself the sole discordant note in an otherwise celestial landscape—save perhaps for the erratic flight of a bat or the coarse, rhythmic pulse of the marsh-dwellers that fell silent as I neared the bank. In those moments, I was frequently seized by the urge to cast myself into the obsidian depths, seeking a final shroud beneath the waves to bury my griefs and my crimes eternally. Yet, the memory of the noble and enduring Isabella stayed my hand; I loved her with a desperate devotion, and her very life was intertwined with my own. I considered, too, my father and my remaining brother. Could I, through a coward's exit, abandon them to the cruel whims of the demon I had unleashed upon their world?

The turnkey fired the little pile, which blazed high and hot. Stooping again to come out at the low-arched door, they left it burning, and retraced their way to the courtyard; seeming to recover their sense of hearing as they came down, until they were in the raging flood once more.

The jailer lit the small heap, and it flared up fiercely, bright and warm. Bending once more to pass through the low, curved doorway, they abandoned the fire as it burned and made their way back to the yard; their hearing seemed to return gradually as they descended, until they stood again amid the roaring deluge.

They are specialists, and once they have finished, you will have clean chimneys without needing to know anything about how chimneys are built.”

Not that, all this time, his mind was, for an instant, free from one oppressive overwhelming sense of the grave that opened at his feet; it was ever present to him, but in a vague and general way, and he could not fix his thoughts upon it. Thus, even while he trembled, and turned burning hot at the idea of speedy death, he fell to counting the iron spikes before him, and wondering how the head of one had been broken off, and whether they would mend it, or leave it as it was. Then, he thought of all the horrors of the gallows and the scaffold—and stopped to watch a man sprinkling the floor to cool it—and then went on to think again.

It wasn’t as if, the entire time, his mind ever stopped being hounded by the heavy, suffocating awareness of an open grave waiting right at his feet. That dread hung over him constantly, but it felt fuzzy and unfocused, like he couldn’t pin his mind down to really grapple with it. So even as he shook all over, his skin prickling with feverish heat at the thought of how soon he would die, he found himself counting the iron spikes set before him, puzzling over how the tip of one had snapped clean off, and if someone would eventually fix it or just leave it broken like that. Next, his mind drifted to every grisly detail of the gallows and the execution scaffold—until he paused to stare at a man sprinkling water across the floor to bring down the temperature—before his wandering thoughts pulled him right back to his grim ruminations.

This conversation had passed in a hollow of the heath near the old Roman road, a place much frequented by Thomasin. And it might have been observed that she did not in future walk that way less often from having met Venn there now. Whether or not Venn abstained from riding thither because he had met Thomasin in the same place might easily have been guessed from her proceedings about two months later in the same year.

Their exchange had taken place in a dip on the moorland near the ancient Roman road, a spot Thomasin often visited. One might have noticed that her encounter with Venn did nothing to discourage her from walking there in the days that followed. As for whether Venn avoided the place or was drawn back to it because of her, the answer could be easily inferred from Thomasin’s behavior roughly two months later that same year.

By an effort, I contained my joy when Clarence brought me the news. But he--he could not contain his. His mouth gushed delight and gratitude in a steady discharge--delight in my good fortune, gratitude to the king for this splendid mark of his favor for me. He could keep neither his legs nor his body still, but pirouetted about the place in an airy ecstasy of happiness.

I managed to hide my excitement with some effort when Clarence delivered the news. But he—he was unable to restrain himself at all. A constant stream of joy and gratitude poured from his lips; he was thrilled by my good luck and deeply thankful to the king for such a grand gesture of favor. He couldn't keep his body still for even a moment, spinning around the room in a lighthearted state of pure bliss.

We were at our supper of beef stew and dried apples when he trotted in as if on the heels of a dog team, and made one of the mess at our table. With the freedom of the camps he assaulted our ears and claimed the fellowship of men lost in the wilds of a hash house. We embraced him as a specimen, and in three minutes we had all but died for one another as friends.

We sat around our evening meal of mutton broth and withered apricots when he strode through the doorway like a man chasing a sled pack, and settled himself among the clutter of our board. Armed with the rough liberty of trail life, he stormed our silence and demanded the brotherhood of souls adrift in a greasy cook tent. We took him up as a curiosity, and within moments had nearly perished of devotion, each for the other, like sworn companions.

“O Clym! please don’t go setting down as my fault what is your obstinate wrongheadedness. If you wished to connect yourself with an unworthy person why did you come home here to do it? Why didn’t you do it in Paris?—it is more the fashion there. You have come only to distress me, a lonely woman, and shorten my days! I wish that you would bestow your presence where you bestow your love!”

“O Clym! Don’t lay the blame on me for what is simply your own stubborn folly. If you were determined to tie yourself to someone beneath you, why come back here to do it? Why not do it in Paris?—it’s far more the custom there. You have returned only to wound me, a solitary woman, and cut short my days. I wish you would give your company where you give your heart!”

“Heh-heh! well, I wish to noise nothing abroad—nothing at all,” murmured Poorgrass, diffidently. “But we be born to things—that’s true. Yet I would rather my trifle were hid; though, perhaps, a high nater is a little high, and at my birth all things were possible to my Maker, and he may have begrudged no gifts.... But under your bushel, Joseph! under your bushel with ’ee! A strange desire, neighbours, this desire to hide, and no praise due. Yet there is a Sermon on the Mount with a calendar of the blessed at the head, and certain meek men may be named therein.”

“Ah—well, I’ve no wish to trumpet anything abroad—nothing whatever,” murmured Moulden, falteringly. “Yet we are born to our lots—that much is certain. Still, I’d sooner my small portion stayed concealed; though, perhaps, a lofty nature is a trifle lofty, and at my making all gifts lay within the Creator’s power, and He may have withheld none.... But keep it beneath your hat, Thomas! beneath your hat, I say! A queer instinct, friends, this urge to hide, and no credit in it. And yet there stands the Beatitudes with a roll of the favoured at the start, and certain unassuming souls may find their place there.”

“Yes, he has,” said Clym in a neutral tone. “Well, I wish with all my heart that I could say, marry him. But I cannot forget what my mother thought on that matter, and it goes rather against me not to respect her opinion. There is too much reason why we should do the little we can to respect it now.”

“That’s right, he has,” Clym said in a flat, uninflected tone. “I wish with all my heart I could tell you to marry him, truly. But I cannot shake the memory of my mother’s feelings on this matter, and it goes against my conscience to dismiss her judgment. We have more than enough cause now to do whatever small things we can to respect her wishes.”

So Mr. Britling was out of conceit with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn and entrusted with the badge of a special constable. The duties of a special constable were chiefly not to understand what was going on in the military sphere, and to do what he was told in the way of watching and warding conceivably vulnerable points. He had also to be available in the event of civil disorder. Mr. Britling was provided with a truncheon and sent out to guard various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-westward of Matching's Easy. It was never very clear to him what he would do if he found a motor-car full of armed enemies engaged in undermining a culvert, or treacherously deepening some strategic ford. He supposed he would either engage them in conversation, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both things simultaneously. But as he really did not believe for a moment that any human being was likely to tamper with the telegraphs, telephones, ways and appliances committed to his care, his uncertainty did not trouble him very much. He prowled the lonely lanes and paths in the darkness, and became better acquainted with a multitude of intriguing little cries and noises that came from the hedges and coverts at night. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, who seemed more than half inclined to give him battle for its prey until he cowed and defeated it with the glare of his electric torch....

So Mr. Britling had grown disenchanted with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn in and entrusted with the insignia of a special constable. The business of a special constable consisted chiefly in not understanding what was happening in the military world, and in doing what he was told, in the matter of keeping watch and guard over points that might conceivably be vulnerable. He was also to be available in the event of civil disturbance. Mr. Britling was supplied with a truncheon and sent out to protect various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-west of Matching’s Easy. It was never quite plain to him what he was supposed to do if he came upon a motor-car full of armed enemies busied in weakening a culvert, or maliciously deepening some strategic ford. He imagined that he would either strike up a conversation with them, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both at once. But since he did not for a moment believe that any human creature was likely to meddle with the telegraphs, telephones, roads, and appliances entrusted to his care, his uncertainty did not much disturb him. He ranged the lonely lanes and paths by night, and came to know better a host of odd little cries and rustlings that rose from the hedges and coverts after dark. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, which seemed more than half disposed to make battle for its prey until he overawed and routed it with the beam of his electric torch....

Some studies adopt different attention or memory mechanisms to catch the emphasis on discourse arguments (Mnih et al., 2014; . exploit the hierarchical attention to capture the focus of different granularities. build upon a semantic memory to store knowledge in the distributed fashion for the task. However, these models have only considered the two arguments independently without the interaction information.

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: The effective representation of arguments is a fundamental aspect of our task. Most existing research maps arguments into dense, continuous vector spaces using various neural architectures, ranging from basic CNNs and RNNs to more complex neural networks (Zhang et al., 2015; Qin et al., 2016b; Rutherford et al., 2016). Certain approaches have adopted distinct attention or memory mechanisms to emphasize key elements within discourse arguments (Mnih et al., 2014); for instance, hierarchical attention is used to capture focus across different granularities, while semantic memory is utilized to store task-related knowledge in a distributed manner. Nevertheless, these models typically process the two arguments as independent entities, thereby overlooking the critical interaction information between them.

“God! We are animals! Brute-beasts!” Martin muttered aloud, as he watched the progress of the fight. It was to him, with his splendid power of vision, like gazing into a kinetoscope. He was both onlooker and participant. His long months of culture and refinement shuddered at the sight; then the present was blotted out of his consciousness and the ghosts of the past possessed him, and he was Martin Eden, just returned from sea and fighting Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He suffered and toiled and sweated and bled, and exulted when his naked knuckles smashed home.

"Good God, we are nothing but beasts! Savage animals!" Martin whispered under his breath while tracking the brawl's every move. To his keen, perceptive eyes, the violence unfolded like a flickering kinetoscope. He found himself trapped between being a witness and a combatant. His recent months of intellectual growth and social polish recoiled from the spectacle, but then the present moment dissolved. The shadows of his history took hold of him, and he was once again the Martin Eden of old, back from a long voyage and trading blows with Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He felt the agony, the labor, the sweat, and the gore, experiencing a primal triumph each time his bare knuckles struck home.

And Mr. Direck saw the perambulator waiting--the mysterious perambulator--a little in the dark beyond the front door.

Teddy and the sons moved quickly to blow out the candles and clear the room, as two drivers emerged from the kitchen area to bring Mr. Lawrence Carmine’s car and the guests' vehicle around to the main entrance. The entire party drifted lightheartedly through the moonlight and past the massive trees toward the front of the house. There, Mr. Direck noticed the baby carriage—that enigmatic pram—waiting in the shadows just past the front door.

“Our friend was born in this village. This town then included the adjoining towns to the north and south. The region was then more sparsely settled, although many houses standing then have disappeared. While he was sleeping peacefully in the cradle, while he was opening on the world childhood's wide, wondering eyes, those great men whose names are our perpetual benediction were planning for freedom from a foreign yoke. While he was passing through the happy years of early-childhood, the fierce clash of arms resounded through the little strip of territory which then made up the United States. I can hardly realize that, as a child, he heard as a fresh, new, real story, of the deeds of Lexington, from the lips of men then young who had been in the fight, or listened as one of an eager group gathered about the fireside, or in the old, now deserted tavern on the turnpike, to the story of Bunker Hill.

He entered the world in this very village. In those days, the settlement stretched to include the neighboring communities both north and south. The countryside was more thinly populated back then, and many homes that once stood have since vanished. As he lay dreaming in his cradle, as his young eyes first opened wide with childhood’s wonder, the great figures whose names we still bless were already scheming to cast off foreign rule. Throughout the carefree seasons of his early youth, the clangor of battle echoed across the narrow stretch of land that then formed the United States. I can scarcely grasp that, as a boy, he heard the exploits of Lexington told as fresh, immediate news from the lips of men still young who had fought there, or sat among a breathless circle by the hearth—or in the old, now-abandoned roadside inn—listening to the tale of Bunker Hill.

Meanwhile, Oliver gradually throve and prospered under the united care of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the kind-hearted Mr. Losberne. If fervent prayers, gushing from hearts overcharged with gratitude, be heard in heaven—and if they be not, what prayers are!—the blessings which the orphan child called down upon them, sunk into their souls, diffusing peace and happiness.

Oliver continued to flourish and grow strong, nurtured by the collective kindness of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the generous Mr. Losberne. If sincere prayers born of profound gratitude find their way to heaven—and truly, what else could?—the blessings the boy invoked for his protectors took root in their hearts, spreading a quiet sense of serenity and contentment.

The Britling child hated Buster Brown. He regarded Buster Brown as a totally unnecessary infant. He detested the way he wore his hair and the peculiar cut of his knickerbockers and--him. He thought Buster Brown the one drop of paraffin in the otherwise delicious feast of the Sunday Supplement. But he was a diplomatic child.

The Thorne boy loathed Sonny Skyler. He considered the perky comic-strip character an entirely unnecessary little upstart. He despised the blunt, too-neat cut of his hair, the stiff, old-fashioned cut of his knickerbockers, and—him, full stop. He thought Sonny Skyler was the single drop of turpentine spoiling the otherwise lovely spread of the Sunday funnies section. But he was a tactful, shrewd child.

He had no opportunity of saying, or so much as thinking, anything else, until he was clear of the Old Bailey; for, the crowd came pouring out with a vehemence that nearly took him off his legs, and a loud buzz swept into the street as if the baffled blue-flies were dispersing in search of other carrion.

He had no chance to utter a single word, or even form a coherent stray thought, until he was well clear of the City Sessions Hall; for the throng poured out behind him with such ferocious force it nearly swept him off his feet, and a raucous droning buzz spilled out onto the street as if a swarm of thwarted carrion flies had dispersed to hunt for fresh rotting quarry.

Our approach, on the other hand, performs equally well in both settings.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: Unlike our method, this approach cannot learn sub-word units on the source side, so a larger vocabulary is needed to reduce out-of-vocabulary tokens. Saunders et al. (2018) interleave words with syntactic representations, producing longer sequences that necessitate gradient accumulation for effective training, yet this yields only a +0.5 BLEU improvement on WAT Japanese–English when using Transformer ensembles. Currey and Heafield (2019) introduce two straightforward data augmentation strategies for integrating source-side syntax: one effective in low-resource scenarios, and another that attains strong performance on a large-scale task. By contrast, our method performs consistently well across both settings.

“I will think of this,” she said hurriedly. “Whether I can honestly make use of you as a friend, or must close with you as a lover—that is what I must ask myself. If I wish to go and decide to accept your company I will signal to you some evening at eight o’clock punctually, and this will mean that you are to be ready with a horse and trap at twelve o’clock the same night to drive me to Budmouth harbour in time for the morning boat.”

“I’ll give this some thought,” she said quickly. “I need to ask myself whether I can honestly rely on you as a friend, or if I must commit to you as a lover. If I decide to leave and want you to come with me, I’ll signal you at exactly eight o’clock one evening. That will be your cue to be ready with a horse and carriage at midnight that same night to drive me to the Budmouth docks in time for the morning boat.”

We test what demographic profiles author attribute tools predict for the translations from various commercially available machine translation tools. We find that independent of the MT system and the translation quality, the predicted demographics differ systematically when translating into English. On average, translations make the author seem substantially older and more male. Translating from English into any of the other languages shows more mixed results, but similar tendencies. for Data Science and Analytics (BIDSA) and the Data and Marketing Insights (DMI) unit.

metadata.title: "You Sound Just Like Your Father" Commercial Machine Translation Systems Include Stylistic Biases

text: We examine which demographic profiles author attribution tools predict for translations produced by a range of commercially available machine translation systems. We find that, regardless of the MT system and the translation quality, the predicted demographics shift systematically when translating into English. On average, translations make the author appear substantially older and more male. Translating from English into other languages yields more mixed results, but similar tendencies are evident.

He spoke to a postboy who was dozing under the gateway; and who, after hearing what he wanted, referred him to the ostler; who after hearing all he had to say again, referred him to the landlord; who was a tall gentleman in a blue neckcloth, a white hat, drab breeches, and boots with tops to match, leaning against a pump by the stable-door, picking his teeth with a silver toothpick.

He addressed a courier boy nodding off under the entry arch, who, after grasping the gist of his request, sent him to speak to the stable groom; the groom, after listening to his full query a second time, directed him to the inn’s landlord, a lanky gentleman wearing a navy cravat, a crisp white hat, taupe breeches, and boots with tops cut to the same neutral hue, propped against a water pump beside the stable threshold, working a silver toothpick between his teeth.

We use the same underlying neural architecture as our edit model to make sure that the difference in results comes from the amount of training data and from using edit of representations only: a two-layer, bi-directional GRU that encodes the sequence of tokens in the method, and an attention-based GRU decoder with a copy mechanism that decodes a sequence of comment tokens. We expect the incorporation of more complicated architectures, e.g., tree-based (Alon et al., 2019) and graph-based (Fernandes et al., 2019) encoders which exploit AST structure, can be applied to both an edit model and a generation model, which we leave for future work.

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

text: To ensure that observed differences in experimental results are driven solely by variations in training data volume and the exclusive use of representation-level edits rather than structural design choices, we adopt an identical underlying neural architecture to that of our proposed edit model: this backbone consists of a two-layer bidirectional GRU which encodes input sequences of method tokens, coupled with an attention-augmented GRU decoder fitted with a copy mechanism to decode output sequences of comment tokens. We anticipate that the incorporation of more complex architectural designs, including the tree-based encoders proposed in Alon et al. (2019) and graph-based encoders introduced in Fernandes et al. (2019), both of which exploit abstract syntax tree (AST) structure, can be readily applied to both edit and standard generation models, a direction we leave for future investigation.

Thus encouraged, the matron drew near to the brink; and even Mr. Bumble himself, impelled by curiousity, ventured to do the same. The turbid water, swollen by the heavy rain, was rushing rapidly on below; and all other sounds were lost in the noise of its plashing and eddying against the green and slimy piles. There had once been a water-mill beneath; the tide foaming and chafing round the few rotten stakes, and fragments of machinery that yet remained, seemed to dart onward, with a new impulse, when freed from the obstacles which had unavailingly attempted to stem its headlong course.

Encouraged by these words, the matron stepped toward the river's edge, and even Mr. Bumble, driven by a growing inquisitiveness, dared to follow her. The murky water, bloated by the heavy rains, surged rapidly below, and every other sound was lost to the roar of its churning and splashing against the slick, mossy pilings. A water-mill had once stood there; the current, foaming and agitated as it struck the few rotting stakes and broken fragments of machinery that remained, seemed to leap forward with a sudden burst of speed once it was clear of the obstacles that had unsuccessfully tried to block its frantic path.

Gabriel was about to advance and restore the missing article when an unexpected performance induced him to suspend the action for the present. The path, after passing the cowshed, bisected the plantation. It was not a bridle-path—merely a pedestrian’s track, and the boughs spread horizontally at a height not greater than seven feet above the ground, which made it impossible to ride erect beneath them. The girl, who wore no riding-habit, looked around for a moment, as if to assure herself that all humanity was out of view, then dexterously dropped backwards flat upon the pony’s back, her head over its tail, her feet against its shoulders, and her eyes to the sky. The rapidity of her glide into this position was that of a kingfisher—its noiselessness that of a hawk. Gabriel’s eyes had scarcely been able to follow her. The tall lank pony seemed used to such doings, and ambled along unconcerned. Thus she passed under the level boughs.

She stepped to the edge of the thicket, craning her neck first one way, then peering over to the far side of the hawthorn hedge. Elias was just preparing to step out and hand back the linen handkerchief she’d dropped moments earlier, when an unanticipated bit of whimsy made him suspend his movement for the time being. The dirt track, once it curved past the weathered stone cowshed, cut straight through the middle of the old pine plantation. It was no wide bridle road—only a narrow footpath worn soft by decades of walkers, and the pine boughs stretched out level barely seven feet above the packed earth, making it impossible for anyone on horseback to sit upright and pass beneath without getting swatted by needles. The young woman, who wore a simple cotton frock instead of a formal riding habit, glanced over her shoulders twice, as if confirming no other soul was nearby to see her, then flipped nimbly backward until she lay flat across the pony’s broad back, her head lolling near its tail, her boots propped against its shoulders, her gaze fixed straight up at the patchy blue sky between the boughs. She shifted into the position as fast as a kingfisher diving for minnows, as quiet as a hawk gliding on a thermal. Elias could barely track the movement, quick as it was. The tall, rangy pony seemed perfectly accustomed to such antics, and plodded along unconcerned without so much as a twitch of its ear. Thus she slipped unhindered beneath the low-hanging boughs.

Most open-domain neural response generation systems suffer from content or style inconsistency (Li et al., 2016b; Gao et al., 2019c) , lack of long-term contextual information (Serban et al., 2017) , and blandness (Li et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2018; Qin et al., 2019) . While these issues can be alleviated by modelling strategies specifically designed to boost information content, a transformer-based architecture like GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) , which uses a multi-layer self-attentive mechanism to allow fully-connected cross-attention to the full context in a computationally efficient manner, seems like a natural choice for exploring a more general solution. Transformer models, for example, allow long-term dependency information to be better be preserved across time (Radford et al., 2018) , thereby improving content consistency. They also have higher model capacity due to their deep structure (up to 48 layers in GPT-2) and are more effective in leveraging large-scale datasets (more than 100 million training instances) than RNN-based approaches (Vaswani et al., 2017) .

metadata.title: DIALOGPT : Large-Scale Generative Pre-training for Conversational Response Generation

text: Most open-domain neural response generation systems suffer from content or style inconsistency (Li et al., 2016b; Gao et al., 2019c), a lack of long-term contextual information (Serban et al., 2017), and blandness (Li et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2018; Qin et al., 2019). Although these problems can be mitigated by modeling strategies specifically designed to increase information content, a transformer-based architecture such as GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018), which employs a multilayer self-attentive mechanism to provide fully connected cross-attention over the full context in a computationally efficient way, appears to be a natural choice for investigating a more general solution. Transformer models, for instance, can better preserve long-term dependency information over time (Radford et al., 2018), thereby improving content consistency. They also offer greater model capacity because of their deep architecture (up to 48 layers in GPT-2) and are more effective than RNN-based approaches at exploiting large-scale datasets (more than 100 million training instances) (Vaswani et al., 2017).

Ruth had only a small thing to ask—just that Norman had planned to escort her to a lecture that evening, but he’d come down with a headache, and she felt so let down, and she still held the tickets, and if he happened to be free, might he be kind enough to accompany her instead?

“Well, somebody has—and look here, neighbours,” Gabriel, though one of the quietest and most gentle men on earth, rose to the occasion, with martial promptness and vigour. “That’s my fist.” Here he placed his fist, rather smaller in size than a common loaf, in the mathematical centre of the maltster’s little table, and with it gave a bump or two thereon, as if to ensure that their eyes all thoroughly took in the idea of fistiness before he went further. “Now—the first man in the parish that I hear prophesying bad of our mistress, why” (here the fist was raised and let fall as Thor might have done with his hammer in assaying it)—“he’ll smell and taste that—or I’m a Dutchman.”

“Well, someone definitely did—and listen up, neighbors,” Gabriel, who was normally one of the quietest, gentlest men you’d ever meet, rose to the occasion with all the brisk, unyielding fire of a soldier jumping into action. “This right here is my fist.” He set his knuckles down, only slightly smaller than an average loaf of bread, exactly dead center of the maltster’s small table, rapping it twice against the worn wood as if to make sure every pair of eyes in the room got the full picture of exactly what that fist could do before he kept speaking. “Now—the first man in this parish I hear badmouthing our mistress, well” —he lifted the fist and slammed it back down the way Thor might have tested the weight of his hammer—“he’s gonna taste and feel every bit of it, and that’s a promise. If I don’t follow through, you can call me a liar.”

It is scarcely necessary to state that Gabriel could not avoid noting the farmer’s bearing to-night towards their entertainer. Yet there was nothing exceptional in his actions beyond what appertained to his time of performing them. It was when the rest were all looking away that Boldwood observed her; when they regarded her he turned aside; when they thanked or praised he was silent; when they were inattentive he murmured his thanks. The meaning lay in the difference between actions, none of which had any meaning of itself; and the necessity of being jealous, which lovers are troubled with, did not lead Oak to underestimate these signs.

Gabriel could hardly fail to mark, without need of saying so, the farmer’s conduct that evening toward their hostess. Still, there was nothing singular in his gestures save the moment he chose for them. While the others averted their gaze, Boldwood watched her; when they looked her way, he glanced off; if they offered thanks or compliments, he kept quiet; if their attention strayed, he whispered his gratitude. The import resided in the contrast among acts that, taken singly, signified nothing; and the jealous watchfulness that afflicts lovers did not prevent Oak from reading these tokens truly.

As I said this I suddenly beheld the figure of a man, at some distance, advancing towards me with superhuman speed. He bounded over the crevices in the ice, among which I had walked with caution; his stature, also, as he approached, seemed to exceed that of man. I was troubled; a mist came over my eyes, and I felt a faintness seize me, but I was quickly restored by the cold gale of the mountains. I perceived, as the shape came nearer (sight tremendous and abhorred!) that it was the wretch whom I had created. I trembled with rage and horror, resolving to wait his approach and then close with him in mortal combat. He approached; his countenance bespoke bitter anguish, combined with disdain and malignity, while its unearthly ugliness rendered it almost too horrible for human eyes. But I scarcely observed this; rage and hatred had at first deprived me of utterance, and I recovered only to overwhelm him with words expressive of furious detestation and contempt.

Just as I spoke, I suddenly glimpsed a figure in the distance, rushing toward me with a speed that defied nature. He leaped effortlessly over the deep cracks in the ice where I had trodden so tentatively; as he neared, his towering height seemed to far surpass that of any mortal. I felt a surge of unease; my vision clouded and a sudden lightheadedness took hold of me, yet the freezing mountain air quickly revived my spirit. As the shape drew closer—a sight both magnificent and repulsive—I recognized the miserable creature I had fashioned. I shuddered with a combination of fury and dread, determined to wait for him and then grapple in a fight to the finish. He reached me; his expression revealed a profound, bitter suffering joined with scorn and ill-will, while his ghastly, inhuman features were almost too revolting for the human eye to endure. But I took little note of this; my intense hatred had at first stolen my breath, and I found my voice only to assault him with a torrent of words filled with violent loathing and disdain.

Wounded, the Knight sank to one knee, as the stage direction required. Men who before this change seemed to have been hid in caves dispersed themselves and were employed in various arts of cultivation. The birds sang in more cheerful notes, and the leaves began to bud forth on the trees. Happy, happy earth! Fit habitation for gods, which, so short a time before, was bleak, damp, and unwholesome. My spirits were elevated by the enchanting appearance of nature; the past was blotted from my memory, the present was tranquil, and the future gilded by bright rays of hope and anticipations of joy.”

The gentle rains and tender warmth of spring transformed the face of the land. Figures who had earlier seemed buried in shadowy hollows now emerged and scattered across the fields, devoting themselves to the various tasks of cultivation. The birds lifted their voices in lighter, gladder strains, and fresh leaves began to unfurl upon the branches. Blessed, blessed earth! A dwelling worthy of gods, where only a brief span before all had been raw, sodden, and sickly. My heart rose with the enchanting beauty of the world around me; the past was erased from my mind, the present lay serene, and the future gleamed with bright shafts of hope and the promise of joy.

A place of business in London like Tellson’s place of business in Paris, would soon have driven the House out of its mind and into the Gazette. For, what would staid British responsibility and respectability have said to orange-trees in boxes in a Bank courtyard, and even to a Cupid over the counter? Yet such things were. Tellson’s had whitewashed the Cupid, but he was still to be seen on the ceiling, in the coolest linen, aiming (as he very often does) at money from morning to night. Bankruptcy must inevitably have come of this young Pagan, in Lombard-street, London, and also of a curtained alcove in the rear of the immortal boy, and also of a looking-glass let into the wall, and also of clerks not at all old, who danced in public on the slightest provocation. Yet, a French Tellson’s could get on with these things exceedingly well, and, as long as the times held together, no man had taken fright at them, and drawn out his money.

A London banking house that mirrored Tellson’s Paris branch would swiftly have lost its wits and found itself in the Gazette. What would sober British prudence and decorum have made of potted orange-trees adorning a bank courtyard, or a painted Cupid poised above the clerks? Yet there they stood. Tellson’s had coated the little god in whitewash, but he still hovered on the ceiling, clad in the airiest drapery, aiming (as is his eternal habit) at lucre from dawn till dusk. Ruin would surely have followed this heathen boy in Lombard-street, along with the curtained recess tucked behind him, and the mirror sunk into the wall, and the far-from-elderly clerks who broke into dances at the merest excuse. Still, a French Tellson’s managed these flourishes perfectly well, and so long as the age kept its seams intact, no one took alarm or withdrew a single sovereign.

Now don't take offense if I point out that, since we're strangers to you, we need to proceed with some caution. I have to put a few questions to you; just answer honestly and openly, and don't worry. Where do you make your home, when you're there?”

The existing neural network-based models have shown great success in recognizing implicit discourse relations. It mainly includes 1) Basic neural networks (Braud and Denis, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015; can learn the dense vector representations of discourse arguments, which can capture the semantic information to some extent. Further studies exploit different attention or memory mechanisms to capture the critical information of argument pairs. 2) Complex neural models (Chen et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2018) utilize gated relevance networks or neural tensor networks to capture the deeper interactions between two discourse arguments. 3) Joint learning architectures (Qin et al., 2017; Bai and Zhao, 2018; Xu et al., 2019) exploit implicit connective cues, different granularity of text, or topic-level relevant information to improve the discourse relation prediction. However, these approaches still have the following drawbacks: 1) do not make full use of the annotated discourse relation signal to explore the argumentrelation features; 2) neglect the extra information in the low-dimensional continuous embedding space, i.e., the direction or structure information of the vectors.

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: Existing neural network-based models have achieved substantial success in recognizing implicit discourse relations. These models mainly include: 1) basic neural networks (Braud and Denis, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015), which can learn dense vector representations of discourse arguments and thereby capture semantic information to some extent; subsequent studies further employ various attention or memory mechanisms to extract the key information from argument pairs. 2) Complex neural models (Chen et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2018), which use gated relevance networks or neural tensor networks to model the deeper interactions between two discourse arguments. 3) Joint learning architectures (Qin et al., 2017; Bai and Zhao, 2018; Xu et al., 2019), which exploit implicit connective cues, different levels of textual granularity, or topic-level relevant information to improve discourse relation prediction. However, these approaches still have the following drawbacks: 1) they do not fully exploit the annotated discourse relation signal to investigate argument-relation features; 2) they overlook the additional information contained in the low-dimensional continuous embedding space, namely the directional or structural information of the vectors.

This dialogue had taken place at Mr. Lorry’s usual desk, with Monseigneur swarming within a yard or two of it, boastful of what he would do to avenge himself on the rascal-people before long. It was too much the way of Monseigneur under his reverses as a refugee, and it was much too much the way of native British orthodoxy, to talk of this terrible Revolution as if it were the only harvest ever known under the skies that had not been sown--as if nothing had ever been done, or omitted to be done, that had led to it--as if observers of the wretched millions in France, and of the misused and perverted resources that should have made them prosperous, had not seen it inevitably coming, years before, and had not in plain words recorded what they saw. Such vapouring, combined with the extravagant plots of Monseigneur for the restoration of a state of things that had utterly exhausted itself, and worn out Heaven and earth as well as itself, was hard to be endured without some remonstrance by any sane man who knew the truth. And it was such vapouring all about his ears, like a troublesome confusion of blood in his own head, added to a latent uneasiness in his mind, which had already made Charles Darnay restless, and which still kept him so.

This exchange unfolded at Mr. Lorry’s regular work desk, with a crowd of refugee aristocrats clustered barely three feet away, loudly bragging about how they would soon take brutal revenge on the lowborn rabble they blamed for their troubles. That kind of talk was par for the course for these deposed nobles navigating their exile, and just as common among rigid, conventional British thinkers of the time: they spoke of the horrific Revolution as if it were the only harvest in human history that had sprung up entirely unseeded. They acted as if no choice, no failure to act, had paved the way for the uprising; as if the people who’d watched France’s millions of destitute citizens suffer, who’d seen the resources that should have made them thrive hoarded and misused for the elite, hadn’t seen the collapse coming for years, and hadn’t documented their observations in clear, unambiguous writing. All this empty posturing, paired with the aristocrats’ absurd schemes to restore a regime that had already completely devoured itself, alienating every last person on earth and even defying all sense of divine justice in the process, was almost impossible for any sensible person who knew the full truth to tolerate without speaking up. And it was this endless stream of loud, foolish rhetoric swirling right around him, buzzing in his ears like the disorienting thud of blood rushing to his own skull, layered over the quiet, underlying anxiety he already held in his chest, that had first left Charles Darnay restless – and that still kept him that way.
