The coat’s hanging strap was tangled tight, and she wrestled with it in open irritation until she ripped the fabric clean through.

One thing was certain: the Morses had not cared to have him for himself or for his work. Therefore they could not want him now for himself or for his work, but for the fame that was his, because he was somebody amongst men, and—why not?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or so. That was the way bourgeois society valued a man, and who was he to expect it otherwise? But he was proud. He disdained such valuation. He desired to be valued for himself, or for his work, which, after all, was an expression of himself. That was the way Lizzie valued him. The work, with her, did not even count. She valued him, himself. That was the way Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old gang valued him. That had been proved often enough in the days when he ran with them; it had been proved that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His work could go hang. What they liked, and were willing to scrap for, was just Mart Eden, one of the bunch and a pretty good guy.

This much was beyond dispute: the Morses had never wanted him for his own sake or for his writing. So they could hardly want him now for his own sake or for his writing, but only for the renown that clung to him, because he counted among men, and—why pretend otherwise?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or thereabouts. That was how the middle class weighed a man, and what right had he to imagine anything different? Yet he was proud. He scorned such reckoning. He longed to be prized for himself, or for his writing, which was, in the end, a part of himself. That was how Lizzie prized him. With her, the writing did not even enter. She prized him, the man. That was how Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old crew prized him. They had shown it often enough in the days when he ran beside them; they had shown it that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His writing could be damned. What they loved, and would fight for, was simply Mart Eden, one of their own and a decent fellow.

His reason and his propensities had seldom any reciprocating influence, having separated by mutual consent long ago: thence it sometimes happened that, while his intentions were as honourable as could be wished, any particular deed formed a dark background which threw them into fine relief. The sergeant’s vicious phases being the offspring of impulse, and his virtuous phases of cool meditation, the latter had a modest tendency to be oftener heard of than seen.

His judgment and his inclinations had long since ceased to act in concert, having parted, as it were, by mutual agreement many years before; and so it came about that, although his purposes were of the purest kind, some single act would sometimes furnish a shadowy backdrop against which they shone out with uncommon brightness. The corporal’s corrupt moods, being born of sudden impulse, and his better ones the fruit of deliberate reflection, the latter possessed only a quiet habit of being more often spoken of than actually observed.

Under the over-swinging lamps--swinging ever brighter in the better streets, and ever dimmer in the worse--and by lighted shops, gay crowds, illuminated coffee-houses, and theatre-doors, to one of the city gates. Soldiers with lanterns, at the guard-house there. “Your papers, travellers!” “See here then, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, getting down, and taking him gravely apart, “these are the papers of monsieur inside, with the white head. They were consigned to me, with him, at the--” He dropped his voice, there was a flutter among the military lanterns, and one of them being handed into the coach by an arm in uniform, the eyes connected with the arm looked, not an every day or an every night look, at monsieur with the white head. “It is well. Forward!” from the uniform. “Adieu!” from Defarge. And so, under a short grove of feebler and feebler over-swinging lamps, out under the great grove of stars.

Beneath the swaying streetlamps—glowing ever more brilliant in the finer quarters, and ever more feeble in the poorer—past bright shopwindows, lively throngs, lit-up cafés, and theatre entrances, they rolled on toward one of the city gates. At the guardhouse, soldiers stood holding lanterns. “Your documents, travellers!” “Here, if you please, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, climbing down and drawing him soberly aside. “These belong to the gentleman inside, the one with the white hair. He was entrusted to me, along with the gentleman himself, at the—” He lowered his voice; the military lanterns bobbed and flickered, and one was passed into the carriage by a uniformed arm. The eyes above that arm fixed on the white-haired gentleman with a look that was no ordinary glance, by day or night. “All is in order. Proceed!” came from the uniform. “Farewell!” from Defarge. And so, beneath a brief avenue of ever-weakening, swaying lamps, they emerged into the vast avenue of stars.

For Billy Jackson was shining down on her, calm and bright and constant through the skylight. There was no world about her. She was sunk in a pit of blackness, with but that small square of pallid light framing the star that she had so whimsically and oh, so ineffectually named. Miss Longnecker must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Billy Jackson. And yet she could not let it be Gamma.

For Leo Marlow was shining down on her, calm, bright, unwavering, through the slanted glass skylight. The rest of the world had vanished entirely. She was sunk in a hollow of thick, unbroken blackness, with only that small square of pallid light framing the star she’d named on such a whimsical, and oh, so futile impulse. Ms. Hale must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Leo Marlow. And yet she could not bear to let it be Gamma.

“You wretched child! What malice of spirit permits you to give breath to such base rumors? You are a cold and pitiless thing.... But I shall see to it that neither you nor any soul in this parish, nor the borough beyond, dares to utter another word of it!” She broke into a restless stride, pacing a frantic path from the hearth to the threshold and back again.

As soon as they were alone, and the door closed, Thomasin said, turning up her pale, tearful face to him, “It is killing me, this, Damon! I did not mean to part from you in anger at Anglebury this morning; but I was frightened and hardly knew what I said. I’ve not let Aunt know how much I suffered today; and it is so hard to command my face and voice, and to smile as if it were a slight thing to me; but I try to do so, that she may not be still more indignant with you. I know you could not help it, dear, whatever Aunt may think.”

The second the door clicked shut behind them and they were fully alone, Thomasin tilted her pale, tear-streaked face up to Damon. “This whole mess is eating me alive, Damon! I never wanted to leave you on bad terms when we were at Anglebury this morning. I was just so shaken I barely knew what was coming out of my mouth. I haven’t told Aunt a thing about how much I’ve suffered today, either. It’s so hard to keep my expression and voice steady, to smile like this is just some trivial annoyance that doesn’t bother me at all, but I’m forcing myself to do it so she doesn’t get even angrier with you. I know you couldn’t help what happened, dear, no matter what Aunt thinks.”

"Then the raw material is handed over to a cook. He is a common soldier who has been made into a cook by a simple ceremony. He is told, 'You are a cook.' He does his best to be. Usually he roasts or bakes to begin with, guessing when the joint is done, afterwards he hacks up what is left of his joints and makes a stew for next day. A stew is hacked meat boiled up in a big pot. It has much fat floating on the top. After you have eaten your fill you want to sit about quiet. The men are fed usually in a large tent or barn. We have a barn. It is not a clean barn, and just to make it more like a picnic there are insufficient plates, knives and forks. (I tell you, no army people can count beyond eight or ten.) The corporals after their morning's work have to carve. When they have done carving they tell me they feel they have had enough dinner. They sit about looking pale, and wander off afterwards to the village pub. (I shall probably become a corporal soon.) In these islands before the war began there was a surplus of women over men of about a million. (See the publications of the Fabian Society, now so popular among the young.) None of these women have been trusted by the government with the difficult task of cooking and giving out food to our soldiers. No man of the ordinary soldier class ever cooks anything until he is a soldier.... All food left over after the stew or otherwise rendered uneatable by the cook is thrown away. We throw away pail-loads. _We bury meat_....

Running hard to the help of Mr. Raeburn was the youngest Britling boy, a beautiful contrast. It was like a puff ball supporting and assisting a conger eel. In front of Mr. Direck the little stout man was being alert. Teddy was supporting the attack near the middle of the field, crying "Centre!" while Mr. Britling, very round and resolute, was bouncing straight towards the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as swiftly as her sister, was between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man's stick had clashed with Cecily's. Confused things happened with sticks and feet, and the little short man appeared to be trying to cut down Cecily as one cuts down a tree, she tried to pass the ball to her centre forward--too late, and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was flickering back towards Mr. Britling's goal in a rush in which Mr. Direck perceived it was his duty to join.

Running hard to lend help to Mr. Raeburn came the youngest Britling boy, a delightful contrast. He was like a puffball propping and assisting a conger eel. Before Mr. Direck, the little stout man was full of alertness. Teddy was backing the attack near the middle of the field, shouting “Centre!” while Mr. Britling, very round and determined, bounced straight toward the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as fast as her sister, stood between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man’s stick struck Cecily’s. A tangle of sticks and feet followed, and the little short man seemed to be trying to bring Cecily down as one fells a tree. She attempted to pass the ball to her centre forward—too late—and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was darting back toward Mr. Britling’s goal in a rush that Mr. Direck saw it was his duty to join.

"My driving leaves something to be desired," said Mr. Britling with an air of frank impartiality. "But I have only just got this car for myself--after some years of hired cars--the sort of lazy arrangement where people supply car, driver, petrol, tyres, insurance and everything at so much a month. It bored me abominably. I can't imagine now how I stood it for so long. They sent me down a succession of compact, scornful boys who used to go fast when I wanted to go slow, and slow when I wanted to go fast, and who used to take every corner on the wrong side at top speed, and charge dogs and hens for the sport of it, and all sorts of things like that. They would not even let me choose my roads. I should have got myself a car long ago, and driven it, if it wasn't for that infernal business with a handle one had to do when the engine stopped. But here, you see, is a reasonably cheap car with an electric starter--American, I need scarcely say. And here I am--going at my own pace."

“My motoring is not above criticism,” said Mr. Alden with a candor that seemed almost judicial. “But I have only lately come into this machine for myself—after a good many years of hiring cars, that indolent contrivance by which people provide car, chauffeur, fuel, tires, insurance, and Heaven knows what else for a fixed sum each month. It used to weary me beyond expression. I cannot now understand how I endured it so long. They would send me one after another of these trim, disdainful lads, who raced when I wished to crawl and crawled when I wished to race, who took every curve on the wrong hand at full tilt, and pursued dogs and poultry as if it were the finest amusement in the world, and all the rest of it. They would not even suffer me to choose my own roads. I should have bought a car years ago and driven it myself, if it had not been for that abominable business of the crank one had to turn when the engine failed. But here, as you see, is a tolerably inexpensive motor with an electric starter—American, I need hardly tell you. And here I am, at last, proceeding at my own tempo.”

“Yes, that’s the ill fortune o’ it,” said Gabriel, in a distressed tone. “And it is because of that very helplessness that I feel bound to go. Good afternoon, ma’am” he concluded, in evident anxiety to get away, and at once went out of the churchyard by a path she could follow on no pretence whatever.

“Yes, that is the mischance of it,” said Gabriel, his voice full of distress. “And it is precisely because of that helplessness that I feel obliged to go. Good afternoon, ma’am,” he finished, with a plain eagerness to escape, and at once left the churchyard by a path she could by no means pretend to follow.

Mr. Direck observed that _The New Republic_ represented a worthy endeavor from "_la Jeunesse Américaine_." Mr. Britling surveyed the worthy endeavor with a weary, indifferent gaze.

After revising the dataset, we focus on the two open questions: which of the remaining errors can be attributed to the models, and what are potential reasons for misclassifications? To answer these, we first create an annotation task instructing the linguists to annotate model misclassifications with their potential causes (Section 4.1). We then categorize and analyze the causes and formulate testable hypotheses that can be automatically verified (Section 4.2). For the automatic analysis, we implemented a baseline and three state-of-the-art models (Section 4.3).

metadata.title: TACRED Revisited: A Thorough Evaluation of the TACRED Relation Extraction Task

text: Having refined the dataset, we turn to two unresolved questions: which of the persistent errors stem from the models themselves, and what underlying factors might explain these misclassifications? To address them, we first design an annotation protocol in which linguists label model misclassifications with likely causal explanations (Section 4.1). We subsequently classify and examine these causes and derive testable hypotheses amenable to automated verification (Section 4.2). For the automatic investigation, we deploy a baseline alongside three state-of-the-art models (Section 4.3).

The young adventurer was of pleasing presence, and moved serenely and watchfully. By daylight he was a salesman in a piano store. He wore his tie drawn through a topaz ring instead of fastened with a stick pin; and once he had written to the editor of a magazine that “Junie’s Love Test” by Miss Libbey, had been the book that had most influenced his life.

The young wanderer had an agreeable look about him, and he carried himself with a quiet, observant grace. During business hours he sold pianos in a downtown shop. He threaded his necktie through a topaz ring rather than securing it with a pin, and he had once sent a letter to a magazine editor confessing that Miss Libbey’s “Junie’s Love Test” was the book that had most shaped his life.

I passed the night wretchedly. Sometimes my pulse beat so quickly and hardly that I felt the palpitation of every artery; at others, I nearly sank to the ground through languor and extreme weakness. Mingled with this horror, I felt the bitterness of disappointment; dreams that had been my food and pleasant rest for so long a space were now become a hell to me; and the change was so rapid, the overthrow so complete!

I passed the night in misery. At times my pulse throbbed so fast and feebly that I could feel the beating of every artery; at others, I seemed almost to sink to the ground from languor and utter weakness. Along with this terror, I suffered the sharp sting of disappointment. Dreams that had long been my sustenance and my pleasant repose had now become a hell to me; and so swift was the transformation, so total the ruin!

It was a long walk to North Oakland, but it was not until he went up the steps and entered his room that he knew he had walked it. He found himself sitting on the edge of the bed and staring about him like an awakened somnambulist. He noticed “Overdue” lying on the table and drew up his chair and reached for his pen. There was in his nature a logical compulsion toward completeness. Here was something undone. It had been deferred against the completion of something else. Now that something else had been finished, and he would apply himself to this task until it was finished. What he would do next he did not know. All that he did know was that a climacteric in his life had been attained. A period had been reached, and he was rounding it off in workman-like fashion. He was not curious about the future. He would soon enough find out what it held in store for him. Whatever it was, it did not matter. Nothing seemed to matter.

The road to North Berkeley had been a long one, but it was not until he climbed the stairs and let himself into his room that he was aware he had traversed it. He found himself seated on the edge of the bed, gazing about him with the blank wonder of a sleepwalker suddenly roused. On the table he saw “Past Due,” and he drew his chair close and took up his pen. There was in him a strict, almost mathematical need for wholeness. Something remained unfinished. It had been put aside until another matter should be brought to an end. Now that other thing was done, and he would give himself to this remaining labor until it too was done. What he should do afterward he did not know. Only this he knew: that a turning point in his life had been reached. A sentence had ended, and he was setting the final stop upon it with careful, workmanlike hands. He felt no curiosity concerning the future. In due time he would learn what awaited him there. Whatever it might be, it no longer seemed to signify. Nothing seemed to signify.

She had a singular insight into life, considering that she had never mixed with it. There are instances of persons who, without clear ideas of the things they criticize have yet had clear ideas of the relations of those things. Blacklock, a poet blind from his birth, could describe visual objects with accuracy; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave excellent lectures on colour, and taught others the theory of ideas which they had and he had not. In the social sphere these gifted ones are mostly women; they can watch a world which they never saw, and estimate forces of which they have only heard. We call it intuition.

Her understanding of life was remarkably profound, especially given that she had never truly participated in it. There are cases of people who, despite having no direct experience of the subjects they analyze, still possess a keen sense of how those subjects relate to one another. Blacklock, a poet blind from birth, was able to describe the visual world with startling precision; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave masterful lectures on the properties of color, teaching others about concepts they could see while he could not. In the social realm, these gifted individuals are often women; they are able to observe a world they have never set foot in and gauge the strength of forces they have only heard about. We label this gift intuition.

Before, I had only imagined the wretchedness of my desolated home; the reality came on me as a new, and a not less terrible, disaster. I tried to calm Ernest; I enquired more minutely concerning my father, and here I named my cousin.

Unchecked, hot tears spilled down my brother’s cheeks; a searing, all-consuming agony coiled through every inch of my frame. Until that moment, I had only conjured mental images of the crippling wretchedness of our empty, gutted home; the harsh reality struck me as a fresh, no less devastating, catastrophe. I fumbled to soothe Kael, pressing for more precise details about our father, then spoke my cousin’s name aloud to ask after her as well.

Troy stood at the entrance to the booth, where a gipsy-woman was frying pancakes over a little fire of sticks and selling them at a penny a-piece, and looked over the heads of the people within. He could see nothing of Pennyways, but he soon discerned Bathsheba through an opening into the reserved space at the further end. Troy thereupon retreated, went round the tent into the darkness, and listened. He could hear Bathsheba’s voice immediately inside the canvas; she was conversing with a man. A warmth overspread his face: surely she was not so unprincipled as to flirt in a fair! He wondered if, then, she reckoned upon his death as an absolute certainty. To get at the root of the matter, Troy took a penknife from his pocket and softly made two little cuts crosswise in the cloth, which, by folding back the corners left a hole the size of a wafer. Close to this he placed his face, withdrawing it again in a movement of surprise; for his eye had been within twelve inches of the top of Bathsheba’s head. It was too near to be convenient. He made another hole a little to one side and lower down, in a shaded place beside her chair, from which it was easy and safe to survey her by looking horizontally.

Rowan paused at the edge of the stall, where a Romani woman was toasting flatcakes above a small blaze of twigs and vending them for a copper each, and peered across the crowd gathered inside. He caught no glimpse of Merrick, but soon spotted Seraphine through a gap leading to the cordoned-off section at the far end. Rowan then withdrew, circled the pavilion into the shadows, and strained his ears. He could hear Seraphine’s tones just beyond the canvas; she was speaking with a man. A flush crept over his cheeks: surely she was not so shameless as to dally at a carnival! He wondered whether she now counted his dying as a settled fact. To uncover the truth, Rowan drew a small blade from his coat and quietly incised two tiny crossing slits in the fabric, which, when the corners were folded back, yielded an opening no larger than a coin. He brought his face near this peephole, then jerked it away in a start; for his gaze had rested scarcely a foot from the crown of Seraphine’s head. It was far too close for comfort. He cut a second aperture slightly to one side and farther down, in a dim nook beside her seat, from which he could easily and safely observe her by looking straight ahead.

text: He went away vexed with himself, and ashamed of having for this one time in his life done anything which could be called underhand. Poor Boldwood had no more skill in finesse than a battering-ram, and he was uneasy with a sense of having made himself to appear stupid and, what was worse, mean. But he had, after all, lighted upon one fact by way of repayment. It was a singularly fresh and fascinating fact, and though not without its sadness it was pertinent and real. In little more than six years from this time Bathsheba might certainly marry him. There was something definite in that hope, for admitting that there might have been no deep thought in her words to Liddy about marriage, they showed at least her creed on the matter.

He stormed off furious at his own choices, mortified that for the first time in his entire life he’d done something anyone could label sneaky or underhanded. Boldwood was never any good at subtle maneuvering, after all—he had as much tact as a sledgehammer. He squirmed under the conviction that he’d come off not just foolish, but far worse: petty and small. Still, he’d stumbled on one small truth as a sort of consolation prize. It was such a vivid, thrilling little detail, and even if it carried a twinge of melancholy, it was unshakably concrete and relevant. A little over six years from that day, Bathsheba could very well agree to marry him. That hope had a real, tangible weight to it; even if she hadn’t put much careful thought into what she’d told Liddy about her marriage plans, those offhand words still revealed her core beliefs on the subject.

Instead of directly correcting users' essays, Write&Improve (writeandimprove.com) only marks highly-likely incorrect words on the grounds that automated grammatical error correction is still very imprecise. Recently, researchers have begun to apply neural network models to both automated essay scoring (AES) and grammatical error detection (GED), gaining significant improvement (e.g., Dong et al. (2017) ; Rei and Søgaard (2018) ). However, these Web services fall short of providing sufficient "coaching" information (e.g., grammar patterns, collocations, examples) to learners to improve their writing skills.

metadata.title: LinggleWrite: a Coaching System for Essay Writing

text: Rather than directly revising users’ essays, Write&Improve (writeandimprove.com) highlights only highly likely incorrect words, motivated by the fact that automated grammatical error correction remains highly imprecise. In recent years, researchers have started to employ neural network models for both automated essay scoring (AES) and grammatical error detection (GED), achieving substantial gains (e.g., Dong et al. (2017); Rei and Søgaard (2018)). Nevertheless, these web services still fail to deliver enough “coaching” information—such as grammar patterns, collocations, and examples—to help learners improve their writing abilities.

And often, at such times, he would abruptly see slouch in among the company a young hoodlum in square-cut coat and under a stiff-rim Stetson hat. It happened to him at the Gallina Society in Oakland one afternoon. As he rose from his chair and stepped forward across the platform, he saw stalk through the wide door at the rear of the great room the young hoodlum with the square-cut coat and stiff-rim hat. Five hundred fashionably gowned women turned their heads, so intent and steadfast was Martin’s gaze, to see what he was seeing. But they saw only the empty centre aisle. He saw the young tough lurching down that aisle and wondered if he would remove the stiff-rim which never yet had he seen him without. Straight down the aisle he came, and up the platform. Martin could have wept over that youthful shade of himself, when he thought of all that lay before him. Across the platform he swaggered, right up to Martin, and into the foreground of Martin’s consciousness disappeared. The five hundred women applauded softly with gloved hands, seeking to encourage the bashful great man who was their guest. And Martin shook the vision from his brain, smiled, and began to speak.

Often during those moments, he’d suddenly notice a young tough slouching into the gathering, wearing a boxy coat and a stiff-brimmed Stetson. It came upon him one afternoon at the Gallina Society in Oakland. As he got up from his seat and moved toward the front of the stage, he watched the young hoodlum in the boxy coat and rigid-brim hat stride through the broad doorway at the back of the hall. Five hundred elegantly dressed women turned their heads, drawn by the intensity and fixity of Martin’s stare, trying to spot what he saw. But they found only the vacant central aisle. He watched the young rough shamble down that aisle and wondered if he’d take off the stiff brim, which Martin had never yet glimpsed him without. Straight down the aisle he came, then up onto the stage. Martin felt like weeping over that spectral young self when he considered everything the boy still had to face. The figure swaggered across the platform, right up to Martin, and then faded from the foreground of his mind. The five hundred women applauded gently with gloved hands, trying to hearten the shy, celebrated man who was their guest. And Martin shook the image from his thoughts, smiled, and began his speech.

The passage to the Conciergerie was short and dark; the night in its vermin-haunted cells was long and cold. Next day, fifteen prisoners were put to the bar before Charles Darnay’s name was called. All the fifteen were condemned, and the trials of the whole occupied an hour and a half.

The corridor leading to the Conciergerie was short and swallowed up in total darkness; the night spent in its vermin-crawling holding cells dragged on endlessly, sharp with unforgiving cold. The following day, fifteen prisoners were brought before the tribunal before Charles Darnay’s name was finally called. Every single one of the fifteen was condemned, and the full batch of trials wrapped up in only an hour and a half total.

“Yes, Miss Manette is going to be married. But not to an Englishman; to one who, like herself, is French by birth. And speaking of Gaspard (ah, poor Gaspard! It was cruel, cruel!), it is a curious thing that she is going to marry the nephew of Monsieur the Marquis, for whom Gaspard was exalted to that height of so many feet; in other words, the present Marquis. But he lives unknown in England, he is no Marquis there; he is Mr. Charles Darnay. D’Aulnais is the name of his mother’s family.”

Indeed, Mademoiselle Beaumont is to be married. But her choice has not fallen upon an Englishman; she weds a countryman, one who, like herself, is French by birth. And touching upon the fate of poor Baptiste—ah, the miserable soul! it was a savage, savage business—it is a most singular irony that she is to join herself to the nephew of that very Seigneur for whose murder Baptiste was swung so many cubits into the air; in other words, the current Seigneur himself. But he lives in shadow in London, renouncing his rank; there, he is known only as Mr. Julian Vane. Valeray is the name of his mother’s family, which he uses to mask his station.

Mr. Britling returned to the Dower House perplexed and profoundly disturbed. He had a distressful sense that things were far more serious with Teddy than he had tried to persuade Letty they were; that "wounded and missing" meant indeed a man abandoned to very sinister probabilities. He was distressed for Teddy, and still more acutely distressed for Mrs. Teddy, whose every note and gesture betrayed suppositions even more sinister than his own. And that preposterous sense of liability, because he had helped Teddy to get his commission, was more distressful than it had ever been. He was surprised that Letty had not assailed him with railing accusations.

Mr. Halloway retreated to Old Orchard, his mind clouded by a heavy, gnawing unease. He was haunted by the conviction that Leo’s situation was far more precarious than the fragile hopes he had offered Sybilla; "unaccounted for" was a designation that likely hid a brutal and lonely end. He felt a deep sorrow for the boy, yet an even sharper pang for the young woman, whose every word and movement hinted at terrors far darker than his own. Most agonizing of all was that irrational sense of debt, the realization that his own hand had smoothed the path to Leo’s enlistment. He found it remarkable, almost unnerving, that Sybilla had not yet lashed out at him with the accusations he felt he deserved.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

The performance profile of CGC-QG (B6) deviates notably from that of all competing baselines: it attains the highest METEOR and ROUGE-L scores across all evaluated methods, yet ranks lowest on BLEU-1. Since CGC-QG relies on word-level content selection, we find it consistently introduces a large number of extraneous tokens into generated questions, resulting in overly long outputs (a mean length of 33.7 tokens, compared to 17.7 tokens for ground-truth questions and 19.3 tokens for our proposed model) that are either unanswerable or contain semantic inconsistencies. Our approach mitigates this error substantially via node-level content selection guided by semantic relational structure, as quantified in Table 3.
3. While both the SRL-based and DP-based semantic graph architectures (P1 and P2, respectively) achieve state-of-the-art BLEU performance, the DP-based graph model (P2) yields a small but consistent performance gain, with a 3.3% improvement in BLEU-4. A plausible explanation for this gap is that SRL parsing is unable to encode fine-grained semantic information within the graph structure, as SRL parsers frequently produce nodes that comprise long, unsegmented token sequences.

Similarly, the largest N -gram match is used for each test sentence with a threshold N M (s i , s j ) ≥ 5. A similarity threshold EM (s i , s j ) ≥ 0.8 is also employed when retrieving similar sentences using distributed representations. The EM model is trained on the source training data with default fasttext params on 200 dimension, and 20 epochs. The faiss search toolkit is used through python API with exact FlatIP index. Building and retrieval times for each algorithm on a 2M sentences translation memory (Europarl corpus) are provided in Table 1 . Note that all retrieval algorithms are significantly faster than NMT Transformer decoding, thus, implying a very limited decoding overhead.

metadata.title: Boosting Neural Machine Translation with Similar Translations

text: We apply fuzzy matching while excluding exact matches, retaining only the single highest-scoring match when \(FM(s_i, s_j) \geq 0.6\) without approximation. Likewise, for each test sentence we select the largest \(N\)-gram match, using a threshold of \(NM(s_i, s_j) \geq 5\). We also adopt a similarity criterion of \(EM(s_i, s_j) \geq 0.8\) when retrieving semantically similar sentences via distributed representations. The EM model is trained on the source-side training data using the default fastText hyperparameters, with 200-dimensional embeddings and 20 training epochs. For search, we use the Faiss toolkit through its Python API with an exact FlatIP index. Table 1 reports the building and retrieval times for each algorithm on a 2M-sentence translation memory derived from the Europarl corpus. Notably, all retrieval methods are substantially faster than NMT Transformer decoding, indicating a very limited decoding overhead.

After a short return of the stupor in which he had been so long plunged, Oliver: urged by a creeping sickness at his heart, which seemed to warn him that if he lay there, he must surely die: got upon his feet, and essayed to walk. His head was dizzy, and he staggered to and fro like a drunken man. But he kept up, nevertheless, and, with his head drooping languidly on his breast, went stumbling onward, he knew not whither.

Oliver drifted back from the stupor that had held him so long, only to feel a slow, sickening dread coil around his heart—a dread that whispered he would surely die if he stayed motionless. He forced himself upright and tried to walk. Dizziness swam through his head, and he reeled and swayed like a drunkard. Yet he remained on his feet, and with his head lolling heavily against his chest, he staggered forward, blind to where his steps might lead.

We used the pretrained GPT weights but finetuned the encoder and decoder weights on our task. For the RNN components of our hierarchical model, we experimented with both GRU (Chung et al., 2015 ) and LSTM (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) variants. The GRU model had two layers in both sen enc and story enc; the LSTM model had four layers each in sen enc and story enc. Both had two fusion layers and the size of the hidden layers for both model variants was 768. We give the results of both variants on the tasks of sentence generation and sentence discrimination in Table 1 . Both perform similarly, with slightly worse loss for the LSTM variant, but faster training and better generation accuracy. Overall, model performance is strong: the LSTM variant picks out the correct sentence 54% of the time and generates it 46% of the time. This indicates that our architecture successfully captures the structure of stories.

metadata.title: Modelling Suspense in Short Stories as Uncertainty Reduction over Neural Representation

text: We initialized the model with pretrained GPT weights, but fine-tuned the encoder and decoder parameters for our task. For the RNN modules in our hierarchical architecture, we evaluated both GRU (Chung et al., 2015) and LSTM (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) configurations. In the GRU setting, both the sen enc and story enc consisted of two layers; in the LSTM setting, each of sen enc and story enc used four layers. Both variants employed two fusion layers, and the hidden dimensionality was 768 for all model components. Table 1 reports the results of both variants on sentence generation and sentence discrimination. The two models exhibit comparable performance: the LSTM variant yields a marginally higher loss, but trains faster and achieves better generation accuracy. Overall, the results are robust; the LSTM model selects the correct sentence 54% of the time and generates it 46% of the time. These findings suggest that the proposed architecture effectively captures story structure.

“Well, sir!” cried Mr. Lorry, thoroughly heated by his indifference, “business is a very good thing, and a very respectable thing. And, sir, if business imposes its restraints and its silences and impediments, Mr. Darnay as a young gentleman of generosity knows how to make allowance for that circumstance. Mr. Darnay, good night, God bless you, sir! I hope you have been this day preserved for a prosperous and happy life.--Chair there!”

“Now look here, sir!” exclaimed Mr. Hale, his cheeks flushed hot with frustration at the other man’s cool unconcern, “trade is an honorable calling, a thoroughly reputable one. And if, sir, trade demands its own measure of discretion, its unspoken reserve, its unavoidable hindrances, Mr. Thorne, as a young man of generous spirit, certainly understands how to make allowances for such a state of things. Mr. Thorne, a very good night to you, and may heaven smile on you, sir! I trust this day has found you well, and that a long, prosperous, joyful life lies ahead of you.--Bring the chair over here!”

Martin talked for fifteen minutes with him, nor could Ruth have wished better behavior on her lover’s part. Not once did his eyes flash nor his cheeks flush, while the calmness and poise with which he talked surprised her. But in Martin’s estimation the whole tribe of bank cashiers fell a few hundred per cent, and for the rest of the evening he labored under the impression that bank cashiers and talkers of platitudes were synonymous phrases. The army officer he found good-natured and simple, a healthy, wholesome young fellow, content to occupy the place in life into which birth and luck had flung him. On learning that he had completed two years in the university, Martin was puzzled to know where he had stored it away. Nevertheless Martin liked him better than the platitudinous bank cashier.

Martin chatted with him for a full fifteen minutes, and Ruth couldn’t have asked for better behavior from her boyfriend. He never once let his eyes flash with irritation or his cheeks flush with frustration, and the steady, unflappable poise he carried through the whole conversation caught her completely off guard. But as far as Martin was concerned, the entire profession of bank cashiers plummeted a good few hundred points in his esteem, and for the rest of the evening he couldn’t shake the belief that “bank cashier” was just another term for someone who spouts empty, overused platitudes. The army officer, by contrast, struck him as good-natured and unpretentious, a healthy, well-adjusted young man who was perfectly content occupying the spot in life that birth and luck had tossed him into. When he learned the officer had completed two years of university, Martin was puzzled to figure out where he’d stored all that education, since he gave no obvious sign of it in casual interaction. Even so, Martin still liked him far better than the trite, cliché-ridden bank cashier.

Three years ago Kraft, Bill Judkins (a poet), and I took our meals at Cypher’s, on Eighth Avenue. I say “took.” When we had money, Cypher got it “off of” us, as he expressed it. We had no credit; we went in, called for food and ate it. We paid or we did not pay. We had confidence in Cypher’s sullenness and smouldering ferocity. Deep down in his sunless soul he was either a prince, a fool or an artist. He sat at a worm-eaten desk, covered with files of waiters’ checks so old that I was sure the bottomest one was for clams that Hendrik Hudson had eaten and paid for. Cypher had the power, in common with Napoleon III. and the goggle-eyed perch, of throwing a film over his eyes, rendering opaque the windows of his soul. Once when we left him unpaid, with egregious excuses, I looked back and saw him shaking with inaudible laughter behind his film. Now and then we paid up back scores.

Three years back, Kraft, Bill Judkins—who wrote verse—and I used to eat at Cypher’s place on Eighth Avenue. I say “used to.” Whenever we had cash, Cypher, as he put it, “took it off of us.” We carried no tab; we simply walked in, ordered, and ate. Sometimes we settled up, sometimes we didn’t. We relied on Cypher’s brooding, pent-up rage. In the lightless depths of his spirit, he was either a nobleman, an idiot, or an artist. He sat behind a worm-gnawed desk buried under stacks of waiters’ checks so ancient I felt certain the bottommost one was for shellfish Hendrik Hudson himself had consumed and paid for. Like Napoleon III and the pop-eyed perch, Cypher could draw a veil across his eyes, clouding the windows of his soul. Once, when we’d walked out without paying, trailing flimsy excuses, I glanced back and saw him quaking with silent laughter behind that veil. Every so often, we cleared our old debts.

They strolled towards a place of seats and hammocks between the big trees and the rose garden, and the talk turned for a time upon Rendezvous. "They have the tidiest garden in Essex," said Manning. "It's not Mrs. Rendezvous' fault that it is so. Mrs. Rendezvous, as a matter of fact, has a taste for the picturesque. She just puts the things about in groups in the beds. She wants them, she says, to grow anyhow. She desires a romantic disorder. But she never gets it. When he walks down the path all the plants dress instinctively.... And there's a tree near their gate; it used to be a willow. You can ask any old man in the village. But ever since Rendezvous took the place it's been trying to present arms. With the most extraordinary results. I was passing the other day with old Windershin. 'You see that there old poplar,' he said. 'It's a willow,' said I. 'No,' he said, 'it did used to be a willow before Colonel Rendezvous he came. But now it's a poplar.'... And, by Jove, it is a poplar!"...

They wandered toward a cluster of wicker chairs and canvas swings nestled between the towering elms and the flowering thickets, where the conversation eventually drifted to Highwood. "It is the most disciplined plot in the county," Sterling remarked. "One cannot blame Clara for its severity. Left to her own devices, she prefers the wild and the whimsical; she scatters seeds with a carefree hand, hoping for a riot of untamed color. She yearns for a beautiful chaos, yet it never quite materializes. The moment the General steps onto the gravel, the perennials seem to snap to attention of their own accord. There is even a specimen near the entrance—a willow, by all accounts. Any local veteran will swear to it. But since the General’s arrival, that tree has been straining to assume a military posture, with the most bizarre consequences. I happened to be walking past with old Barnaby recently. 'Have a look at that tall cypress,' he muttered. 'That’s a willow, surely,' I replied. 'No,' he insisted, 'it was a willow once, before the Colonel took command. But these days, sir, it’s a cypress.' And remarkably enough, it truly has become a cypress!"

“I suppose I do,” he admitted. “That uncertainty is what gives the journey its weight. It is not the state of being known, but the arduous climb toward it, that truly signifies. Besides, for me, such acclaim would be nothing more than a tool for a different end. I crave that distinction deeply, for that specific reason and that alone.”

He gave her a full account of Clym’s affliction, and of the state in which he was living; then, referring to Thomasin, touched gently upon the apparent sadness of her days. “Now, ma’am, depend upon it,” he said, “you couldn’t do a better thing for either of ’em than to make yourself at home in their houses, even if there should be a little rebuff at first.”

He walked her through every detail of Clym’s debilitating condition and the sparse, isolated rhythm his daily life had fallen into, before shifting the conversation to Thomasin and gently noting the quiet, unmissable sadness that seemed to hang over all her days lately. “Look, ma’am, you can take my word for it,” he said, “there’s no kinder, more useful thing you could do for either of them than to drop by their homes and make yourself comfortable like you belong there, even if you get a small, cold rebuff the first time you show up.”

This was enough to re-establish him in his original opinion. He knew now that he was right, and that Troy was wrong. Every voice in nature was unanimous in bespeaking change. But two distinct translations attached to these dumb expressions. Apparently there was to be a thunder-storm, and afterwards a cold continuous rain. The creeping things seemed to know all about the later rain, but little of the interpolated thunder-storm; whilst the sheep knew all about the thunder-storm and nothing of the later rain.

That was all it took to snap him back to the convictions he’d held from the start. He knew now he was right, and Troy was wrong. Every small signal from the natural world aligned to confirm a shift was on its way. But these wordless, quiet cues carried two very separate interpretations. It was clear a thunderstorm would roll through first, followed by an unbroken stretch of cold, steady rain. The small crawling creatures seemed to sense every detail of the coming rain, but had no idea the thunderstorm would strike first; the sheep, by contrast, knew full well the thunderstorm was imminent, but had no notion of the lingering cold rain that would come after it.

The smoke at this corner was stifling, and Clark, a nimble fellow, having been handed a bucket of water, bathed Oak’s face and sprinkled him generally, whilst Gabriel, now with a long beech-bough in one hand, in addition to his crook in the other, kept sweeping the stack and dislodging all fiery particles.

Billy Smallbury, who had earlier been riding in the wagon, had by now located a ladder. Mark Clark climbed it and steadied himself next to Oak on the roof’s edge. At this corner the smoke was thick and choking, so Clark—quick and agile—took a bucket passed up to him and doused Oak’s face, splashing water over his head and shoulders. Meanwhile, Gabriel, gripping a long beech branch in one hand and his shepherd’s crook in the other, went on beating the stack, knocking away every burning ember he could reach.

Then afterwards he had escaped. In quite a little time he had escaped. He had been in a railway station somewhere in Belgium; locked in a waiting-room with three or four French prisoners, and the junction had been bombed by French and British aeroplanes. Their guard and two of the prisoners had been killed. In the confusion the others had got away into the town. There were trucks of hay on fire, and a store of petrol was in danger. "After that one was bound to escape. One would have been shot if one had been found wandering about."

His getaway followed shortly thereafter. It had taken remarkably little time for him to break free. He had been confined within a Belgian railway station, held in a waiting area alongside several French captives, when Allied aircraft launched an assault on the junction. The explosion claimed the lives of their sentry and two of the other detainees. Amidst the ensuing turmoil, the remaining men managed to flee into the nearby streets. Wagons filled with hay were ablaze, and a nearby fuel depot was on the verge of igniting. "In such circumstances, flight was the only option. To be discovered roaming the area would have meant certain execution."

He and the two ladies had been very carefully made acquainted by Mr. Brownlow with the nature of the admissions which had been forced from Monks; and although they knew that the object of their present journey was to complete the work which had been so well begun, still the whole matter was enveloped in enough of doubt and mystery to leave them in endurance of the most intense suspense.

They exchanged very few words along the way. Oliver was caught in a flurry of restlessness and unresolvable doubt that left him unable to collect his thoughts, barely even able to speak at all, and that same frayed unease seemed to weigh just as heavily on his two female traveling companions, if not more. Mr. Brownlow had already carefully walked all three of them through the full substance of the confessions Monks had been pressured into making, and while they understood their present journey was meant to see through the final steps of the work that had already been so successfully launched, the whole affair was still shrouded in enough ambiguity and secrecy to leave them caught in a state of nearly unbearable, taut suspense.

I am quite at a loss for a name. And yet—no—it is the parish surgeon! Not that I intend to name him elderly, for his years are not so very advanced, after all. Truly, I observed a certain tenderness in his manner when he last attended your sickbed!

With the exponential growth of the scientific literature (Bornmann and Mutz, 2015) , retrieving relevant scientific papers becomes increasingly difficult. Keywords, also referred to as keyphrases, provide an effective way to supplement paper indexing and improve retrieval effectiveness in scientific digital libraries (Barker et al., 1972; Zhai, 1997; Lu and Kipp, 2014) . However, only few documents have assigned keyphrases, and those who do were, for the most part, selflabeled by their authors, thus exhibiting annotation inconsistencies (Strader, 2011; Suzuki et al., 2011) . This has motivated an active line of research on automatic keyphrase extraction (see Hasan and Ng (2014) for an overview) and, more recently, keyphrase generation (Meng et al., 2017) , where the task is to find a set of words and phrases that represents the main content of a document.

metadata.title: Keyphrase Generation for Scientific Document Retrieval

text: With the exponential expansion of the scientific literature (Bornmann and Mutz, 2015), identifying relevant research papers is becoming increasingly challenging. Keywords, also known as keyphrases, offer an effective means of augmenting paper indexing and enhancing retrieval performance in scientific digital libraries (Barker et al., 1972; Zhai, 1997; Lu and Kipp, 2014). Nonetheless, only a small fraction of documents are assigned keyphrases, and those that are are, in most cases, self-labeled by their authors, leading to annotation inconsistencies (Strader, 2011; Suzuki et al., 2011). This limitation has spurred extensive research on automatic keyphrase extraction (see Hasan and Ng (2014) for a survey) and, more recently, keyphrase generation (Meng et al., 2017), in which the objective is to identify a set of words and phrases that captures the central content of a document.

Our goal is to find out whether the keyphrase generation models described above are reliable enough to be beneficial for document retrieval. To do so, we contrast the performance of the retrieval models with and without automatically predicted keyphrases. Two initial indexing configurations are also examined: title and abstract only (T +A), and title, abstract and author keywords (T +A+K). The idea here is to investigate whether generated keyphrases simply act as a proxy for author keywords, or instead supplement them. Unless mentioned otherwise, the top-5 predicted keyphrases are used to expand documents, which is in accordance with the average number of author keywords in NTCIR-2. We evaluate retrieval performance in terms of MAP and omit P@10 for brevity. We use the Student's paired t-test to assess statistical significance of our retrieval results at p < 0.05 (Smucker et al., 2007) .

metadata.title: Keyphrase Generation for Scientific Document Retrieval

text: We aim to determine whether the keyphrase generation models described above are sufficiently reliable to improve document retrieval. To this end, we compare the performance of retrieval models with and without automatically predicted keyphrases. We also examine two baseline indexing configurations: title and abstract only (T + A), and title, abstract, and author-assigned keywords (T + A + K). This comparison is intended to reveal whether generated keyphrases merely replicate author keywords or provide complementary value. Unless stated otherwise, we expand each document with its top five predicted keyphrases, matching the average number of author keywords in the NTCIR-2 collection. Retrieval performance is evaluated using mean average precision (MAP); P@10 is omitted for brevity. Statistical significance of the retrieval results is assessed with the Student paired t-test at p < 0.05 (Smucker et al., 2007).

In the short time he had had to collect his senses, the boy had firmly resolved that, whether he died in the attempt or not, he would make one effort to dart upstairs from the hall, and alarm the family. Filled with this idea, he advanced at once, but stealthily.

In the fleeting interval permitted him to master his terror, the lad had forged a grim determination that, regardless of the mortal peril, he would stake everything on a single rush for the staircase to awaken the household. Possessed by this desperate intent, he stole forward on the instant, treading with the silent caution of a shadow.

I pray you let the drama halt while Chorus stalks to the footlights and drops an epicedian tear upon the fatness of Mr. Hoover. Tune the pipes to the tragedy of tallow, the bane of bulk, the calamity of corpulence. Tried out, Falstaff might have rendered more romance to the ton than would have Romeo’s rickety ribs to the ounce. A lover may sigh, but he must not puff. To the train of Momus are the fat men remanded. In vain beats the faithfullest heart above a 52-inch belt. Avaunt, Hoover! Hoover, forty-five, flush and foolish, might carry off Helen herself; Hoover, forty-five, flush, foolish and fat is meat for perdition. There was never a chance for you, Hoover.

I beg you, let the action pause while Chorus steps down to the footlights and sheds a dirge-like tear over the ample girth of Mr. Hoover. Let the pipes be tuned to the tragedy of tallow, to the curse of heaviness, the disaster of corpulence. Trimmed down, Falstaff might have lent more romance to the ton than Romeo’s frail ribs could ever yield to the pound. A lover may sigh, but he must not wheeze. The fat men are consigned to the retinue of Momus. In vain does the most faithful heart beat above a fifty-two-inch belt. Begone, Hoover! Hoover, at forty-five, flushed and foolish, might have borne Helen herself away; Hoover, forty-five, flushed, foolish, and fat is food for damnation. You never had a chance, Hoover.

With his straw in his mouth, Mr. Cruncher sat watching the two streams, like the heathen rustic who has for several centuries been on duty watching one stream--saving that Jerry had no expectation of their ever running dry. Nor would it have been an expectation of a hopeful kind, since a small part of his income was derived from the pilotage of timid women (mostly of a full habit and past the middle term of life) from Tellson’s side of the tides to the opposite shore. Brief as such companionship was in every separate instance, Mr. Cruncher never failed to become so interested in the lady as to express a strong desire to have the honour of drinking her very good health. And it was from the gifts bestowed upon him towards the execution of this benevolent purpose, that he recruited his finances, as just now observed.

Clamping a frayed wheat stalk between his teeth, Mr. Rook sat gazing at the two churning currents, much like the pagan goatherd of local lore who has stood watch over a single moorland stream for near a thousand years—except that Tom Rook held no expectation those waters would ever run dry. That would hardly have been a hopeful wish anyway, for a tidy slice of his income came from escorting skittish gentlewomen (most of them plump, well past middle age) across the slippery crossing from Calthorpe & Sons Bank’s side of the tide to the opposite embankment. Brief as each of these little journeys was, Mr. Rook never failed to work up such a show of fond regard for his passenger that he would profess a fervent wish to have the honor of toasting her excellent health. And the small gratuities pressed upon him to fund this kindly act were exactly the contributions that padded his earnings, as just observed.

She can gauge him by them. They will show him up for what he is. And after all, he is a mere boy of twenty-one. Ruth is no more than a child. It is calf love with the pair of them, and they will grow out of it.”

“Let her spend as much time with that boy as she pleases,” was Mr. Hale’s blunt counsel. “The more she learns who he really is, the faster that silly infatuation will fizzle, I’d put good money on it. Go out of your way to host young folks at the house—girls her age, and all manner of young men: sharp, driven men who’ve already built something of themselves, or are well on their way to doing so, men of her own social station, proper gentlemen. She’ll hold him up next to them, and they’ll lay bare exactly how unfit he is. He’s barely twenty-one, after all, still little more than a boy himself. Lila’s hardly more than a child either. Mr. Chitling was older in years than the Dodger: having perhaps numbered eighteen winters; but there was a degree of deference in his deportment towards that young gentleman which seemed to indicate that he felt himself conscious of a slight inferiority in point of genius and professional aquirements. He had small twinkling eyes, and a pock-marked face; wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His wardrobe was, in truth, rather out of repair; but he excused himself to the company by stating that his “time” was only out an hour before; and that, in consequence of having worn the regimentals for six weeks past, he had not been able to bestow any attention on his private clothes. Mr. Chitling added, with strong marks of irritation, that the new way of fumigating clothes up yonder was infernal unconstitutional, for it burnt holes in them, and there was no remedy against the county. The same remark he considered to apply to the regulation mode of cutting the hair: which he held to be decidedly unlawful. Mr. Chitling wound up his observations by stating that he had not touched a drop of anything for forty-two moral long hard-working days; and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

His clothes, in truth, were in a poor state; but he made his excuse to the company by saying that his “time” had only run out an hour earlier, and that, after wearing the regimentals for the past six weeks, he had not had a chance to give any attention to his own wardrobe. He said the same complaint applied to the approved way of cutting the hair, which he considered flatly unlawful. He ended by saying that he had not taken a drop of anything for forty-two long, hard, moral days of labor, and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

As for Bathsheba, she was not deceived into the belief that Farmer Boldwood had walked by on business or in idleness. She collected the probabilities of the case, and concluded that she was herself responsible for Boldwood’s appearance there. It troubled her much to see what a great flame a little wildfire was likely to kindle. Bathsheba was no schemer for marriage, nor was she deliberately a trifler with the affections of men, and a censor’s experience on seeing an actual flirt after observing her would have been a feeling of surprise that Bathsheba could be so different from such a one, and yet so like what a flirt is supposed to be.

Bathsheba, for her part, was under no illusion that Farmer Boldwood had passed by on some errand or merely out of idle wandering. She weighed the likelihoods and understood that his presence there was her own doing. It disturbed her deeply to realize how great a blaze a small spark might ignite. Bathsheba was not one to scheme for a husband, nor did she consciously play with men’s hearts, and any seasoned critic who watched a true coquette at work and then observed her would have been astonished to find Bathsheba so unlike that figure, yet so closely resembling what a flirt is imagined to be.

We assemble a large number of test suites inspired by the methodology of experimental sentenceprocessing and psycholinguistic research. Each test suite contains a number of ITEMS (typically between 20 and 30), and each item appears in several CONDITIONS: across conditions, a given item will differ only according to a controlled manipulation designed to target a particular feature of grammatical knowledge. Each test suite contains at least one PREDICTION, which specifies inequalities between surprisal values at pairs of regions/conditions that should hold if a model has learned the appropriate syntactic generalization.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: We construct a large set of test suites inspired by methods from experimental sentence processing and psycholinguistic research. Each test suite includes a number of items, typically between 20 and 30, and each item appears in multiple conditions: across conditions, a given item differs only in a controlled manipulation intended to target a specific aspect of grammatical knowledge. Each test suite contains at least one prediction, which states inequalities between surprisal values at pairs of regions or conditions that should hold if a model has learned the relevant syntactic generalization.

If I wait for you to go tomorrow another day will be lost; and I know I shall be unable to rest another night without having been. I want to get this settled, and will. You must visit her afterwards—it will be all the same.”

If I wait for you until tomorrow, another day will be gone, and I know I should be unable to rest through another night without having been there. I want this business brought to an end, and I will have it so. You must call on her afterward—it will amount to the same thing.”

He accompanied her up the hill, explaining to her the details of his forthcoming tenure of the other farm. They spoke very little of their mutual feeling; pretty phrases and warm expressions being probably unnecessary between such tried friends. Theirs was that substantial affection which arises (if any arises at all) when the two who are thrown together begin first by knowing the rougher sides of each other’s character, and not the best till further on, the romance growing up in the interstices of a mass of hard prosaic reality. This good-fellowship—_camaraderie_—usually occurring through similarity of pursuits, is unfortunately seldom superadded to love between the sexes, because men and women associate, not in their labours, but in their pleasures merely. Where, however, happy circumstance permits its development, the compounded feeling proves itself to be the only love which is strong as death—that love which many waters cannot quench, nor the floods drown, beside which the passion usually called by the name is evanescent as steam.

They said little of the feeling they held in common; fine phrases and tender declarations were likely needless between such long-tried friends. Theirs was that solid affection which springs, if it springs at all, only when two people drawn together first come to know one another’s rougher sides, and not the better until later, the romance taking shape in the crevices of a mass of plain, stubborn reality. Such companionship—_camaraderie_—usually born of shared work, is unhappily seldom added to love between man and woman, since men and women are brought together not in labor but only in pleasure. Yet where favorable fortune allows it to grow, the mingled bond proves itself the sole love strong as death—that love which many waters cannot quench, nor the floods drown, and beside which the passion commonly given that name is as fleeting as steam.

To-day there were more changes on the bill of fare than usual. The soups were lighter; pork was eliminated from the entrées, figuring only with Russian turnips among the roasts. The gracious spirit of spring pervaded the entire menu. Lamb, that lately capered on the greening hillsides, was becoming exploited with the sauce that commemorated its gambols. The song of the oyster, though not silenced, was _dimuendo con amore_. The frying-pan seemed to be held, inactive, behind the beneficent bars of the broiler. The pie list swelled; the richer puddings had vanished; the sausage, with his drapery wrapped about him, barely lingered in a pleasant thanatopsis with the buckwheats and the sweet but doomed maple.

Today the menu had shifted more than usual. The soups were thinner; pork had vanished from the entrées, appearing only beside Russian turnips among the roasts. A gentle breath of spring ran through every dish. Lamb, which not long ago had frolicked on the greening slopes, was now being dressed in a sauce that recalled its playful leaps. The oyster’s voice, though not yet hushed, was fading in a tender decrescendo. The frying-pan seemed to rest idly behind the kindly shelter of the broiler. The list of pies grew longer; the heavier puddings had disappeared; the sausage, wrapped snugly in its own covering, lingered faintly in a quiet meditation on mortality alongside the buckwheat cakes and the sweet, soon-to-be-sacrificed maple.

“This is a common case--the enchanting of a thing to one eye and leaving it in its proper form to another. You have heard of it before, Sandy, though you haven't happened to experience it. But no harm is done. In fact, it is lucky the way it is. If these ladies were hogs to everybody and to themselves, it would be necessary to break the enchantment, and that might be impossible if one failed to find out the particular process of the enchantment. And hazardous, too; for in attempting a disenchantment without the true key, you are liable to err, and turn your hogs into dogs, and the dogs into cats, the cats into rats, and so on, and end by reducing your materials to nothing finally, or to an odorless gas which you can't follow--which, of course, amounts to the same thing. But here, by good luck, no one's eyes but mine are under the enchantment, and so it is of no consequence to dissolve it. These ladies remain ladies to you, and to themselves, and to everybody else; and at the same time they will suffer in no way from my delusion, for when I know that an ostensible hog is a lady, that is enough for me, I know how to treat her.”

This is a frequent occurrence—the way a spell might alter something in one man's sight while leaving it in its true form for another. You’ve heard of such things before, Sandy, even if you haven't witnessed it personally. But there is no real damage here. In truth, it’s a stroke of luck. If these ladies looked like swine to themselves and the rest of the world, we would be forced to break the enchantment, which might be impossible without knowing the exact ritual involved. It’s a dangerous business, too; if you try to reverse a spell without the correct formula, you're liable to make a mistake and turn your hogs into dogs, then cats, then rats, until you eventually reduce your subjects to nothing at all, or perhaps a scentless vapor that vanishes—which amounts to the same thing. Fortunately, only my vision is clouded, so there is no reason to undo the magic. These women remain noble ladies to you and everyone else; they won't suffer from my misunderstanding, for once I realize that a supposed pig is actually a lady, that is enough for me to treat her with the proper respect.

Another part of the room soon riveted her gaze. At the other side of the chimney stood the settle, which is the necessary supplement to a fire so open that nothing less than a strong breeze will carry up the smoke. It is, to the hearths of old-fashioned cavernous fireplaces, what the east belt of trees is to the exposed country estate, or the north wall to the garden. Outside the settle candles gutter, locks of hair wave, young women shiver, and old men sneeze. Inside is Paradise. Not a symptom of a draught disturbs the air; the sitters’ backs are as warm as their faces, and songs and old tales are drawn from the occupants by the comfortable heat, like fruit from melon plants in a frame.

Her attention was soon seized by another corner of the room. Opposite the chimney stood the high-backed settle, that indispensable companion to a hearth so wide and open that only a stiff wind can draw the smoke upward. It is to the fireplaces of old, cavernous rooms what a sheltering belt of trees is to an exposed estate, or a north wall to a garden. Beyond the settle, candles flicker, strands of hair lift and stir, young women tremble, and old men catch cold. Within it lies Paradise. Not the faintest whisper of a draught troubles the air; the sitters’ backs bask as warmly as their faces, and the snug heat coaxes songs and old stories from them as gently as a frame coaxes melons from the vine.

That Oliver Twist was moved to resignation by the example of these good people, I cannot, although I am his biographer, undertake to affirm with any degree of confidence; but I can most distinctly say, that for many months he continued meekly to submit to the domination and ill-treatment of Noah Claypole: who used him far worse than before, now that his jealousy was roused by seeing the new boy promoted to the black stick and hat-band, while he, the old one, remained stationary in the muffin-cap and leathers. Charlotte treated him ill, because Noah did; and Mrs. Sowerberry was his decided enemy, because Mr. Sowerberry was disposed to be his friend; so, between these three on one side, and a glut of funerals on the other, Oliver was not altogether as comfortable as the hungry pig was, when he was shut up, by mistake, in the grain department of a brewery.

As Oliver Twist’s biographer, I can’t claim with any real confidence that the example of these virtuous people prompted Oliver to resign himself to his circumstances; but I can state unequivocally that for months on end he meekly endured the domination and abuse of Noah Claypole. Noah treated him far worse than he had before, his jealousy sparked when he saw the new boy promoted to the black walking stick and official hatband, while he, the longer-serving staff member, was stuck wearing the same old muffin cap and leather work gear he’d had for ages. Charlotte picked on him too, just because Noah did, and Mrs. Sowerberry made no secret of being his sworn enemy, all because Mr. Sowerberry was inclined to be kind to him. Caught between these three ganging up on him on one side, and a constant glut of funerals keeping him overworked on the other, Oliver was quite a bit less comfortable than that hungry pig that once got accidentally locked in a brewery’s grain storage room.

The heavens will never let her pass from this world so early in her days.”

As mentioned above, we employ a sequence encoder to convert the input sentence into vector representations. The sequence encoder follows the multi-layer Transformer architecture described in Vaswani et al. (2017) . At the bottom layer, each token is firstly transformed into the concatenation of features learned by a character-level convolutional neural network (charCNN, Kim et al., 2016) and randomly initialized embeddings for its lemma, part-of-speech tag, and named entity tag. Additionally, we also include features learned by pre-trained language model BERT (Devlin et al., 2019 ). 2 Formally, for an input sequence w 1 , w 2 , . . . , w n with length n, we insert a special token BOS at the beginning of the sequence. For clarity, we omit the detailed transformations (Vaswani et al., 2017) and denote the final output from our sequence encoder as {h 0 , h 1 , . . . , h n } ∈ R d , where h 0 corresponds the special token BOS and serves as an overall rep-resentation while others are considered as contextualized word representations. Note that the sequence encoder only needs to be invoked once, and the produced text memories are used for the whole parsing procedure.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: As noted earlier, we use a sequence encoder to map the input sentence into vector representations. This encoder adopts the multi-layer Transformer architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017). At the lowest layer, each token is first converted into a concatenation of features derived from a character-level convolutional neural network (charCNN; Kim et al., 2016) and randomly initialized embeddings for its lemma, part-of-speech tag, and named entity tag. We further incorporate features obtained from the pre-trained language model BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Formally, given an input sequence w₁, w₂, …, wₙ of length n, we prepend a special BOS token at the start of the sequence. For brevity, we omit the detailed transformations (Vaswani et al., 2017) and denote the final output of our sequence encoder as {h₀, h₁, …, hₙ} ∈ ℝᵈ, where h₀ corresponds to the special BOS token and serves as an overall representation, while the remaining vectors are treated as contextualized word representations. Note that the sequence encoder needs to be run only once; the resulting text memories are then used throughout the entire parsing process.

metadata.title: Bridging Anaphora Resolution as Question Answering

text: In English, the preposition “of” in the syntactic pattern “NP1 of NP2” expresses a range of associative relations between noun phrases that encompass many bridging relations. For example, “the chairman of IBM” denotes a professional role within an organization, whereas “the price of the stock” denotes an attribute of an object. Such patterns have also been used to estimate the proportion of bridging relations. These patterns reflect how humans explain bridging anaphora. It appears that the most natural way to interpret the meaning of a bridging anaphor a is to answer the question “a of what?” based on the surrounding context of a.

text: Inspecting the heads in GPT-2 also indicates that they encode distinct syntactic structure. Figure 5a presents several heads from different layers that appear to acquire the AUX dependency. Heads in earlier layers exhibit a preference for the AUX pattern, but they also misinterpret "to" as a preposition even when it is immediately followed by a verb. This suggests that these heads are chiefly matching the token "to" itself rather than attending to its contextual meaning.

Using keywords-based web scrapping tools, we collected a raw corpus of 4 million sentences, called CrawlWeb, that in fine contained a mixture of French, English, Spanish, MSA and Arabizi texts. Since we are only interested in NArabizi, we designed a classifier to extract proper sentences from that raw corpus. The corpus we used as gold standard is made of 9k sentences of attested NArabizi from our original corpus and 18k of French and English tweets. Using language identification (Lui and Baldwin, 2012), we convert each sentence from the gold-standard corpus to a feature vector containing language-identification scores and use it as input to a SVM classifier with a classical 80/10/10 split. With a precision and recall score of 94%, we filtered out 173k code-mixed sentences out of the CrawlWeb corpus. Preliminary experiments showed promising initial results, but further analysis pointed out a high level of noise in this initial set, both in terms of erroneous language identification and on the amount of remnant ASCII artifacts that could not easily be removed without impacting the valid NArabizi sentences.

metadata.title: Building a User-Generated Content North-African Arabizi Treebank: Tackling Hell

text: Using keyword-based web scraping tools, we collected a raw corpus of 4 million sentences, referred to as CrawlWeb, which ultimately contained a mixture of French, English, Spanish, MSA, and Arabizi texts. Since our focus is exclusively on NArabizi, we developed a classifier to extract appropriate sentences from this raw corpus. The gold-standard corpus used for this purpose consists of 9k sentences of attested NArabizi drawn from our original corpus and 18k French and English tweets. Using language identification (Lui and Baldwin, 2012), we transformed each sentence in the gold-standard corpus into a feature vector containing language-identification scores and used these vectors as input to an SVM classifier with a standard 80/10/10 split. Achieving 94% precision and recall, we filtered out 173k code-mixed sentences from the CrawlWeb corpus. Preliminary experiments yielded promising initial results, but further analysis revealed a high level of noise in this initial set, both in terms of incorrect language identification and in the amount of residual ASCII artifacts that could not easily be removed without affecting valid NArabizi sentences.
