“But it isn’t quite that, exactly. If a man alters his name and the rest of it, and contrives to deceive the world and even his own wife, he’s a fraud, and in the eyes of the law a sheer rogue, and by that reckoning a downright truant vagabond; and that is a matter fit to be punished.”

Troy was full of activity, but his activities were less of a locomotive than a vegetative nature; and, never being based upon any original choice of foundation or direction, they were exercised on whatever object chance might place in their way. Hence, whilst he sometimes reached the brilliant in speech because that was spontaneous, he fell below the commonplace in action, from inability to guide incipient effort. He had a quick comprehension and considerable force of character; but, being without the power to combine them, the comprehension became engaged with trivialities whilst waiting for the will to direct it, and the force wasted itself in useless grooves through unheeding the comprehension.

Troy was always on the move, but his constant activity was less purposeful than a steam engine and more like the unplanned spread of a climbing plant; he never chose a clear core aim or direction for his efforts, so he simply poured energy into whatever random thing chance dropped in front of him. That’s why, while he could occasionally turn out brilliantly sharp, off-the-cuff lines when speaking, his actions were far worse than unremarkable—he simply couldn’t guide the first flickers of effort into anything coherent. He had a quick, perceptive mind and no shortage of inherent force of character, but he lacked the ability to make those two strengths work in tandem. His sharp perception would fixate on meaningless small details while it waited for his will to give it a clear goal, and all that inherent grit burned away on pointless, repetitive dead ends because he never let his good judgment steer it.

Got to have right heat, right pressure, and run ’em through three times. Look at that!” He held a cuff aloft. “Couldn’t do it better by hand or on a tiler.”

“Oh, Anthony,” sighed Aunt Ellen, “I wish you would not think so much of money. Wealth is nothing where a true affection is concerned. Love is all-powerful. If he only had spoken earlier! She could not have refused our Richard. But now I fear it is too late. He will have no opportunity to address her. All your gold cannot bring happiness to your son.”

My dear Arthur," Aunt Beatrice sighed, "I wish you would not dwell so upon finance. Opulence is a trifle against a sincere bond; love is invincible. Had he but voiced his intentions sooner! She could never have turned our Julian away. But I fear the hour has slipped by, leaving him no occasion to speak. All your hoarded treasures cannot purchase the contentment your son requires.

She felt wretchedly certain that if he revisited her just at this nick of time, and came into contact with Boldwood, a fierce quarrel would be the consequence. She panted with solicitude when she thought of possible injury to Troy. The least spark would kindle the farmer’s swift feelings of rage and jealousy; he would lose his self-mastery as he had this evening; Troy’s blitheness might become aggressive; it might take the direction of derision, and Boldwood’s anger might then take the direction of revenge.

She knew with a sinking dread that if Troy returned to her now, at this precise and perilous moment, and crossed paths with Boldwood, a violent clash would follow. Her breath quickened with anxious care at the mere thought of harm befalling Troy. The faintest ember would ignite the farmer’s quick-burning fury and envy; all self-command would desert him, as it had only hours ago; Troy’s careless gaiety might sharpen into provocation, veering toward mockery, and Boldwood’s wrath might then veer toward vengeance.

I wanted to stop the whole thing and set the slaves free, but that would not do. I must not interfere too much and get myself a name for riding over the country's laws and the citizen's rights roughshod. If I lived and prospered I would be the death of slavery, that I was resolved upon; but I would try to fix it so that when I became its executioner it should be by command of the nation.

I longed to halt the entire affair and loose the bondsmen on the spot, yet such a course was impossible. I dared not meddle overmuch and earn a reputation for trampling the laws of the land and the liberties of its people underfoot. Should I survive and thrive, I meant to be the ruin of bondage—on that I was fixed—but I would strive to arrange matters so that when I struck the fatal blow, it would fall by the nation’s own decree.

This assertion, however, was hardly borne out by the event. Again the instruments ended the tune; again they recommenced with as much fire and pathos as if it were the first strain. The air was now that one without any particular beginning, middle, or end, which perhaps, among all the dances which throng an inspired fiddler’s fancy, best conveys the idea of the interminable—the celebrated “Devil’s Dream.” The fury of personal movement that was kindled by the fury of the notes could be approximately imagined by these outsiders under the moon, from the occasional kicks of toes and heels against the door, whenever the whirl round had been of more than customary velocity.

That declaration, however, was scarcely justified by what followed. Once more the instruments brought the air to its close; once more they struck up again with all the same ardor and emotion, as though it were only their opening phrase. It was now that sort of tune with no discernible first act, middle, or finish, which, among all the dances that crowd a gifted fiddler’s imagination, perhaps most nearly suggests the endless—the much-vaunted “Old Nick’s Reverie.” The wildness of bodily motion awakened by the wildness of the music could be faintly divined by those observers in the moonlight from the rare thuds of toe and heel against the door, whenever the circling had grown faster than usual.

But such thoughts and visions did not prevent him from following Professor Caldwell closely. And as he followed, comprehendingly and critically, he noted the unbroken field of the other’s knowledge. As for himself, from moment to moment the conversation showed him gaps and open stretches, whole subjects with which he was unfamiliar. Nevertheless, thanks to his Spencer, he saw that he possessed the outlines of the field of knowledge. It was a matter only of time, when he would fill in the outline. Then watch out, he thought—’ware shoal, everybody! He felt like sitting at the feet of the professor, worshipful and absorbent; but, as he listened, he began to discern a weakness in the other’s judgments—a weakness so stray and elusive that he might not have caught it had it not been ever present. And when he did catch it, he leapt to equality at once.

Still, none of those wandering thoughts and daydreams stopped him from keeping pace right at Professor Caldwell’s side. As he walked along, engaging with every word both thoughtfully and skeptically, he couldn’t help but notice how seamlessly the professor’s expertise spanned every topic they touched on. For his own part, the discussion kept turning up gaps in his own learning, vast blank swathes of entire subject areas he’d never even dabbled in. Even so, his deep dive into Spencer’s work had given him a clear sketch of the full landscape of all there was to know, he realized. Filling in every empty spot on that sketch was just a matter of putting in the time. When that day came, everyone better watch their step, he smirked to himself—shallow waters ahead for anyone who thought they could outmatch him. For a while he felt like he could kneel right there at the professor’s feet, eager to soak up every last word, reverent of the older man’s smarts. But the longer he listened, the more he picked up on a faint flaw in the professor’s reasoning, a quirk so scattered and hard to pin down he never would have spotted it if it didn’t pop up again and again. The second he pinned that flaw down, he felt himself standing on even ground with the man immediately.

The judge and Ruth’s father were deep in talk about union politics, the state of affairs in town, and socialism, and Mr. Morse kept trying to needle Martin about the last subject. Eventually Judge Blount peered across the table, his expression one of kindly, paternal sympathy. Martin kept his amusement to himself.

The authors of the dataset used it to build a Question Answering system. Then, they evaluated the system using 1.5k questions. For each question, they took the top 10 system responses and manually annotated if the answer was correct. Positive responses to 250 questions were further processed and only relevant continuous parts of responses were selected by human annotators. Following this procedure, we have manually extracted shorter responses from the remaining positive examples. Finally, we used these annotations to create positive question-answer pairs.

metadata.title: KLEJ: Comprehensive Benchmark for Polish Language Understanding

text: The creators of the dataset employed it to develop a Question Answering system, which they subsequently evaluated on 1,500 questions. For each question, the top ten system responses were retrieved and manually annotated for correctness. The positive responses for 250 questions underwent further processing, during which human annotators selected only the relevant continuous segments of the responses. Adopting the same procedure, we manually extracted shorter responses from the remaining positive instances. Finally, these annotations were used to construct positive question-answer pairs.

It was curious to observe some faces which stood out prominently from among the group. There was the chairman himself, (the landlord of the house,) a coarse, rough, heavy built fellow, who, while the songs were proceeding, rolled his eyes hither and thither, and, seeming to give himself up to joviality, had an eye for everything that was done, and an ear for everything that was said—and sharp ones, too. Near him were the singers: receiving, with professional indifference, the compliments of the company, and applying themselves, in turn, to a dozen proffered glasses of spirits and water, tendered by their more boisterous admirers; whose countenances, expressive of almost every vice in almost every grade, irresistibly attracted the attention, by their very repulsiveness. Cunning, ferocity, and drunkeness in all its stages, were there, in their strongest aspect; and women: some with the last lingering tinge of their early freshness almost fading as you looked: others with every mark and stamp of their sex utterly beaten out, and presenting but one loathsome blank of profligacy and crime; some mere girls, others but young women, and none past the prime of life; formed the darkest and saddest portion of this dreary picture.

It was strange to pick out certain visages that seemed to rise distinctly from the crowd. At the center sat the host himself—the tavern’s proprietor—a thick-set, vulgar, heavy-handed man, who, while the ditties rang on, let his gaze wander freely around, and, though he feigned full surrender to merriment, missed no gesture enacted nor whisper uttered—and those senses were keen. Close beside him clustered the performers: accepting, with the practiced indifference of their trade, the crowd’s flattery, and drinking one by one from a dozen offered tumblers of gin and water, thrust forward by their louder, rowdier partisans—faces so stamped with nearly every shade and species of vice that their very ugliness seized the eye. Guile, savagery, and intoxication in every degree were on display in their most vivid forms; and women: some with the final ghost of youthful bloom fading even as you watched; others with every trace and sign of womanhood so thoroughly hammered out that nothing remained but a sickening vacancy of debauchery and guilt; a few mere girls, others scarcely grown, none beyond the height of their years—these composed the gloomiest, most pitiable fragment of the whole bleak scene.

“You don’t know the social mill, dad. She’s part of the stream that turns it. Every hour and minute of her time is arranged for days in advance. I must have that girl, dad, or this town is a blackjack swamp forevermore. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

“You don’t understand the social machine, father. She belongs to the current that keeps it moving. Every hour and every minute of her life is booked days ahead. I’ve got to have that girl, father, or this town will be nothing but a blackjack swamp from now on. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

But he promised everything; and on my side he made me promise over and over again that I would remain his friend, and never turn against him or cast any enchantments upon him. Then he worked his way out, staying himself with his hand along the wall, like a sick person.

The poor boy was so shaken he could barely get a word out in reply to me. It was gut-wrenching to look at a person so frozen with terror, so frayed of nerve, so thoroughly demoralized. He promised to do anything I asked of him, but first he made me vow over and over that I would remain his friend, never turn against him, never cast any enchantments to harm him. After that he picked his way out of the room, steadying himself with one hand along the wall every step of the way, as if he were gravely ill.

Dusty. I am familiar with a companion who harbors a mutton-jowled youth; a 'grand boy,' as he is called; with a spherical cranium, crimsoned jowls, and staring orbs; a detestable creature; with a torso and limbs that appear to be bursting from the seams of his indigo attire; possessing the roar of a boatswain and the voracity of a beast. I recognize him! The miscreant!

"Terribly Lax. But then nowadays Everybody _is_ so Lax. And he's very Good to my Coal Club; I don't know what we should do without him. So I just admonish him. And if he doesn't go to church, well, anyhow he doesn't go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but anyhow he's not a dissenter...."

There was some friendly banter between the good man and Mr. Britling over bringing Mr. Direck to church on Sunday morning. “He’s terribly lax,” Mr. Dimple said to Mr. Direck, beaming. “Terribly lax. But then these days everybody is so lax. And he’s very good to my Coal Club; I don’t know what we should do without him. So I just keep after him a little. And if he doesn’t go to church, well, at any rate he doesn’t go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but at least he isn’t a dissenter....”

This company of pilgrims resembled Chaucer's in this: that it had in it a sample of about all the upper occupations and professions the country could show, and a corresponding variety of costume. There were young men and old men, young women and old women, lively folk and grave folk. They rode upon mules and horses, and there was not a side-saddle in the party; for this specialty was to remain unknown in England for nine hundred years yet.

The assembly of travelers mirrored Chaucer’s pilgrims in one distinct way: it comprised a cross-section of nearly every prominent trade and profession the nation possessed, reflected in a diverse array of garments. The group featured a mix of the aged and the young, men and women alike, some brimming with energy while others remained stoic. They journeyed atop horses and mules, yet not a single side-saddle was among them, for that specific convenience would not be introduced to England for another nine hundred years.

“Now, let me recommend you,” pursued Stryver, “to look it in the face. I have looked it in the face, in my different way; look it in the face, you, in your different way. Marry. Provide somebody to take care of you. Never mind your having no enjoyment of women’s society, nor understanding of it, nor tact for it. Find out somebody. Find out some respectable woman with a little property--somebody in the landlady way, or lodging-letting way--and marry her, against a rainy day. That’s the kind of thing for _you_. Now think of it, Sydney.”

“Now, I advise you,” persisted Thorne, “to confront the matter directly. I have faced it after my own fashion; you must face it after yours. Take a wife. Secure someone to oversee your comfort. Pay no heed to your lack of relish for feminine company, nor your ignorance of their nature, nor your want of social grace. Seek someone out. Find a reputable woman with a modest inheritance—someone of the domestic or house-managing sort—and wed her as a hedge against hard times. That is precisely the arrangement for a man like you. Reflect upon it, Elias.”

They sat round a little table under the cedar trees amusing themselves with these experiments, and after that Cecily and Mr. Britling and the two small boys entertained themselves by drawing pigs with their eyes shut, and then Mr. Britling and Teddy played hard at Badminton until it was time for tea. And Cecily sat by Mr. Direck and took an interest in his accident, and he told her about summer holidays in the Adirondacks and how he loved to travel. She said she would love to travel. He said that so soon as he was better he would go on to Paris and then into Germany. He was extraordinarily curious about this Germany and its tremendous militarism. He'd far rather see it than Italy, which was, he thought, just all art and ancient history. His turn was for modern problems. Though of course he didn't intend to leave out Italy while he was at it. And then their talk was scattered, and there was great excitement because Herr Heinrich had lost his squirrel.

The group clustered around a small table beneath the cedar boughs, passing the time with their little experiments. Once that wrapped up, Cecily, Mr. Britling, and the two young boys amused themselves by sketching pigs with their eyes closed, before Mr. Britling and Teddy launched into a fierce game of badminton that lasted right up until tea was due to be served. Cecily sat beside Mr. Direck in the meantime, asking after the details of his injury, and he told her of summer holidays spent in the Adirondacks, and how much he loved to wander the world. She admitted she ached to travel too. He explained that as soon as he recovered fully, he planned to head first to Paris, then on into Germany. He was intensely curious about the country, and its pervasive, overwhelming militarism; he’d far rather explore that than Italy, which he dismissed as little more than a landscape of art and ancient history. His interest lay squarely in modern issues, though he did not intend to skip Italy entirely on his trip, of course. Their conversation was soon broken off, though, as a flurry of excitement swept the gathering: Herr Heinrich’s pet squirrel had gone missing.

“Dearest Clerval,” exclaimed I, “how kind, how very good you are to me. This whole winter, instead of being spent in study, as you promised yourself, has been consumed in my sick room. How shall I ever repay you? I feel the greatest remorse for the disappointment of which I have been the occasion, but you will forgive me.”

My dear Clerval," I cried, "you have been so incredibly good to me. You spent this entire winter cooped up in my sickroom instead of focusing on your studies as you had planned. I truly don't know how I will ever make it up to you. I feel such deep regret for being the cause of your disappointment, but I know you will forgive me.

He fled to his stateroom, where he lurked until the steamer was clear of the dock. In the dining saloon, at luncheon, he found himself in the place of honor, at the captain’s right; and he was not long in discovering that he was the great man on board. But no more unsatisfactory great man ever sailed on a ship. He spent the afternoon in a deck-chair, with closed eyes, dozing brokenly most of the time, and in the evening went early to bed.

He retreated to his cabin and stayed hidden there until the steamer had pulled away from the pier. When he entered the dining saloon for lunch, he was shown to the seat of honor, directly at the captain’s right hand, and it didn’t take him long to realize he was regarded as the most important person aboard. Yet no more discontented celebrity had ever traveled on that vessel. He passed the afternoon slumped in a deck-chair, eyes shut, slipping in and out of a fitful doze, and retired to his berth early that evening.

“Well, no. A philosopher once said in my hearing that the old builders, who worked when art was a living thing, had no respect for the work of builders who went before them, but pulled down and altered as they thought fit; and why shouldn’t we? ‘Creation and preservation don’t do well together,’ says he, ‘and a million of antiquarians can’t invent a style.’ My mind exactly. I am for making this place more modern, that we may be cheerful whilst we can.”

“Well, no. I once heard a philosopher remark that the ancient builders, those who labored when art was still alive, held no reverence for their predecessors’ work—they tore down and reshaped as they saw fit. Why shouldn’t we do the same? ‘Creation and preservation are ill-matched companions,’ he said, ‘and a million antiquarians could never devise a new style.’ That echoes my own thoughts exactly. I believe in modernizing this place, so we might enjoy some cheer while we still can.”

On a night in mid-July, one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came in late, from Tellson’s, and sat himself down by Lucie and her husband in the dark window. It was a hot, wild night, and they were all three reminded of the old Sunday night when they had looked at the lightning from the same place.

On a night in mid-July, seventeen hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came home late from Tellson’s and seated himself with Lucie and her husband by the darkened window. It was a hot, restless night, and the three of them were all reminded of that old Sunday evening when they had watched the lightning from the same spot.

It struck him motionless. The gaoler standing at his side, and the other gaolers moving about, who would have been well enough as to appearance in the ordinary exercise of their functions, looked so extravagantly coarse contrasted with sorrowing mothers and blooming daughters who were there--with the apparitions of the coquette, the young beauty, and the mature woman delicately bred--that the inversion of all experience and likelihood which the scene of shadows presented, was heightened to its utmost. Surely, ghosts all. Surely, the long unreal ride some progress of disease that had brought him to these gloomy shades!

It froze him where he stood. The jailer beside him, and the others drifting through their duties—men whose looks would have passed without remark in the common run of their work—now seemed grotesquely rough set against the grieving mothers and fresh-faced daughters gathered there, against the spectres of the flirt, the youthful beauty, the gently reared matron. The shadowy scene, already a reversal of all that was natural and probable, sharpened into something absolute. Surely, every one a phantom. Surely, the long hallucinatory journey some fevered advance of illness that had delivered him into these sombre depths.

Mr. Ford, wearing on his expressive face an air of mild and cheerful compliance, began to reach for his pocket, then suddenly turned to Mr. Ends and said that he had left his money at home. Mr. Ends plainly resented this, and Martin saw his arm twitch as though to guard his trouser pocket. Martin knew the money was there.

Be it known that the noble lord and renowned knight, Sir Alaric Le Grand, having graciously consented to encounter the King’s minister, Harlan Morgan, surnamed the Master, in requital of an affront long since delivered, the two shall meet in the lists by Camelot at the fourth hour of the morning on the sixteenth day of the coming month. The combat shall be to the uttermost, for the said affront was of a mortal nature and admits of no common terms.

Similarly, during two or three hours of drawl, and the winnowing of many bushels of words, Madame Defarge’s frequent expressions of impatience were taken up, with marvellous quickness, at a distance: the more readily, because certain men who had by some wonderful exercise of agility climbed up the external architecture to look in from the windows, knew Madame Defarge well, and acted as a telegraph between her and the crowd outside the building.

The people directly behind Madame Defarge, explaining the reason for her satisfaction to those behind them, and those in turn to others, and so on, soon set the neighbouring streets ringing with applause. In the same way, during two or three hours of slow, lingering speech and the sifting of many bushels of words, Madame Defarge’s frequent signs of impatience were carried, with astonishing speed, to a distance; and the message traveled all the more easily because certain men, who by some marvelous feat of nimbleness had climbed the outer framework to peer in at the windows, knew Madame Defarge well, and served as a kind of telegraph between her and the crowd outside the building.

It had been their custom to play skat with Herr Heinrich, who had shown them the game very explicitly and thoroughly. But there was no longer any Herr Heinrich--and somehow German games were already out of fashion. The two philosophers admitted that they had already considered skat to be complicated without subtlety, and that its chief delight for them had been the pink earnestness of Herr Heinrich, his inability to grasp their complete but tacit comprehension of its innocent strategy, and his invariable ill-success to bring off the coups that flashed before his imagination.

They used to play skat regularly with Herr Heinrich, who had taught them the rules with painstaking clarity. But Herr Heinrich was gone now—and, in any case, German card games had fallen from favor. The two philosophers confessed that they had long found skat elaborate yet lacking in real depth, and that its main pleasure had lain in Herr Heinrich’s flushed seriousness, his failure to perceive that they fully yet silently grasped its guileless tactics, and his unfailing inability to execute the brilliant moves his mind kept conjuring.

He went downstairs and out into the street, breathing great breaths of air. He had been suffocating in that atmosphere, while the apprentice’s chatter had driven him frantic. There had been times when it was all he could do to refrain from reaching over and mopping Jim’s face in the mush-plate. The more he had chattered, the more remote had Ruth seemed to him. How could he, herding with such cattle, ever become worthy of her? He was appalled at the problem confronting him, weighted down by the incubus of his working-class station. Everything reached out to hold him down—his sister, his sister’s house and family, Jim the apprentice, everybody he knew, every tie of life. Existence did not taste good in his mouth. Up to then he had accepted existence, as he had lived it with all about him, as a good thing. He had never questioned it, except when he read books; but then, they were only books, fairy stories of a fairer and impossible world. But now he had seen that world, possible and real, with a flower of a woman called Ruth in the midmost centre of it; and thenceforth he must know bitter tastes, and longings sharp as pain, and hopelessness that tantalized because it fed on hope.

He descended the narrow stairwell and stepped into the open air, drawing deep, desperate lungfuls of the evening. The stagnant atmosphere inside had begun to choke him, and Arthur’s relentless, vapid prattle had pushed his nerves to a jagged edge. More than once, he had felt a violent impulse to seize the boy by the scruff and bury his grinning face in the bowl of cold porridge. Every mindless syllable Arthur uttered seemed to widen the gulf between himself and Elena. How could a man mired in such common company ever hope to stand beside her?

The sheer scale of his predicament struck him with a heavy dread, the leaden weight of his birthright pinning him to the earth. It was as if every thread of his life—his sister’s cramped parlor, her noisy brood, the apprentice’s idiocy, and every familiar face—conspired to drag him back into the mud. The very flavor of his life had turned to ash. Until this moment, he had moved through his days with a blunt acceptance, viewing his lot as natural and sufficient. Doubts had only ever flickered when he retreated into novels, but those had been mere fables—glimpses of a porcelain reality that could never truly exist. Now, however, he had glimpsed that world in the flesh, a tangible paradise with Elena at its radiant heart. From this night on, his bread would taste of bile, his desires would cut like glass, and he would be haunted by a despair that was all the more cruel for its refusal to let go of hope.

Then, to business. I tested the electric signals from the gatling platform to the cave, and made sure that they were all right; I tested and retested those which commanded the fences--these were signals whereby I could break and renew the electric current in each fence independently of the others at will. I placed the brook-connection under the guard and authority of three of my best boys, who would alternate in two-hour watches all night and promptly obey my signal, if I should have occasion to give it --three revolver-shots in quick succession. Sentry-duty was discarded for the night, and the corral left empty of life; I ordered that quiet be maintained in the cave, and the electric lights turned down to a glimmer.

Then it was down to business. I checked the electric signals running from the gatling platform to the cave and satisfied myself that they were in working order; I checked and rechecked those controlling the fences—signals by which I could interrupt and restore the electric current in each fence separately, whenever I chose. I put the brook connection under the supervision of three of my best boys, who were to take turns on two-hour watches throughout the night and, if necessary, obey my signal at once—three revolver shots in rapid succession. Sentry duty was suspended for the night, and the corral was left deserted; I gave orders that silence be kept in the cave and that the electric lights be dimmed to a faint glow.

And this double refraction of his mind by which a concentrated and individualised Britling did but present a larger impersonal Britling beneath, carried with it a duplication of his conscience and sense of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was answerable for his private honour and his debts and the Dower House he had made and so on, but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. The world from the latter point of view was his egg. He had a subconscious delusion that he had laid it. He had a subconscious suspicion that he had let it cool and that it was addled. He had an urgency to incubate it. The variety and interest of his talk was largely due to that persuasion, it was a perpetual attempt to spread his mental feathers over the task before him....

And this double refraction of his mind, by which the intensified and particular Britling merely revealed a larger, impersonal Britling underneath, brought with it a duplication of conscience and of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was accountable for his private honour, his debts, the Dower House he had created, and the rest; but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. From that point of view the world was his egg. He had a subconscious illusion that he himself had laid it. He had a subconscious fear that he had let it grow cold and that it was spoiled. He felt an urgent need to sit upon it and warm it. The richness and variety of his conversation were largely due to this conviction; it was a continual effort to spread his mental feathers over the work before him....

We pass rapidly on into the month of March, to a breezy day without sunshine, frost, or dew. On Yalbury Hill, about midway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge, where the turnpike road passes over the crest, a numerous concourse of people had gathered, the eyes of the greater number being frequently stretched afar in a northerly direction. The groups consisted of a throng of idlers, a party of javelin-men, and two trumpeters, and in the midst were carriages, one of which contained the high sheriff. With the idlers, many of whom had mounted to the top of a cutting formed for the road, were several Weatherbury men and boys—among others Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

Time moved forward quickly into the month of March, to a blustery day that lacked both the warmth of the sun and the crispness of frost or dew. At the summit of Yalbury Hill, halfway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge where the main road crosses the ridge, a large crowd had assembled, their gazes frequently fixed on the northern horizon. This gathering consisted of a local throng, a contingent of javelin-men, and two trumpeters; in their center stood several carriages, one of which held the high sheriff. Among the spectators, many of whom had climbed the steep embankments flanking the road, were several men and boys from Weatherbury—including Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

Gabriel’s farm had been stocked by a dealer—on the strength of Oak’s promising look and character—who was receiving a percentage from the farmer till such time as the advance should be cleared off. Oak found that the value of stock, plant, and implements which were really his own would be about sufficient to pay his debts, leaving himself a free man with the clothes he stood up in, and nothing more.

Elias Thorne’s upland farm had been fully stocked by a regional supply merchant—who’d agreed to the arrangement solely on the weight of Thorne’s earnest bearing and solid local reputation—who collected a fixed percentage of the farm’s monthly earnings until the full sum of the initial advance was repaid. When Thorne tallied all his holdings, he discovered the total worth of livestock, crop stores, and tools that rightfully belonged to him was barely enough to settle all his outstanding debts, leaving him unburdened by obligations but owning nothing beyond the scuffed work clothes he had on his back.

Naturally," Mr. Britling remarked, "none of this will be accomplished without a struggle. We cannot expect to reclaim a nation we’ve ignored for so long without experiencing significant domestic tension. Yet, here in England, we are capable of making these transitions without resorting to an uprising. That is where our true power lies...

We were brought up together; there was not quite a year difference in our ages. I need not say that we were strangers to any species of disunion or dispute. Harmony was the soul of our companionship, and the diversity and contrast that subsisted in our characters drew us nearer together. Elizabeth was of a calmer and more concentrated disposition; but, with all my ardour, I was capable of a more intense application and was more deeply smitten with the thirst for knowledge. She busied herself with following the aerial creations of the poets; and in the majestic and wondrous scenes which surrounded our Swiss home —the sublime shapes of the mountains, the changes of the seasons, tempest and calm, the silence of winter, and the life and turbulence of our Alpine summers—she found ample scope for admiration and delight. While my companion contemplated with a serious and satisfied spirit the magnificent appearances of things, I delighted in investigating their causes. The world was to me a secret which I desired to divine. Curiosity, earnest research to learn the hidden laws of nature, gladness akin to rapture, as they were unfolded to me, are among the earliest sensations I can remember.

We grew up together, with less than a year between our ages. I need hardly say that we knew nothing of estrangement or quarrels. Harmony was the very heart of our friendship, and the differences in our natures only drew us closer. Elizabeth was calmer and more self-possessed; for all my eagerness, I was capable of a more sustained application and was more keenly driven by a hunger for knowledge. She occupied herself with pursuing the airy visions of poets, and in the grand and marvelous scenery that encircled our Swiss home—the sublime forms of the mountains, the shifting seasons, storm and stillness, the hush of winter, and the vitality and turmoil of our Alpine summers—she found abundant cause for wonder and pleasure. While my companion regarded the splendid spectacle of the world with a grave and contented mind, I took delight in probing its causes. The world was, to me, a mystery I longed to unravel. Curiosity, an earnest desire to discover nature’s hidden laws, and a joy bordering on ecstasy as they were revealed to me, are among the earliest feelings I can recall.

"My one consolation," he said, "in this storm is a talk I had last month with a young Irishwoman in Meath. She was a young person of twelve, and she took a fancy to me--I think because I went with her in an alleged dangerous canoe she was forbidden to navigate alone. All day the eternal Irish Question had banged about over her observant head. When we were out on the water she suddenly decided to set me right upon a disregarded essential. 'You English,' she said, 'are just a bit disposed to take all this trouble seriously. Don't you fret yourself about it... Half the time we're just laffing at you. You'd best leave us all alone....'"

My only comfort in the midst of this upheaval," he remarked, "is a conversation I had last month with a young Irish girl in Meath. She was only twelve years old, and she took a liking to me—mostly, I suspect, because I joined her in a supposedly hazardous canoe she was forbidden from rowing alone. All through the day, the incessant arguments regarding the Irish Question had been swirling around her observant head. Once we were out on the water, she decided to enlighten me on a crucial point that everyone else seemed to ignore. 'You English,' she said, 'have a tendency to take all this conflict far too seriously. Don't let it trouble you... Half the time we’re just laughing at you. You really ought to just leave us be.'

The rise of encoder-decoder models (Cho et al., 2014; Sutskever et al., 2014) as well as the attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017) and copy mechanisms (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016) has dramatically changed the landscape, and now one can perform the task relatively easily with an encoder-decoder model such as Transformer provided that a sufficient amount of data is available. For example, Li et al. (2018) introduce a deep reinforcement learning framework for paraphrasing, consisting of a generator and an evaluator. Yin et al. (2019) formalize the problem of text edit as learning and utilization of edit representations and propose an encoder-decoder model for the task. Zhao et al. (2018) integrate paraphrasing rules with the Transformer model for text simplification. Zhao et al. (2019) proposes a method for English grammar correction using a Transformer and copy mechanism.

metadata.title: Fact-based Text Editing

text: The advent of encoder-decoder architectures (Cho et al., 2014; Sutskever et al., 2014), together with attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017) and copy mechanisms (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016), has substantially reshaped the field, such that the task can now be addressed relatively straightforwardly with an encoder-decoder model such as Transformer, assuming sufficient training data are available. For instance, Li et al. (2018) propose a deep reinforcement learning framework for paraphrasing that comprises a generator and an evaluator. Yin et al. (2019) cast text editing as the learning and use of edit representations and present an encoder-decoder model for this purpose. Zhao et al. (2018) combine paraphrasing rules with the Transformer model for text simplification. Zhao et al. (2019) introduce a method for English grammar correction based on a Transformer and a copy mechanism.

"Nor did I. But I do now.... I have suddenly found it and seen it plain. I see it so plain that I am amazed that I have not always seen it.... It is, you see, so easy to understand that there is a God, and how complex and wonderful and brotherly He is, when one thinks of those dear boys who by the thousand, by the hundred thousand, have laid down their lives.... Ay, and there were German boys too who did the same.... The cruelties, the injustice, the brute aggression--they saw it differently. They laid down their lives--they laid down their lives.... Those dear lives, those lives of hope and sunshine....

I didn't either. But I do at this moment. It has finally revealed itself to me, and I see it with total clarity. I see it so vividly that I am stunned it wasn't obvious to me all along. You see, it becomes quite simple to believe in God—to see how intricate, magnificent, and compassionate He is—when you reflect on those young men who, by the thousands and hundreds of thousands, gave up their lives. Yes, and there were German boys who did exactly the same. The cruelty, the unfairness, the raw violence—they perceived it all differently. They sacrificed themselves; they gave everything they had. All those precious lives, so full of potential and light.

“O, that’s no inconvenience,” said Clym, smiling rather sadly. And then the party drove off and vanished in the night shades, and Yeobright entered the house. The ticking of the clock was the only sound that greeted him, for not a soul remained; Christian, who acted as cook, valet, and gardener to Clym, sleeping at his father’s house. Yeobright sat down in one of the vacant chairs, and remained in thought a long time. His mother’s old chair was opposite; it had been sat in that evening by those who had scarcely remembered that it ever was hers. But to Clym she was almost a presence there, now as always. Whatever she was in other people’s memories, in his she was the sublime saint whose radiance even his tenderness for Eustacia could not obscure. But his heart was heavy, that Mother had _not_ crowned him in the day of his espousals and in the day of the gladness of his heart. And events had borne out the accuracy of her judgment, and proved the devotedness of her care. He should have heeded her for Eustacia’s sake even more than for his own. “It was all my fault,” he whispered. “O, my mother, my mother! would to God that I could live my life again, and endure for you what you endured for me!”

“Oh, that is of no consequence,” said Adrian, with a smile that was more sorrowful than gay. Then the little party set off, and was soon swallowed by the darkness; and Valen crossed the threshold and went in. The house was met only by the measured ticking of the clock, for no living creature remained within. Mercer, who served him as cook, manservant, and gardener, had gone to sleep at his father’s house. Valen sank into one of the empty chairs and sat there long, lost in thought. Opposite him stood his mother’s old chair; that very evening others had occupied it, and scarcely remembered that it had once belonged to her. Yet to Valen she was almost a visible presence in that room, as she had been always. Whatever place she held in other men’s recollection, in his mind she was the lofty saint whose brightness even his love for Lena could not quite eclipse. Still his heart was oppressed because his mother had not crowned him on the day of his marriage, on the day that should have been the gladness of his soul. And all that had happened since had justified her discernment and shown the depth of her devotion. He ought to have listened to her, for Lena’s sake no less than his own. “It was all my doing,” he murmured. “Oh, my mother, my mother! if only I might live my life once more, and bear for you what you bore for me!”

I made about three passes in the air, and then there was an awful crash and that old tower leaped into the sky in chunks, along with a vast volcanic fountain of fire that turned night to noonday, and showed a thousand acres of human beings groveling on the ground in a general collapse of consternation. Well, it rained mortar and masonry the rest of the week. This was the report; but probably the facts would have modified it.

I banked three times overhead, cutting through the cool night air, before a thunderous crash split the dark. The weathered old tower blew apart in jagged chunks, hurled skyward alongside a roiling volcanic geyser of flame that turned midnight as bright as full noon, illuminating a thousand acres of huddled, crumpled people all undone by overwhelming, paralyzing terror. Mortar and chipped masonry rained down on the surrounding land for the rest of the week, or so the account claims; the actual facts of the incident would almost certainly have tempered those outlandish details.

Oak, upon hearing these remarks, became more curious to observe her features, but this prospect being denied him by the hooding effect of the cloak, and by his aërial position, he felt himself drawing upon his fancy for their details. In making even horizontal and clear inspections we colour and mould according to the wants within us whatever our eyes bring in. Had Gabriel been able from the first to get a distinct view of her countenance, his estimate of it as very handsome or slightly so would have been as his soul required a divinity at the moment or was ready supplied with one. Having for some time known the want of a satisfactory form to fill an increasing void within him, his position moreover affording the widest scope for his fancy, he painted her a beauty.

When Ash heard these words, his desire to study her features grew keener; yet the hooded fall of the cloak denied him that sight, and his lofty vantage did no more to help him, so that he found himself compelled to invent their particulars out of his own imagination. Even in the plainest, most level act of looking, we shape and tint what our eyes present to us according to some inward need. Had Raphael, from the first, been granted a clear view of her face, his judgment of it, whether as truly beautiful or only passably so, would have answered to the divinity his soul then demanded, or was prepared at once to accept. Since for some time he had been conscious of a need for some fitting image to occupy a swelling emptiness within him, and since his situation gave his fancy the fullest license, he endowed her with beauty.

For most languages, there exists a male bias in predictions of the original language. The translated English versions create an even stronger skew. The notable exception is French, which most translation engines render in a demographically faithful manner. Dutch is slightly worse, followed by Italian (note, though, that the Italian data was so heavily imbalanced that we could not sample an even distribution for the test data). Somewhat surprisingly, the gender skew is strongest for German, swinging by as much as 15 percentage points.

metadata.title: "You Sound Just Like Your Father" Commercial Machine Translation Systems Include Stylistic Biases

text: In the majority of languages, predictions based on the original language exhibit a male bias. The translated English versions amplify this skew further. A notable exception is French, which most machine translation systems render in a way that closely reflects demographic distributions. Dutch performs slightly worse, followed by Italian—though it should be noted that the Italian dataset was so severely imbalanced that it was not possible to draw an evenly distributed sample for the test data. Somewhat surprisingly, the gender skew is most pronounced for German, shifting by as much as 15 percentage points.

Language Model BLEU scores often fail to reflect the fluency level of translated texts (Edunov et al., 2019) . Hence, we use the language model (LM) of the target language to regularize the crosslingual semantic similarity metrics, by coupling our cross-lingual similarity scores with a GPT language model of the target language (Radford et al., 2018) . We expect the language model to penalize translationese, i.e., unnatural word-by-word translations and boost the performance of our metrics. 5 judgments are based on comparing human references and system predictions. We will discuss this discrepancy in §5.3.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Language model BLEU scores often do not accurately capture the fluency of translated text (Edunov et al., 2019). Accordingly, we regularize the crosslingual semantic similarity metrics with a target-language language model (LM), coupling our cross-lingual similarity scores with a GPT language model for the target language (Radford et al., 2018). We expect this LM to penalize translationese, that is, unnatural word-for-word translations, and thereby improve the performance of our metrics. Five judgments are obtained by comparing human references with system predictions. We discuss this discrepancy in §5.3.

With the next morning, there came a rumour, that two men and a boy were in the cage at Kingston, who had been apprehended over night under suspicious circumstances; and to Kingston Messrs. Blathers and Duff journeyed accordingly. The suspicious circumstances, however, resolving themselves, on investigation, into the one fact, that they had been discovered sleeping under a haystack; which, although a great crime, is only punishable by imprisonment, and is, in the merciful eye of the English law, and its comprehensive love of all the King’s subjects, held to be no satisfactory proof, in the absence of all other evidence, that the sleeper, or sleepers, have committed burglary accompanied with violence, and have therefore rendered themselves liable to the punishment of death; Messrs. Blathers and Duff came back again, as wise as they went.

At daybreak, word spread that three strangers—two grown men and a lad—had been locked in the Kingston jail, seized the previous evening on questionable grounds. Accordingly, Messrs. Crane and Tully set off for Kingston. Yet the questionable grounds, upon closer scrutiny, dwindled to a single detail: they had been found asleep beneath a hayrick. Now, though this is indeed a serious offense, it warrants only confinement, and in the charitable view of English law, with its ample affection for every subject of the Crown, mere slumber under straw, absent any further proof, cannot be taken as certain evidence that the slumberer or slumberers have perpetrated a violent burglary and thus made themselves answerable to the gallows. Crane and Tully, therefore, returned no wiser than when they departed.

Her nerves extended into those tresses, and her temper could always be softened by stroking them down. When her hair was brushed she would instantly sink into stillness and look like the Sphinx. If, in passing under one of the Egdon banks, any of its thick skeins were caught, as they sometimes were, by a prickly tuft of the large _Ulex Europæus_—which will act as a sort of hairbrush—she would go back a few steps, and pass against it a second time.

Her moods flowed into those locks, and the gentle drawing of a comb through them could always soothe her spirit. While her hair was being smoothed she would lapse at once into quietude and resemble a stone effigy. If, while walking beneath one of the heath’s overhanging thickets, a stray strand happened to snag on a bristling spray of the great _Ulex Europæus_—which serves as a natural brush—she would retrace her steps and brush deliberately against it once more.

A restless fifteen minutes stretched between the courier’s exit and the renewed thud of hooves beyond the door. She dared not look out now; instead, bent above the ancient escritoire where she had penned her note, she shut her eyes tightly, as though to bar the entry of both dread and expectation.

In a gesture born of urgency, compelled by her condition and the conductor’s summoning, he pulled the trembling arm from his shoulder around his neck, raised her slightly, and hastened her inside. He settled her just past the threshold, steadying her as she clung to him.

I cannot enjoy delicacies; good things are wasted upon me. Well, I ought to turn that defect to advantage, and by being able to do without what other people require I can spend what such things cost upon anybody else.”

“I can’t say for certain, other than that there are so many things other people hold dear that I don’t care for at all; that’s part of why I feel I ought to go through with this. For one thing, I have very few physical needs. Fancy delicacies hold no pleasure for me; all those fine, costly indulgences would be wasted on someone who can’t appreciate them. Well, I ought to turn that quirk of mine to good use: since I can get by perfectly well without all the things other people rely on, I can put the money those things would cost toward anyone who actually needs it.”

I had some most elegant people in it last summer—no trouble at all, and paid in advance to the minute. The water’s at the end of the hall. Sprowls and Mooney kept it three months. They done a vaudeville sketch. Miss B’retta Sprowls—you may have heard of her—Oh, that was just the stage names—right there over the dresser is where the marriage certificate hung, framed. The gas is here, and you see there is plenty of closet room. It’s a room everybody likes. It never stays idle long.”

“This is the room,” the housekeeper announced, her voice burred deep in her throat. “A lovely room. Doesn’t come free often. Last summer I had some truly refined guests—no trouble whatsoever, and their payment arrived right on the dot. The water closet’s down the hall. Sprowls and Mooney had it for three months. They performed a vaudeville act. Miss B’retta Sprowls—the name might ring a bell—well, those were just their stage names—right there above the dresser is where the marriage certificate used to hang, all framed. The gas jet’s here, and you can see there’s ample closet space. It’s a room that charms everyone. It never stays empty long.”

The whole Britling family, who were lamenting the loss of their Belgians, welcomed the coming of the twenty-five with great enthusiasm. It made them feel that they were doing something useful once more. For three days Mrs. Britling had to feed her new lodgers--the kitchen motors had as usual gone astray--and she did so in a style that made their boastings about their billet almost insufferable to the rest of their battery. The billeting allowance at that time was ninepence a head, and Mr. Britling, ashamed of making a profit out of his country, supplied not only generous firing and lighting, but unlimited cigarettes, cards and games, illustrated newspapers, a cocoa supper with such little surprises as sprats and jam roly-poly, and a number of more incidental comforts. The men arrived fasting under the command of two very sage middle-aged corporals, and responded to Mrs. Britling's hospitalities by a number of good resolutions, many of which they kept. They never made noises after half-past ten, or at least only now and then when a singsong broke out with unusual violence; they got up and went out at five or six in the morning without a sound; they were almost inconveniently helpful with washing-up and tidying round.

The Britling household, still mourning their departed Belgian guests, greeted the twenty-five new arrivals with open arms. Their presence restored a sense of purpose. For three days Mrs. Britling—whose kitchen machinery had, predictably, malfunctioned—fed the soldiers so lavishly that their tales of the billet became nearly unbearable to the rest of their battery. The official allowance was ninepence per man, but Mr. Britling, unwilling to profit from his nation’s need, provided not only generous heat and light but endless cigarettes, cards, games, illustrated papers, a cocoa supper featuring modest treats like sprats and jam roly-poly, and countless smaller comforts. The men, who had marched in hungry under two quietly seasoned corporals, repaid Mrs. Britling’s kindness with a string of earnest promises, many of which they honored. They kept silent after half-past ten—barring the occasional sing-song that erupted with sudden fervor—rose and slipped out at five or six without a whisper, and proved almost excessively eager to help with washing-up and tidying.

For the core results in this paper, we use the Conceptual Captions dataset (Sharma et al., 2018) as our English-annotated generation labels, in order to capture web-scale phenomena related to image captioning. In addition, we use Google Translate as the translation engine (both for the run-time translations needed for the TGT approach and the training-time translations needed for the TTG and PLuGS approaches), targeting French, Italian, German, Spanish, and Hindi as target languages. We use the standard training and validation splits from Conceptual Captions for developing our models. We report the results using a set of 1,000 randomly samples images from the Open Images Dataset (Kuznetsova et al., 2018) . We refer to this test set as OID1k when reporting our results.

metadata.title: Cross-modal Language Generation using Pivot Stabilization for Web-scale Language Coverage

text: Our primary experiments rely on the Conceptual Captions dataset (Sharma et al., 2018) to supply English-annotated generation targets, enabling us to investigate web-scale image captioning phenomena. We employ Google Translate as the machine translation system, both for on-the-fly translations required by the TGT method and for offline translations needed by the TTG and PLuGS strategies, with French, Italian, German, Spanish, and Hindi serving as the target languages. Model development is conducted on the standard training and validation partitions of Conceptual Captions. For evaluation, we measure performance on a collection of 1,000 randomly selected images from the Open Images Dataset (Kuznetsova et al., 2018), which we denote as OID1k when presenting our findings.

“Well, then, there 's photographing,” said Captain Abel. “It doos seem amazing, as the minister said: you set down, and square yourself, and slick your hair, and stare stiddy into a funnel, and a man ducks his head under a covering, and pop! there you be, as natural as life,--if not more so. And when Uncle Capen was a young man, there wasn't nothing but portraits and minnytures, and these black-paper-and-scissors portraits,--what do they call 'em? Yes, sir, all that come in under his observation.”

“Well, then, there’s photography,” said Captain Silas. “It does seem astonishing, as the minister said: you sit down, and square yourself up, and smooth your hair, and stare steady into a little funnel, and a man ducks his head under a cloth, and pop! there you are, as natural as life—if not more so. And when Uncle Hiram was a young man, there was nothing but portraits and miniatures, and those black-paper-and-scissors likenesses—what do they call them? Yes, sir, all that came within his observation.”

It may be too late, I don’t know, but let it not be a minute later!”

It might already be too late, I cannot tell, but do not let another moment slip!”

Now old Merlin stepped into view and cast a dainty web of gossamer threads over Sir Sagramor which turned him into Hamlet's ghost; the king made a sign, the bugles blew, Sir Sagramor laid his great lance in rest, and the next moment here he came thundering down the course with his veil flying out behind, and I went whistling through the air like an arrow to meet him --cocking my ear the while, as if noting the invisible knight's position and progress by hearing, not sight. A chorus of encouraging shouts burst out for him, and one brave voice flung out a heartening word for me--said:

“But the upshot of it all—of my thinking and reading and loving—is that I am going to move to Grub Street. I shall leave masterpieces alone and do hack-work—jokes, paragraphs, feature articles, humorous verse, and society verse—all the rot for which there seems so much demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, and the newspaper short-story syndicates, and the syndicates for the Sunday supplements. I can go ahead and hammer out the stuff they want, and earn the equivalent of a good salary by it. There are free-lances, you know, who earn as much as four or five hundred a month. I don’t care to become as they; but I’ll earn a good living, and have plenty of time to myself, which I wouldn’t have in any position.”

The end result of all my reflections, my studies, and my passions is this: I am bound for Grub Street. I’ll abandon any thought of masterpieces and turn to hack-work—jokes, squibs, feature pieces, light verse, society rhymes—all the fluff that seems to be in such endless demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, the short-story syndicates, and the syndicates that feed the Sunday supplements. I can churn out exactly the stuff they want and pull in the equivalent of a decent salary. There are free-lances, as you know, who make four or five hundred a month. I have no wish to become one of them; but I’ll earn a comfortable living and keep ample time for myself, which no fixed position would ever allow me.

Then fetching ink and a quilt from the rickety bureau by the window, she blackened the feet of the image to the extent presumably covered by shoes; and on the instep of each foot marked cross-lines in the shape taken by the sandalstrings of those days. Finally she tied a bit of black thread round the upper part of the head, in faint resemblance to a snood worn for confining the hair.

Mistress Craythorne hunted through her belongings until she unearthed a scrap of the slenderest crimson cord, carried it below, and fastened it about the figurine’s throat. Then, retrieving a pot of lampblack and a feather-stuffed counterpane from the tottering escritoire beside the casement, she darkened the feet of the little figure up to the line where slippers would have covered them, and upon the arch of each foot etched crossing strokes to mimic the crisscross thongs of the era’s sandals. At last she looped a strand of jet thread around the crown of the head, a ghost of the caul once used to bind the tresses.

Nothing could be more complete than the alteration that had taken place in my feelings since the night of the appearance of the dæmon. I had before regarded my promise with a gloomy despair as a thing that, with whatever consequences, must be fulfilled; but I now felt as if a film had been taken from before my eyes and that I for the first time saw clearly. The idea of renewing my labours did not for one instant occur to me; the threat I had heard weighed on my thoughts, but I did not reflect that a voluntary act of mine could avert it. I had resolved in my own mind that to create another like the fiend I had first made would be an act of the basest and most atrocious selfishness, and I banished from my mind every thought that could lead to a different conclusion.

The transformation of my inner state since the night the creature appeared was absolute. I had previously regarded my promise with a sense of hopeless resignation, believing it a burden that must be endured regardless of the cost; now, however, it felt as though a shroud had been lifted from my sight, granting me a sudden, piercing clarity. Not for a single moment did I consider returning to my work. Though the monster’s threats haunted my thoughts, I refused to believe that any voluntary action of mine should be taken to avert his wrath. I had resolved that to fashion another being like the first would be an act of the most profound and wicked selfishness, and I cast out every impulse that might have swayed me from this conviction.

While Mr. Britling was trying to find his duty in the routine of a special constable, Mrs. Britling set to work with great energy to attend various classes and qualify herself for Red Cross work. And early in October came the great drive of the Germans towards Antwerp and the sea, the great drive that was apparently designed to reach Calais, and which swept before it multitudes of Flemish refugees. There was an exodus of all classes from Antwerp into Holland and England, and then a huge process of depopulation in Flanders and the Pas de Calais. This flood came to the eastern and southern parts of England and particularly to London, and there hastily improvised organisations distributed it to a number of local committees, each of which took a share of the refugees, hired and furnished unoccupied houses for the use of the penniless, and assisted those who had means into comfortable quarters. The Matching's Easy committee found itself with accommodation for sixty people, and with a miscellaneous bag of thirty individuals entrusted to its care, who had been part of the load of a little pirate steam-boat from Ostend. There were two Flemish peasant families, and the rest were more or less middle-class refugees from Antwerp. They were brought from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings, and there distributed, under the personal supervision of Lady Homartyn and her agent, among those who were prepared for their entertainment. There was something like competition among the would-be hosts; everybody was glad of the chance of "doing something," and anxious to show these Belgians what England thought of their plucky little country. Mr. Britling was proud to lead off a Mr. Van der Pant, a neat little bearded man in a black tail-coat, a black bowler hat, and a knitted muffler, with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign-looking bicycle, to the hospitalities of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had escaped from Antwerp at the eleventh hour, he had caught a severe cold and, it would seem, lost his wife and family in the process; he had much to tell Mr. Britling, and in his zeal to tell it he did not at once discover that though Mr. Britling knew French quite well he did not know it very rapidly.

While Mr. Britling groped for his duty in the tedium of a special constable’s round, Mrs. Britling flung herself into a flurry of classes, determined to earn her place in the Red Cross. Then, early in October, came the great German thrust toward Antwerp and the sea—a drive plainly aimed at Calais—and before it fled a tide of Flemish refugees. All ranks poured out of Antwerp into Holland and England, and a vast emptying of Flanders and the Pas de Calais followed. This human flood washed into the eastern and southern counties, especially London, where hastily cobbled relief bodies funnelled it out to local committees. Each committee took its quota, found and furnished empty houses for the destitute, and guided those with money into decent lodgings. The Matching’s Easy committee discovered it had beds for sixty but only a mixed bag of thirty souls to mind—part of the cargo of a rakish little steamer from Ostend. Among them were two Flemish peasant families; the rest were mostly middle-class fugitives from Antwerp. They were conveyed from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings and there apportioned, under Lady Homartyn’s eye and her agent’s, to households ready to receive them. A kind of rivalry sprang up among the would-be hosts; everyone relished the chance to “do something” and was eager to show the Belgians what England felt for their plucky little land. Mr. Britling was proud to usher a Mr. Van der Pant—a trim, bearded man in a black frock-coat, black bowler, and knitted muffler, burdened with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign bicycle—into the comforts of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had slipped out of Antwerp at the final moment, had caught a nasty cold, and, it seemed, mislaid his wife and family along the way; he had a great deal to tell Mr. Britling, and in his eagerness did not immediately notice that while Mr. Britling knew French respectably, he did not know it fast.

text: Ikey went behind the prescription desk. There he crushed to a powder two soluble tablets, each containing a quarter of a grain of morphia. To them he added a little sugar of milk to increase the bulk, and folded the mixture neatly in a white paper. Taken by an adult this powder would insure several hours of heavy slumber without danger to the sleeper. This he handed to Chunk McGowan, telling him to administer it in a liquid if possible, and received the hearty thanks of the backyard Lochinvar.

Ikey stepped behind the prescription counter. Once there, he ground two dissolvable tablets—each holding a quarter grain of morphine—into a fine powder. He mixed in a small pinch of milk sugar to bulk the dose up slightly, then folded the combined mixture tidily into a slip of white paper. For a full-grown adult, this powder would guarantee several hours of deep, uninterrupted sleep with no risk of harm to the person taking it. He handed the folded packet to Chunk McGowan, advised him to mix it into a drink if at all possible when giving it out, and got an effusive, heartfelt thank-you from that scrappy backyard Lochinvar.

Initial works in AMR-to-text used transducers (Flanigan et al., 2016) , phrase-based machine translation (Pourdamghani et al., 2016) and neural sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models with linearized graphs (Konstas et al., 2017) . Cao and Clark (2019) leverage constituency parsing for generation. Beck et al. (2018) improve upon prior RNN graph encoding (Song et al., 2018) with Levi Graph Transformations. Damonte and Cohen (2019) compare multiple representations and find graph encoders to be the best. Guo et al. (2019) use RNN graph encoders with dense graph convolutional encoding. Ribeiro et al. (2019) use RNN encoders with dual graph representations. Transformer-based seq2seq (Vaswani et al., 2017) was first applied to AMR-to-text in (Sinh and Le Minh, 2019) . Zhu et al. (2019) greatly improve over the prior state-of-the-art by modifying self-attention to account for AMR graph structure. Using transformers has also been recently explored by Wang et al. (2020) who propose a mutli-head graph attention mechanism.

metadata.title: GPT-too: A Language-Model-First Approach for AMR-to-Text Generation

text: Early work on AMR-to-text generation employed transducers (Flanigan et al., 2016), phrase-based machine translation (Pourdamghani et al., 2016), and neural sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models with linearized graphs (Konstas et al., 2017). Cao and Clark (2019) exploit constituency parsing for generation. Beck et al. (2018) build on earlier RNN graph encoding work (Song et al., 2018) by introducing Levi Graph Transformations. Damonte and Cohen (2019) evaluate several representations and show that graph encoders perform best. Guo et al. (2019) adopt RNN graph encoders together with dense graph convolutional encoding. Ribeiro et al. (2019) use RNN encoders with dual graph representations. Transformer-based seq2seq (Vaswani et al., 2017) was first applied to AMR-to-text by Sinh and Le Minh (2019). Zhu et al. (2019) substantially surpass the previous state of the art by modifying self-attention to incorporate AMR graph structure. The use of transformers has also recently been investigated by Wang et al. (2020), who propose a multi-head graph attention mechanism.

The captain was a fishy-eyed Norwegian who somehow had fallen into possession of a complete Shakespeare, which he never read, and Martin had washed his clothes for him and in return been permitted access to the precious volumes. For a time, so steeped was he in the plays and in the many favorite passages that impressed themselves almost without effort on his brain, that all the world seemed to shape itself into forms of Elizabethan tragedy or comedy and his very thoughts were in blank verse. It trained his ear and gave him a fine appreciation for noble English; withal it introduced into his mind much that was archaic and obsolete.

The skipper, a Norwegian with dull, glassy eyes, had somehow acquired a full collection of Shakespeare that he ignored entirely. Martin had taken to laundering the man’s garments as payment for the privilege of poring over the treasured books. Before long, he became so saturated with the dramas and the numerous lines that lodged themselves naturally in his memory that the entire world appeared to take the shape of an Elizabethan play. He even found himself thinking in the steady rhythm of blank verse. While this immersion refined his sense of rhythm and instilled a deep respect for elegant English, it simultaneously filled his head with a great deal of antiquated and useless language.

When I came to again, I was sitting under an oak tree, on the grass, with a whole beautiful and broad country landscape all to myself--nearly. Not entirely; for there was a fellow on a horse, looking down at me--a fellow fresh out of a picture-book. He was in old-time iron armor from head to heel, with a helmet on his head the shape of a nail-keg with slits in it; and he had a shield, and a sword, and a prodigious spear; and his horse had armor on, too, and a steel horn projecting from his forehead, and gorgeous red and green silk trappings that hung down all around him like a bedquilt, nearly to the ground.

When I came to myself again, I was seated on the grass beneath an oak tree, with a whole broad, lovely stretch of country spread out before me—almost to myself. Not quite, for there was a man on horseback looking down at me, a man who seemed to have stepped straight out of a storybook. He was clad from head to foot in ancient iron armor, with a helmet upon his head shaped like a nail keg and slit for sight; he carried a shield, a sword, and a monstrous spear, and even his horse wore armor, with a steel horn jutting from its forehead and splendid red-and-green silk trappings hanging all about it like a bedspread, almost to the ground.

Mr. Britling temporised by saying he would "make inquiries," and put Mr. Van der Pant off for two days. Then he decided to go up to London with him and "make inquiries on the spot." Mr. Van der Pant did not discover his family, but Mr. Britling discovered the profound truth of a comment of Herr Heinrich's which he had hitherto considered utterly trivial, but which had nevertheless stuck in his memory. "The English," Herr Heinrich had said, "do not understanding indexing. It is the root of all good organisation."

Mr. Britling stalled for time, promising to "investigate the matter," and thus managed to delay Mr. Van der Pant for two days. Eventually, he resolved to travel to London with him to conduct his search firsthand. While the trip failed to reunite Mr. Van der Pant with his family, it led Mr. Britling to recognize the deep validity of a remark by Herr Heinrich—one he had once dismissed as insignificant, yet which had remained lodged in his mind. "The English," Herr Heinrich had observed, "lack a grasp of indexing, which is the very foundation of all efficient organization."

“Good night to you,” the great-uncle said, his tone polished but empty of real warmth. “I look forward to the pleasure of your company at the breakfast table come morning. Rest well.—See that my grand-nephew is escorted to his chamber with a lamp, won’t you?” He waited until the hovering footman had turned to leave, then muttered under his breath, “And burn the boy alive in his bed if you’re so inclined, for all I care,” before he rang his small brass call bell once more, summoning his personal valet to his own private bedroom.

Such a perfect, delicate, and necessary finish did the figure give to the dark pile of hills that it seemed to be the only obvious justification of their outline. Without it, there was the dome without the lantern; with it the architectural demands of the mass were satisfied. The scene was strangely homogeneous, in that the vale, the upland, the barrow, and the figure above it amounted only to unity. Looking at this or that member of the group was not observing a complete thing, but a fraction of a thing.

So perfect, so subtle, and so essential was the figure’s effect on the dark cluster of hills that it seemed the only plain reason for their shape. Without it, there was a dome lacking its lantern; with it, the whole architectural need of the mass was fulfilled. The scene was remarkably unified, for the valley, the upland, the barrow, and the figure standing above it together made only a single whole. To look at one part or another of the grouping was not to see something complete, but merely a fragment of one.
