metadata.title: A Graph Auto-encoder Model of Derivational Morphology

text: Encoder. The encoder $g_\theta$ incorporates the adjacency matrix $B$ of the directed graph (DG) $B$ as a primary input. This allows us to represent the stem and affix group forms, $f_s$ and $f_a$, through the associative connections they establish within the mental lexicon. Because the DG does not account for semantic relationships between nodes in $S$ and $A$, we reintroduce semantic content via auxiliary feature vectors $x_s, x_a \in \mathbb{R}^n$ for the stem and affix group embeddings $m_s$ and $m_a$, which are trained independently on the SR corpora. Consequently, the input to $g_\theta$ is structured to provide synergistic information: the associative framework provided by $B$ and the semantic representations captured in $x_s$ and $x_a$.

Well, the king was out of the hole; and on terms satisfactory to the Church and the rest of the aristocracy, no doubt. Men write many fine and plausible arguments in support of monarchy, but the fact remains that where every man in a State has a vote, brutal laws are impossible. Arthur's people were of course poor material for a republic, because they had been debased so long by monarchy; and yet even they would have been intelligent enough to make short work of that law which the king had just been administering if it had been submitted to their full and free vote. There is a phrase which has grown so common in the world's mouth that it has come to seem to have sense and meaning--the sense and meaning implied when it is used; that is the phrase which refers to this or that or the other nation as possibly being “capable of self-government”; and the implied sense of it is, that there has been a nation somewhere, some time or other which _wasn't_ capable of it--wasn't as able to govern itself as some self-appointed specialists were or would be to govern it. The master minds of all nations, in all ages, have sprung in affluent multitude from the mass of the nation, and from the mass of the nation only--not from its privileged classes; and so, no matter what the nation's intellectual grade was; whether high or low, the bulk of its ability was in the long ranks of its nameless and its poor, and so it never saw the day that it had not the material in abundance whereby to govern itself. Which is to assert an always self-proven fact: that even the best governed and most free and most enlightened monarchy is still behind the best condition attainable by its people; and that the same is true of kindred governments of lower grades, all the way down to the lowest.

The king had finally escaped his predicament, surely under conditions that satisfied both the Church and the nobility. Many eloquent justifications are written in defense of kingship, but the truth is simple: wherever universal suffrage exists, cruel laws cannot endure. Arthur’s subjects were admittedly poor candidates for a republic, having been diminished by centuries of royal oppression; yet even they would have been wise enough to dismantle the harsh statutes the king had just upheld if the matter were put to a free vote. A particular phrase has become so common that it is often mistaken for logic—the idea that a nation might not be “capable of self-government.” This suggests that there has ever been a people less fit to govern themselves than the self-anointed specialists who would presume to do it for them. In every era, the finest minds have risen in great numbers from the common folk, not the privileged few. No matter the general state of a nation’s intellect, the vast majority of its capability lies with the nameless poor; consequently, no society has ever lacked the internal strength to manage its own destiny. It is an undeniable fact that even the most enlightened monarchy lags behind the potential of a self-governing people, a truth that applies to every form of autocracy, from the most refined to the most base.

An oil painting caught and held him. A heavy surf thundered and burst over an outjutting rock; lowering storm-clouds covered the sky; and, outside the line of surf, a pilot-schooner, close-hauled, heeled over till every detail of her deck was visible, was surging along against a stormy sunset sky. There was beauty, and it drew him irresistibly. He forgot his awkward walk and came closer to the painting, very close. The beauty faded out of the canvas. His face expressed his bepuzzlement. He stared at what seemed a careless daub of paint, then stepped away. Immediately all the beauty flashed back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” was his thought, as he dismissed it, though in the midst of the multitudinous impressions he was receiving he found time to feel a prod of indignation that so much beauty should be sacrificed to make a trick. He did not know painting. He had been brought up on chromos and lithographs that were always definite and sharp, near or far. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in the show windows of shops, but the glass of the windows had prevented his eager eyes from approaching too near.

An oil painting seized his attention. A massive surf roared and shattered against a jutting rock; dark storm clouds smothered the sky; and beyond the breaking waves, a pilot schooner, sails hauled tight, leaned so steeply that every line of her deck lay exposed as she drove forward against a turbulent sunset. There was beauty in it, and it pulled him with irresistible force. He forgot his clumsy gait and moved nearer to the painting, very near. The beauty dissolved from the canvas. His face clouded with bewilderment. He peered at what now looked like a careless smear of paint, then stepped back. Instantly all the beauty flared back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” he thought, dismissing it, though amid the countless impressions flooding in on him he still found room for a stab of resentment that so much beauty should be wasted on a trick. He knew nothing of painting. He had been raised on chromolithographs that stayed crisp and distinct, whether viewed up close or from a distance. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in shop windows, but the glass had always kept his hungry eyes from pressing too near.

We introduce the KLEJ benchmark, a comprehensive set of evaluation tasks for the Polish language understanding. Its goal is to drive the development of better NLU models, so careful selection of tasks was crucial. We mainly focused on a variety of text genres, objectives, text lengths, and difficulties, which allows us to assess the models across different axes. As a result, KLEJ benchmark proves to be both challenging and diverse, as there is no single model that outperforms others on all tasks.

metadata.title: KLEJ: Comprehensive Benchmark for Polish Language Understanding

text: We introduce the KLEJ benchmark, a comprehensive collection of evaluation tasks for Polish language understanding. Its purpose is to promote the development of improved NLU models, which made careful task selection essential. We primarily emphasized a range of text genres, objectives, text lengths, and difficulty levels, enabling us to evaluate models along multiple dimensions. As a result, the KLEJ benchmark is both challenging and diverse, since no single model outperforms the others on all tasks.

This challenge has been tackled by exploiting huge amounts of hand-annotated data in a supervised fashion (Raganato et al., 2017b; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2019; Vial et al., 2019; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020) or, alternatively, by harnessing structured information (Agirre et al., 2014; Moro et al., 2014; , such as that available within existing lexical knowledge bases (LKBs) like WordNet (Fellbaum, 1998) . Despite achieving better overall results, supervised systems require tremendous efforts in order to produce data for several languages (Navigli, 2018; Pasini, 2020) , whereas knowledge-based approaches can easily be applied in multilingual environments due to the wide array of languages covered by LKBs like Ba-belNet 1 (Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012) , or the Open Multilingual WordNet (Bond and Foster, 2013) . Moreover, it is widely acknowledged that the performance of a knowledge-based WSD system is strongly correlated with the structure of the LKB employed (Boyd-Graber et al., 2006; Lemnitzer et al., 2008; Navigli and Lapata, 2010; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2010) . In fact, the knowledge available within LKBs reflects the fact that words can be linked via two types of semantic relations: paradigmatic relations -i.e. the most frequently encountered relations in LKBs -concern the substitution of lexical units, and determine to which level in a hierarchy a language unit belongs by semantic analogy with units similar to it; conversely, syntagmatic relations concern the positioning of such units, by linking elements belonging to the same hierarchical level (e.g., words), which appear in the same context (e.g., a sentence). As a case in point, a paradigmatic relation exists, independently of a given context, between the words farm n and workplace n (where a farm is a type of workplace), whereas a syntagmatic relation is entertained between the words work v and farm n , e.g., in the sentence 'her husband works in a farm as a labourer.'

metadata.title: Personalized PageRank with Syntagmatic Information for Multilingual Word Sense Disambiguation

text: This challenge has been addressed either by leveraging large volumes of manually annotated data in a supervised manner (Raganato et al., 2017b; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2019; Vial et al., 2019; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020) or by drawing on structured information (Agirre et al., 2014; Moro et al., 2014), such as that contained in existing lexical knowledge bases (LKBs) like WordNet (Fellbaum, 1998). Although supervised systems generally attain superior overall performance, they demand substantial effort to generate training data across multiple languages (Navigli, 2018; Pasini, 2020), whereas knowledge-based methods can be readily deployed in multilingual settings thanks to the broad language coverage of LKBs such as BabelNet (Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012) or the Open Multilingual WordNet (Bond and Foster, 2013). Furthermore, it is well established that the performance of a knowledge-based word sense disambiguation system is closely tied to the structure of the underlying LKB (Boyd-Graber et al., 2006; Lemnitzer et al., 2008; Navigli and Lapata, 2010; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2010). Indeed, the knowledge encoded in LKBs reflects the fact that words can be connected through two kinds of semantic relations: paradigmatic relations—the type most commonly found in LKBs—pertain to the substitution of lexical units and determine the hierarchical level a language unit occupies through semantic analogy with similar units; syntagmatic relations, by contrast, concern the arrangement of such units, linking elements that belong to the same hierarchical level (e.g., words) and co-occur in the same context (e.g., a sentence). For instance, a paradigmatic relation holds, independently of any specific context, between the words farm (noun) and workplace (noun) (since a farm is a kind of workplace), whereas a syntagmatic relation obtains between work (verb) and farm (noun), as in the sentence “her husband works in a farm as a labourer.”

You want that sense altogether; therefore I am no more annoyed when I think of the expression, than I should be annoyed by a man’s opinion of a picture of mine, who had no eye for pictures: or of a piece of music of mine, who had no ear for music.”

You are utterly wanting in that faculty; I am therefore no more troubled by your speech than I would be by a blind man’s critique of my canvases, or a deaf man’s estimation of my sonatas.”

“The government of France were greatly enraged at the escape of their victim and spared no pains to detect and punish his deliverer. The plot of Felix was quickly discovered, and De Lacey and Agatha were thrown into prison. The news reached Felix and roused him from his dream of pleasure. His blind and aged father and his gentle sister lay in a noisome dungeon while he enjoyed the free air and the society of her whom he loved. This idea was torture to him. He quickly arranged with the Turk that if the latter should find a favourable opportunity for escape before Felix could return to Italy, Safie should remain as a boarder at a convent at Leghorn; and then, quitting the lovely Arabian, he hastened to Paris and delivered himself up to the vengeance of the law, hoping to free De Lacey and Agatha by this proceeding.

The ruling council of Veyra seethed with unbridled fury when their high-profile political prisoner slipped their grasp, and poured every resource at their disposal into hunting down the person who’d orchestrated his breakout. Elias’s role in the plot was uncovered within days, and his blind, elderly father Thibault and soft-spoken younger sister Elodie were seized and thrown into a squalid, vermin-ridden city jail. Word of the arrests found Elias while he was still drifting in the giddy haze of his new romance, and the shock ripped him violently out of his sunlit reverie. The thought that his infirm father and gentle sister were rotting in a reeking, lightless cell while he wandered free in the southern countryside, doted on by the woman he adored, felt like a hot knife twisted steadily between his ribs. He struck a hasty deal with Kadir, the freed Levantine prisoner: if the man found a safe chance to flee the region before Elias could return from his journey, Lira, Kadir’s daughter and Elias’s beloved, would take up temporary residence as a boarder in the coastal convent at San Luca until he could send for her. Leaving the dark-eyed young woman he loved behind, he raced straight to the Veyraussian capital, turning himself over to the courts without resistance, hoping his voluntary surrender would convince the authorities to release his father and sister from their captivity.

His machine-made storiettes, though he hated them and derided them, were successful. By means of them he redeemed all his pledges, paid most of his bills, and bought a new set of tires for his wheel. The storiettes at least kept the pot a-boiling and gave him time for ambitious work; while the one thing that upheld him was the forty dollars he had received from _The White Mouse_. He anchored his faith to that, and was confident that the really first-class magazines would pay an unknown writer at least an equal rate, if not a better one. But the thing was, how to get into the first-class magazines. His best stories, essays, and poems went begging among them, and yet, each month, he read reams of dull, prosy, inartistic stuff between all their various covers. If only one editor, he sometimes thought, would descend from his high seat of pride to write me one cheering line! No matter if my work is unusual, no matter if it is unfit, for prudential reasons, for their pages, surely there must be some sparks in it, somewhere, a few, to warm them to some sort of appreciation. And thereupon he would get out one or another of his manuscripts, such as “Adventure,” and read it over and over in a vain attempt to vindicate the editorial silence.

The formulaic, churned-out short tales he cranked out may have filled him with loathing and scorn, but they sold reliably enough. With the earnings, he made good on every outstanding promise, settled nearly all his debts, and picked up a fresh set of rubber tires for his scuffed secondhand bicycle. These throwaway stories at least kept food on the table and freed up hours for the serious, ambitious writing he cared about; the single bright spot holding his resolve together was the forty-dollar check he’d gotten from *The Copper Sparrow* a few weeks prior. He pinned all his hope to that small win, certain that top-tier literary journals would pay an untested writer at least that much per word, if not a far higher rate. The problem, of course, was breaking into those elite publications. His finest short fiction, personal essays, and verse circulated through their slush piles unbidden and unwanted, even as he pored through their issues each month and found page after page of flat, lifeless, unoriginal work filling their pages. If just one editor, he often mused, would climb down from their lofty, condescending perch to send him even a single line of encouragement! Even if his work was odd, even if it didn’t fit their editorial guidelines for practical, risk-averse reasons, there had to be a flicker of something worthwhile in it somewhere, enough to spark at least a glimmer of recognition from them. In those moments he’d yank one of his carefully drafted manuscripts off his shelf, *Wanderer’s Fall* among them, and reread it again and again, fruitlessly trying to work out why the editors had left him hanging without a word.

So he must have sat, and presently he lay on some rough bedding and suffered, untended, in infinite discomfort; lay motionless and thought at times, it may be, of Matching's Easy and wondered what Hugh and Teddy were doing. Then he became fevered, and the world grew bright-coloured and fantastic and ugly for him. Until one day an infinite weakness laid hold of him, and his pain grew faint and all his thoughts and memories grew faint--and still fainter....

Thus he would have remained seated, until at last he stretched out upon coarse coverlets and endured, alone, a vast unease; lay still and now and then, perhaps, recalled Matching’s Easy and mused what Hugh and Teddy might be about. Then a fever took him, and the world turned garish and grotesque and monstrous in his sight. Until a day came when a boundless frailty seized him, and his suffering dimmed, and all his recollections and fancies dimmed—and dimmer still….

Mr. Bumble was fairly taken by surprise, and fairly beaten. He had a decided propensity for bullying: derived no inconsiderable pleasure from the exercise of petty cruelty; and, consequently, was (it is needless to say) a coward. This is by no means a disparagement to his character; for many official personages, who are held in high respect and admiration, are the victims of similar infirmities. The remark is made, indeed, rather in his favour than otherwise, and with a view of impressing the reader with a just sense of his qualifications for office.

Mr. Bumble found himself utterly startled and thoroughly outmatched. He possessed a marked inclination for tyranny, taking no small delight in the practice of casual cruelty, and was therefore—needless to state—a craven at heart. This is far from a slight upon his nature, for many esteemed and admired public figures suffer from comparable frailties. The observation is offered, in truth, more to his credit than not, aiming to furnish the reader with a proper appreciation of his fitness for authority.

And here he was alive! (Hold close to him.) Only it was so good to be sure--after all her torment; to hold him, to hang about him, to feel the solid man, kissing her, weeping too, weeping together with her. "Teddy my love!"

Hadn’t she always known he was alive? And there he stood, alive indeed! (Keep close to him.) Yet it was so sweet to be certain at last—after all that agony—to clasp him, to cling about him, to feel the firm body of him, kissing her, and crying too, crying with her. “Tom, my darling!”

Such sordid things as stabbing affrays were evidently not fit subjects for conversation with a lady. People in the books, in her walk of life, did not talk about such things—perhaps they did not know about them, either.

A tremor of shock hit him, and a faint flush of shame colored his weather-beaten face, though in his own mind it burned with the intensity of an open furnace door in the engine room. Crude matters like knife fights were clearly unsuitable topics for a lady’s ears. The refined people in novels, those from her station in life, did not discuss such brutality—perhaps they were entirely unaware that such things even existed.

I doubt you must have been a solitary prisoner to understand these perplexed distinctions.”

“No. That was a different matter altogether. It rose before the troubled reach of my sight, yet it never stirred. The image my thoughts went after was another child, and one more substantial than the first. Of her outward look I know nothing beyond this—that she was like her mother. The other possessed that same resemblance as well, as you do, yet she was not the same. Do you follow me, Clara? Hardly, I think? I fear one must have lived alone in a prison to make sense of such tangled distinctions.”

That is why I decline to hear his pleas, whatever they may be. I did not condemn him through hearsay or doubtful evidence, and that is why I made no charge. But, since he persists in hearing what he already well knows, you may convey the matter.

“Inform the gentleman that there is no need for me to restate my principles once more. He is fully aware of what they were and what they remain today. In relation to this matter, unwavering loyalty and honesty are my highest priorities. Tell him that I have scrutinized my own soul as thoroughly as one can, and I understand its vulnerabilities just as clearly as its requirements. This is precisely why I refuse to listen to his petitions, no matter what they might be. I did not judge him based on rumors or questionable proof, which is why I never leveled a formal accusation. Yet, since he insists on hearing what he already understands perfectly well, you may relay the facts to him.”

And she slipped down from the horse and ran a little way and stood. I looked up and saw, far off in the shade of a tree, half a dozen armed knights and their squires; and straightway there was bustle among them and tightening of saddle-girths for the mount. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, if I had not been lost in thinking about how to banish oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without disobliging anybody. I lit up at once, and by the time I had got a good head of reserved steam on, here they came. All together, too; none of those chivalrous magnanimities which one reads so much about --one courtly rascal at a time, and the rest standing by to see fair play. No, they came in a body, they came with a whirr and a rush, they came like a volley from a battery; came with heads low down, plumes streaming out behind, lances advanced at a level. It was a handsome sight, a beautiful sight--for a man up a tree. I laid my lance in rest and waited, with my heart beating, till the iron wave was just ready to break over me, then spouted a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen the wave go to pieces and scatter! This was a finer sight than the other one.

And she slid from the horse, ran a short distance, and stopped. Looking up, I saw, in the shade of a tree far off, some half dozen armed knights with their squires; and at once there was commotion among them and a tightening of saddle-girths for the charge. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, had I not been absorbed in plans for how to sweep oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without offending anyone. I lit it at once, and by the time I had built up a proper reserve of steam, there they came. All at once, too; none of those chivalrous courtesies one reads of so often--one polished villain at a time, while the rest stand aside to watch fair play. No, they came together, they came with a whir and a rush, they came like a battery’s volley; came with heads bent low, plumes streaming behind, lances leveled straight ahead. It was a fine sight, a splendid sight—for a man in a tree. I set my lance in rest and waited, my heart pounding, until the iron wave was just about to break over me, then blew a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen that wave break apart and scatter! That was a finer sight than the first.

And indeed, as for those low ruffians yonder, do you think they have not had enough already, but still crave more?”

He swore that he would not bet, and he kept his word; for when he left the town at nine that night, his money was reduced by only a few shillings.

Dictating translations dates back to the time when secretaries transcribed dictaphone content on a typewriter (Theologitis, 1998) ; however, the use of automatic speech recognition also has a long history for translation (Dymetman et al., 1994; Brousseau et al., 1995) . A more recent approach, called SEECAT (Martinez et al., 2014) , investigates the use of automatic speech recognition (ASR) in PE and argues that its combination with typing could boost productivity. A survey regarding speech usage with PE trainees (Mesa-Lao, 2014) finds that they have a positive attitude towards speech input and would consider adopting it, but only as a complement to other modalities. In a small-scale study, Zapata et al. (2017) found that ASR for PE was faster than ASR for translation from scratch. Due to these benefits, commercial CAT tools like memoQ and MateCat are also beginning to integrate ASR.

metadata.title: MMPE: A Multi-Modal Interface for Post-Editing Machine Translation

text: The practice of dictating translations can be traced back to the era when secretaries typed up dictaphone recordings (Theologitis, 1998); nevertheless, the application of automatic speech recognition to translation has an even longer research history (Dymetman et al., 1994; Brousseau et al., 1995). More recently, the SEECAT approach (Martinez et al., 2014) examines the use of automatic speech recognition (ASR) in PE and contends that combining speech input with typing may improve productivity. A survey of speech use among PE trainees (Mesa-Lao, 2014) reports that they hold generally favorable views of speech input and would be open to adopting it, though only as a supplement to other input modalities. In a small-scale experiment, Zapata et al. (2017) showed that ASR for PE was faster than ASR for translation from scratch. Owing to these advantages, commercial CAT systems such as memoQ and MateCat are likewise beginning to incorporate ASR.

In her desire to make atonement she took flowers from a vase by the window, and began laying them around the dead girl’s head. Bathsheba knew no other way of showing kindness to persons departed than by giving them flowers. She knew not how long she remained engaged thus. She forgot time, life, where she was, what she was doing. A slamming together of the coach-house doors in the yard brought her to herself again. An instant after, the front door opened and closed, steps crossed the hall, and her husband appeared at the entrance to the room, looking in upon her.

Driven by a need for penance, she gathered blossoms from a vase near the window and started arranging them around the head of the deceased girl. Bathsheba could conceive of no other gesture of compassion for the dead save offering them flowers. She had no sense of how long she lingered at this task. Time, existence, her surroundings, her own actions—all slipped from her awareness. The sharp clap of the coach-house doors banging shut in the yard jolted her back to herself. A moment later, the front door swung open and then closed, footsteps crossed the hall, and her husband stood in the doorway, gazing in at her.

He occupied rooms in the Bank, in his fidelity to the House of which he had grown to be a part, like strong root-ivy. It chanced that they derived a kind of security from the patriotic occupation of the main building, but the true-hearted old gentleman never calculated about that. All such circumstances were indifferent to him, so that he did his duty. On the opposite side of the courtyard, under a colonnade, was extensive standing--for carriages--where, indeed, some carriages of Monseigneur yet stood. Against two of the pillars were fastened two great flaring flambeaux, and in the light of these, standing out in the open air, was a large grindstone: a roughly mounted thing which appeared to have hurriedly been brought there from some neighbouring smithy, or other workshop. Rising and looking out of window at these harmless objects, Mr. Lorry shivered, and retired to his seat by the fire. He had opened, not only the glass window, but the lattice blind outside it, and he had closed both again, and he shivered through his frame.

He lived in rooms at the Bank, clinging to the House that had absorbed him as ivy clings to a wall with deep, tenacious roots. It so happened that his presence in the main building offered a certain patriotic reassurance, but the honest old man never paused to reckon such things. So long as he did his duty, all else was immaterial to him. Across the courtyard, beneath a colonnade, stretched a broad standing for carriages—where, in fact, a few of Monseigneur’s carriages still remained. Two pillars held a pair of blazing flambeaux, and in their glare, exposed to the night air, stood a large grindstone: a rough, hastily mounted thing that looked as though it had been dragged there from some nearby smithy or workshop. Mr. Lorry rose, gazed out at these unremarkable objects, shivered, and withdrew to his seat beside the fire. He had opened not only the glazed window but the lattice blind beyond it, and after closing both again, a tremor ran through his whole frame.

We based our data collection efforts on a rich and publicly available database of language puzzles maintained by the organizers of NACLO. 2 This resource contains puzzles from IOL and a wide range of local competitions 3 . We only included puzzles written in English (or translated to English) to ensure a quality transcription and to enable error analysis. Expert solutions are available for most puzzles; we excluded the rest. In addition to the translation puzzle type shown in Table 1 , we also collected 'matching' puzzles. These are two-step puzzles, in which the participants first align a shuffled set of sentences to obtain parallel data, and then translate a set of unseen sentences. We converted these puzzles to the translation puzzle format by referring to the solution files to align the training sentence pairs. Appendix A.1 describes how we selected the puzzles and how we transcribed them into a machine-readable format.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: Our data collection builds upon the extensive, publicly accessible puzzle repository curated by the NACLO organizers. This repository encompasses problems from the International Linguistics Olympiad and numerous regional contests. We restricted our selection to puzzles authored in or translated into English, thereby guaranteeing high-quality transcription and facilitating systematic error analysis. For the majority of puzzles, expert solutions exist; we discarded those lacking such solutions. Beyond the translation puzzle category illustrated in Table 1, we also gathered ‘matching’ puzzles. These involve a two-stage process where participants first align a scrambled sentence set to derive parallel data, then translate a held-out sentence collection. We transformed these into the translation puzzle format by leveraging the solution files to align the training sentence pairs. Appendix A.1 details our puzzle selection criteria and the procedure for converting them into a machine-readable representation.

She passed with her candle into an inner room, where, among other utensils, were two large brown pans, containing together perhaps a hundredweight of liquid honey, the produce of the bees during the foregoing summer. On a shelf over the pans was a smooth and solid yellow mass of a hemispherical form, consisting of beeswax from the same take of honey. Susan took down the lump, and cutting off several thin slices, heaped them in an iron ladle, with which she returned to the living-room, and placed the vessel in the hot ashes of the fireplace. As soon as the wax had softened to the plasticity of dough she kneaded the pieces together. And now her face became more intent. She began moulding the wax; and it was evident from her manner of manipulation that she was endeavouring to give it some preconceived form. The form was human.

Candle in hand, she stepped into a back room where, amid other household items, stood two broad brown basins holding close to a hundred pounds of liquid honey, the bees’ yield from the summer just past. On a shelf above them rested a firm, yellow hemisphere of beeswax drawn from that same harvest. Susan lifted the block down, sliced off several thin pieces, and piled them into an iron ladle. Carrying it back to the living-room, she set the ladle among the hot embers of the hearth. Once the wax softened to the pliancy of dough, she worked the fragments together. Her expression now sharpened with concentration. She began to shape the wax, and the way her hands moved made it clear she was striving to model a form she already held in mind. The form was a human one.

He realized how much he slept, and how much he desired to sleep. Of old, he had hated sleep. It had robbed him of precious moments of living. Four hours of sleep in the twenty-four had meant being robbed of four hours of life. How he had grudged sleep! Now it was life he grudged. Life was not good; its taste in his mouth was without tang, and bitter. This was his peril. Life that did not yearn toward life was in fair way toward ceasing. Some remote instinct for preservation stirred in him, and he knew he must get away. He glanced about the room, and the thought of packing was burdensome. Perhaps it would be better to leave that to the last. In the meantime he might be getting an outfit.

It hit him just how much time he spent asleep these days, and how badly he craved even more of it. Once, he’d loathed sleep, seen it as a thief snatching precious hours he could have spent fully alive. Four hours of rest out of every twenty-four had felt like four full hours of his life ripped away; he’d begrudged every single second he spent unconscious. Now, though, it was waking life he resented. Nothing about being alive felt good anymore; the very taste of existence on his tongue was flat, no spark to it at all, just a sharp, lingering bitterness. That was the danger he was in. When a life had no drive to keep going, it was already halfway to fading out entirely. Some faint, distant survival instinct tugged at the back of his mind, and he knew he had to get out of there. He scanned the room around him, and the thought of packing up all his things felt so heavy and draining he could barely stomach it. Maybe it made more sense to leave that task for the very end. In the meanwhile, he could go pick up the travel outfit and supplies he’d need first.

“Oh, I don’t know, pray let me go!” she said, her bosom beginning to rise. “I am afraid what to do! I want to be just to you, and to be that seems to be wronging myself, and perhaps it is breaking the commandments. There is considerable doubt of his death, and then it is dreadful; let me ask a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, if I ought or no!”

“Oh, I cannot say—please, let me go!” she cried, her breath quickening as her chest began to heave. “I am terrified of what I should do! I truly wish to be fair to you, yet to do so feels like a betrayal of myself, and perhaps even a sin. There is such deep uncertainty regarding his death, and the thought is horrifying; let me consult a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, to see if I am truly free to act!”

Two other passengers, besides the one, were plodding up the hill by the side of the mail. All three were wrapped to the cheekbones and over the ears, and wore jack-boots. Not one of the three could have said, from anything he saw, what either of the other two was like; and each was hidden under almost as many wrappers from the eyes of the mind, as from the eyes of the body, of his two companions. In those days, travellers were very shy of being confidential on a short notice, for anybody on the road might be a robber or in league with robbers. As to the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could produce somebody in “the Captain’s” pay, ranging from the landlord to the lowest stable non-descript, it was the likeliest thing upon the cards. So the guard of the Dover mail thought to himself, that Friday night in November, one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five, lumbering up Shooter’s Hill, as he stood on his own particular perch behind the mail, beating his feet, and keeping an eye and a hand on the arm-chest before him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay at the top of six or eight loaded horse-pistols, deposited on a substratum of cutlass.

Beside the lone one, two more passengers were trudging up the hill alongside the mail-coach. All three were muffled to the cheekbones and over their ears, and all wore jack-boots. None of the three could have told, from anything visible, what either of the other two looked like; each, to the eyes of the mind as much as to the eyes of the body, was hidden from his companions by nearly as many wrappings. In those days, travellers were very cautious about becoming confidential at short notice, since anyone on the road might prove to be a robber, or in collusion with robbers. And as for the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could furnish someone in “the Captain’s” pay, from the landlord down to the most insignificant stable-hand, that seemed the likeliest possibility of all. So the guard on the Dover mail reflected, on that Friday night in November of 1775, as he laboured up Shooter’s Hill, standing on his own special perch behind the coach, stamping his feet and keeping both eye and hand on the arm-chest in front of him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay atop six or eight loaded horse-pistols, all resting on a base of cutlass.

In order to obtain competent models that can provide automatic counter-narrative hints and suggestions to NGO operators, we have to overcome the data bottleneck/limitations, i.e. either the limited amount of training data in NICHE or its repetitiveness in CROWD, especially for using neural NLP approaches. Since pre-trained Language Models (LMs) have achieved promising results when fine-tuned on challenging generation tasks such as chit-chat dialog Golovanov et al., 2019) , we propose using a recent large-scale language model GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) .

metadata.title: Generating Counter Narratives against Online Hate Speech: Data and Strategies

text: To develop effective models capable of supplying NGO operators with automated counter-narrative hints and suggestions, we must address the data bottleneck: the restricted quantity of training data in NICHE and the repetitiveness of data in CROWD, limitations that are particularly acute when employing neural NLP methods. Given that pre-trained language models have yielded strong results when fine-tuned on demanding generation tasks such as chit-chat dialogue (Golovanov et al., 2019), we propose leveraging the recent large-scale language model GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019).

“I was brought here, by the most dreadful and agonising of all apprehensions,” said the young man; “the fear of losing the one dear being on whom my every wish and hope are fixed. You had been dying; trembling between earth and heaven. We know that when the young, the beautiful, and good, are visited with sickness, their pure spirits insensibly turn towards their bright home of lasting rest; we know, Heaven help us! that the best and fairest of our kind, too often fade in blooming.”

“I only ended up here because I was seized by the most agonizing, gut-deep terror I’ve ever known,” the young man said. “I was terrified I’d lose the only beloved person every single one of my wishes and hopes is anchored to. You’d been at death’s door, trembling between this world and whatever comes after. We all know that when the young, beautiful, and kind fall ill, their pure, unspoiled spirits slowly turn toward that bright, permanent home of eternal rest. We know, heaven help us, that the best and loveliest among us far too often fade away when they’re still in the full bloom of life.”

They talked on; she, in her gently persistent way, returning always to the need of thorough grounding in education and to the advantages of Latin as part of the foundation for any career. She drew her ideal of the successful man, and it was largely in her father’s image, with a few unmistakable lines and touches of color from the image of Mr. Butler. He listened eagerly, with receptive ears, lying on his back and looking up and joying in each movement of her lips as she talked. But his brain was not receptive. There was nothing alluring in the pictures she drew, and he was aware of a dull pain of disappointment and of a sharper ache of love for her. In all she said there was no mention of his writing, and the manuscripts he had brought to read lay neglected on the ground.

The conversation continued; she, with her soft but steady determination, kept returning to the necessity of a rigorous academic foundation and the value of Latin as a cornerstone for any profession. She described her vision of a truly accomplished man, a figure modeled largely after her father, though colored by certain unmistakable traits belonging to Mr. Butler. He lay on his back and listened with rapt attention, gazing upward and finding a quiet joy in the very movement of her lips as she spoke. Yet, his mind remained unpersuaded. There was nothing enticing in the life she envisioned, and he felt a heavy sense of disappointment coupled with a sharp, piercing love for her. Throughout her talk, she made no mention of his own work, and the manuscripts he had hoped to read to her sat forgotten on the grass.

In the next morning’s paper Martin learned a great deal more about himself that was new to him. “We are the sworn enemies of society,” he found himself quoted as saying in a column interview. “No, we are not anarchists but socialists.” When the reporter pointed out to him that there seemed little difference between the two schools, Martin had shrugged his shoulders in silent affirmation. His face was described as bilaterally asymmetrical, and various other signs of degeneration were described. Especially notable were his thuglike hands and the fiery gleams in his blood-shot eyes.

By the next morning’s edition, Julian was introduced to a version of himself he had never met. “We are the avowed adversaries of the commonweal,” the column quoted him as asserting during a brief interrogation. “Make no mistake: we are not insurrectionists, but communalists.” When the correspondent suggested that the two ideologies were essentially the same, Julian was depicted as having offered a mute, shrugging concession. The text went on to map the supposed irregularities of his skull, citing them as evidence of a primitive character. His fingers were described as those of a common ruffian, and much was made of the predatory, blood-rimmed glare that supposedly defined his expression.

And yet his crime was committed more in thoughtlessness than deliberate depravity. He had said she had red hair. Well, she had; but that was no way to speak of it. When red-headed people are above a certain social grade their hair is auburn.

Wildeve stood looking on the ground beside Venn’s feet, till he allowed his eyes to travel upwards over Diggory’s form, as illuminated by the candle, to his head and face. “Ha-ha! Well, I suppose I deserve it, considering how I have played with them both,” he said at last, as much to himself as to Venn. “But of all the odd things that ever I knew, the oddest is that you should so run counter to your own interests as to bring this to me.”

Wildeve kept his eyes fixed on the earth near Venn’s boots until he let his gaze climb slowly up Diggory’s figure, bathed in the candle’s glow, to rest upon his features. “Ha! Well, I suppose this is my due, given how I’ve toyed with both women,” he remarked eventually, half to himself and half to Venn. “But of all the peculiar turns I’ve witnessed, the most bizarre is that you would work so directly against your own advantage to bring this to me.”

He had laid aside his coat and waistcoat; his shirt was open at the throat, as it used to be when he did that work; and even the old haggard, faded surface of face had come back to him. He worked hard--impatiently--as if in some sense of having been interrupted.

He’d shed his coat and waistcoat, his shirt gaping open at the throat just as it always was when he did this particular work; even that old haggard, faded cast to his features had settled back across his face. He labored fiercely, sharp with impatience, as if he carried the quiet sense that he’d been pulled away from the task before he could finish it.

He was a fairly well-educated man for one of middle class—exceptionally well educated for a common soldier. He spoke fluently and unceasingly. He could in this way be one thing and seem another: for instance, he could speak of love and think of dinner; call on the husband to look at the wife; be eager to pay and intend to owe.

For a man of middle-class background, he was reasonably well educated—exceptionally learned, even, for a common rank-and-file soldier. He spoke smoothly, fluently, and almost without pause. This gift of gab let him embody one thing while appearing to be another: he could gush about the tender thrills of love while his thoughts drifted to his next meal, drop by a husband’s home for a supposed visit when his real target was the man’s wife, or act impatient to settle a tab when he fully intended to leave the debt unpaid.

“Supposing they were ashamed, I don’t see why they shouldn’t have done it here-right,” said a wide-spread woman whose stays creaked like shoes whenever she stooped or turned. “’Tis well to call the neighbours together and to hae a good racket once now and then; and it may as well be when there’s a wedding as at tide-times. I don’t care for close ways.”

“Even if they were feeling ashamed, I still don’t see why they couldn’t have held the whole thing right here,” said the broad, ample-framed woman, whose stiff stays creaked like well-worn shoes every time she stooped or turned. “It’s a fine thing to gather all the neighbors together and raise a good, lively ruckus now and again; a wedding’s just as fitting an occasion for it as any seasonal holiday, if you ask me. I’ve never had any patience for stuffy, close-mouthed ways of doing things.”

The piece was the well-known play of Saint George, and all who were behind the scenes assisted in the preparations, including the women of each household. Without the co-operation of sisters and sweethearts the dresses were likely to be a failure; but on the other hand, this class of assistance was not without its drawbacks. The girls could never be brought to respect tradition in designing and decorating the armour; they insisted on attaching loops and bows of silk and velvet in any situation pleasing to their taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve, all alike in the view of these feminine eyes were practicable spaces whereon to sew scraps of fluttering colour.

The production in question was the beloved, widely known staging of Saint George, and everyone working backstage pitched in with the preparations, including the women from every local household. If sisters and sweethearts hadn’t lent their support, the costumes would almost certainly have turned out poorly; that said, this particular brand of assistance was not without its downsides. No one could ever get these young women to honor longstanding tradition when designing and decorating the armor; they insisted on sewing silk and velvet loops and bows anywhere that suited their personal taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve—every last one of these components, in these women’s eyes, was a perfectly usable surface to stitch on scraps of bright, fluttering color.

Let me stay here, and be a servant. Don’t send me back to the wretched place I came from. Have mercy upon a poor boy, sir!”

“There, you mistake,” he had rushed on. “All persons in society, all cliques in society—or, rather, nearly all persons and cliques—ape their betters. Now, who are the best betters? The idlers, the wealthy idlers. They do not know, as a rule, the things known by the persons who are doing something in the world. To listen to conversation about such things would mean to be bored, wherefore the idlers decree that such things are shop and must not be talked about. Likewise they decree the things that are not shop and which may be talked about, and those things are the latest operas, latest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, automobiles, horse shows, trout fishing, tuna-fishing, big-game shooting, yacht sailing, and so forth—and mark you, these are the things the idlers know. In all truth, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the funniest part of it is that many of the clever people, and all the would-be clever people, allow the idlers so to impose upon them. As for me, I want the best a man’s got in him, call it shop vulgarity or anything you please.”

“There you err,” he had plunged ahead. “Every circle in society, every coterie—or, rather, nearly every person and set—mimics its superiors. And who, pray, are the foremost superiors? The leisured, the moneyed idlers. They remain ignorant, by and large, of the matters familiar to those who truly accomplish something in the world. To endure talk of such matters would be tedium, so the idlers pronounce those subjects shop and forbid their discussion. Equally they ordain what is not shop and may be spoken of, and those topics are the freshest operas, freshest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, motorcars, horse shows, trout angling, tuna angling, big-game stalking, yacht cruising, and the like—and observe, these are the very things the idlers know. In sober fact, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the most amusing stroke is that many clever people, and all the would-be clever people, permit the idlers to foist this upon them. For my part, I demand the best a man possesses, call it shop coarseness or what you will.”

“Of course it couldn’t,” replied Sikes; “I know’d that, directly I see him coming through Clerkenwell, with the books under his arm. It’s all right enough. They’re soft-hearted psalm-singers, or they wouldn’t have taken him in at all; and they’ll ask no questions after him, fear they should be obliged to prosecute, and so get him lagged. He’s safe enough.”

“Naturally not,” Sikes answered. “I figured that the moment I spotted him walking through Clerkenwell, those books tucked under his arm. It’s perfectly fine. They’re tender-minded hymn-chanters, else they’d never have sheltered him in the first place; and they won’t go prying into his affairs, afraid they’d have to press charges and get him transported. He’s quite secure.”

The restaurant was not so showy or pretentious as the one further down Broadway, which he always preferred, but it was nearly so. The tables were well filled with prosperous-looking diners, there was a good orchestra, playing softly enough to make conversation a possible pleasure, and the cuisine and service were beyond criticism. His companion, even in her cheap hat and dress, held herself with an air that added distinction to the natural beauty of her face and figure. And it is certain that she looked at Chandler, with his animated but self-possessed manner and his kindling and frank blue eyes, with something not far from admiration in her own charming face.

The bistro wasn’t quite as ostentatious or overdone as the spot three blocks further down Michigan Avenue that Elias usually favored, but it came remarkably close. Every table was occupied by well-heeled patrons, a small chamber ensemble played low enough that easy, unstrained conversation never felt like a chore, and both the menu and the waitstaff’s attentiveness were unimpeachable. His dining companion, even in her thrifted wool hat and simple, unadorned cotton dress, carried herself with a quiet grace that lent an extra edge of elegance to her already striking features and willowy frame. And there was no mistaking the warm, near-admiring glint in her own soft, expressive face as she watched Elias, his manner lively but never unsteady, his bright, honest blue eyes sparkling as he spoke.

The mouth seemed formed less to speak than to quiver, less to quiver than to kiss. Some might have added, less to kiss than to curl. Viewed sideways, the closing-line of her lips formed, with almost geometric precision, the curve so well known in the arts of design as the cima-recta, or ogee. The sight of such a flexible bend as that on grim Egdon was quite an apparition. It was felt at once that the mouth did not come over from Sleswig with a band of Saxon pirates whose lips met like the two halves of a muffin. One had fancied that such lip-curves were mostly lurking underground in the South as fragments of forgotten marbles. So fine were the lines of her lips that, though full, each corner of her mouth was as clearly cut as the point of a spear. This keenness of corner was only blunted when she was given over to sudden fits of gloom, one of the phases of the night-side of sentiment which she knew too well for her years.

That mouth looked shaped not so much for speaking as for trembling, not so much for trembling as for kissing. Some would have added that it was made less for kissing than to curl softly at the corners. Seen in profile, the seam where her lips pressed together traced, with nearly mathematical exactness, the curve familiar to decorative artists as the cima-recta, or ogee. To catch sight of such a soft, supple curve on harsh, unforgiving Egdon Heath was little short of a ghostly apparition. You knew immediately that this mouth had not crossed the sea from Sleswig alongside a band of Saxon raiders, whose lips fit together as plain and unshapely as the two halves of a cut muffin. Most would have assumed such delicate lip curves only existed half-buried in southern soil, broken fragments of long-forgotten marble statues. The lines of her lips were so exquisitely shaped that, even at their fullest, each corner of her mouth was chiseled as sharply as the tip of a spear. That sharp definition at the corners softened only when she was seized by sudden bouts of melancholy, one facet of the darker, more brooding side of feeling that she knew far too intimately for someone so young.

text: They fished with a spade, at first. Presently the honoured parent appeared to be adjusting some instrument like a great corkscrew. Whatever tools they worked with, they worked hard, until the awful striking of the church clock so terrified Young Jerry, that he made off, with his hair as stiff as his father’s.

They began by fishing with a spade. After a while, the respected father seemed to be setting some contrivance in order, a thing like a monstrous corkscrew. Whatever tools they used, they used them laboriously, and kept at it with all their might, until the dreadful clang of the church clock so alarmed Little Ned that he fled away, his hair standing as bristly as his father’s.

This paper presents EVIDENCEMINER, a webbased system for textual evidence discovery for life sciences (Figure 1 ). Given a query as a natural language statement, EVIDENCEMINER automatically retrieves sentence-level textual evidence from a background corpora of biomedical literature. EVIDENCEMINER is constructed in a completely automated way without any human effort for training data annotation. It is supported by novel data-driven methods for distantly supervised named entity recognition and open information extraction. EVIDENCEMINER relies on external knowledge bases to provide distant supervision for named entity recognition (NER) (Shang et al., 2018b; Wang et al., , 2019 . Based on the entity annotation results, it automatically extracts informative meta-patterns (textual patterns containing entity types, e.g., CHEMICAL inhibit DIS-EASE) from sentences in the background corpora. Wang et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2018a,b) . Sentences with meta-patterns that better match the query statement is more likely to be textual evidence. The entities and patterns are precomputed and indexed offline to support fast online evidence retrieval. The annotation results are also highlighted in the original document for better visualization. EVIDENCEMINER also includes analytic functionalities such as the most frequent entity and relation summarization. The contributions and features of the EVIDENCEMINER system are summarized as follows.

metadata.title: EVIDENCEMINER: Textual Evidence Discovery for Life Sciences

text: We introduce EVIDENCEMINER, a web-based platform for automatically discovering textual evidence in the life sciences (Figure 1). When supplied with a natural language statement as a query, the system retrieves sentence-level evidence from a background collection of biomedical publications. EVIDENCEMINER is built in a fully automated fashion, requiring no manual annotation of training data. It leverages novel, data-driven techniques for distantly supervised named entity recognition and open information extraction. For named entity recognition (NER), the system draws on external knowledge bases to provide distant supervision (Shang et al., 2018b; Wang et al., 2019). Using the resulting entity annotations, it automatically derives informative meta-patterns—textual templates that incorporate entity types, such as CHEMICAL inhibit DISEASE—from sentences in the background corpora (Wang et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2018a,b). Sentences whose meta-patterns align more closely with the query are prioritized as potential evidence. Entities and patterns are precomputed and indexed offline, enabling rapid online retrieval. Annotated results are also highlighted within the source document to enhance visual inspection. EVIDENCEMINER further offers analytical capabilities, including summaries of the most frequent entities and relations. We summarize the system’s contributions and features below.

I attempted to accompany them and proceeded a short distance from the house, but my head whirled round, my steps were like those of a drunken man, I fell at last in a state of utter exhaustion; a film covered my eyes, and my skin was parched with the heat of fever. In this state I was carried back and placed on a bed, hardly conscious of what had happened; my eyes wandered round the room as if to seek something that I had lost.

I tried to follow after them, moving no more than a few paces beyond the door, but the world began to spin, my legs stumbled as though I were drunk, and I collapsed at length in complete fatigue. A mist dimmed my sight, and my flesh burned dry with fever’s heat. They bore me inside and laid me upon a bed, while I barely grasped what had occurred; my gaze drifted across the chamber, searching for something I had misplaced.

I was lost. There was no help for me. I was dazed, stupefied; I had no command over myself, I only wandered purposely about, like one out of his mind; so the soldiers took hold of me, and pulled me along with them, out of the cell and along the maze of underground corridors, and finally into the fierce glare of daylight and the upper world. As we stepped into the vast enclosed court of the castle I got a shock; for the first thing I saw was the stake, standing in the center, and near it the piled fagots and a monk. On all four sides of the court the seated multitudes rose rank above rank, forming sloping terraces that were rich with color. The king and the queen sat in their thrones, the most conspicuous figures there, of course.

I was utterly disoriented. There was no rescue coming for me. My head throbbed, my senses felt thick and unmoored; I had no mastery over my own body, only stumbled aimlessly back and forth as if robbed of my reason, until the guards seized me by the shoulders and dragged me along with them, out of the cold, narrow cell, through the winding labyrinth of underground corridors, and finally into the brutal, blinding glare of daylight and the world above. The second we stepped into the fortress’s vast walled courtyard, a jolt of terror shot through me; the first thing I laid eyes on was the wooden post driven into the earth at its center, piled stacks of dry kindling heaped beside it, and a robed friar standing a few paces off. On all four sides of the courtyard, seated crowds rose in tier after tier, forming sloping terraces stippled with the rich, bright colors of their garments. The sovereign and his consort sat on their carved thrones, naturally the most striking figures in the entire assembly.

“Yes, without a drop o’ rain, thank God. But I was coming to tell ’ee of something else which is quite different from what we have lately had in the family. I am sent by the rich gentleman at the Woman, that we used to call the landlord, to tell ’ee that Mrs. Wildeve is doing well of a girl, which was born punctually at one o’clock at noon, or a few minutes more or less; and ’tis said that expecting of this increase is what have kept ’em there since they came into their money.”

“Aye, the dry spell holds, and a mercy it is. But I’ve come to bring word of a matter quite removed from our recent family trials. I was dispatched by the monied master of the Golden Lion—him we used to call the publican—to inform you that Mrs. Vane is doing bravely with a new daughter, born just as the clock struck one this afternoon, or thereabouts; and the talk is that waiting for this little soul is what has anchored them to the village ever since they came into their great fortune.”

Letty's face changed in those pitiful weeks when she was denying death. She lost her pretty colour, she became white; her mouth grew hard and her eyes had a hard brightness. She never wept, she never gave a sign of sorrow, and she insisted upon talking about Teddy, in a dry offhand voice. Constantly she referred to his final return. "Teddy," she said, "will be surprised at this," or "Teddy will feel sold when he sees how I have altered that."

In those miserable weeks when she refused to accept death, Letty’s face altered. Her pretty colour faded; she turned pale, and her mouth set into hardness, while her eyes took on a fixed, hard shine. She never cried, never showed any sign of grief, and she kept talking about Teddy in a dry, casual tone. All the time she spoke of his eventual return. “Teddy,” she would say, “will be surprised at this,” or, “Teddy will feel cheated when he sees how I’ve changed that.”

“Wait! Now, you see, the thing is very simple; this time you'll understand it. For instance, it takes your woman 42 days to earn her gown, at 2 mills a day--7 weeks' work; but ours earns hers in forty days--two days _short_ of 7 weeks. Your woman has a gown, and her whole seven weeks wages are gone; ours has a gown, and two days' wages left, to buy something else with. There--_now_ you understand it!”

“Hold on! There, you see, it’s perfectly plain; this time you’ll grasp it. Say your wife needs 42 days to pay for her dress, at 2 mills per day—seven full weeks of labor; but mine earns hers in forty days—two days shy of seven weeks. Your wife has a dress, and her entire seven weeks’ pay is spent; mine has a dress, and two days’ wages remaining, to put toward something else. There—now you’ve got it!”

Never was ingenuity exercised so sorely as the traveller here exercised hers. Every conceivable aid, method, stratagem, mechanism, by which these last desperate eight hundred yards could be overpassed by a human being unperceived, was revolved in her busy brain, and dismissed as impracticable. She thought of sticks, wheels, crawling—she even thought of rolling. But the exertion demanded by either of these latter two was greater than to walk erect. The faculty of contrivance was worn out. Hopelessness had come at last.

No wanderer had ever strained their cleverness as thin as Elowen did that day. Every plausible trick, tool, ruse, or makeshift she could imagine for slipping across those final, desperate eight hundred yards without being spotted cycled through her racing mind, only to be discarded as unworkable. She considered propping herself on scavenged branches, rigging a low rolling frame, creeping on hands and knees—she even entertained the thought of tucking into a tight ball and rolling the whole stretch. But both crawling and rolling would demand far more physical exertion than simply striding upright, conspicuous as that would be. Every last spark of resourcefulness had burned down to ash. Hopelessness settled over her at last, cold and unyielding.

“Maria,” Martin announced that night, “I’m going to leave you. And you’re going to leave here yourself soon. Then you can rent the house and be a landlord yourself. You’ve a brother in San Leandro or Haywards, and he’s in the milk business. I want you to send all your washing back unwashed—understand?—unwashed, and to go out to San Leandro to-morrow, or Haywards, or wherever it is, and see that brother of yours. Tell him to come to see me. I’ll be stopping at the Metropole down in Oakland. He’ll know a good milk-ranch when he sees one.”

“Maria,” Martin declared that night, “I am moving on. And you will be leaving this place yourself before long. You’ll be the one renting the house out, a landlady in your own right. You have a brother in the dairy trade over in San Leandro or Haywards. I want you to return all this laundry just as it is—unwashed, do you understand?—and go see him tomorrow. Tell him to come find me at the Metropole in Oakland. He’ll be able to recognize a quality milk ranch when he sees one.”

London!—that great place!—nobody—not even Mr. Bumble—could ever find him there! He had often heard the old men in the workhouse, too, say that no lad of spirit need want in London; and that there were ways of living in that vast city, which those who had been bred up in country parts had no idea of. It was the very place for a homeless boy, who must die in the streets unless some one helped him. As these things passed through his thoughts, he jumped upon his feet, and again walked forward.

London! That sprawling, endless city! No one could ever track him down there, not even Mr. Bumble himself. He had often heard the elderly men in the workhouse say, too, that no plucky lad need ever go hungry in London, that the vast metropolis held all sorts of ways to get by that people raised in the countryside could never even fathom. It was exactly the place for a homeless boy, one who would surely die out on the roads if no one stopped to help him. As these thoughts ran through his mind, he leaped to his feet and kept moving forward.

He caught a Telegraph Avenue car that was going to Berkeley. It was crowded with youths and young men who were singing songs and ever and again barking out college yells. He studied them curiously. They were university boys. They went to the same university that she did, were in her class socially, could know her, could see her every day if they wanted to. He wondered that they did not want to, that they had been out having a good time instead of being with her that evening, talking with her, sitting around her in a worshipful and adoring circle. His thoughts wandered on. He noticed one with narrow-slitted eyes and a loose-lipped mouth. That fellow was vicious, he decided. On shipboard he would be a sneak, a whiner, a tattler. He, Martin Eden, was a better man than that fellow. The thought cheered him. It seemed to draw him nearer to Her. He began comparing himself with the students. He grew conscious of the muscled mechanism of his body and felt confident that he was physically their master. But their heads were filled with knowledge that enabled them to talk her talk,—the thought depressed him. But what was a brain for? he demanded passionately. What they had done, he could do. They had been studying about life from the books while he had been busy living life. His brain was just as full of knowledge as theirs, though it was a different kind of knowledge. How many of them could tie a lanyard knot, or take a wheel or a lookout? His life spread out before him in a series of pictures of danger and daring, hardship and toil. He remembered his failures and scrapes in the process of learning. He was that much to the good, anyway. Later on they would have to begin living life and going through the mill as he had gone. Very well. While they were busy with that, he could be learning the other side of life from the books.

He boarded a Shattuck Avenue tram bound for the Berkeley hills. The car brimmed with young men and boys, all of them chanting college anthems and now and then loosing sharp fraternity cries. He watched them with a detached, searching gaze. They were students of the university—her university. They moved in her social sphere, shared her classrooms, might speak with her, might glimpse her any ordinary day if the whim took them. It puzzled him that the whim did not take them, that they had chosen an evening’s carousal instead of her company, her conversation, the chance to gather round her in reverent, devoted attention. His mind drifted. He marked one with thin-slitted eyes and a mouth too slack at the corners. That one, he judged, was mean-hearted. On a ship he would prove a skulker, a complainer, a tale-bearer. He, Martin Eden, stood above that breed. The thought braced him. It seemed to lessen the distance between himself and Her. He fell to measuring his own worth against the students. He became aware of his body’s corded machinery and felt a sure pride that he could master any of them in physical things. Yet their heads held the learning that let them speak her language—the reflection weighed him down. But what, after all, was a brain meant for? he asked with sudden heat. Whatever they had done, he could do. They had been pulling life from printed pages while he had been wrestling life itself. His mind stored just as much knowledge as theirs, only of another grain. How many of them could whip a lanyard knot, or stand a wheel, or keep a lookout watch? His past unrolled before him like a moving panorama of risk and mettle, privation and labor. He recalled his stumbles and narrow escapes while he learned. That much, at least, he had gained. In time they would have to start living life and grinding through the mill as he had ground. So be it. While they were taken up with that, he could be mastering the other side of life from books.

“Well,” he confessed, “I was a bit shivery at first; but I got used to it. I used to feel sorry for that poor girl, though. That made me forget to be afraid. She was such a beauty, in spirit as well as in appearance, and she was only slightly touched; yet she was doomed to lie there, living the life of a primitive savage and rotting slowly away. Leprosy is far more terrible than you can imagine it.”

“Well,” he admitted, “I felt a chill of fear at first, but I grew accustomed to it. Still, my heart ached for that poor girl, and pity drove out the dread. She was lovely in soul as much as in face, and her mind was only lightly clouded; yet she was condemned to languish there, living like some primitive creature while her body decayed by inches. Leprosy is more dreadful than you could ever conceive.”

The little attenuated voices of the children brought to her ear in distinct utterance the words they sang without thought or comprehension—

“It seems so babyish for me to be going to night school. But I wouldn’t mind that if I thought it would pay. But I don’t think it will pay. I can do the work quicker than they can teach me. It would be a loss of time—” he thought of her and his desire to have her—“and I can’t afford the time. I haven’t the time to spare, in fact.”

“It feels so childish, attending evening classes at my age. Still, I could bear that if I believed it would be worth the effort. But I doubt it will be. I can pick things up faster than any instructor could impart them. It would only squander my hours—” his mind turned to her and the ache to possess her—“and time is the one luxury I cannot afford. Truthfully, I have none to lose.”

Meantime there was one thing which had got pushed into the background of my mind. That was the half-conviction that when the nature of my proposed calamity should be reported to those superstitious people, it would have such an effect that they would want to compromise. So, by and by when I heard footsteps coming, that thought was recalled to me, and I said to myself, “As sure as anything, it's the compromise. Well, if it is good, all right, I will accept; but if it isn't, I mean to stand my ground and play my hand for all it is worth.”

Meanwhile, a thought had been lurking in the back of my mind, half-formed: that once word of the disaster I intended reached those superstitious folk, they’d be shaken enough to seek a settlement. So when I caught the sound of approaching footsteps, that notion surfaced again, and I told myself, “This will be the compromise, I’m certain. If it’s fair, fine—I’ll take it. But if not, I’m resolved to hold firm and play my hand for everything it’s worth.”

To see him hand the same to Charley Bates; and finally to behold them, both running away round the corner at full speed!

Imagine Julian’s profound shock and trepidation as he lingered a few yards distant, staring with eyes strained to their very brims, at the sight of the Rogue thrusting a hand deep into the elder’s coat and retrieving a silken square! To witness him pass the prize to young Arthur; and at last to see the pair of them take flight, vanishing around the street corner at a desperate pace!

He professed to be calling upon Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and to a certain extent he was; but he had a quick eye for the door or windows; his glance roved irrelevantly as he talked. A faint expectation of Cissie came in with him and hovered about him, as the scent of violets follows the flower.

He claimed he had dropped by to pay a call on Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and that was true, up to a point; but his gaze kept flicking toward the front door and the windows, straying unmoored from the topic of conversation the whole time he spoke. A soft, unspoken hope of running into Cissie had stepped over the threshold with him, lingering around him as unshakable as the scent of violets clings to its bloom.

Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR; Banarescu et al., 2013) is a framework for encoding English language 1 meaning as structural-semantic graphs using a fork of Propbank (Kingsbury and Palmer, 2002; O'Gorman et al., 2018) for its semantic frames with additional AMR-specific roles. The graphs are connected, directed, with node and edge labels, and may have multiple roots but always have exactly one distinguished top node. AMR corpora, such as the recent AMR Annotation Release 3.0 (LDC2020T02), 2 encode the graphs in a format called PENMAN notation (Matthiessen and Bateman, 1991) . PENMAN notation is a text stream and is thus linear, but it first uses bracketing to capture a spanning tree over the graph, then inverted edge labels and references to node IDs to capture re-entrancies. Proper interpretation 1 Variations exist for other languages (e.g., Li et al., 2016; , but AMR is primarily English and is not an interlingua (Xue et al., 2014 ).

metadata.title: Penman: An Open-Source Library and Tool for AMR Graphs

text: Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR; Banarescu et al., 2013) constitutes a formalism for representing the semantics of English sentences as structural-semantic graphs, drawing on a variant of Propbank (Kingsbury and Palmer, 2002; O’Gorman et al., 2018) for its inventory of semantic frames augmented with AMR-specific roles. These graphs are connected, directed, and labeled on both nodes and edges; they may contain multiple roots yet always designate exactly one distinguished top node. AMR resources, including the recent AMR Annotation Release 3.0 (LDC2020T02), serialize these graphs using PENMAN notation (Matthiessen and Bateman, 1991). Although PENMAN notation is a linear text stream, it first employs bracketing to encode a spanning tree over the graph and then represents re-entrancies through inverted edge labels and node-identifier references. Accurate interpretation 1 Adaptations exist for other languages (e.g., Li et al., 2016), but AMR remains predominantly English and does not serve as an interlingua (Xue et al., 2014).

They traversed the lower room, slowly, and with caution; for Monks started at every shadow; and Mr. Bumble, holding his lantern a foot above the ground, walked not only with remarkable care, but with a marvellously light step for a gentleman of his figure: looking nervously about him for hidden trap-doors. The gate at which they had entered, was softly unfastened and opened by Monks; merely exchanging a nod with their mysterious acquaintance, the married couple emerged into the wet and darkness outside.

They crossed the lower chamber with slow and wary steps; for Valen started at each passing shade, while Mr. Pruitt, carrying his lantern a foot above the floor, moved not only with singular caution, but with a wonderfully light tread for so stout a gentleman, glancing anxiously this way and that for concealed trap-doors. The gate by which they had come in was silently unbarred and drawn open by Valen; and after a grave nod to their secretive companion, the wedded pair passed forth into the dripping blackness beyond.

Eight dollars a week or a million a year—what is the difference? A mathematician or a wit would give you the wrong answer. The magi brought valuable gifts, but that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later on.

Jim snapped abruptly from his reverie and wrapped Della in his arms. For ten seconds, let us discreetly turn our attention to some trifling object elsewhere. Whether one earns eight dollars weekly or a million annually—what does it matter? A mathematician or a jester would offer you an incorrect reply. The wise men carried precious offerings, yet that particular truth was not one of them. This cryptic remark will be made clear in due course.
