There was no customer in the shop save Jacques the Third, with his twitching fingers and hoarse, raucous voice. This was the man he had once seen among the jury, and he now stood at the small counter, taking his drink while he talked with the Defarges, husband and wife. The Vengeance took her part in the exchange as freely as though she were one of the house itself.

“Show myself a man of spirit?... Ah, well! let me take the name of drunkard humbly—let me be a man of contrite knees—let it be! I know that I always do say ‘Please God’ afore I do anything, from my getting up to my going down of the same, and I be willing to take as much disgrace as there is in that holy act. Hah, yes!... But not a man of spirit? Have I ever allowed the toe of pride to be lifted against my hinder parts without groaning manfully that I question the right to do so? I inquire that query boldly?”

“Prove myself a man of mettle?... Ah, very well! I will accept the title of drunkard meekly—let me be a man bowed on penitent knees—so be it! I am aware that I utter ‘God willing’ before every deed, from my rising to my resting, and I am ready to bear whatever shame such a sacred habit may carry. Hah, indeed!... Yet not a man of spirit? Have I ever permitted pride’s boot to be aimed at my backside without stoutly groaning that I dispute the privilege to do so? I put that question boldly?”

ConverRT (Henderson et al., 2019a ) is a lightweight sentence encoder implemented as a dualencoder network that models the interaction between inputs/contexts and relevant (follow-up) responses. In other words, it performs conversational pretraining based on response selection on the Reddit corpus (Henderson et al., 2019a,b ). It utilizes subword-level tokenization and is very compact and resource-efficient (i.e. it is 59MB in size and can be trained in less than 1 day on 12 GPUs) while achieving state-of-the-art performance on conversational tasks (Casanueva et al., 2020; Bunk et al., 2020) . Through pretrained ConveRT representa-tions, we can leverage conversational cues from over 700M conversational turns for the few-shot span extraction task. 3

metadata.title: Span-ConveRT: Few-shot Span Extraction for Dialog with Pretrained Conversational Representations

text: By this time stupor had given place to anxiety, and anxiety began to make room for hunger and thirst. A form now appeared upon the rise on the other side of the swamp, half-hidden by the mist, and came towards Bathsheba. The woman—for it was a woman—approached with her face askance, as if looking earnestly on all sides of her. When she got a little further round to the left, and drew nearer, Bathsheba could see the newcomer’s profile against the sunny sky, and knew the wavy sweep from forehead to chin, with neither angle nor decisive line anywhere about it, to be the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

By that point, the dazed stupor she’d been trapped in had given way to quiet anxiety, and that anxiety soon made room for sharp, insistent pangs of hunger and thirst. Just then, a figure appeared on the low rise on the far side of the swamp, half-shrouded in mist, and began moving toward Bathsheba. It was a woman, approaching with her head tilted askance, as if she was carefully scanning every part of the area around her. When she veered a little further left and drew closer still, Bathsheba could make out the newcomer’s profile outlined against the bright, sunlit sky. She knew that soft, flowing curve stretching from forehead to chin, with no sharp angles or defined harsh lines anywhere along it, instantly: it was the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

Create test suites Non-technical users can use SyntaxGym's browser-based interface to design and submit their own psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3) . Separately, the site supports uploading pre-made test suites as a JSON-formatted file. This functionality may be useful for advanced users who prefer to automatically generate test suites. 2 Submit language models Users interested in evaluating their own language models first create a public Docker image conforming to the API specified by the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). After users submit these language models on the SyntaxGym site, the models are automatically validated for conformity to the API by the SyntaxGym backend. Valid models are added to the SyntaxGym collection, and will be evaluated on all past and future available test suites in the Gym.

metadata.title: SyntaxGym: An Online Platform for Targeted Evaluation of Language Models

text:
1. Test suite construction
Non-technical users can leverage SyntaxGym’s browser-native interface to design and submit their custom psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3). In parallel, the platform supports upload of pre-built test suites in JSON file format, a functionality intended for advanced users who prefer to generate test suites through automated workflows.
2. Language model submission
Users wishing to evaluate their own custom language models first produce a public Docker image that conforms to the API defined in the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). Following submission of these language models via the SyntaxGym site, the platform’s backend automatically validates that the models meet the required API specifications. Validated models are added to the public SyntaxGym model collection, and will be run against all existing and future test suites available in the repository.

Miss Corner was drawn into the sunset party. She talked to Mr. Carmine and displayed, Mr. Direck thought, great originality of mind. She said "The City of the Sun" was like the cities the boys sometimes made on the playroom floor. She said it was the dearest little city, and gave some amusing particulars. She described the painted walls that made the tour of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. She asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian nor Chinese Utopias.

She said that “The City of the Sun” was like the cities the boys sometimes built on the playroom floor. She called it the dearest little city and went on to give some entertaining details. She described the painted walls, which made the circuit of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. Then she asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian or Chinese Utopias.

With a wild rattle and clatter, and an inhuman abandonment of consideration not easy to be understood in these days, the carriage dashed through streets and swept round corners, with women screaming before it, and men clutching each other and clutching children out of its way. At last, swooping at a street corner by a fountain, one of its wheels came to a sickening little jolt, and there was a loud cry from a number of voices, and the horses reared and plunged.

With a furious rattling and clanging, and a kind of reckless disregard that is hard to imagine in these times, the carriage hurtled through the streets and swung round the corners, while women shrieked ahead of it and men seized one another and dragged children aside to escape its path. At last, as it swept around a corner beside a fountain, one of the wheels gave a small, dreadful jolt, and a cry rose from several voices, while the horses reared and struggled.

“Well, your want of love seems to me the one thing that takes away harm from such an agreement with him. If wild heat had to do wi’ it, making ye long to over-come the awkwardness about your husband’s vanishing, it mid be wrong; but a cold-hearted agreement to oblige a man seems different, somehow. The real sin, ma’am in my mind, lies in thinking of ever wedding wi’ a man you don’t love honest and true.”

“It seems to me that your lack of affection is the very thing that strips this arrangement of its guilt. Had you been driven by some feverish impulse to ignore the shadow of your husband’s disappearance, it might well be a sin; yet a dispassionate pact made merely to assist him feels altogether different. To my mind, ma’am, the true transgression lies in the prospect of wedlock with a man you do not honestly and deeply love.”

Logistic regression on bag-of-words is the best of the baselines, beating out the histogram-based methods. While Gehrmann et al. (2019) report an AUC of 0.87 on classifying text as real or generated using logistic regression on the four buckets of the GLTR system, we report AUC between 0.52 and 0.56 for this task. The discrepancy is likely due to the fact that the human-written text in our discriminator training set comes from the same distribution as the text used to train the language model, while in GLTR the human text comes from children's books, scientific abstracts, and newspaper articles. The selection of training data for learned detection systems is crucial. In real-world applications, the choice ought to reflect the genres that builders of text-generation systems are trying to impersonate. Fine-tuned BERT In Figure 1a , we begin by observing discriminator accuracy as a function of excerpt length and sampling method. As can be intuitively expected, as sequence length increases, so too does accuracy. For unconditioned text decoded with nucleus (p0.96) and untruncated (p1.0) random sampling, we find discriminator accuracy increases from 55%, near random, to about 81% for the longest sequences tested. In contrast, discriminators trained and evaluated on top-k achieve over 80% accuracy even on 16-token excerpts.

metadata.title: Automatic Detection of Generated Text is Easiest when Humans are Fooled

text: Logistic regression applied to bag-of-words features serves as the most effective baseline, surpassing histogram-based techniques. Although Gehrmann et al. (2019) achieved an AUC of 0.87 when classifying text as real or generated by applying logistic regression to the four GLTR system buckets, we observed an AUC between 0.52 and 0.56 for the same task. This variation likely results from the fact that our discriminator’s human-written training data shares the same distribution as the text used to train the language model, while the human-authored text in GLTR is sourced from children’s literature, scientific abstracts, and news reports. Selecting appropriate training data is vital for learned detection systems; for real-world use, the data should correspond to the genres that generation systems are designed to emulate. Regarding the fine-tuned BERT model, Figure 1a depicts discriminator accuracy relative to excerpt length and sampling method. As might be expected, accuracy correlates positively with sequence length. For unconditioned text produced via nucleus sampling ($p=0.96$) and untruncated random sampling ($p=1.0$), accuracy increases from a near-chance 55% to roughly 81% for the longest tested sequences. Conversely, discriminators trained and tested on top-$k$ sampling exceed 80% accuracy even on excerpts of only 16 tokens.

We introduced the first treebank for an Arabic dialect spoken in North-Africa and written in romanized form, NArabizi. More over, being made of user-generated content, this treebank covers a large variety of language variation among native speakers and displays a high level of codeswitching. Annotated with 4 standard morphosyntactic layers, two of them following the Universal Dependency annotation scheme, and provided with translation to French as well as glosses and word language identification, we believe that this corpus will be useful for the community at large, both for linguistic purposes and as training data for resource-scarce NLP in a high-variability scenario. In addition to the annotated data, we provide around 1 million tokens (over 46k sentences) of unlabeled NArabizi content, resulting in the largest dataset available for this dialect. Our corpora are freely available 14 under the CC-BY-SA license and the NArabizi treebank is also released as part of the Universal Dependencies project.

metadata.title: Building a User-Generated Content North-African Arabizi Treebank: Tackling Hell

text: We introduce the first treebank for NArabizi, an Arabic dialect spoken in North Africa and written in romanized form. Moreover, because it is composed of user-generated content, this treebank encompasses a wide range of linguistic variation among native speakers and exhibits a high degree of code-switching. Annotated with four standard morphosyntactic layers, two of which follow the Universal Dependencies annotation scheme, and supplemented with French translations, glosses, and word-level language identification, we believe that this corpus will be valuable to the broader community, both for linguistic analysis and as training data for resource-scarce NLP in a highly variable setting. In addition to the annotated data, we provide approximately 1 million tokens of unlabeled NArabizi content, corresponding to more than 46,000 sentences, which makes this the largest dataset currently available for this dialect. Our corpora are freely available under the CC-BY-SA license, and the NArabizi treebank is also released as part of the Universal Dependencies project.

Natural language processing (NLP) is one of the fastest growing field in computational linguistics and artificial intelligence, e.g. ACL has experienced a 140% growth from 2017 (1419 submissions) to 2020 (3429 submissions). Plus, there are more than 4000 pre-prints published at ArXiv in 2019. As a result, it has become increasingly stressful for researchers to keep up with the evolution of new methods. Today, the common way for researchers to find relevant papers is via searching keywords in Google Scholar 2 or Semantic Scholar 3 . Although these search engines are great at curating all the papers, they are limited in the following ways: (1) they are based on classic information retrieval methods, and do not handle natural language queries well, e.g. what effects can we get from label smoothing? (2) they are designed to find relevant documents (title and abstract) instead of direct answers to users' questions. Often researchers are looking for answers on specific research questions, e.g, how to prevent posterior collapse in VAE? or how much is it to label sentences via crowdsourcing? With current search engine, it requires one to read several papers to find these answer. Therefore, it is necessary to create better tools for researchers to find answers from the scientific publications in a more efficient manner.

metadata.title: Talk to Papers: Bringing Neural Question Answering to Academic Search

text: Natural language processing (NLP) is one of the most rapidly expanding fields within computational linguistics and artificial intelligence; for instance, the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL) saw a 140% increase in submissions between 2017 (1,419) and 2020 (3,429). Furthermore, over 4,000 pre-prints were published on arXiv in 2019 alone. Consequently, it has become increasingly challenging for researchers to remain current with the evolution of new methodologies. Presently, the standard approach for identifying relevant literature involves keyword searches on platforms such as Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar. While these search engines are effective at indexing vast quantities of research, they are limited in several respects: (1) they rely on traditional information retrieval techniques and often fail to process natural language queries effectively, such as "what are the effects of label smoothing?"; and (2) they are optimized for retrieving relevant documents based on titles and abstracts rather than providing direct answers to specific inquiries. Researchers frequently seek precise answers to technical questions, such as how to mitigate posterior collapse in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) or the costs associated with crowdsourced sentence labeling. With existing search engines, finding such information typically requires synthesizing content from multiple papers. Therefore, it is essential to develop more sophisticated tools that enable researchers to extract answers from scientific publications more efficiently.

“Cain Ball, you be no longer a babe and suckling,” said Joseph in the sepulchral tone the circumstances demanded, “and you know what taking an oath is. ’Tis a horrible testament mind ye, which you say and seal with your blood-stone, and the prophet Matthew tells us that on whomsoever it shall fall it will grind him to powder. Now, before all the work-folk here assembled, can you swear to your words as the shepherd asks ye?”

“Cain Ball, you are no mere child at the breast any longer,” Joseph intoned, his voice as grave and hollow as the moment required. “You understand what it means to swear an oath. It is a fearsome covenant, mark you—one you speak aloud and bind with your very life’s seal, and the prophet Matthew warns that on whomever it falls, it will crush him utterly to dust. Now, before all the laborers gathered here, can you swear to your testimony as the shepherd demands?”

Oliver Twist’s ninth birthday found him a pale thin child, somewhat diminutive in stature, and decidedly small in circumference. But nature or inheritance had implanted a good sturdy spirit in Oliver’s breast. It had had plenty of room to expand, thanks to the spare diet of the establishment; and perhaps to this circumstance may be attributed his having any ninth birth-day at all. Be this as it may, however, it _was_ his ninth birthday; and he was keeping it in the coal-cellar with a select party of two other young gentleman, who, after participating with him in a sound thrashing, had been locked up for atrociously presuming to be hungry, when Mrs. Mann, the good lady of the house, was unexpectedly startled by the apparition of Mr. Bumble, the beadle, striving to undo the wicket of the garden-gate.

No one could have imagined that such a mode of cultivation would yield a thriving or abundant harvest. On the morning of his ninth birthday, Oliver Twist was a pallid, slender boy, short for his age and alarmingly narrow around the middle. Still, either through birth or some hidden bequest, a tenacious spirit had taken root inside him. It had been granted ample space to grow, owing to the meager rations of the workhouse, and it may well have been this very fact that allowed him to reach a ninth birthday at all. Whatever the truth of it, that day was indeed his ninth birthday, and he was spending it in the coal-cellar alongside a chosen company of two other boys, who, after sharing a vigorous beating with him, had been shut away for the outrageous crime of daring to feel hunger. Just then, Mrs. Mann, the household’s kindly matron, was suddenly alarmed by the sight of Mr. Bumble the beadle, struggling to unfasten the latch of the garden gate.

All the air round was so thick and dark, the people were so passionately revengeful and fitful, the innocent were so constantly put to death on vague suspicion and black malice, it was so impossible to forget that many as blameless as her husband and as dear to others as he was to her, every day shared the fate from which he had been clutched, that her heart could not be as lightened of its load as she felt it ought to be. The shadows of the wintry afternoon were beginning to fall, and even now the dreadful carts were rolling through the streets. Her mind pursued them, looking for him among the Condemned; and then she clung closer to his real presence and trembled more.

The atmosphere was so dense and oppressive, the crowds so savagely vindictive and erratic, the blameless so frequently executed on mere suspicion and dark spite, and it was so impossible to dismiss the thought that many as guiltless as her husband and as cherished by others as he was by her daily met the doom he had narrowly escaped, that her spirit could not shake off its burden as she knew it should. The gloom of the winter dusk was creeping in, and even at that hour the grim tumbrels were rumbling through the thoroughfares. Her thoughts tracked them, seeking his face among the doomed; then she pressed nearer to his living form and shivered more violently.

The very next day Yeobright went to Humphrey’s cottage, and borrowed of him leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook, to use till he should be able to purchase some for himself. Then he sallied forth with his new fellow-labourer and old acquaintance, and selecting a spot where the furze grew thickest he struck the first blow in his adopted calling. His sight, like the wings in Rasselas, though useless to him for his grand purpose, sufficed for this strait, and he found that when a little practice should have hardened his palms against blistering he would be able to work with ease.

The morning after, Yeobright made his way to Humphrey’s cottage and asked to borrow leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook until he could buy his own. Thus equipped, he set out with his new workmate—who was also an old friend—and chose a patch where the furze stood thickest, driving the first stroke of his new trade. His vision, like the wings in Rasselas, though worthless for the great design he had once cherished, proved sufficient for this narrow path, and he sensed that once a little practice had toughened his palms against blisters, the labour would come easily.

About two hours after this occurrence we heard the ground sea, and before night the ice broke and freed our ship. We, however, lay to until the morning, fearing to encounter in the dark those large loose masses which float about after the breaking up of the ice. I profited of this time to rest for a few hours.

Some two hours later, the surge of the ground sea became audible, and before nightfall the ice fractured, setting our vessel free. We remained hove-to until morning, however, fearing the dark and those massive, drifting floes that wander after the pack breaks up. I seized this time to find a few hours of rest.

“Another circumstance strengthened and confirmed these feelings. Soon after my arrival in the hovel I discovered some papers in the pocket of the dress which I had taken from your laboratory. At first I had neglected them, but now that I was able to decipher the characters in which they were written, I began to study them with diligence. It was your journal of the four months that preceded my creation. You minutely described in these papers every step you took in the progress of your work; this history was mingled with accounts of domestic occurrences. You doubtless recollect these papers. Here they are. Everything is related in them which bears reference to my accursed origin; the whole detail of that series of disgusting circumstances which produced it is set in view; the minutest description of my odious and loathsome person is given, in language which painted your own horrors and rendered mine indelible. I sickened as I read. ‘Hateful day when I received life!’ I exclaimed in agony. ‘Accursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even _you_ turned from me in disgust? God, in pity, made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image; but my form is a filthy type of yours, more horrid even from the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, fellow devils, to admire and encourage him, but I am solitary and abhorred.’

“Another event deepened and confirmed these emotions. Not long after I reached the hut, I found some papers in the pocket of the garment I had taken from your laboratory. At first I paid them no attention; but once I could read the characters in which they were written, I set myself to examine them carefully. They proved to be your journal for the four months before my creation. In these pages you recorded in the smallest detail each stage of your work; mixed with this were notes of domestic events. You surely remember these papers. They are here. In them is everything that concerns my cursed beginning; the entire account of that revolting chain of circumstances that brought it about is laid bare; and the most exact description of my vile, detestable form is given in language that reflected your own terror and made mine unchangeable. I grew faint as I read. ‘Miserable day when I was given life!’ I cried in torment. ‘Accursed maker! Why did you fashion a monster so monstrous that even you recoiled from me in horror? God, in mercy, made man fair and attractive, in his own likeness; but my shape is a foul copy of yours, made more dreadful by the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, his fellow demons, to admire him and cheer him on, but I am alone and loathed.’

Why did I not die? More miserable than man ever was before, why did I not sink into forgetfulness and rest? Death snatches away many blooming children, the only hopes of their doting parents; how many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb! Of what materials was I made that I could thus resist so many shocks, which, like the turning of the wheel, continually renewed the torture?

Why haven’t I died? More wretched than any person who has ever walked this earth, why didn’t I slip into total forgetfulness and finally rest? Death snatches countless thriving children from the world, the only hope their doting parents live for; how many brides and young lovers are bursting with health and bright possibility one day, only to become fodder for worms and the slow rot of the grave the next? What even am I made of, that I can withstand so many blows, each one spinning back around like a turning wheel to heap fresh torture on me again and again?

Christian had been saying to them while the door was leaving its latch, “To think that I, who go from home but once in a while, and hardly then, should have been there this morning!”

Preparations were made for the event, congratulatory visits were received, and all wore a smiling appearance. I shut up, as well as I could, in my own heart the anxiety that preyed there and entered with seeming earnestness into the plans of my father, although they might only serve as the decorations of my tragedy. Through my father’s exertions a part of the inheritance of Elizabeth had been restored to her by the Austrian government. A small possession on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was agreed that, immediately after our union, we should proceed to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the beautiful lake near which it stood.

Preparations for the occasion were soon underway; well-wishers arrived with congratulations, and every face beamed with good cheer. I concealed as best I could the gnawing dread within my own heart, and with feigned earnestness I entered into my father’s designs, though they seemed to me only the ornamental drapery of my impending tragedy. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s inheritance had been reclaimed for her by the Austrian government. A modest estate on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was settled that, immediately after our marriage, we would journey to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the lovely lake on whose banks it lay.

With the glance the calm fixity of her features sublimed itself to an expression of refinement and warmth; it was like garish noon rising to the dignity of sunset in a couple of seconds.

Yeobright and Eustacia held each other’s gaze for one fleeting instant, as though both were turning over the memory of that short, shared moonlit interlude they had experienced together. In that single glance, the calm, fixed set of her features softened and deepened into an expression of gentle refinement and warmth; it was as if bright, garish midday had lifted into the quiet dignity of sunset in the space of just two seconds.

“You have given me a glimpse of fairyland,” Martin said on the ferry-boat. “It makes life worth while to meet people like that. My mind is all worked up. I never appreciated idealism before. Yet I can’t accept it. I know that I shall always be a realist. I am so made, I guess. But I’d like to have made a reply to Kreis and Hamilton, and I think I’d have had a word or two for Norton. I didn’t see that Spencer was damaged any. I’m as excited as a child on its first visit to the circus. I see I must read up some more. I’m going to get hold of Saleeby. I still think Spencer is unassailable, and next time I’m going to take a hand myself.”

“You’ve let me peer into an enchanted realm,” Martin said as the ferry churned across the water. “Meeting people like that makes existence feel worthwhile. My thoughts are spinning. I’d never truly grasped idealism before. Yet I can’t embrace it. I know I’ll always be a realist—it’s simply how I’m built, I suppose. Still, I wish I’d answered Kreis and Hamilton, and I believe I’d have had a few things to say to Norton as well. I didn’t see Spencer suffer any real blow. I’m as thrilled as a child stepping into a circus for the first time. Clearly, I need to read more. I’m going to find Saleeby. I remain convinced Spencer is beyond reproach, and next time I intend to join the fray myself.”

“Oh, that's all right, that's all right, give us a rest; never mind about the direction, _hang_ the direction--I beg pardon, I beg a thousand pardons, I am not well to-day; pay no attention when I soliloquize, it is an old habit, an old, bad habit, and hard to get rid of when one's digestion is all disordered with eating food that was raised forever and ever before he was born; good land! a man can't keep his functions regular on spring chickens thirteen hundred years old. But come--never mind about that; let's--have you got such a thing as a map of that region about you? Now a good map--”

“Oh, it’s fine, it’s fine, just give us a break; forget the directions, to hell with the directions—I apologize, I sincerely apologize, I’m not myself today; don’t mind me when I ramble, it’s an old habit, a nasty old habit, tough to shake when your digestion’s all wrecked from eating food that was grown ages and ages before you were born; good heavens, a man can’t keep his system running right on thirteen-hundred-year-old spring chickens. But come on—let that go; tell me—do you happen to have a map of that area with you? Now a decent map—”

Defarge refreshed himself with a draught of wine--but, he took less than was given to the stranger, as being himself a man to whom it was no rarity--and stood waiting until the countryman had made his breakfast. He looked at no one present, and no one now looked at him; not even Madame Defarge, who had taken up her knitting, and was at work.

Laurent roused himself with a swallow of rough red wine—he took far less than he’d served the unknown traveler, for it was no scarce luxury to a man who lived above the tavern’s cellar—and stood waiting while the hill country farmer finished his morning meal. He cast no glance at any person present, and not a soul in the taproom paid him any mind now; not even his wife Elodie, who had picked up her knitting and was working stitches in steady, unhurried motions.

My life, as it passed thus, was indeed hateful to me, and it was during sleep alone that I could taste joy. O blessed sleep! Often, when most miserable, I sank to repose, and my dreams lulled me even to rapture. The spirits that guarded me had provided these moments, or rather hours, of happiness that I might retain strength to fulfil my pilgrimage. Deprived of this respite, I should have sunk under my hardships. During the day I was sustained and inspirited by the hope of night, for in sleep I saw my friends, my wife, and my beloved country; again I saw the benevolent countenance of my father, heard the silver tones of my Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval enjoying health and youth. Often, when wearied by a toilsome march, I persuaded myself that I was dreaming until night should come and that I should then enjoy reality in the arms of my dearest friends. What agonising fondness did I feel for them! How did I cling to their dear forms, as sometimes they haunted even my waking hours, and persuade myself that they still lived! At such moments vengeance, that burned within me, died in my heart, and I pursued my path towards the destruction of the dæmon more as a task enjoined by heaven, as the mechanical impulse of some power of which I was unconscious, than as the ardent desire of my soul.

The life I led during those days was utterly repugnant to me, and only in the sanctuary of sleep could I experience any sense of gladness. O, holy rest! Many times, when my despair was most profound, I would fall into a slumber where my dreams offered a comfort bordering on bliss. It seemed as if protective spirits had arranged these fleeting hours of peace to ensure I possessed the fortitude to finish my grim journey. Had I been denied such respite, I would have certainly collapsed beneath my burdens. During the daylight hours, I was buoyed by the promise of the dark, for in my dreams I walked again with my friends, my wife, and through the landscapes of my native land. I saw once more the gentle face of my father, heard the sweet, clear notes of Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval in all his youthful vitality. Often, when worn down by a grueling day's march, I told myself that the sunlit world was the illusion and that I would wake to the truth only when night restored me to the arms of those I held dear. What a piercing, desperate love I bore for them! I reached out to their beloved ghosts even when I was awake, convincing myself they had never truly left me. At such times, the vengeance that scorched my heart would grow cold, and I followed the trail of the monster more as a divinely appointed chore—a mechanical impulse of some hidden force—than as the fervent obsession of my own spirit.

Although the current state-of-the-art method for abstractive summarization could only achieve a ROUGE-1 score of less than 40 on the Gigaword dataset, generated headlines actually look very fluent. This is probably because the encoder-decoder model acquired a strong language model from the vast amount of supervision data. However, some studies reported that the generated headlines often deviate from the content of the original document (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019) . They addressed the problem where an abstractive model made mistakes in facts (e.g., tuples of subjects, predicates, and objects).

metadata.title: Improving Truthfulness of Headline Generation
metadata.section: Unexpected outputs 2.1 Examples of unexpected outputs

text: While current state-of-the-art abstractive summarization models yield ROUGE-1 scores below 40 on the Gigaword benchmark, the resulting headlines exhibit a high degree of linguistic fluency. This quality is likely a consequence of the encoder-decoder architecture internalizing a robust language model from extensive supervised training data. Nevertheless, several studies have observed that these generated headlines frequently diverge from the source document's underlying content (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019). These works specifically highlight instances where abstractive models introduce factual inconsistencies, such as errors involving subject-predicate-object tuples.

With Bathsheba a hastened act was a rash act; but, as does not always happen, time gained was prudence insured. It must be added, however, that time was very seldom gained. At this period the single opinion in the parish on herself and her doings that she valued as sounder than her own was Gabriel Oak’s. And the outspoken honesty of his character was such that on any subject, even that of her love for, or marriage with, another man, the same disinterestedness of opinion might be calculated on, and be had for the asking. Thoroughly convinced of the impossibility of his own suit, a high resolve constrained him not to injure that of another. This is a lover’s most stoical virtue, as the lack of it is a lover’s most venial sin. Knowing he would reply truly she asked the question, painful as she must have known the subject would be. Such is the selfishness of some charming women. Perhaps it was some excuse for her thus torturing honesty to her own advantage, that she had absolutely no other sound judgment within easy reach.

For Bathsheba, any choice made in a rush was always a reckless one; and unusually, whenever she gave herself time to deliberate, she was guaranteed to act sensibly. The catch, though, was that she almost never carved out that extra time for herself. Right then, the only person in the whole parish whose take on her and her choices she trusted more than her own judgment was Gabriel Oak. His character was so unflinchingly, bluntly honest that she could count on him to give a fully unbiased take no matter the topic, even if it was about her having feelings for another man, or marrying someone else—all she had to do was ask. He was fully convinced there was no chance she’d ever return his own romantic feelings, so he held himself to a strict, noble code not to sabotage any other suitor’s shot with her. That’s the most stoic, unselfish virtue a person in love can have, just as failing to live up to that standard is the most forgivable fault a lover can commit. She knew he’d give her a truthful answer, so she asked him the question anyway, even though she had to have realized how much that topic would hurt him. That’s the kind of casual selfishness some utterly captivating women carry with them. To be fair, there was a small excuse for her leaning on his honesty for her own gain even when it pained him: she truly didn’t have anyone else whose good sense she could easily access to weigh in on the matter.

DailyDialog contains human-written dialogues about daily topics (e.g. ordinary life, relationships, work, etc) collected by crowd-sourcing. Crowdworkers also annotated utterances with generic DA labels from the set {Inform, Question, Directive, Commissive}. Dialogues in this corpus contain a few utterances (≈ 8) making them more on topic and less dispersed. However, utterances are long in terms of the number of words (≈ 15). SwitchBoard contains informal English dialogues collected from phone conversations between two mutually unknown human participants. The participants were given only one of 70 possible topics as initial topic to start a conversation but they were free to diverge from that topic during the conversation. So, there is no concrete topic associated with each dialogue in this dataset as it is the case for dialogues in DailyDialog.

metadata.title: Dialogue Coherence Assessment Without Explicit Dialogue Act Labels

text: DailyDialog consists of human-authored dialogues on everyday themes—such as daily life, relationships, and work—gathered through crowd-sourcing. Crowdworkers also labelled utterances with general dialogue act categories drawn from the set {Inform, Question, Directive, Commissive}. The dialogues in this corpus are relatively short in terms of the number of utterances (≈ 8), which keeps them more thematically focused and less digressive. At the same time, the utterances are lengthy in terms of word count (≈ 15). SwitchBoard, by contrast, comprises informal English dialogues recorded from telephone conversations between two mutually unacquainted participants. Participants were supplied with only one of 70 possible topics as a starting point for the conversation but were free to depart from that topic as the exchange unfolded. Consequently, unlike the dialogues in DailyDialog, no fixed topic is associated with each dialogue in this dataset.

Monday was a public holiday, the First Monday in August, and the day consecrated by long-established custom to the Matching's Easy Flower Show in Claverings Park. The day was to live in Mr. Britling's memory with a harsh brightness like the brightness of that sunshine one sees at times at the edge of a thunderstorm. There were tents with the exhibits, and a tent for "Popular Refreshments," there was a gorgeous gold and yellow steam roundabout with motor-cars and horses, and another in green and silver with wonderfully undulating ostriches and lions, and each had an organ that went by steam; there were cocoanut shies and many ingenious prize-giving shooting and dart-throwing and ring-throwing stalls, each displaying a marvellous array of crockery, clocks, metal ornaments, and suchlike rewards. There was a race of gas balloons, each with a postcard attached to it begging the finder to say where it descended, and you could get a balloon for a shilling and have a chance of winning various impressive and embarrassing prizes if your balloon went far enough--fish carvers, a silver-handled walking-stick, a bog-oak gramophone-record cabinet, and things like that. And by a special gate one could go for sixpence into the Claverings gardens, and the sixpence would be doubled by Lady Homartyn and devoted next winter to the Matching's Easy coal club. And Mr. Britling went through all the shows with his boys, and finally left them with a shilling each and his blessing and paid his sixpence for the gardens and made his way as he had promised, to have tea with Lady Homartyn.

Monday fell upon the First Monday in August, a bank holiday hallowed by immemorial rite to the Matching's Easy Flower Show within the precincts of Claverings Park. That day would sear itself into Mr. Britling’s recollection with a stark brilliance, akin to the glare that sometimes flares along the hem of a gathering storm. There stood pavilions housing the displays, and a marquee devoted to "Popular Refreshments"; a resplendent gold-and-yellow steam carousel whirled with motorcars and chargers, while another, sheathed in green and silver, bore ostriches and lions that rose and fell in wondrous undulation—each machine possessed a steam-driven organ. Cocoanut shies abounded, together with an array of artful shooting, dart-hurling, and quoit-tossing booths, every one proffering a dazzling hoard of crockery, timepieces, gilt ornaments, and similar trophies. A race of gas-balloons was held, each bearing a postcard that entreated the discoverer to record where it alighted; for a shilling one might purchase a balloon and stand to gain sundry imposing yet awkward prizes should it travel far enough—fish carvers, a silver-mounted walking-stick, a bog-oak cabinet for gramophone records, and objects of that ilk. And through a private gate, for the sum of sixpence, one could enter the gardens of Claverings, that sixpence to be matched by Lady Homartyn and consecrated the following winter to the Matching's Easy coal fund. Mr. Britling traversed the whole fête with his sons, at length bestowing upon each a shilling and his paternal goodwill, then tendered his sixpence at the garden gate and bent his steps, as he had pledged, toward tea with Lady Homartyn.

At length, when nine o’clock had come, and they began to think they were to hear no more that night, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room, followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whom Oliver almost shrieked with surprise to see; for they told him it was his brother, and it was the same man he had met at the market-town, and seen looking in with Fagin at the window of his little room. Monks cast a look of hate, which, even then, he could not dissemble, at the astonished boy, and sat down near the door. Mr. Brownlow, who had papers in his hand, walked to a table near which Rose and Oliver were seated.

Finally, when nine o’clock arrived and they had begun to assume the night's business was over, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room. They were followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whose appearance made Oliver nearly cry out in shock. They informed him that this was his brother—the same individual he had encountered in the market town and later seen peering through his bedroom window with Fagin. Monks directed a look of pure, undisguised malice at the stunned boy before taking a seat near the door. Mr. Brownlow, clutching a handful of documents, walked over to a table where Rose and Oliver were sitting.

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, raising his hand towards the moon. “I have looked at her from my prison-window, when I could not bear her light. I have looked at her when it has been such torture to me to think of her shining upon what I had lost, that I have beaten my head against my prison-walls. I have looked at her, in a state so dull and lethargic, that I have thought of nothing but the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of perpendicular lines with which I could intersect them.” He added in his inward and pondering manner, as he looked at the moon, “It was twenty either way, I remember, and the twentieth was difficult to squeeze in.”

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, lifting his hand toward the moon. “I have watched her from my prison window when I could not endure her light. I have watched her when it tortured me to think of her shining on what I had lost, so that I struck my head against the prison walls. I have watched her in such a dull, stupefied state that I have thought of nothing except the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of upright lines with which I might cross them.” He added, in his inward, meditative way, as he gazed at the moon, “It was twenty each way, I remember, and the twentieth was hard to fit in.”

When the quiet of the garret had been long undisturbed, and his heaving breast and shaken form had long yielded to the calm that must follow all storms--emblem to humanity, of the rest and silence into which the storm called Life must hush at last--they came forward to raise the father and daughter from the ground. He had gradually dropped to the floor, and lay there in a lethargy, worn out. She had nestled down with him, that his head might lie upon her arm; and her hair drooping over him curtained him from the light.

After the attic had remained silent for some time, and his heavy breathing and trembling frame had finally surrendered to the stillness that inevitably follows a crisis—a symbol of the ultimate peace and silence that must eventually quiet the storm of human life—the others moved closer to lift the father and daughter from the floor. He had slowly collapsed onto the boards, lying in a state of utter, worn-out lethargy. She had lowered herself with him, cushioning his head upon her arm, while her loose hair fell over him like a curtain to shield him from the light.

“Jerry, if you wish to take something to eat, you can. But, keep in the way. You will be sure to hear when the jury come in. Don’t be a moment behind them, for I want you to take the verdict back to the bank. You are the quickest messenger I know, and will get to Temple Bar long before I can.”

“Jerry, if you’d like to grab a bite, go ahead. Just stay close by. You’ll definitely hear when the jury returns. Don’t fall a second behind them—I need you to carry the verdict straight to the bank. You’re the fastest courier I’ve got, and you’ll reach Temple Bar well ahead of me.”

Clarence was twenty-two now, and was my head executive, my right hand. He was a darling; he was equal to anything; there wasn't anything he couldn't turn his hand to. Of late I had been training him for journalism, for the time seemed about right for a start in the newspaper line; nothing big, but just a small weekly for experimental circulation in my civilization-nurseries. He took to it like a duck; there was an editor concealed in him, sure. Already he had doubled himself in one way; he talked sixth century and wrote nineteenth. His journalistic style was climbing, steadily; it was already up to the back settlement Alabama mark, and couldn't be told from the editorial output of that region either by matter or flavor.

Clarence was now twenty-two and served as my chief lieutenant, my indispensable right hand. He was a marvel, capable of rising to any occasion; there was simply no task he couldn't master. Lately, I had been mentoring him in the field of journalism, as the time felt right to launch a newspaper—nothing major, just a modest weekly for trial distribution in my various outposts of progress. He embraced the work instinctively; a true editor was hidden within him. He had already developed a dual nature: he spoke the local sixth-century dialect but wrote in the nineteenth-century style. His journalistic prose was steadily improving; it had already reached the standard of a rural Alabama paper, matching that region's editorial output perfectly in both content and tone.

It was a desperate place for him to be in, and might cost him his life, but it was no use to argue with him. If he considered his knightly honor at stake here, that was the end of argument; he would stay, and nothing could prevent it; I was aware of that. And so I dropped the subject. The woman spoke:

"You think you are out of it for good and all. So did we think. We were as smug as you are when France went down in '71.... Yours is only one further degree of insularity. You think this vacuous aloofness of yours is some sort of moral superiority. So did we, so did we....

“You believe you are finished with it for all time. So did we once believe. We were every bit as complacent as you when France collapsed in ’71.... Your case is merely another step in the same insularity. You imagine that your empty detachment is a kind of moral elevation. So did we, so did we....

The Doctor was in his best condition, and looked specially young. The resemblance between him and Lucie was very strong at such times, and as they sat side by side, she leaning on his shoulder, and he resting his arm on the back of her chair, it was very agreeable to trace the likeness.

The Doctor was at his finest, appearing almost youthful. In these moments, the likeness between him and Lucie grew remarkably vivid; as they sat close together, her head nestled against his shoulder and his arm draped along the back of her chair, it was a quiet pleasure to follow the lines of that resemblance.

Adding additional lexical, syntactic, and semantic input features to neural RE approaches has been shown to considerably improve performance (Zeng et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017 Zhang et al., , 2018 . Features include e.g. casing, named entity, part-of-speech and dependency information. Most recently, pre-learned contextualized word representations (deep language representations) emerged, capturing syntactic and semantic information useful to a wide range of downstream tasks Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018) . We therefore evaluate the effect of adding explicit named entity and grammatical role information (through entity masking) on our pre-learned sentence representations, and compare it to adding contextualized word representations computed by ELMo and BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) as additional input features.

metadata.title: Probing Linguistic Features of Sentence-Level Representations in Neural Relation Extraction

text: Adding additional lexical, syntactic, and semantic input features to neural RE approaches has been shown to substantially improve performance (Zeng et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017, 2018). Such features include, for example, casing, named-entity, part-of-speech, and dependency information. More recently, pre-trained contextualized word representations (deep language representations) have emerged, capturing syntactic and semantic information that is useful for a wide range of downstream tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We therefore evaluate the effect of adding explicit named-entity and grammatical-role information, through entity masking, to our pre-trained sentence representations, and compare this with adding contextualized word representations computed by ELMo and BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) as additional input features.

“Monseigneur, the good God knows; but I don’t ask it. My petition is, that a morsel of stone or wood, with my husband’s name, may be placed over him to show where he lies. Otherwise, the place will be quickly forgotten, it will never be found when I am dead of the same malady, I shall be laid under some other heap of poor grass. Monseigneur, they are so many, they increase so fast, there is so much want. Monseigneur! Monseigneur!”

“Monseigneur, only the good God knows; I do not. I do not ask for that. All I beg is that a little stone or piece of wood, bearing my husband’s name, be put above him, so people may know where he is buried. Otherwise the spot will soon be forgotten, and it will never be found again when I too am dead of the same sickness, and I shall be laid beneath some other poor mound of grass. Monseigneur, there are so many of them, and they multiply so quickly, and there is such misery. Monseigneur! Monseigneur!”

At length, a low cry of pain broke the stillness that prevailed; and uttering it, the boy awoke. His left arm, rudely bandaged in a shawl, hung heavy and useless at his side; the bandage was saturated with blood. He was so weak, that he could scarcely raise himself into a sitting posture; when he had done so, he looked feebly round for help, and groaned with pain. Trembling in every joint, from cold and exhaustion, he made an effort to stand upright; but, shuddering from head to foot, fell prostrate on the ground.

After a long stretch of unbroken quiet, a thin, pained whimper cut through the prevailing stillness, and the boy jolted awake. His left arm, crudely wrapped in a shawl, dangled heavy and useless at his side, the bandage soaked completely through with blood. He was so weak he could barely push himself up into a sitting position; once he’d managed the effort, he glanced dazedly around for help, groaning with sharp, throbbing pain. Every joint trembled with cold and total exhaustion, and he strained to pull himself upright, only to shudder violently from head to foot and collapse fully prone onto the ground.

degrades the BLEU score at all cutoff points. When using cross-entropy based sorting of the data, cutting off 40% of the lowest scoring training pairs increased BLEU by 0.67 points when compared to using the full training set. If more than 40% of the data is removed, the BLEU score starts to decrease. Surprisingly, ROC AUC based sorting, which requires a manually annotated development set, produces worse results than cross-entropy. ROC AUC reaches a maximum gain of 0.26 BLEU points over using the whole data set when 20% of the data is truncated from the noisy end. English to Finnish translations show similar results, as illustrated in figure 3, although the BLEU scores are overall lower as it is common in systems translating into morphologically rich languages. Again, cross-entropy based models perform better than ROC AUC based ones: at 80% cutoff cross-entropy model has 0.18 point and ROC AUC model has 0.12 point improvement over using the whole data. The increases in scores are more modest in English to Finnish translations than in Finnish to English translations.

metadata.title: OpusFilter: A Configurable Parallel Corpus Filtering Toolbox

text: Removing low-quality training pairs according to cross-entropy-based sorting degrades the BLEU score at every cutoff threshold. Eliminating the bottom 40% of training pairs, as ranked by cross-entropy, yields a BLEU improvement of 0.67 points over employing the full dataset. Beyond 40% removal, the BLEU score declines. Notably, ROC‑AUC-based sorting—which relies on a manually annotated development set—performs worse than cross-entropy-based sorting, achieving a maximum gain of only 0.26 BLEU points over the complete dataset when the noisiest 20% of the data is discarded. English-to-Finnish translations exhibit comparable trends, as shown in Figure 3, although the absolute BLEU scores are lower, as is typical for systems translating into morphologically rich languages. Here, too, cross-entropy-based models outperform ROC‑AUC-based ones: at an 80% retention cutoff, the cross-entropy model yields a 0.18-point improvement and the ROC‑AUC model a 0.12-point improvement over using all data. The score increases are more modest for English-to-Finnish than for Finnish-to-English translations.

He glanced around at his friend reading the letter and saw the books on the table. Into his eyes leaped a wistfulness and a yearning as promptly as the yearning leaps into the eyes of a starving man at sight of food. An impulsive stride, with one lurch to right and left of the shoulders, brought him to the table, where he began affectionately handling the books. He glanced at the titles and the authors’ names, read fragments of text, caressing the volumes with his eyes and hands, and, once, recognized a book he had read. For the rest, they were strange books and strange authors. He chanced upon a volume of Swinburne and began reading steadily, forgetful of where he was, his face glowing. Twice he closed the book on his forefinger to look at the name of the author. Swinburne! he would remember that name. That fellow had eyes, and he had certainly seen color and flashing light. But who was Swinburne? Was he dead a hundred years or so, like most of the poets? Or was he alive still, and writing? He turned to the title-page . . . yes, he had written other books; well, he would go to the free library the first thing in the morning and try to get hold of some of Swinburne’s stuff. He went back to the text and lost himself. He did not notice that a young woman had entered the room. The first he knew was when he heard Arthur’s voice saying:-

We stood there awhile, in the thick darkness and stillness, looking toward the red blur in the distance, and trying to make out the meaning of a far-away murmur that rose and fell fitfully on the night. Sometimes it swelled up and for a moment seemed less remote; but when we were hopefully expecting it to betray its cause and nature, it dulled and sank again, carrying its mystery with it. We started down the hill in its direction, and the winding road plunged us at once into almost solid darkness--darkness that was packed and crammed in between two tall forest walls. We groped along down for half a mile, perhaps, that murmur growing more and more distinct all the time. The coming storm threatening more and more, with now and then a little shiver of wind, a faint show of lightning, and dull grumblings of distant thunder. I was in the lead. I ran against something--a soft heavy something which gave, slightly, to the impulse of my weight; at the same moment the lightning glared out, and within a foot of my face was the writhing face of a man who was hanging from the limb of a tree! That is, it seemed to be writhing, but it was not. It was a grewsome sight. Straightway there was an ear-splitting explosion of thunder, and the bottom of heaven fell out; the rain poured down in a deluge. No matter, we must try to cut this man down, on the chance that there might be life in him yet, mustn't we? The lightning came quick and sharp now, and the place was alternately noonday and midnight. One moment the man would be hanging before me in an intense light, and the next he was blotted out again in the darkness. I told the king we must cut him down. The king at once objected.

We lingered for a duration in the dense shadow and silence, watching a ruby smudge on the horizon and straining to interpret a distant hum that ebbed and flowed fitfully through the night. At times it swelled, seeming for a moment more proximate; yet, when we anticipated it might reveal its source, it grew dull and subsided, its secret still guarded. We set off down the slope toward it, the winding track casting us at once into a nearly palpable darkness—an obscurity packed tight between two high ramparts of timber. We groped forward for perhaps half a mile, the murmur growing increasingly audible. The impending storm became more threatening, bringing occasional tremors of wind, pale flickers of lightning, and the low growls of remote thunder. I was at the front. I collided with an object—a soft, heavy mass that gave way slightly under my momentum; in that same instant, the lightning blazed, and a foot from my face hung the distorted visage of a man dangling from a branch! It seemed to twitch, though it was actually still. It was a morbid sight. Immediately, an ear-splitting crash of thunder followed, and the very floor of heaven gave way; the rain fell in a deluge. Regardless, we had to attempt to cut him down, for there was a chance that a spark of life remained, was there not? The lightning became rapid and piercing, the woods alternating between the clarity of midday and the void of midnight. One heartbeat the man was suspended before me in a fierce light, and the next he was vanished into the gloom. I told the Sovereign we must lower him. The Sovereign at once demurred.

But the most radical change of all, and the one that pleased her most, was the change in his speech. Not only did he speak more correctly, but he spoke more easily, and there were many new words in his vocabulary. When he grew excited or enthusiastic, however, he dropped back into the old slurring and the dropping of final consonants. Also, there was an awkward hesitancy, at times, as he essayed the new words he had learned. On the other hand, along with his ease of expression, he displayed a lightness and facetiousness of thought that delighted her. It was his old spirit of humor and badinage that had made him a favorite in his own class, but which he had hitherto been unable to use in her presence through lack of words and training. He was just beginning to orientate himself and to feel that he was not wholly an intruder. But he was very tentative, fastidiously so, letting Ruth set the pace of sprightliness and fancy, keeping up with her but never daring to go beyond her.

But the deepest alteration of all, and the one that gave her the keenest pleasure, was the change in his speech. He not only spoke more correctly, but with greater ease, and his vocabulary had grown rich with many unfamiliar words. Yet when excitement or ardor took hold of him, he slipped back into the old drawl and the soft swallowing of final sounds. There was, too, at times an uneasy hesitation as he tried out the new words he had acquired. On the other hand, with this new fluency there came a quickness and playful wit in his thinking that delighted her. It was the same old turn for humor and banter that had once made him a favorite among his own companions, though until now he had been unable to bring it forth in her presence for want of words and training. He was only just beginning to find his bearings and to feel that he was not altogether a stranger. But he proceeded with great caution, almost with punctilious care, letting Clara determine the tempo of gayety and invention, matching her pace, yet never venturing to pass beyond it.

“My dear Mr. Lorry, it is because I am a Frenchman born, that the thought (which I did not mean to utter here, however) has passed through my mind often. One cannot help thinking, having had some sympathy for the miserable people, and having abandoned something to them,” he spoke here in his former thoughtful manner, “that one might be listened to, and might have the power to persuade to some restraint. Only last night, after you had left us, when I was talking to Lucie--”

“My good Mr. Sterling, it is my very identity as a son of France that forces this reflection—a thought I had not meant to voice aloud—to weigh so heavily upon me. It is impossible to avoid the hope, given my long-held compassion for the wretched and the inheritance I surrendered to them,” he remarked, slipping back into his pensive state, “that I might be heard, and that I might possess the influence to counsel them toward moderation. Even last night, after you had gone, while I was speaking with Elena—”

Our goal is to build a framework that will allow users to quickly build text generation demos using existing modeling techniques. This design allows the framework to be almost agnostic to the ongoing development of text generation techniques (Gao et al., 2019a) . Instead, we focus on the organic integration of models and the interfaces for the final demo/app. From a top-down view, our design are bounded to two markets: text processing assistant, and conversational AI, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Two demos are present as examples in these domains: document auto-completion and Sherlock Holmes. We further breakdown these demos into several tasks, designed to be shared across different demos. We also designed several strategies to integrate multiple models to generate text. These strategies allow each model to plug-in without heavy constraints on the architecture of the models, as detailed in Section 4.

metadata.title: MIXINGBOARD: a Knowledgeable Stylized Integrated Text Generation Platform

text: Our objective is to develop a framework that enables users to rapidly construct text generation demonstrations using existing modeling techniques. This architecture makes the framework largely agnostic to the continued evolution of text generation methods (Gao et al., 2019a). Rather than centering on specific generation advances, we emphasize the seamless integration of models with the interfaces used in the final demo or application. From a high-level perspective, our design is constrained to two application areas: text processing assistants and conversational AI, as shown in Fig. 2. We present two example demos in these domains: document auto-completion and Sherlock Holmes. We further decompose these demos into a set of tasks that can be shared across different demos. In addition, we devise several strategies for combining multiple models to generate text. These strategies enable each model to be incorporated without imposing heavy architectural constraints, as discussed in Section 4.

“Several changes, in the meantime, took place in the cottage. The presence of Safie diffused happiness among its inhabitants, and I also found that a greater degree of plenty reigned there. Felix and Agatha spent more time in amusement and conversation, and were assisted in their labours by servants. They did not appear rich, but they were contented and happy; their feelings were serene and peaceful, while mine became every day more tumultuous. Increase of knowledge only discovered to me more clearly what a wretched outcast I was. I cherished hope, it is true, but it vanished when I beheld my person reflected in water or my shadow in the moonshine, even as that frail image and that inconstant shade.

In the interval, the household underwent subtle transformations. Safie’s arrival brought a quiet radiance into the lives of those within, and I perceived that a fuller measure of comfort now blessed them. Felix and Agatha gave their hours more freely to leisure and talk, aided in their tasks by hired hands. They were not wealthy, yet they wore their lot with tranquil satisfaction; their spirits rested in calm contentment, while mine grew daily more tempestuous. Each new insight only sharpened the truth that I was a miserable exile. I clung to hope, indeed, but it dissolved whenever I glimpsed my form in a pool’s mirror or my shadow cast by the moon—as fleeting as that fragile reflection and that wavering darkness.

While he ruminated a footstep descended the stairs. It was the nurse, who brought in her hand a rolled mass of wet paper. The woman was so engrossed with her occupation that she hardly saw Venn. She took from a cupboard some pieces of twine, which she strained across the fireplace, tying the end of each piece to the firedog, previously pulled forward for the purpose, and, unrolling the wet papers, she began pinning them one by one to the strings in a manner of clothes on a line.

As he was still musing, footsteps came down the stairs. It was the nurse, carrying in her hand a rolled bundle of wet paper. She was so absorbed in what she was doing that she scarcely noticed Venn. She took some lengths of twine from a cupboard, stretched them across the fireplace, and tied each end to the firedog, which had already been drawn forward for that purpose. Then, unrolling the damp sheets, she began pinning them up one by one on the strings, like clothes on a line.

Joe Larrabee came out of the post-oak flats of the Middle West pulsing with a genius for pictorial art. At six he drew a picture of the town pump with a prominent citizen passing it hastily. This effort was framed and hung in the drug store window by the side of the ear of corn with an uneven number of rows. At twenty he left for New York with a flowing necktie and a capital tied up somewhat closer.

Joe Larrabee hailed from the post-oak flatlands of the Midwest, bursting with innate genius for visual art from the moment he left those rural stretches. At six years old, he drew a sketch of the town pump, with one of the community’s most prominent residents hurrying past it in a rush. The piece was framed and hung in the local drugstore window, right alongside the ear of corn that had an odd, uneven number of rows. When he turned twenty, he set off for New York, wearing a loose, flowing necktie and carrying a small pool of savings he’d stowed away far more tightly than that casual neckwear was knotted.

“But she didn’t come to see me, though I asked her, before I married, if she would come. Had she come, or had I gone there, she would never have died saying, ‘I am a broken-hearted woman, cast off by my son.’ My door has always been open to her—a welcome here has always awaited her. But that she never came to see.”

“Still, she never came to call on me, even when I pressed her to visit months before I took my wedding vows. Had she made the journey, or had I traveled back to her small cottage, she never would have breathed her last murmuring, ‘I am a woman with a shattered heart, discarded by my own son.’ My door has never been barred to her—warmth and a place at my table have always waited, whenever she might choose to arrive. But she never came to claim that invitation.”

And also, he wanted to see the old country from which his maternal grandmother had sprung. Wasn't there even now in his bedroom in New York a water-colour of Market Saffron church, where the dear old lady had been confirmed? And generally he wanted to see Europe. As an interesting side show to the excursion he hoped, in his capacity of the rather underworked and rather over-salaried secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought, to discuss certain agreeable possibilities with Mr. Britling, who lived at Matching's Easy.

On top of everything else, he’d long been curious to visit the old country his maternal grandmother had hailed from. Didn’t he still keep a watercolor of Market Saffron church propped on a shelf in his New York bedroom, the very church where that dear old woman had been confirmed as a young girl? More broadly, he was just eager to explore Europe at large, full stop. As a low-stakes, enjoyable side piece of the excursion, he hoped to connect with Mr. Britling, who lived in Matching’s Easy, to talk through a few promising potential opportunities in his capacity as secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought— a position that left him drastically underworked and noticeably overpaid, if anyone bothered to ask.
