Aaron Miller (born August 11, 1971) is an American former professional ice hockey defenseman who played in the National Hockey League for the Quebec Nordiques and the Colorado Avalanche; he was born in Buffalo, New York, and also played for the Ottawa Senators.

Observing that she did not reply he regarded her more closely. She was almost weeping. Images of a future never to be enjoyed, the revived sense of her bitter disappointment, the picture of the neighbour’s suspended ridicule which was raised by Wildeve’s words, had been too much for proud Eustacia’s equanimity.

Seeing that she made no answer, he looked at her more closely. She was nearly in tears. The thought of a future she was never to know, the renewed pang of her bitter disappointment, and the image of the neighbor’s mockery, now half-aroused by Wildeve’s words, had been too much for proud Eustacia’s composure.

"Here I am planning the real map of the world," he said. "Every sort of district that has a character of its own must have its own rule; and the great republic of the united states of the world must keep the federal peace between them all. That's the plain sense of life; the federal world-republic. Why do we bother ourselves with loyalties to any other government but that? It needs only that sufficient men should say it, and that republic would be here now. Why have we loitered so long--until these tragic punishments come? We have to map the world out into its states, and plan its government and the way of its tolerations."

“I’m here drafting the true, living map of the whole globe,” he remarked. “Every region with its own distinct cultural character gets to set its own local rules, and the grand global federated republic will uphold shared, impartial peace between every one of them. That’s the simple, unvarnished truth of how life ought to work: a unified world commonwealth. Why waste our loyalty on any smaller, pettier governing body when that’s the only cause worth standing for? All it takes is enough people standing together to demand it, and that republic would be ours tomorrow. Why have we dragged our feet for so long, letting these cruel, avoidable tragedies strike us first? We just need to carve the globe into its natural self-governing states, lay out the structure of its shared governance, and write the terms that protect mutual tolerance across every border.”

“The crime for which I am imprisoned, Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, and for which I shall be summoned before the tribunal, and shall lose my life (without your so generous help), is, they tell me, treason against the majesty of the people, in that I have acted against them for an emigrant. It is in vain I represent that I have acted for them, and not against, according to your commands. It is in vain I represent that, before the sequestration of emigrant property, I had remitted the imposts they had ceased to pay; that I had collected no rent; that I had had recourse to no process. The only response is, that I have acted for an emigrant, and where is that emigrant?

“The offense for which I languish in this cell, Citizen formerly the Count, and for which I shall shortly be hauled before the bar to surrender my life—barring your most gracious intercession—is, I am informed, a betrayal of the sovereign people. They charge me with having conspired against the nation in the service of an exile. I protest in vain that my labors were for their sake, not their ruin, and were carried out in strict accordance with your mandates. I argue to no effect that, prior to the confiscation of your estates, I had already waived the levies they could no longer afford, sought no arrears, and initiated no legal seizures. The singular answer I receive is that I have served a fugitive, and the cry remains: where is this master of yours?”

“O, sir, at another time you shall know my name, and who my mother was, and who my father, and how I never knew their hard, hard history. But I cannot tell you at this time, and I cannot tell you here. All that I may tell you, here and now, is, that I pray to you to touch me and to bless me. Kiss me, kiss me! O my dear, my dear!”

“Oh, sir, someday I’ll share my name with you, and tell you about my mother and my father, and how I was never told the harsh, bitter story of their lives. But I can’t speak of it now, and I can’t speak of it in this place. All I can say, here and now, is that I beg you to lay your hands on me and give me your blessing. Kiss me, kiss me! Oh, my darling, my darling!”

Then she broke away into the praises of serenity. Never had she thought so much of his beautiful "Silent Places" as she did now. How she longed to take refuge in some such dreamland from violence and treachery and foolish rumours! She was weary of every reality. She wanted to fly away into some secret hiding-place and cultivate her simple garden there--as Voltaire had done.... Sometimes at night she was afraid to undress. She imagined the sound of guns, she imagined landings and frightful scouts "in masks" rushing inland on motor bicycles....

Then she began to speak fervently about the virtues of tranquility. She had never appreciated his exquisite "Silent Places" as deeply as she did in that moment. She felt a profound longing to retreat into such a dreamscape, seeking shelter from the brutality, betrayal, and mindless gossip of the world. The weight of reality had become unbearable. She wanted to vanish into a private sanctuary and quietly tend her own garden, just as Voltaire had once suggested. At night, she was sometimes too terrified even to undress. Her mind was filled with the imagined roar of artillery, the threat of coastal invasions, and the sight of ghoulish, masked scouts on motorcycles racing through the countryside.

By my authority as executive I threw Merlin into prison--the same cell I had occupied myself. Then I gave public notice by herald and trumpet that I should be busy with affairs of state for a fortnight, but about the end of that time I would take a moment's leisure and blow up Merlin's stone tower by fires from heaven; in the meantime, whoso listened to evil reports about me, let him beware. Furthermore, I would perform but this one miracle at this time, and no more; if it failed to satisfy and any murmured, I would turn the murmurers into horses, and make them useful. Quiet ensued.

Exercising my sovereign prerogative, I consigned Merlin to the very dungeon that had once held me. Then I proclaimed far and wide, by clarion and crier, that for a fortnight I should be absorbed in matters of governance; thereafter, I might snatch a brief hour to shatter Merlin’s stone spire with celestial flame. Meanwhile, let any who gave ear to slanders against me take heed. Moreover, this single marvel would I work for now, and none other; should it prove insufficient and any dare mutter, those malcontents I would transform to draught horses and set them to labor. A hush descended.

We hypothesize that the preservation of a relation in translation is related to the ability of a zero-shot parser to predict it. In order to control for obvious covariates, we introduce two control variables: (1) SOURCE-SIDE HARDNESS (test-set F-scores attained by the parser on English dependency relations) and (2) TARGET-SIDE HARDNESS (F-scores attained by a parser trained on the targetlanguage UD GSD corpus on the target-language test set). We use a mixed-effects model with PRESERVATION, SOURCE-SIDE HARDNESS, and TARGET-SIDE HARDNESS as fixed effects, random intercepts for language, and F-scores for dependency relations as the dependent variable. We then used likelihood-ratio test to compute p-values for the difference in predictive power between the model without PRESERVATION and one with it. The p-value (using Holm correction) is highly significant (< 0.001) for the PUD corpora, and for GSD it is significant with p = 0.02.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: We hypothesize that the preservation of a relation in translation is associated with the ability of a zero-shot parser to predict it. To control for obvious covariates, we introduce two control variables: (1) SOURCE-SIDE HARDNESS (test-set F-scores achieved by the parser on English dependency relations) and (2) TARGET-SIDE HARDNESS (F-scores achieved by a parser trained on the target-language UD GSD corpus on the target-language test set). We fit a mixed-effects model with PRESERVATION, SOURCE-SIDE HARDNESS, and TARGET-SIDE HARDNESS as fixed effects, random intercepts for language, and F-scores for dependency relations as the dependent variable. We then use a likelihood-ratio test to compute p-values for the difference in predictive power between the model without PRESERVATION and the model that includes it. The p-value (after Holm correction) is highly significant (< 0.001) for the PUD corpora, and for GSD it is significant with p = 0.02.

His complex anger flamed afresh, and Ruth was in terror of him. Never had she seen him so angry, and it was all mystified and unreasonable to her comprehension. And yet, through her very terror ran the fibres of fascination that had drawn and that still drew her to him—that had compelled her to lean towards him, and, in that mad, culminating moment, lay her hands upon his neck. She was hurt and outraged by what had taken place, and yet she lay in his arms and quivered while he went on muttering, “The beasts! The beasts!” And she still lay there when he said: “I’ll not bother your table again, dear. They do not like me, and it is wrong of me to thrust my objectionable presence upon them. Besides, they are just as objectionable to me. Faugh! They are sickening. And to think of it, I dreamed in my innocence that the persons who sat in the high places, who lived in fine houses and had educations and bank accounts, were worth while!”

A new surge of his intricate rage flared up, and Ruth felt a cold terror take hold of her. She had never seen him so consumed by fury, a state that remained entirely baffling and irrational to her understanding. Even so, through her fear ran the same magnetic threads of fascination that had always pulled her toward him—the same impulse that had forced her to lean in and, in a frantic, climactic moment, clasp her hands behind his neck. Though she felt wounded and insulted by what had transpired, she stayed trembling in his arms as he continued to mutter, “The animals! The wretched animals!” She remained there as he added, “I won’t trouble your dinner table again, my dear. They have no liking for me, and it is a mistake for me to impose my unwelcome presence on them. In truth, they are just as loathsome to me. It’s revolting. To think that in my ignorance I actually believed those in high society, with their grand estates, educations, and fortunes, were people of any real value!”

The bright interest of this consulship helped Mr. Direck more than anything to get the better of his Robinson-anecdote crave, and when presently he found his dialogue with Mr. Britling resumed, he turned at once to this remarkable discovery of his long lost and indeed hitherto unsuspected relative. "It's an American sort of thing to do, I suppose," he said apologetically, "but I almost thought of going on, on Monday, to Market Saffron, which was the locality of the Hinkinsons, and just looking about at the tombstones in the churchyard for a day or so."

The vivid novelty of this official role did more than any other remedy to quiet Mr. Direck’s hunger for the Robinson story, and when his talk with Mr. Britling soon picked up again, he veered without pause toward the astonishing unearthing of a kinsman so long vanished and wholly unimagined. “I guess it’s a typically American impulse,” he said with a note of apology, “but I half considered pushing on, come Monday, to Market Saffron—the old Hinkinson country—and spending a day or two poking among the gravestones in the churchyard.”

The questions and answers contain numerous technical terms. We estimated the number of mentions of technical support entities with a model built with the mention detection data produced by our annotators during their training period (see Section 3.2). On average the training set questions contain 1.67 detected mentions of errors, error codes, error messages or log messages (we do not further extract mentions from error messages or log messages, hence the subsequent counts are from other parts of the question), 3.8 mentions of hardware or software products or components, 2.0 mentions of parameters, settings, or configurations, and 2.23 mentions of operations or specific commands issued by the person asking the question, among others. Many of these terms are likely not part of the vocabulary of most generalpurpose contextual language models. Hence, one of the reasons for including the whole Technotes corpus is to provide data for enhancing the language models by appropriately enlarging the vocabulary to include technical support terms.

metadata.title: The TechQA Dataset

text: The questions and answers contain many technical terms. We estimated the number of mentions of technical support entities using a model trained on the mention detection data produced by our annotators during their training period (see Section 3.2). On average, the training-set questions contain 1.67 detected mentions of errors, error codes, error messages, or log messages (we do not extract additional mentions from error messages or log messages, so the counts reported below come from other parts of the question), 3.8 mentions of hardware or software products or components, 2.0 mentions of parameters, settings, or configurations, and 2.23 mentions of operations or specific commands issued by the person asking the question, among others. Many of these terms are likely outside the vocabulary of most general-purpose contextual language models. Accordingly, one reason for including the full Technotes corpus is to provide data that can improve the language models by appropriately expanding the vocabulary to include technical support terms.

But in the afternoon Mr. Manning telephoned to comfort his friend. A reassuring despatch from General French had been published and--all was well--practically--and the British had been splendid. They had been fighting continuously for several days round and about Mons; they had been attacked at odds of six to one, and they had repulsed and inflicted enormous losses on the enemy. They had established an incontestable personal superiority over the Germans. The Germans had been mown down in heaps; the British had charged through their cavalry like charging through paper. So at last and very gloriously for the British, British and German had met in battle. After the hard fighting of the 26th about Landrecies, the British had been comparatively unmolested, reinforcements covering double the losses had joined them and the German advance was definitely checked ... Mr. Britling's mind swung back to elation. He took down the entire despatch from Mr. Manning's dictation, and ran out with it into the garden where Mrs. Britling, with an unwonted expression of anxiety, was presiding over the teas of the usual casual Sunday gathering.... The despatch was read aloud twice over. After that there was hockey and high spirits, and then Mr. Britling went up to his study to answer a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean, the first letter that had come from her since their breach at the outbreak of the war, and which he was now in a better mood to answer than he had been hitherto.

It was mid-afternoon when Mr. Manning telephoned to buoy his friend’s spirits. A reassuring dispatch from General French had just been made public, and all was, for all practical purposes, well; the British troops had performed superbly. They had been locked in nonstop combat for days in and around Mons, attacked by forces outnumbering them six to one, yet they had repulsed the assault and inflicted devastating losses on the enemy. They had established an undeniable edge in individual fighting prowess over the Germans: enemy troops were cut down in heaps, and British soldiers charged through German cavalry formations as easily as if they were tearing through paper. This was the first full pitched battle between British and German forces, and it had ended in resounding British glory. After the brutal fighting on the 26th near Landrecies, the British had faced barely any harassment, fresh reinforcements numbering twice their recent losses had joined their ranks, and the German advance was definitively halted.

Mr. Britling’s mood swung sharply back to elation. He scribbled down the full dispatch as Mr. Manning dictated it over the line, then hurried out to the garden, where Mrs. Britling was overseeing tea for the usual casual Sunday gathering of guests, a rare expression of anxiety tugging at her features. The dispatch was read aloud twice to the group. After that came a game of hockey and raucous high spirits, before Mr. Britling headed up to his study to reply to a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean. It was the first correspondence he had received from her since their falling out the moment war broke out, and he was far better disposed to answer it warmly now than he had been at any point prior.

“Not till you let me go—not till you let me go—Never—never!” screamed the girl. Sikes looked on, for a minute, watching his opportunity, and suddenly pinioning her hands dragged her, struggling and wrestling with him by the way, into a small room adjoining, where he sat himself on a bench, and thrusting her into a chair, held her down by force. She struggled and implored by turns until twelve o’clock had struck, and then, wearied and exhausted, ceased to contest the point any further. With a caution, backed by many oaths, to make no more efforts to go out that night, Sikes left her to recover at leisure and rejoined Fagin.

“Not until you let me go—not until you let me go—Never—never!” the girl shrieked. Sikes watched her for a moment, waiting for his chance, and then, with a sudden grip that trapped her arms, hauled her, fighting and twisting against him, into a little room next door. There he settled himself on a bench, and, forcing her into a chair, held her there by main strength. She struggled and pleaded by turns until twelve o’clock struck, and then, tired out and spent, gave over the struggle at last. After warning her, with many oaths, not to make any further attempt to leave that night, Sikes left her to come round as she could and went back to Fagin.

For users who prefer a condensed summary of the report, Xiaomingbot can provide a short gist version using a pre-trained text summarization model. We choose to use the said model instead of generating the summary directly from the table data because the former can create more general content, and can be employed to process manually written reports as well. There are two approaches to summarize a text: extractive and abstractive summarization. Extractive summarization trains a sentence selection model to pick the important sentences from an input article, while an abstractive summarization will further rephrase the sentences and explore the potential for combining multiple sentences into a simplified one. We trained two summarization models. One is a general text summarization using a BERT-based sequence labelling network. We use the TTNews dataset, a Chinese single document summarization dataset for training from NLPCC 2017 and 2018 shared tasks (Hua et al., 2017; Li and Wan, 2018) . It includes 50,000 Chinese documents with human written summaries. The article is separated into a sequence of sentences. The BERT-based summarization model output 0-1 labels for all sentences.

metadata.title: Xiaomingbot: A Multilingual Robot News Reporter

text: For users who prefer a condensed version of the report, Xiaomingbot can generate a brief gist using a pre-trained text summarization model. We chose this model rather than producing the summary directly from the table data because the former can generate more general content and can also be applied to manually written reports. There are two main approaches to text summarization: extractive and abstractive summarization. Extractive summarization trains a sentence selection model to identify the important sentences in an input article, whereas abstractive summarization further rephrases sentences and investigates the possibility of combining multiple sentences into a simplified one. We trained two summarization models. One is a general text summarization model based on a BERT-based sequence labeling network. For training, we use the TTNews dataset, a Chinese single-document summarization dataset from the NLPCC 2017 and 2018 shared tasks (Hua et al., 2017; Li and Wan, 2018). It contains 50,000 Chinese documents with human-written summaries. The article is segmented into a sequence of sentences, and the BERT-based summarization model outputs 0-1 labels for all sentences.

Since the young man's departure he had sent two postcards of greeting directly to the "Familie Britling," and one letter through the friendly intervention of Mr. Britling's American publisher. Once also he sent a message through a friend in Norway. The postcards simply recorded stages in the passage of a distraught pacifist across Holland to his enrolment. The letter by way of America came two months later. He had been converted into a combatant with extreme rapidity. He had been trained for three weeks, had spent a fortnight in hospital with a severe cold, and had then gone to Belgium as a transport driver--his father had been a horse-dealer and he was familiar with horses. "If anything happens to me," he wrote, "please send my violin at least very carefully to my mother." It was characteristic that he reported himself as very comfortably quartered in Courtrai with "very nice people." The niceness involved restraints. "Only never," he added, "do we talk about the war. It is better not to do so." He mentioned the violin also in the later communication through Norway. Therein he lamented the lost fleshpots of Courtrai. He had been in Posen, and now he was in the Carpathians, up to his knees in snow and "very uncomfortable...."

After the young man left, he mailed two quick greeting postcards directly addressed to the “Familie Britling,” and one letter passed along by Mr. Britling’s friendly American publisher. He also sent a short update once through a mutual acquaintance based in Norway. The postcards did little more than mark each leg of the frazzled pacifist’s journey across Holland as he made his way to enlist. The letter routed through America arrived two months after his departure. He’d been transitioned into a combat role extremely quickly. He’d completed three weeks of training, spent a full fortnight in the hospital with a severe cold, then been deployed to Belgium as a transport driver—his father had worked as a horse dealer, so he knew his way around horses very well. “If anything happens to me,” he wrote, “please at least send my violin to my mother, packed as carefully as you possibly can.” It was perfectly in keeping with his character that he reported staying in very comfortable lodgings in Courtrai with “really nice people.” That niceness came with unspoken constraints, though. “The one thing we never do,” he added, “is talk about the war. It’s better not to bring it up.” He mentioned the violin again in the later communication sent via Norway. In that note, he mourned the easy, pleasant comforts he’d left behind in Courtrai. He’d been stationed in Posen after that, and now he was in the Carpathians, wading through snow up to his knees and “very uncomfortable….”

“I have saved you from being ill-used once, and I will again, and I do now,” continued the girl aloud; “for those who would have fetched you, if I had not, would have been far more rough than me. I have promised for your being quiet and silent; if you are not, you will only do harm to yourself and me too, and perhaps be my death. See here! I have borne all this for you already, as true as God sees me show it.”

“I got you out of being mistreated once, I’ll do it again, and I’m doing it right now,” the girl said loudly. “If I hadn’t come for you, the people who were supposed to fetch you would’ve been way rougher than I am. I already gave my word you’d stay calm and keep quiet. If you don’t, you’ll only hurt both yourself and me, and you might even be the reason I end up dead. Look here! I’ve already put up with all of this for you, and God as my witness, every bit of what I’m saying is true.”

“I thought so. Now, I care a little for your good opinion, and I want to explain something—I have longed to do it ever since I returned, and you looked so gravely at me. For if I were to die—and I may die soon—it would be dreadful that you should always think mistakenly of me. Now, listen.”

“I thought as much. Now, your good opinion matters to me, just a little, and I need to make something clear—I’ve been aching to do this ever since I came back and you met me with that solemn gaze. Because if I were to die—and I might die soon—it would be unbearable for you to go on thinking wrongly of me. So now, listen.”

“On the contrary—” Brissenden paused and ran an insolent eye over Martin’s objective poverty, passing from the well-worn tie and the saw-edged collar to the shiny sleeves of the coat and on to the slight fray of one cuff, winding up and dwelling upon Martin’s sunken cheeks. “On the contrary, hack-work is above you, so far above you that you can never hope to rise to it. Why, man, I could insult you by asking you to have something to eat.”

“On the contrary—” Brissenden stopped and let his eyes travel with open insolence over Martin’s plain poverty, from the threadbare tie and the frayed collar to the polished sleeves of the coat and the slight wear at one cuff, ending with a long look at Martin’s hollow cheeks. “On the contrary, hack work is beneath you—so far beneath you that you can never even hope to reach it. Why, man, I could offend you by asking you to eat something.”

It was the old crowd in which he found himself—the old crowd, with here and there a gap, and here and there a new face. The fellows were not bricklayers, but, as in the old days, they attended all Sunday picnics for the dancing, and the fighting, and the fun. Martin drank with them, and began to feel really human once more. He was a fool to have ever left them, he thought; and he was very certain that his sum of happiness would have been greater had he remained with them and let alone the books and the people who sat in the high places. Yet the beer seemed not so good as of yore. It didn’t taste as it used to taste. Brissenden had spoiled him for steam beer, he concluded, and wondered if, after all, the books had spoiled him for companionship with these friends of his youth. He resolved that he would not be so spoiled, and he went on to the dancing pavilion. Jimmy, the plumber, he met there, in the company of a tall, blond girl who promptly forsook him for Martin.

He found himself surrounded by the familiar old crew—faces he’d known for half his life, dotted with empty spots where old companions used to be, and the occasional unfamiliar newcomer. None of them were stonemasons, but just like they’d done back in their younger years, they showed up to every Sunday riverside picnic for the foot-stomping dancing, the scrappy backlot fights, and the rowdy, unplanned joy of it all. Leo drank round after round with them, and for the first time in months, he felt like a real, flesh-and-blood person instead of a ghost haunting library stacks. He’d been an idiot to ever walk away, he thought; he was certain his total share of happiness would have been far greater if he’d stayed right here, never touching the stuffy leather-bound books or simpering at the pretentious wealthy folks who spent their nights in gilded, high-up drawing rooms. Still, the cheap draft beer didn’t hit the way it used to. It was flat, strangely bitter, nothing like the crisp cold pints he remembered from years back. Elias had ruined him for low-grade bar beer, he concluded, and a cold little niggling thought wondered if all those books had ruined him for the company of the friends he’d grown up with. He shook the thought off, determined not to let that kind of distance take root, and wandered over to the open-air dance pavilion. He ran into Mickey the pipefitter there, accompanied by a lanky, ash-blonde girl who immediately left Mickey’s side to make her way straight over to Leo.

Out of each week’s earnings Chandler set aside $1. At the end of each ten weeks with the extra capital thus accumulated, he purchased one gentleman’s evening from the bargain counter of stingy old Father Time. He arrayed himself in the regalia of millionaires and presidents; he took himself to the quarter where life is brightest and showiest, and there dined with taste and luxury. With ten dollars a man may, for a few hours, play the wealthy idler to perfection. The sum is ample for a well-considered meal, a bottle bearing a respectable label, commensurate tips, a smoke, cab fare and the ordinary etceteras.

Every week, Chandler tucked one dollar away from his earnings. Once every ten weeks, with that small stockpile of savings, he bought himself a proper gentleman’s evening from the discount shelf of thrifty old Father Time. He dressed himself in the kind of fine garments worn by millionaires and heads of state, then headed for the city’s most glittering, ostentatious neighborhood, where he ate a meal curated for elegance and indulgence. Ten dollars is more than enough for a man to pull off the role of a carefree, wealthy idler flawlessly for a few hours. The sum covers a thoughtfully chosen meal, a bottle of reputable wine, appropriately generous tips, a smoke, cab fare, and all the usual small incidental costs that come with such an excursion.

We also include various heuristic functions, each specialised in the recognition of specific types of named entities. Several functions are dedicated to the recognition of proper names based on casing, part-of-speech tags or dependency relations. In addition, we integrate a variety of handcrafted functions relying on regular expressions to detect occurrences of various entities (see Appendix A for details). A probabilistic parser specialised in the recognition of dates, times, money amounts, percents, and cardinal/ordinal values (Braun et al., 2017) is also incorporated. Document-level relations All labelling functions described above rely on local decisions on tokens or phrases. However, texts are not loose collections of words, but exhibit a high degree of internal coherence (Grosz and Sidner, 1986; Grosz et al., 1995) which can be exploited to further improve the annotations.

metadata.title: Named Entity Recognition without Labelled Data: A Weak Supervision Approach

text: We also incorporate a range of heuristic functions, each specialized for recognizing particular types of named entities. Several functions are devoted to identifying proper names on the basis of casing, part-of-speech tags, or dependency relations. In addition, we integrate a variety of handcrafted functions that rely on regular expressions to detect occurrences of different entities (see Appendix A for details). We also include a probabilistic parser specialized in recognizing dates, times, monetary amounts, percentages, and cardinal/ordinal values (Braun et al., 2017). Document-level relations All of the labeling functions described above depend on local decisions about tokens or phrases. However, texts are not merely loose collections of words; they display a high degree of internal coherence (Grosz and Sidner, 1986; Grosz et al., 1995), which can be exploited to further improve the annotations.

She stumbled and crumpled to the ground, her vision nearly completely obscured by blood pouring in thick streams from a deep gash across her forehead. Fighting through waves of sharp, disorienting pain, she strained to push herself up onto her knees, fumbling inside her bodice to draw out a white handkerchief—Rose Maylie’s very own. Clasping it tight in her folded hands, she lifted it as high toward the heavens as her waning strength would permit, then breathed out a quiet, shaking prayer begging her Creator for mercy.

To this end, Yuan et al. (2018) introduced a new setting that the KG model should predict multiple keyphrases and simultaneously decide the suitable keyphrase number for the given document. Two models with a sequential decoding process, catSeq and catSeqD, are proposed in Yuan et al. (2018) . The catSeq is also an attentional encoder-decoder model with copy mechanism (See et al., 2017) , but adopting new training and inference setup to fit the new setting. The cat-SeqD is an extension of catSeq with orthogonal regularization (Bousmalis et al., 2016) and target encoding. Lately, Chan et al. (2019) proposed a reinforcement learning based fine-tuning method, which fine-tunes the pre-trained models with adaptive rewards for generating more sufficient and accurate keyphrases. We follow the same setting with Yuan et al. (2018) and propose an exclusive hierarchical decoding method for the KG problem. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time the hierarchical decoding is explored in the KG problem. Different from the hierarchical decoding in other areas (Fan et al., 2018; Yarats and Lewis, 2018; Tan et al., 2017; Chen and Zhuge, 2018) , we rescale the attention score of each WD step with the corresponding PD attention score to provide aspect guidance when generating keyphrases. Moreover, either a soft or a hard exclusion mechanism is innovatively incorporated in the decoding process to improve generation diversity.

metadata.title: Exclusive Hierarchical Decoding for Deep Keyphrase Generation

text: To this end, Yuan et al. (2018) introduced a novel setting in which a KG model must predict multiple keyphrases while also determining the appropriate number of keyphrases for a given document. In Yuan et al. (2018), two models with sequential decoding, catSeq and catSeqD, were proposed. catSeq is likewise an attentional encoder-decoder model with a copy mechanism (See et al., 2017), but it adopts a new training and inference framework tailored to this setting. catSeqD extends catSeq by incorporating orthogonal regularization (Bousmalis et al., 2016) and target encoding. More recently, Chan et al. (2019) proposed a reinforcement learning-based fine-tuning approach that adapts pre-trained models with dynamic rewards to generate more complete and accurate keyphrases. Following the same setting as Yuan et al. (2018), we propose an exclusive hierarchical decoding method for the KG task. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to explore hierarchical decoding for KG. Unlike hierarchical decoding methods in other domains (Fan et al., 2018; Yarats and Lewis, 2018; Tan et al., 2017; Chen and Zhuge, 2018), we rescale the attention score at each WD step by the corresponding PD attention score to provide aspect guidance during keyphrase generation. In addition, we innovatively incorporate either a soft or a hard exclusion mechanism into the decoding procedure to enhance generation diversity.

Statistical alignment models directly build on the lexical translation models of Brown et al. (1993) , known as the IBM models. The most popular statistical alignment tool is GIZA++ Ney, 2000b, 2003; Gao and Vogel, 2008) . For optimal performance, the training pipeline of GIZA++ relies on multiple iterations of IBM Model 1, Model 3, Model 4 and the HMM alignment model (Vogel et al., 1996) . Initialized with parameters from previous models, each subsequent model adds more assumptions about word alignments. Model 2 introduces non-uniform distortion, and Model 3 introduces fertility. Model 4 and the HMM alignment model introduce relative distortion, where the likelihood of the position of each alignment link is conditioned on the position of the previous alignment link. While simpler and faster tools exist such as FastAlign (Dyer et al., 2013) , which is based on a reparametrization of IBM Model 2, the GIZA++ implementation of Model 4 is still used today in applications where alignment quality is important.

metadata.title: End-to-End Neural Word Alignment Outperforms GIZA++

text: Statistical alignment models are built directly on the lexical translation models of Brown et al. (1993), commonly referred to as the IBM models. The most widely used statistical alignment tool is GIZA++ (Ney, 2000b, 2003; Gao and Vogel, 2008). To achieve optimal performance, the GIZA++ training pipeline depends on multiple iterations of IBM Model 1, Model 3, Model 4, and the HMM alignment model (Vogel et al., 1996). With parameters initialized from earlier models, each successive model incorporates additional assumptions about word alignment. Model 2 adds non-uniform distortion, and Model 3 adds fertility. Model 4 and the HMM alignment model introduce relative distortion, in which the probability of each alignment link’s position is conditioned on the position of the preceding alignment link. Although simpler and faster tools such as FastAlign (Dyer et al., 2013), which is based on a reparametrization of IBM Model 2, are available, the GIZA++ implementation of Model 4 remains in use today in applications where alignment quality is important.

The Boss has never stirred--sleeps like a stone. If he does not wake to-day we shall understand what kind of a sleep it is, and his body will then be borne to a place in one of the remote recesses of the cave where none will ever find it to desecrate it. As for the rest of us--well, it is agreed that if any one of us ever escapes alive from this place, he will write the fact here, and loyally hide this Manuscript with The Boss, our dear good chief, whose property it is, be he alive or dead.

Our leader has not moved; he remains in a slumber as heavy as stone. If he does not rouse himself by nightfall, we will finally understand the true nature of his rest, and we shall carry his body to a secluded hollow deep within the cave where it can never be found or disturbed. As for the survivors, we have resolved that if any one of us manages to escape this place alive, he will record the event here and faithfully conceal this manuscript with our beloved chief, for it is his rightful property, whether he breathes or not.

The third draught that I craved from the fount of knowledge was enlightenment concerning the character known as A Man About Town. He was more vague in my mind than a type should be. We must have a concrete idea of anything, even if it be an imaginary idea, before we can comprehend it. Now, I have a mental picture of John Doe that is as clear as a steel engraving. His eyes are weak blue; he wears a brown vest and a shiny black serge coat. He stands always in the sunshine chewing something; and he keeps half-shutting his pocket knife and opening it again with his thumb. And, if the Man Higher Up is ever found, take my assurance for it, he will be a large, pale man with blue wristlets showing under his cuffs, and he will be sitting to have his shoes polished within sound of a bowling alley, and there will be somewhere about him turquoises.

The third sip I desired from the wellspring of understanding was illumination regarding the figure called A Man About Town. He lingered in my thoughts more indistinctly than any archetype should. We must possess a tangible notion of a thing, even an invented one, before we can truly grasp it. For instance, I carry a mental image of John Doe as crisp as an etched copperplate. His gaze is a watery blue; he sports a tan waistcoat and a glossy black serge jacket. He is forever poised in sunlight, chewing on something, his pocket knife half-closed and flicked open again with his thumb. And should the Man Higher Up ever be discovered, you may rely on my word—he will be a tall, pallid gentleman with blue wristbands peeking beneath his sleeves, seated to have his boots buffed within earshot of a skittle alley, and somewhere near him there will be turquoises.

He grew quite eager for the day when his old comrade should be afforded so grand a stage for the exercise of his particular gifts.

Such views of life were to some extent the natural begettings of her situation upon her nature. To dwell on a heath without studying its meanings was like wedding a foreigner without learning his tongue. The subtle beauties of the heath were lost to Eustacia; she only caught its vapours. An environment which would have made a contented woman a poet, a suffering woman a devotee, a pious woman a psalmist, even a giddy woman thoughtful, made a rebellious woman saturnine.

Her outlook on life was, in part, the inevitable offspring of her circumstances acting upon her character. To inhabit a heath without deciphering its language was akin to marrying a stranger without ever learning his speech. The heath’s delicate loveliness escaped Eustacia entirely; she perceived only its mists. A setting that might have shaped a contented soul into a poet, a sorrowful one into a mystic, a devout one into a hymnist, or even a frivolous one into a thinker, instead turned a defiant woman sullen.
